dṛśyate = is seen (pass.)		 Thirty-six (ṣattriṃśat) finger-widths (aṅgulaḥ) the breath (haṃsaḥ) makes (kurute) its journey (prayāṇam) outside (bahiḥ). By the left-right path (vāmadakṣiṇamārgeṇa) restlessness (cañcalatvam) is not (na) seen (dṛśyate). Breath measurement and balance
[√kṛ - to make, √dṛś - to see]
36-finger external breath; ida-pingala balance.		 The Goose (haṃsa) that is the out- (baḥ) and in- (sa) breaths extends thirty-six fingers externally. When the flow in the left and right channels is constant and all the clogged channels become pure then the yogi is ready to control the breath.
śuddhimeti yadā sarvaṃ nāḍīcakraṃ malākulam |
tadaiva jāyate yogī prāṇasaṃgrahaṇe kṣamaḥ ||76||

De-sandhi:
śuddhim eti yadā sarvam nāḍī-cakram malākulam |
tadā eva jāyate yogī prāṇa-saṃgrahaṇe kṣamaḥ ||		 śuddhim = purification (acc.)
eti = attains (3rd sg.)
yadā = when
sarvam = all (nom.)
nāḍī-cakram = channel-wheel (nom.)
malākulam = polluted (nom.)
tadā = then
eva = indeed
jāyate = becomes (3rd sg.)
yogī = yogi (nom.)
prāṇa-saṃgrahaṇe = in breath retention (loc.)
kṣamaḥ = capable (nom.)		 When (yadā) the whole (sarvam) polluted (malākulam) nāḍī-wheel (nāḍīcakram) attains (eti) purification (śuddhim), then (tadā) indeed (eva) the yogi (yogī) becomes (jāyate) capable (kṣamaḥ) in breath retention (prāṇasaṃgrahaṇe). Nāḍī purification for breath control
[√i - to go, √jan - to be born]
Channel purification prerequisite for advanced prāṇāyāma.		 When the flow in the left and right channels is constant (76) and all the clogged channels become pure then the yogi is ready to control the breath.
baddhapadmāsano yogī prāṇaṃ candreṇa pūrayet |
dhārayitvā yathāśakti bhūyaḥ sūryeṇa recayet ||77||

De-sandhi:
baddha-padma-āsanaḥ yogī prāṇam candreṇa pūrayet |
dhārayitvā yathā-śakti bhūyaḥ sūryeṇa recayet ||		 baddha-padma-āsanaḥ = in bound lotus posture (nom.)
yogī = yogi (nom.)
prāṇam = breath (acc.)
candreṇa = through moon/left nostril (inst.)
pūrayet = should fill (opt.)
dhārayitvā = having retained (abs.)
yathā-śakti = according to capacity (adv.)
bhūyaḥ = again
sūryeṇa = through sun/right nostril (inst.)
recayet = should exhale (opt.)		 The yogi (yogī) in bound lotus posture (baddhapadmāsanaḥ) should fill (pūrayet) breath (prāṇam) through the moon (candreṇa), having retained (dhārayitvā) according to capacity (yathāśakti), again (bhūyaḥ) should exhale (recayet) through the sun (sūryeṇa). Lunar-solar breathing technique
[√pṝ - to fill, √dhṛ - to hold, √ric - to empty]
Ida inhalation, retention, pingala exhalation.		 Sitting in the lotus position, the yogi should fill himself up with prāṇa using the moon [i.e. the left channel], hold it for as long as he can and exhale through the sun [i.e. the right channel].
amṛtodadhimadhyasthaṃ kṣīradhavalaprabhām |
dhyātvā candramaso bimbaṃ prāṇāyāme sukhī bhavet ||78||

De-sandhi:
amṛta-udadhi-madhya-stham kṣīra-dhavala-prabhām |
dhyātvā candramasaḥ bimbam prāṇāyāme sukhī bhavet ||		 amṛta-udadhi-madhya-stham = situated in nectar-ocean center (acc.)
kṣīra-dhavala-prabhām = milk-white brilliance (acc.)
dhyātvā = having meditated (abs.)
candramasaḥ = of moon (gen.)
bimbam = orb (acc.)
prāṇāyāme = in breath control (loc.)
sukhī = happy (nom.)
bhavet = becomes (opt.)		 Having meditated (dhyātvā) on the moon's (candramasaḥ) orb (bimbam) situated (stham) in the center (madhya) of the nectar ocean (amṛtodadhi), with milk-white (kṣīradhavala) brilliance (prabhām), one becomes (bhavet) happy (sukhī) in breath control (prāṇāyāme). Lunar visualization in prāṇāyāma
[√dhyai - to meditate, √bhū - to become]
Cooling lunar imagery for breath practice.		 Visualising the disc of the moon as the nectar of immortality, looking like curd, as white as milk, he gets pleasure in controlling the breath.
prāṇaṃ sūryeṇa cākṛṣya pūrayedudaram śanaiḥ |
viśiṣṭavatkumbhakaṃ kṛtvā punaścandreṇa recayet ||79||

De-sandhi:
prāṇam sūryeṇa ca ākṛṣya pūrayet udaram śanaiḥ |
viśiṣṭa-vat kumbhakam kṛtvā punaḥ candreṇa recayet ||		 prāṇam = breath (acc.)
sūryeṇa = with sun/right nostril (inst.)
ca = and
ākṛṣya = having drawn (abs.)
pūrayet = should fill (opt.)
udaram = belly (acc.)
śanaiḥ = slowly (adv.)
viśiṣṭa-vat = in special way (adv.)
kumbhakam = retention (acc.)
kṛtvā = having done (abs.)
punaḥ = again
candreṇa = through moon/left nostril (inst.)
recayet = should exhale (opt.)		 Having drawn (ākṛṣya) breath (prāṇam) with the sun (sūryeṇa) and (ca), one should fill (pūrayet) the belly (udaram) slowly (śanaiḥ). Having done (kṛtvā) retention (kumbhakam) in a special way (viśiṣṭavat), again (punaḥ) one should exhale (recayet) through the moon (candreṇa). Complete breath practice cycle
[√kṛṣ - to draw, √pṝ - to fill, √ric - to empty]
Solar inhalation, belly breathing, retention, lunar exhalation.		 He should slowly fill up his belly by drawing in prāṇa through the sun. After holding the breath as prescribed he should again exhale through the moon.
prajvalajjvalanajvālāpuñcamādityamaṇḍalam |
dhyātvā nābhisthitam yogī praṇayāme sukhī bhavet ||80||

De-sandhi:
prajvala-jjalana-jvālā-puñca-āditya-maṇḍalam |
dhyātvā nābhi-sthitam yogī prāṇa-yāme sukhī bhavet ||		 prajvala = blazing/bright (adj.)
jjalana = burning/fiery (adj.)
jvālā = flame (f.)
puñca = bunch/cluster (n.)
āditya = sun (m.)
maṇḍalam = disc/orb (acc.)
dhyātvā = having meditated (abs.)
nābhi-sthitam = situated at the navel (acc.)
yogī = yogi (nom.)
prāṇa-yāme = in breath control (loc.)
sukhī = comfortable/happy (nom.)
bhavet = becomes (opt.)		 Having meditated (dhyātvā) on the solar disc (ādityamaṇḍalam) with clusters of blazing burning flames (prajvalajjvalanajvālāpuñca), situated at the navel (nābhisthitam), the yogi (yogī) becomes (bhavet) comfortable (sukhī) in breath control (praṇayāme). Solar meditation at navel center
[Visualization of fiery solar energy at manipura chakra during prāṇāyāma]		 Meditating on the orb of the sun as a mass of blazing flames, he gets pleasure in controlling the breath.
praṇam cediḍayā pibenniyāmitaṃ bhuyo nyayā recayet |
piitvā piṇgalayā samīraṇamalaṃ baddhā tyajedvāmayā ||81||

De-sandhi:
prāṇam ca iḍayā pibet niyāmitam bhuyas tu ayā recayet |
pītvā piṇgalayā samīraṇam alam baddhvā tyajet vāmayā ||		 prāṇam = breath (acc.)
ca = and
iḍayā = through ida (inst.)
pibet = should drink/inhale (opt.)
niyāmitam = controlled/regulated (acc.)
bhuyas = again
tu = but
ayā = through this (inst.)
recayet = should exhale (opt.)
pītvā = having drunk/inhaled (abs.)
piṇgalayā = through pingala (inst.)
samīraṇam = wind/breath (acc.)
alam = enough/sufficient
baddhvā = having bound/retained (abs.)
tyajet = should release (opt.)
vāmayā = through the left (inst.)		 One should drink (pibet) controlled (niyāmitaṃ) breath (prāṇam) through iḍā (iḍayā) and (ca) again (bhuyo) exhale (recayet) through it; having drunk (pītvā) sufficient (alam) wind (samīraṇam) through piṅgalā (piṇgalayā), having retained (baddhvā) it, one should release (tyajet) through the left (vāmayā). Alternate nostril breathing sequence
[Systematic practice switching between iḍā (left) and piṅgalā (right) channels]		 If he performs a controlled inhalation through the left channel (iḍā) and again exhales through the other channel, then inhales through the right channel (piṅgalā), holds it sufficiently long, releases it through the left [channel] and meditates on the two orbs of the sun and moon in this way, all the ascetic's channels are purified after three months.
sūryācandramasoranena vidhinā bimbadvayaṃ dhyāyataṃ |
śuddhā nāḍīgaṇā bhavanti yaminaṃ māsatrayāḍūrdhvataḥ ||82||

De-sandhi:
sūrya-candramasoh anena vidhinā bimba-dvayam dhyāyatam |
śuddhāḥ nāḍī-gaṇāḥ bhavanti yaminām māsa-trayāt ūrdhvataḥ ||		 sūrya = sun (m.)
candramasoh = of the moon (gen.)
anena = by this
vidhinā = method/procedure (inst.)
bimba-dvayam = pair of discs (acc.)
dhyāyatam = should meditate (imp.)
śuddhāḥ = pure/clean (nom. pl.)
nāḍī-gaṇāḥ = groups of channels (nom. pl.)
bhavanti = become (pres.)
yaminām = of yogis (gen. pl.)
māsa-trayāt = from three months (abl.)
ūrdhvataḥ = upward/after		 By this (anena) method (vidhinā) one should meditate (dhyāyataṃ) on the pair of discs (bimbadvayaṃ) of sun and moon (sūryācandramasoḥ). The groups of channels (nāḍīgaṇā) become (bhavanti) pure (śuddhā) for yogis (yaminaṃ) after (ūrdhvataḥ) three months (māsatrayāt). Timeline for nāḍī purification
[Solar-lunar meditation practice yields channel purification in three months]		 As a result of cleansing the channels, the yogi can hold his breath as long as he wants, his
yyatheṣṭaṃ dhāraṇā dvāyoranalasya pradīpanam |
nādābhivyaktirārogyaṃ jāyate nāḍiśodhanāṭ ||83||

De-sandhi:
yatha-iṣṭam dhāraṇā dvayoh analasya pradīpanam |
nāda-abhivyaktiḥ ārogyam jāyate nāḍī-śodhanāt ||		 yatha-iṣṭam = as desired
dhāraṇā = concentration (f.)
dvayoh = of both (gen. dual)
analasya = of fire (gen.)
pradīpanam = kindling/illumination (n.)
nāda = inner sound (m.)
abhivyaktiḥ = manifestation (f.)
ārogyam = health (n.)
jāyate = arises/is born (pres.)
nāḍī-śodhanāt = from channel purification (abl.)		 As desired (yatheṣṭaṃ) concentration (dhāraṇā) of both (dvāyoḥ), kindling (pradīpanam) of fire (analasya), manifestation (abhivyaktiḥ) of inner sound (nāda) and health (ārogyaṃ) arise (jāyate) from channel purification (nāḍiśodhanāt). Results of nāḍī purification
[Multiple benefits: concentration, digestive fire, inner sound, and health]		 inner fire is kindled, the inner sounds are revealed and he is free from disease.
prāṇo dehasthito vāyurāyāmaśca nibabdhanam |
ekaśvāsamayī mātroddhāṭayedgagane gatim ||84||

De-sandhi:
prāṇaḥ deha-sthitaḥ vāyuḥ āyāmaḥ ca nibandhanam |
eka-śvāsa-mayī mātrā uddhāṭayet gagane gatim ||		 prāṇaḥ = vital breath (nom.)
deha-sthitaḥ = body-dwelling (nom.)
vāyuḥ = wind/air (nom.)
āyāmaḥ = extension/control (nom.)
ca = and
nibandhanam = restraint/binding (n.)
eka = one
śvāsa-mayī = breath-consisting (f.)
mātrā = measure/unit (f.)
uddhāṭayet = should lift up (opt.)
gagane = in the sky/space (loc.)
gatim = movement/path (acc.)		 Vital breath (prāṇo) is the wind (vāyur) dwelling in the body (dehasthito), extension (āyāmaś) and (ca) restraint (nibabdhanam). One unit (mātrā) consisting of a single breath (ekaśvāsamayī) should lift up (uddhāṭayet) the movement (gatim) in space (gagane). Definition of prāṇāyāma components
[Technical breakdown of breath control elements and their spatial effects]		 Prāṇa is air in the body; āyāma is [its] restraint. A measure of one breath opens the path to the skull (gagana, lit. "sky").
recakaḥ pūrakaścaiva kumbhakaḥ praṇavātmakaḥ |
prāṇāyāmo bhavettredhā mātrādvādaśasaṃyutaḥ ||85||

De-sandhi:
recakaḥ pūrakaḥ ca eva kumbhakaḥ praṇava-ātmakaḥ |
prāṇāyāmaḥ bhavet tredhā mātrā-dvādaśa-saṃyutaḥ ||		 recakaḥ = exhalation (nom.)
pūrakaḥ = inhalation (nom.)
ca eva = and indeed
kumbhakaḥ = retention (nom.)
praṇava-ātmakaḥ = having the nature of OM (nom.)
prāṇāyāmaḥ = breath control (nom.)
bhavet = becomes (opt.)
tredhā = in three ways
mātrā = units/measures (f.)
dvādaśa = twelve
saṃyutaḥ = combined with (nom.)		 Exhalation (recakaḥ), inhalation (pūrakaś) and indeed (caiva) retention (kumbhakaḥ) having the nature of OM (praṇavātmakaḥ) — breath control (prāṇāyāmo) becomes (bhavet) threefold (tredhā) combined with (saṃyutaḥ) twelve (dvādaśa) units (mātrā). Three components of prāṇāyāma with timing
[Classical definition with OM-based counting system of 12 units]		 Exhalation, inhalation and restraint have oṃ as their essence. Breath-control (prāṇāyāma) is in units of twelve measures and threefold.
prāṇayāmaśca bhānuśca mātrādvādaśayānvitaḥ |
nirdahenmalajātāni bahirantaḥ śarīriṇāṃ ||86||

De-sandhi:
prāṇa-yāmaḥ ca bhānuḥ ca mātrā-dvādaśaya-anvitaḥ |
nirdahet mala-jātāni bahiḥ-antaḥ śarīriṇām ||		 prāṇa-yāmaḥ = breath control (nom.)
ca = and
bhānuḥ = sun/solar energy (nom.)
ca = and
mātrā-dvādaśaya = with twelve measures (inst.)
anvitaḥ = endowed with (nom.)
nirdahet = burns away (opt.)
mala-jātāni = born impurities (acc. pl.)
bahiḥ-antaḥ = external-internal
śarīriṇām = of embodied beings (gen. pl.)		 Breath control (prāṇayāmaś) and (ca) solar energy (bhānuś) and (ca) endowed with (anvitaḥ) twelve measures (mātrādvādaśaya) burns away (nirdahet) impurities born (malajātāni) externally and internally (bahirantaḥ) of embodied beings (śarīriṇāṃ). Purifying effects of regulated breathing
[Solar prāṇāyāma with 12-count timing removes all bodily impurities]		 Both breath control and the sun have twelve parts. They consume the external and internal impurities of the embodied. Yogis should always know the moon and the sun to have twelve parts.
mātrādvādaśasaṃyuktaniśākaradivākarau |
doṣajākamapadhnantai jṇātavyau yogibhiḥ sadā ||87||

De-sandhi:
mātrā-dvādaśa-saṃyukta-niśākara-divākarau |
doṣa-jāt-akamat-adhvantau jñātavyau yogibhiḥ sadā ||		 mātrā-dvādaśa = twelve measures
saṃyukta = combined (adj.)
niśākara = moon/night-maker (m.)
divākarau = sun/day-maker (nom. dual)
doṣa-jāt = born from defects (abl.)
akamat = without desire
adhvantau = destroyers (nom. dual)
jñātavyau = should be known (nom. dual)
yogibhiḥ = by yogis (inst. pl.)
sadā = always		 Combined with (saṃyukta) twelve measures (mātrādvādaśa), the moon and sun (niśākaradivākarau), destroyers (adhvantau) of defects born (doṣajāt) without desire (akamat), should be known (jñātavyau) by yogis (yogibhiḥ) always (sadā). Lunar-solar breathing for defect removal
[12-count moon-sun meditation destroys constitutional imbalances effortlessly]		 In inhalation there are twelve oṃs, in breath retention sixteen are taught [and] in exhalation ten. That is said to be breath control.
pūrake dvādaśa jñeyāḥ kumbhake ṣodaśa smṛtāḥ |
recake daśa omkāraḥ prāṇayāmaḥ sa uccyate ||88||

De-sandhi:
pūrake dvādaśa jñeyāḥ kumbhake ṣodaśa smṛtāḥ |
recake daśa om-kāraḥ prāṇa-yāmaḥ saḥ uccyate ||		 pūrake = in inhalation (loc.)
dvādaśa = twelve
jñeyāḥ = should be known (nom. pl.)
kumbhake = in retention (loc.)
ṣodaśa = sixteen
smṛtāḥ = remembered/counted (nom. pl.)
recake = in exhalation (loc.)
daśa = ten
om-kāraḥ = OM sounds (nom. pl.)
prāṇa-yāmaḥ = breath control (nom.)
saḥ = that
uccyate = is called/said (pass.)		 In inhalation (pūrake) twelve (dvādaśa) should be known (jñeyāḥ), in retention (kumbhake) sixteen (ṣodaśa) are counted (smṛtāḥ), in exhalation (recake) ten (daśa) OM sounds (omkāraḥ) — that (sa) is called (uccyate) breath control (prāṇayāmaḥ). Standard prāṇāyāma timing ratio
[Classic 12:16:10 count ratio for inhalation:retention:exhalation with OM]		 Breath-control's definition is thus: in the lowest there are twelve measures, in the middle twice that and in the highest three times.
adhame dvā daśa proktā madhyame dviduṇāḥ smṛtāḥ |
uttame triguṇā mātrā prāṇāyāmasya nirṇayaḥ ||89||

De-sandhi:
adhame dvā daśa proktāḥ madhyame dvi-guṇāḥ smṛtāḥ |
uttame tri-guṇāḥ mātrāḥ prāṇāyāmasya nirṇayaḥ ||		 adhame = in the lowest (loc.)
dvā daśa = two tens/twenty
proktāḥ = spoken/prescribed (nom. pl.)
madhyame = in the middle (loc.)
dvi-guṇāḥ = doubled (nom. pl.)
smṛtāḥ = remembered/counted (nom. pl.)
uttame = in the highest (loc.)
tri-guṇāḥ = tripled (nom. pl.)
mātrāḥ = measures (nom. pl.)
prāṇāyāmasya = of breath control (gen.)
nirṇayaḥ = determination/classification (nom.)		 In the lowest (adhame) twenty (dvā daśa) are prescribed (proktā), in the middle (madhyame) doubled (dviduṇāḥ) are counted (smṛtāḥ), in the highest (uttame) tripled (triguṇā) measures (mātrā) — this is the classification (nirṇayaḥ) of breath control (prāṇāyāmasya). Three levels of prāṇāyāma practice
[Progressive stages: basic (20), intermediate (40), advanced (60) count measures]
adhame tveka uddhāto dvāduddhātau ca madhyame |
uddhātatritayaṃ prāhuḥ prāṇāyāme tathottame ||90||

De-sandhi:
adhame tu eka uddhātaḥ dvā uddhātau ca madhyame |
uddhāta-tritayam prāhuḥ prāṇāyāme tathā uttame ||		 adhame = in the lowest (loc.)
tu = but/indeed
eka = one
uddhātaḥ = elevation/lifting (nom.)
dvā uddhātau = two elevations (nom. dual)
ca = and
madhyame = in the middle (loc.)
uddhāta-tritayam = three elevations (acc.)
prāhuḥ = they say (pres.)
prāṇāyāme = in breath control (loc.)
tathā = thus/so
uttame = in the highest (loc.)		 In the lowest (adhame) indeed (tu) one (eka) elevation (uddhāto), and (ca) two elevations (dvāduddhātau) in the middle (madhyame), three elevations (uddhātatritayaṃ) they say (prāhuḥ) thus (tathā) in the highest (uttame) breath control (prāṇāyāme). Progressive elevation stages
[Ascending levels of pranic elevation: 1, 2, and 3 elevations respectively]		 In the lowest there is one eruption (udghāta), in the middle, two, and in the highest breath-control three eruptions are taught.
adhame ca gharmaḥ kampo bhavati madhyame |
uttiṣṭhaty uttame deho baddhapamāsano muhuḥ ||91||

De-sandhi:
adhame ca gharmaḥ kampaḥ bhavati madhyame | utतिṣṭhati uttame dehaḥ baddha-padma-āsanaḥ muhuḥ ||		 adhame = in the lowest (loc.)
ca = and
gharmaḥ = heat/intense heat (nom.)
kampaḥ = trembling/shaking (nom.)
bhavati = occurs/happens (pres.)
madhyame = in the middle (loc.)
uttiṣṭhati = rises up (pres.)
uttame = in the highest (loc.)
dehaḥ = body (nom.)
baddha-padma = bound lotus
āsanaḥ = seated/in posture (nom.)
muhuḥ = repeatedly/again and again		 In the lowest (adhame) and (ca) intense heat (gharmaḥ), trembling (kampo) occurs (bhavati) in the middle (madhyame). In the highest (uttame) the body (deho) rises up (uttiṣṭhaty) repeatedly (muhuḥ) in bound lotus posture (baddhapamāsano). Progressive physical effects of prāṇāyāma
[Three stages: heat generation, trembling, and bodily elevation in lotus posture]		 In the lowest intense heat [arises], in the middle there is trembling and in the highest the body, bound in the lotus posture, rises up repeatedly.
baddhapadmāsano yogī namaskṛtya śivaṃ gurum |
nāsāgradṛiṣṭirekānte praṇāyāmam samācaret ||92||

De-sandhi:
baddha-padma-āsanaḥ yogī namaskṛtya śivam gurum | nāsā-agra-dṛṣṭiḥ ekānte prāṇa-yāmam samācaret ||		 baddha-padma = bound lotus
āsanaḥ = seated/in posture (nom.)
yogī = yogi (nom.)
namaskṛtya = having bowed/paid reverence (abs.)
śivam = auspicious/Śiva (acc.)
gurum = guru/teacher (acc.)
nāsā-agra = tip of the nose
dṛṣṭiḥ = gaze/vision (nom.)
ekānte = in a secluded place (loc.)
prāṇa-yāmam = breath control (acc.)
samācaret = should practice (opt.)		 The yogi (yogī) seated in bound lotus posture (baddhapadmāsano), having bowed (namaskṛtya) to the auspicious guru (śivaṃ gurum), with gaze at the tip of the nose (nāsāgradṛiṣṭir) in a secluded place (ekānte), should practice (samācaret) breath control (praṇāyāmam). Proper prāṇāyāma practice setup
[Prerequisites: lotus posture, guru reverence, nose-tip gazing, solitary location]		 Sitting in the lotus posture in a secluded place the yogi should pay reverence to his guru, Śiva, look at the tip of his nose and practice breath-restraint.
ūrdhvamākṛṣya cāpānavātaṃ prāṇe niyojayet |
mūrddhānaṃ nayate śaktyā sarvapāpaiḥ pramucyate ||93||

De-sandhi:
ūrdhvam ākṛṣya ca apāna-vātam prāṇe niyojayet | mūrddhānam nayate śaktyā sarva-pāpaiḥ pramucyate ||		 ūrdhvam = upward
ākṛṣya = having drawn (abs.)
ca = and
apāna-vātam = apāna breath/wind (acc.)
prāṇe = in/with prāṇa (loc.)
niyojayet = should join/unite (opt.)
mūrddhānam = to the head (acc.)
nayate = leads/takes (pres.)
śaktyā = by power/by the goddess (inst.)
sarva-pāpaiḥ = from all sins (abl.)
pramucyate = is freed/liberated (pres. pass.)		 Having drawn (ākṛṣya) the apāna breath (apānavātaṃ) upward (ūrdhvam) and (ca), one should unite (niyojayet) it with prāṇa (prāṇe). By the power (śaktyā) it leads (nayate) to the head (mūrddhānaṃ) and one is freed (pramucyate) from all sins (sarvapāpaiḥ). Kuṇḍalinī awakening through breath union
[Drawing apāna upward, uniting with prāṇa, leading divine power to crown, liberation from karma]		 He should draw up the apāna breath, join it with the prāṇa breath and lead [them] up to the head using the goddess [Kuṇḍalinī]. He is freed from all sins.
dvārāṇāṃ navakaṃ nirudhya marutaṃ pītvā dṛḍhaṃ dhāritam
nītvākāśamapānavahnisahitaṃ śaktyā samucchalitam |
ātmadhyānayutaḥ praṇāmavidhināvaṣṭabhya mūrdhnā bhujaṃ
yāvattiṣṭhati tāvadāyanaśataṃ śreyaḥphalaṃ vindati ||94||

De-sandhi:
dvārāṇām navakam nirudhya marutam pītvā dṛḍham dhāritam
nītvā ākāśam apāna-vahni-sahitam śaktyā samucchalitam |
ātma-dhyāna-yutaḥ praṇāma-vidhinā avaṣṭabhya mūrdhnā bhujam
yāvat tiṣṭhati tāvat āyana-śatam śreyaḥ-phalam vindati ||		 dvārāṇām = of the doors/openings (gen. pl.)
navakam = group of nine (acc.)
nirudhya = having blocked (abs. ni-√rudh)
marutam = breath/wind (acc.)
pītvā = having drunk/inhaled (abs. √pā)
dṛḍham = firmly (adv.)
dhāritam = held (PPP acc.)
nītvā = having led (abs. √nī)
ākāśam = to the ether/space (acc.)
apāna-vahni-sahitam = together with apāna and fire (acc.)
śaktyā = by the power/goddess (inst.)
samucchalitam = jerked/lifted up (PPP)
ātma-dhyāna-yutaḥ = endowed with self-meditation (nom.)
praṇāma-vidhinā = in the manner of salutation (inst.)
avaṣṭabhya = having supported (abs. ava-√stambh)
mūrdhnā = with the head (inst.)
bhujam = arm (acc.)
yāvat = as long as
tiṣṭhati = he remains (3rd sg.)
tāvat = so long
āyana-śatam = hundred-fold (acc.)
śreyaḥ-phalam = supreme fruit (acc.)
vindati = obtains (3rd sg.)		 Having blocked (nirudhya) the nine (navakam) doors (dvārāṇām), having inhaled (pītvā) the breath (marutam) and held it (dhāritam) firmly (dṛḍham), having led (nītvā) it to the ether (ākāśam) together with apāna and fire (apānavahnisahitam), lifted up (samucchalitam) by the goddess (śaktyā) — endowed with meditation on the self (ātmadhyānayutaḥ), in the manner of salutation (praṇāmavidhinā), having supported (avaṣṭabhya) the head (mūrdhnā) with the arm (bhujam), for as long as (yāvat) he remains (tiṣṭhati), for that long (tāvat) he obtains (vindati) the supreme fruit (śreyaḥphalam) a hundred-fold (āyanaśatam). Nine-door breath retention with head-support posture
[Blocking nine bodily openings, inhaling, leading breath with apāna and digestive fire to ether via kuṇḍalinī, self-meditation in salutation posture yields hundredfold reward]		 Blocking the nine doors he should inhale air, hold it firmly and lead it to the ether together with apāna and fire, jerking it by means of the goddess [Kuṇḍalinī]. For as long as he remains meditating on the self while [supporting the head with the arm] in the manner of paying homage he obtains the ultimate reward a hundred times over.
prāṇāyāmo bhavatyevaṃ pātakendhanapāvakaḥ |
bhavodadhimahāsetuḥ sevito yogibhiḥ sadā ||95||

De-sandhi:
prāṇāyāmaḥ bhavati evam pātaka-indhana-pāvakaḥ |
bhava-udadhi-mahā-setuḥ sevitaḥ yogibhiḥ sadā ||		 prāṇāyāmaḥ = breath control (nom.)
bhavati = is/becomes (3rd sg.)
evam = thus
pātaka-indhana-pāvakaḥ = fire with sins as fuel (BV, nom.)
bhava-udadhi = ocean of existence (TP)
mahā-setuḥ = great bridge (nom.)
sevitaḥ = resorted to/practiced (PPP nom.)
yogibhiḥ = by yogis (inst. pl.)
sadā = always/constantly		 Breath control (prāṇāyāmaḥ) thus (evam) is (bhavati) a fire (pāvakaḥ) that uses sins as fuel (pātakendhanam), a great bridge (mahāsetuḥ) across the ocean of existence (bhavodadhi), always (sadā) resorted to (sevitaḥ) by yogis (yogibhiḥ). Prāṇāyāma as destroyer of sins and bridge to liberation
[Two powerful metaphors: fire burning sins as fuel; bridge crossing saṃsāra's ocean]		 Breath control like this is the burner of sins; a bridge across the ocean of existence, continuously resorted to by yogis.
BENEFITS OF THE SIX LIMBS - ṣaḍaṅgaphalam (VERSES 96-100)
āsanena rujo hanti prāṇāyāmena pātakam |
pratyāhāreṇa yogīndro vikāraṃ hanti mānasam ||96||

De-sandhi:
āsanena rujaḥ hanti prāṇāyāmena pātakam |
pratyāhāreṇa yogī-indraḥ vikāram hanti mānasam ||		 āsanena = by posture (inst.)
rujaḥ = diseases (acc. pl.)
hanti = destroys (3rd sg. √han)
prāṇāyāmena = by breath control (inst.)
pātakam = sin (acc.)
pratyāhāreṇa = by withdrawal (inst.)
yogī-indraḥ = lord of yogis (nom.)
vikāram = disturbance/modification (acc.)
hanti = destroys (3rd sg.)
mānasam = mental (acc. adj.)		 By posture (āsanena) one destroys (hanti) diseases (rujaḥ), by breath control (prāṇāyāmena) sin (pātakam); by withdrawal (pratyāhāreṇa) the lord of yogis (yogīndraḥ) destroys (hanti) mental (mānasam) disturbance (vikāram). Three limbs: āsana removes disease, prāṇāyāma removes sin, pratyāhāra removes mental disturbance
[Progressive purification through first three limbs of ṣaḍaṅga-yoga]		 By postures one gets rid of diseases, by breath control, sins; by withdrawal the lord of yogis gets rid of distraction.
dhāraṇayā manodhairyaṃ dhyānādaiśvaryamadbhutam |
samādhermokṣamāpnoti tyaktvā karma śubhāśubham ||97||

De-sandhi:
dhāraṇayā manaḥ-dhairyam dhyānāt aiśvaryam adbhutam |
samādheḥ mokṣam āpnoti tyaktvā karma śubha-aśubham ||		 dhāraṇayā = by concentration (inst.)
manaḥ-dhairyam = steadiness of mind (acc.)
dhyānāt = from meditation (abl.)
aiśvaryam = lordship/power (acc.)
adbhutam = wondrous/amazing (acc.)
samādheḥ = from absorption (abl.)
mokṣam = liberation (acc.)
āpnoti = obtains (3rd sg. √āp)
tyaktvā = having abandoned (abs. √tyaj)
karma = action (acc.)
śubha-aśubham = good and bad (acc.)		 By concentration (dhāraṇayā) [one attains] steadiness of mind (manodhairyam), from meditation (dhyānāt) wondrous (adbhutam) power (aiśvaryam). From absorption (samādheḥ) one obtains (āpnoti) liberation (mokṣam), having abandoned (tyaktvā) good and bad (śubhāśubham) action (karma). Three higher limbs: dhāraṇā gives mental stability, dhyāna gives powers, samādhi gives mokṣa
[Completion of ṣaḍaṅga path: concentration → meditation → absorption → liberation beyond karma]		 By fixation [the yogi] gets constancy of mind, by meditation (dhyāna), amazing power. By absorption [the yogi] obtains liberation, having cast off good and bad deeds.
prāṇāyāmadviṣaḍkena pratyāhāraḥ prakīrtitaḥ |
pratyāhāradviṣaḍkena jāyate dhāraṇā śubhā ||98||

De-sandhi:
prāṇāyāma-dvi-ṣaṭ-kena pratyāhāraḥ prakīrtitaḥ |
pratyāhāra-dvi-ṣaṭ-kena jāyate dhāraṇā śubhā ||		 prāṇāyāma = breath control
dvi-ṣaṭ-kena = by twice-six (=12) (inst.)
pratyāhāraḥ = withdrawal (nom.)
prakīrtitaḥ = declared/constituted (PPP nom.)
pratyāhāra-dvi-ṣaṭ-kena = by twelve withdrawals (inst.)
jāyate = arises/is produced (3rd sg. pass.)
dhāraṇā = concentration (nom.)
śubhā = auspicious (nom. f.)		 Withdrawal (pratyāhāraḥ) is declared (prakīrtitaḥ) as twelve (dviṣaḍkena) breath controls (prāṇāyāma). By twelve (dviṣaḍkena) withdrawals (pratyāhāra) auspicious (śubhā) concentration (dhāraṇā) arises (jāyate). Quantitative progression: 12 prāṇāyāmas = 1 pratyāhāra; 12 pratyāhāras = 1 dhāraṇā
[Mathematical structure of yogic progression through the limbs]		 Twelve breath-controls constitute withdrawal. Twelve withdrawals make fixation.
dhāraṇā dvādaśa proktaṃ dhyānaṃ dhyānaviśāradaiḥ |
dhyānadvādaśakenaiva samādhirabhidhīyate ||99||

De-sandhi:
dhāraṇāḥ dvādaśa proktam dhyānam dhyāna-viśāradaiḥ |
dhyāna-dvādaśakena eva samādhiḥ abhidhīyate ||		 dhāraṇāḥ = concentrations (nom. pl.)
dvādaśa = twelve
proktam = declared (PPP nom.)
dhyānam = meditation (nom.)
dhyāna-viśāradaiḥ = by experts in meditation (inst. pl.)
dhyāna-dvādaśakena = by twelve meditations (inst.)
eva = indeed
samādhiḥ = absorption (nom.)
abhidhīyate = is defined/called (pass. 3rd sg.)		 Twelve (dvādaśa) concentrations (dhāraṇāḥ) are declared (proktam) by experts in meditation (dhyānaviśāradaiḥ) to be meditation (dhyānam). By twelve meditations (dhyānadvādaśakena) indeed (eva) absorption (samādhiḥ) is defined (abhidhīyate). Continuation: 12 dhāraṇās = 1 dhyāna; 12 dhyānas = 1 samādhi
[Complete mathematical framework: 12³ prāṇāyāmas ultimately equal one samādhi]		 Expert meditators say that twelve fixations make meditation [and] absorption is defined as twelve meditations.
yatsamādhiphalaṃ jyotiranantaṃ viśvatomukham |
tasmindṛṣṭe kriyā karma yātāyātaṃ nivartate ||100||

De-sandhi:
yat samādhi-phalam jyotiḥ anantam viśvataḥ-mukham |
tasmin dṛṣṭe kriyā karma yāta-āyātam nivartate ||		 yat = which (rel. nom.)
samādhi-phalam = fruit of absorption (nom.)
jyotiḥ = light/flame (nom.)
anantam = infinite/eternal (nom.)
viśvataḥ-mukham = facing all directions (nom.)
tasmin = in that (loc.)
dṛṣṭe = when seen (loc. abs. PPP)
kriyā = rites (nom.)
karma = action (nom.)
yāta-āyātam = going and coming (nom.)
nivartate = ceases (3rd sg. ni-√vṛt)		 That which (yat) is the fruit of absorption (samādhiphalam) is the light (jyotiḥ) — infinite (anantam), facing all directions (viśvatomukham). When that (tasmin) is seen (dṛṣṭe), rites (kriyā), action (karma), and transmigration (yātāyātam) cease (nivartate). Ultimate fruit: omnidirectional eternal light; cessation of karma and rebirth
[Samādhi reveals infinite light; seeing it ends ritual obligation, karmic fruits, and cycle of birth-death]		 The result of absorption is an eternal flame shining in all directions. When that is seen, rites, [the fruits of] action and transmigration cease.
saṃbodhyāsānamekabuddhiracalaṃ karṇākṣināsāpuṭa-
dvārāṇyaṅgulibhirniyamya pavanaṃ vaktreṇa cāpūritam |
dhṛtvā vakṣasi vahnyapānasahitaṃ mūrdhnisthitaṃ kārayed
evaṃ yāti śivena tatra samatāṃ yogī vinaṣṭāmayaḥ ||101||

De-sandhi:
saṃbodhya āsānam eka-buddhiḥ acalam karṇa-akṣi-nāsā-puṭa-
dvārāṇi aṅgulibhiḥ niyamya pavanam vaktreṇa ca āpūritam |
dhṛtvā vakṣasi vahni-apāna-sahitam mūrdhni sthitam kārayet
evam yāti śivena tatra samatām yogī vinaṣṭa-āmayaḥ ||		 saṃbodhya = having assumed (abs. sam-√budh)
āsānam = posture (acc.)
eka-buddhiḥ = one-minded/focussed (nom.)
acalam = steady/motionless (acc.)
karṇa-akṣi-nāsā-puṭa-dvārāṇi = openings of ears, eyes, nose cavities (acc. pl.)
aṅgulibhiḥ = with fingers (inst. pl.)
niyamya = having closed/restrained (abs. ni-√yam)
pavanam = breath (acc.)
vaktreṇa = through mouth (inst.)
ca = and
āpūritam = filled/inhaled (PPP acc.)
dhṛtvā = having held (abs. √dhṛ)
vakṣasi = in the chest (loc.)
vahni-apāna-sahitam = together with fire and apāna (acc.)
mūrdhni = in the head (loc.)
sthitam = placed/fixed (PPP acc.)
kārayet = should make (opt. caus.)
evam = thus
yāti = goes (3rd sg.)
śivena = with Śiva (inst.)
tatra = there
samatām = equality (acc.)
yogī = yogi (nom.)
vinaṣṭa-āmayaḥ = whose diseases are destroyed (BV nom.)		 Having assumed (saṃbodhya) a steady (acalam) posture (āsānam), one-minded (ekabuddhiḥ), having closed (niyamya) the openings (dvārāṇi) of ears, eyes, and nose cavities (karṇākṣināsāpuṭa) with the fingers (aṅgulibhiḥ), and (ca) having filled (āpūritam) the breath (pavanam) through the mouth (vaktreṇa), having held (dhṛtvā) it in the chest (vakṣasi), one should make (kārayet) it fixed (sthitam) in the head (mūrdhni) together with fire and apāna (vahnyapānasahitam). Thus (evam) the yogi (yogī), his diseases destroyed (vinaṣṭāmayaḥ), goes (yāti) there (tatra) to equality (samatām) with Śiva (śivena). Ṣaṇmukhī mudrā: closing sensory openings, mouth-breathing, chest retention, raising to head
[Complete prāṇāyāma with sensory withdrawal; disease destruction; attainment of Śiva-equality]		 Having assumed a steady yoga posture with focussed mind, the yogi should close the openings of his ears, eyes and nose with his fingers and inhale through his mouth. After holding [the breath] in his chest, he should fix it, together with fire and apāna, in his head. In this way, with all his diseases destroyed, he becomes equal with Śiva.
SIGNS OF PROGRESS AND WARNINGS - siddhilakṣaṇam (VERSES 102-106)
gaganaṃ pavane prapte dhvanirutpadyate mahān |
ghaṇṭādīnāṃ pravādyānāṃ siddhistasya na dūrataḥ ||102||

De-sandhi:
gaganam pavane prapte dhvaniḥ utpadyate mahān |
ghaṇṭā-ādīnām pravādya-ānām siddhiḥ tasya na dūrataḥ ||		 gaganam = sky/ether/brahmarandhra (acc.)
pavane = when breath (loc.)
prapte = having reached (loc. abs.)
dhvaniḥ = sound (nom.)
utpadyate = arises (pass. 3rd sg.)
mahān = great (nom.)
ghaṇṭā-ādīnām = of bells etc. (gen. pl.)
pravādya-ānām = of instruments (gen. pl.)
siddhiḥ = success (nom.)
tasya = his (gen.)
na = not
dūrataḥ = far away (adv.)		 When the breath (pavane) has reached (prapte) the ether/skull (gaganam), a great (mahān) sound (dhvaniḥ) arises (utpadyate) of bells (ghaṇṭādīnām) and other instruments (pravādyānām). Success (siddhiḥ) for him (tasya) is not (na) far away (dūrataḥ). Nāda: inner sounds as sign of progress
[Breath reaching brahmarandhra produces mystical bell sounds; indicates imminent success]		 When the breath reaches the skull (gaganam) a great noise of bells and other instruments arises [and] success is not far away.
prāṇāyāmena yuktena sarvarogakṣayo bhavet |
ayuktābhyāsayogena sarvarogasamudbhavaḥ ||103||

De-sandhi:
prāṇāyāmena yuktena sarva-roga-kṣayaḥ bhavet |
ayukta-abhyāsa-yogena sarva-roga-samudbhavaḥ ||		 prāṇāyāmena = by breath control (inst.)
yuktena = correct/proper (inst.)
sarva-roga-kṣayaḥ = destruction of all diseases (nom.)
bhavet = may be/becomes (opt.)
ayukta-abhyāsa-yogena = by improper practice (inst.)
sarva-roga-samudbhavaḥ = arising of all diseases (nom.)		 By correct (yuktena) breath control (prāṇāyāmena) there may be (bhavet) destruction of all diseases (sarvarogakṣayaḥ). By improper practice (ayuktābhyāsayogena) [there is] the arising of all diseases (sarvarogasamudbhavaḥ). Dual potential: correct practice heals, incorrect practice harms
[Critical warning about proper technique; same practice can cure or cause disease]		 By means of correct breath-control all diseases disappear; incorrect practice can cause all manner of disease.
hikkā śvāsaśca kāsaśca śiraḥkarṇākṣivedanāḥ |
bhavanti vividhā doṣāḥ pavanasya vyatikramāt ||104||

De-sandhi:
hikkā śvāsaḥ ca kāsaḥ ca śiraḥ-karṇa-akṣi-vedanāḥ |
bhavanti vividhāḥ doṣāḥ pavanasya vyatikramāt ||		 hikkā = hiccups (nom.)
śvāsaḥ = asthma/labored breathing (nom.)
ca = and
kāsaḥ = cough (nom.)
ca = and
śiraḥ-karṇa-akṣi-vedanāḥ = pains in head, ears, eyes (nom. pl.)
bhavanti = arise (3rd pl.)
vividhāḥ = various (nom. pl.)
doṣāḥ = disorders/problems (nom. pl.)
pavanasya = of breath (gen.)
vyatikramāt = from transgression/improper practice (abl.)		 Hiccups (hikkā), asthma (śvāsaḥ), and (ca) cough (kāsaḥ), and (ca) pains in head, ears, and eyes (śiraḥkarṇākṣivedanāḥ) — various (vividhāḥ) disorders (doṣāḥ) arise (bhavanti) from improper practice (vyatikramāt) of breath (pavanasya). Specific symptoms of improper prāṇāyāma
[Medical warning: respiratory, neurological, and sensory disorders from incorrect technique]		 Hiccups, asthma, coughing, pain in the head, ears or eyes: various problems can arise from improper breath control.
yathā siṃho gajo vyāghro bhavedvaśyaḥ śanaiḥ śanaiḥ |
anyathā hanti yantāraṃ tathā vāyurasevitaḥ ||105||

De-sandhi:
yathā siṃhaḥ gajaḥ vyāghraḥ bhavet vaśyaḥ śanaiḥ śanaiḥ |
anyathā hanti yantāram tathā vāyuḥ asevitaḥ ||		 yathā = just as
siṃhaḥ = lion (nom.)
gajaḥ = elephant (nom.)
vyāghraḥ = tiger (nom.)
bhavet = becomes (opt.)
vaśyaḥ = tamed/controlled (nom.)
śanaiḥ śanaiḥ = very slowly (adv.)
anyathā = otherwise
hanti = kills/harms (3rd sg.)
yantāram = trainer (acc.)
tathā = so too
vāyuḥ = breath (nom.)
asevitaḥ = improperly practiced (nom.)		 Just as (yathā) a lion (siṃhaḥ), elephant (gajaḥ), or tiger (vyāghraḥ) becomes (bhavet) tamed (vaśyaḥ) very slowly (śanaiḥ śanaiḥ) — otherwise (anyathā) it harms (hanti) the trainer (yantāram) — so too (tathā) the breath (vāyuḥ) [when] improperly practiced (asevitaḥ). Wild animal metaphor for breath training
[Breath like lion/elephant/tiger: must be tamed gradually or it destroys the practitioner]		 In the same way that a lion, elephant [or] tiger must be brought under control very slowly or they will harm their trainer, so too must the breath be trained [very slowly].
yuktaṃ yuktaṃ tyajedvāyuṃ yuktaṃ yuktaṃ ca pūrayet |
yuktaṃ yuktaṃ ca badhnīyādevaṃ siddhimavāpnuyāt ||106||

De-sandhi:
yuktam yuktam tyajet vāyum yuktam yuktam ca pūrayet |
yuktam yuktam ca badhnīyāt evam siddhim avāpnuyāt ||		 yuktam yuktam = correctly, properly (adv. repeated)
tyajet = should release/exhale (opt.)
vāyum = breath (acc.)
yuktam yuktam = correctly
ca = and
pūrayet = should fill/inhale (opt.)
yuktam yuktam = correctly
ca = and
badhnīyāt = should bind/retain (opt.)
evam = thus
siddhim = success (acc.)
avāpnuyāt = should attain (opt.)		 One should exhale (tyajet) the breath (vāyum) correctly (yuktam yuktam), and (ca) inhale (pūrayet) correctly (yuktam yuktam), and (ca) retain (badhnīyāt) correctly (yuktam yuktam). Thus (evam) one should attain (avāpnuyāt) success (siddhim). Triple emphasis on correctness in all three phases
[Recaka, pūraka, kumbhaka all require proper technique; repetition stresses importance]		 One should exhale correctly, inhale correctly and hold the breath correctly; thus does one attain success.
PRATYĀHĀRA - SENSE WITHDRAWAL (pratyāhāraḥ)
caratāṃ cakṣurādīnāṃ viṣayeṣu yathākramam |
yatpratyāharaṇaṃ teṣāṃ pratyāhāraḥ sa ucyate || 103 ||

De-sandhi:
caratām cakṣuḥ-ādīnām viṣayeṣu yathā-kramam |
yat pratyāharaṇam teṣām pratyāhāraḥ saḥ ucyate ||		 caratām = of those moving (gen. pl. pres. part. √car)
cakṣuḥ-ādīnām = of the eye and others [senses] (gen. pl.)
viṣayeṣu = among the sense-objects (loc. pl.)
yathā-kramam = in sequence, respectively (adv.)
yat = which (rel. pron. nom. n.)
pratyāharaṇam = act of withdrawing (nom. n., prati-ā-√hṛ)
teṣām = of them (gen. pl.)
pratyāhāraḥ = withdrawal (nom. m.)
saḥ = that (nom. m.)
ucyate = is called (3rd sg. pass. √vac)		 That which is the act of withdrawing (yat pratyāharaṇam) of them (teṣām) — of the eye and other [senses] (cakṣurādīnām) as they move (caratām) among the sense-objects (viṣayeṣu) in sequence (yathākramam) — that (saḥ) is called (ucyate) pratyāhāra (pratyāhāraḥ). Technical definition: pratyāhāra = withdrawing the senses from their objects
[cakṣuḥ-ādi = eye etc. = the five jñānendriyas; viṣaya = sense-field; yathākramam = each in its proper domain]		 The act of withdrawing the sight and other [senses] as they move among their respective fields of operation is called withdrawal (pratyāhāra).
yathā pratāpakālastho raviḥ pratyāharet prabhām |
tṛtīyāṅgasthito yogī vikāraṃ hanti mānasam || 104 ||

De-sandhi:
yathā pratāpa-kāla-sthaḥ raviḥ pratyāharet prabhām |
tṛtīya-aṅga-sthitaḥ yogī vikāram hanti mānasam ||		 yathā = just as (adv.)
pratāpa-kāla-sthaḥ = standing at the time of heat/noon (nom. m.)
raviḥ = the sun (nom. m.)
pratyāharet = withdraws/draws in (3rd sg. opt. prati-ā-√hṛ)
prabhām = radiance/shadow (acc. f.)
tṛtīya-aṅga-sthitaḥ = situated in the third limb (nom. m.)
yogī = yogi (nom. m.)
vikāram = disturbance/modification (acc. m.)
hanti = destroys (3rd sg. pres. √han)
mānasam = mental (acc. m.)		 Just as (yathā) the sun (raviḥ) standing at noon (pratāpakālasthaḥ) draws in (pratyāharet) shadow (prabhām), so the yogi (yogī) situated in the third limb (tṛtīyāṅgasthitaḥ) destroys (hanti) mental (mānasam) disturbance (vikāram). Noon sun destroys shadows; third aṅga (pratyāhāra) destroys mental disturbance
[pratāpa-kāla = noon when shadows vanish; tṛtīya-aṅga = pratyāhāra as 3rd of 6 aṅgas; vikāra = mental fluctuation]		 In the same way that at noon the sun draws in shadow, so the yogi practising the third auxiliary removes disturbance of the mind.
aṅgamadhye yathāṅgānāṃ kūrmaḥ saṃkocamācaret |
yogī pratyāharatyevam indriyāṇi tathātmani || 105 ||

De-sandhi:
aṅga-madhye yathā aṅgānām kūrmaḥ saṃkocam ācaret |
yogī pratyāharati evam indriyāṇi tathā ātmani ||		 aṅga-madhye = into the middle of the body/shell (loc.)
yathā = just as (adv.)
aṅgānām = of the limbs (gen. pl.)
kūrmaḥ = tortoise (nom. m.)
saṃkocam = contraction (acc. m., sam-√kuc)
ācaret = would perform (3rd sg. opt. ā-√car)
yogī = yogi (nom. m.)
pratyāharati = withdraws (3rd sg. pres. prati-ā-√hṛ)
evam = thus, in this way (adv.)
indriyāṇi = the senses (acc. pl. n.)
tathā = so, likewise (adv.)
ātmani = into the self (loc.)		 Just as (yathā) a tortoise (kūrmaḥ) would perform (ācaret) contraction (saṃkocam) of its limbs (aṅgānām) into the middle of [its body] (aṅgamadhye), so (tathā) the yogi (yogī) thus (evam) withdraws (pratyāharati) the senses (indriyāṇi) into the self (ātmani). Classic kūrma simile (cf. BhG 2.58): tortoise retracts limbs → yogi retracts senses into ātman
[saṃkoca = contraction/retraction; ātmani = into the Self, not just "body"]		 Just as a tortoise draws its limbs into itself, so does the yogi withdraw his senses into himself.
yaṃ yaṃ śṛṇoti karṇābhyāṃ priyamapyathavāpriyam |
taṃ tamātmeti vijñāya pratyāharati yogavit || 106 ||

De-sandhi:
yam yam śṛṇoti karṇābhyām priyam api athavā apriyam |
tam tam ātmā iti vijñāya pratyāharati yoga-vit ||		 yam yam = whatever (rel. pron. acc. m., repeated)
śṛṇoti = he hears (3rd sg. pres. √śru)
karṇābhyām = with the two ears (inst. dual)
priyam = pleasant (acc. m.)
api = even, also
athavā = or
apriyam = unpleasant (acc. m.)
tam tam = that, each one (acc. m., correlative)
ātmā iti = "this is the Self" (nom. + quotative)
vijñāya = having recognized (gerund vi-√jñā)
pratyāharati = withdraws (3rd sg. pres.)
yoga-vit = knower of yoga (nom. m.)		 Whatever (yaṃ yam) he hears (śṛṇoti) with the ears (karṇābhyām) — pleasant (priyam) or (athavā) unpleasant (apriyam) — having recognized (vijñāya) each of those (taṃ tam) as "this is the Self" (ātmeti), the knower of yoga (yogavit) withdraws (pratyāharati). Method: recognize every sound-object as ātman → automatic withdrawal of hearing
[Key insight: pratyāhāra here is not suppression but recognition (vijñāya) — non-dual technique]		 Whatever [sense-object] he hears with his ears, good or bad, the knower of yoga should recognise it as his self and withdraw [his sense of hearing from it].
aramyamathavā ramyam yaṃ yaṃ paśyati cakṣuṣā |
taṃ tamātmeti vijñāya pratyāharati yogavit || 107 ||

De-sandhi:
aramyam athavā ramyam yam yam paśyati cakṣuṣā |
tam tam ātmā iti vijñāya pratyāharati yoga-vit ||		 aramyam = unbeautiful, unpleasant (acc. m., a-ramya)
athavā = or
ramyam = beautiful, pleasant (acc. m.)
yam yam = whatever (rel. pron. acc.)
paśyati = he sees (3rd sg. pres. √paś/√dṛś)
cakṣuṣā = with the eye (inst. sg.)
tam tam = each of those (acc.)
ātmā iti = "this is the Self"
vijñāya = having recognized (gerund)
pratyāharati = withdraws
yoga-vit = knower of yoga		 Whatever (yaṃ yam) he sees (paśyati) with the eye (cakṣuṣā) — unbeautiful (aramyam) or (athavā) beautiful (ramyam) — having recognized (vijñāya) each of those (taṃ tam) as "this is the Self" (ātmeti), the knower of yoga (yogavit) withdraws (pratyāharati). Same method applied to sight: beautiful/ugly → both recognized as Self
[ramya from √ram "to delight"; aramya/ramya = ugly/beautiful covers all visual experience]		 Whatever [sense-object] he sees with his eyes, beautiful or not, the knower of yoga should recognise it as his self and withdraw [his sense of sight from it].
uṣṇaṃ vāpyathavānuṣṇaṃ yaṃ yaṃ spṛśati carmaṇā |
taṃ tamātmeti vijñāya pratyāharati yogavit || 108 ||

De-sandhi:
uṣṇam vā api athavā anuṣṇam yam yam spṛśati carmaṇā |
tam tam ātmā iti vijñāya pratyāharati yoga-vit ||		 uṣṇam = hot (acc. m.)
vā api = or also
athavā = or else
anuṣṇam = not-hot, cold (acc. m., an-uṣṇa)
yam yam = whatever
spṛśati = he touches (3rd sg. pres. √spṛś)
carmaṇā = with the skin (inst. sg., carman)
tam tam = each of those
ātmā iti = "this is the Self"
vijñāya = having recognized
pratyāharati = withdraws
yoga-vit = knower of yoga		 Whatever (yaṃ yam) he touches (spṛśati) with the skin (carmaṇā) — hot (uṣṇam) or (athavā) not-hot (anuṣṇam) — having recognized (vijñāya) each of those (taṃ tam) as "this is the Self" (ātmeti), the knower of yoga (yogavit) withdraws (pratyāharati). Touch sense: hot/cold → recognized as Self → withdrawal
[carman = skin (organ of touch); uṣṇa/anuṣṇa = hot/cold polarity]		 Whatever [sense-object] he touches with his skin, hot or not, the knower of yoga should recognise it as his self and withdraw [his sense of touch from it].
agaulyamathavā gaulyam yaṃ yaṃ spṛśati jihvayā |
taṃ tamātmeti vijñāya pratyāharati yogavit || 109 ||

De-sandhi:
agaulyam athavā gaulyam yam yam spṛśati jihvayā |
tam tam ātmā iti vijñāya pratyāharati yoga-vit ||		 agaulyam = not-sweet, unpleasant taste (acc. m., a-gaulya)
athavā = or
gaulyam = sweet, pleasant taste (acc. m., from gula = molasses/sweetness)
yam yam = whatever (rel. pron. acc.)
spṛśati = he touches/tastes (3rd sg. pres. √spṛś)
jihvayā = with the tongue (inst. sg. f.)
tam tam = each of those (acc.)
ātmā iti = "this is the Self"
vijñāya = having recognized (gerund vi-√jñā)
pratyāharati = withdraws (3rd sg. pres.)
yoga-vit = knower of yoga (nom. m.)		 Whatever (yaṃ yam) he tastes (spṛśati) with the tongue (jihvayā) — not-sweet (agaulyam) or (athavā) sweet (gaulyam) — having recognized (vijñāya) each of those (taṃ tam) as "this is the Self" (ātmeti), the knower of yoga (yogavit) withdraws (pratyāharati). Taste sense: sweet/not-sweet → recognized as Self → withdrawal
[jihvā = tongue (organ of taste); gaulya from gula (jaggery/sweetness); spṛśati here = "touches with tongue" = tastes]		 Whatever [sense-object] he touches with his tongue, sweet or not, the knower of yoga should recognise it as his self and withdraw [his sense of taste from it].
sugandhaṃ vā vigandhaṃ vā yaṃ yaṃ jighrati nāsayā |
taṃ tamātmeti vijñāya pratyāharati yogavit || 110 ||

De-sandhi:
sugandham vā vigandham vā yam yam jighrati nāsayā |
tam tam ātmā iti vijñāya pratyāharati yoga-vit ||		 sugandham = fragrant, good-smelling (acc. m., su-gandha)
vā = or
vigandham = bad-smelling, stinking (acc. m., vi-gandha)
vā = or
yam yam = whatever (rel. pron. acc.)
jighrati = he smells (3rd sg. pres. √ghrā, desiderative form)
nāsayā = with the nose (inst. sg. f.)
tam tam = each of those (acc.)
ātmā iti = "this is the Self"
vijñāya = having recognized (gerund)
pratyāharati = withdraws
yoga-vit = knower of yoga		 Whatever (yaṃ yam) he smells (jighrati) with the nose (nāsayā) — fragrant (sugandham) or (vā) foul-smelling (vigandham) — having recognized (vijñāya) each of those (taṃ tam) as "this is the Self" (ātmeti), the knower of yoga (yogavit) withdraws (pratyāharati). Smell sense: fragrant/foul → recognized as Self → withdrawal
[nāsā = nose; gandha = smell; su-gandha/vi-gandha = good/bad smell; jighrati = intensive/desiderative of √ghrā "to smell"]		 Whatever he smells with his nose, fragrant or not, the knower of yoga should recognise it as his self and withdraw [his sense of smell from it].
🌙☀️ ESOTERIC PRATYĀHĀRA: SUN, MOON, AND NECTAR (Verses 111-115) ☀️🌙
Internal alchemy of solar/lunar forces — The yogic body as cosmic laboratory
Verse 111:

candrāmṛtamayīṃ dhārām āhārayati bhāskaraḥ |
yat pratyāharaṇaṃ tasya pratyāhāraḥ sa ucyate ||111||

De-sandhi:
candra-amṛta-mayīm dhārām āhārayati bhāskaraḥ |
yat pratyāharaṇam tasya pratyāhāraḥ saḥ ucyate ||		 candra-amṛta-mayīm = consisting of lunar nectar (acc. f., -mayī suffix)
dhārām = stream, flow (acc. f. dhārā)
āhārayati = draws toward itself, absorbs (caus. √hṛ)
bhāskaraḥ = the sun, "light-maker" (nom. m.)
yat = that which (rel. pron. nom. n.)
pratyāharaṇam = withdrawal, drawing back (nom. n.)
tasya = of it/that (gen.)
pratyāhāraḥ = pratyāhāra (nom. m.)
saḥ = that (nom. m.)
ucyate = is called (pass. √vac)		 The sun (bhāskaraḥ) draws toward itself (āhārayati) the stream (dhārām) consisting of lunar nectar (candrāmṛtamayīm). That which (yat) is the withdrawal (pratyāharaṇam) of it (tasya) — that (saḥ) is called (ucyate) pratyāhāra (pratyāhāraḥ). Etymology of pratyāhāra: withdrawing the lunar nectar from the sun's consumption
[āhārayati from ā-√hṛ caus. = "causes to draw toward"; pratyāharaṇa = prati + ā + √hṛ = "drawing back" — the reversal of āhāra; the sun normally consumes (āhāra) the nectar, but the yogi withdraws (pratyāhāra) it back; this gives esoteric etymology linking pratyāhāra to nectar preservation]		 The sun absorbs the nectar-stream of the moon. The withdrawal of that [nectar from the sun] is called pratyāhāra.
Verse 112:

ekā strī bhujyate dvābhyām āgatā somamaṇḍalāt |
tṛtīyo ramate yo 'tra sa bhaved ajarāmaraḥ ||112||

De-sandhi:
ekā strī bhujyate dvābhyām āgatā soma-maṇḍalāt |
tṛtīyaḥ ramate yaḥ atra saḥ bhavet ajara-amaraḥ ||		 ekā = one (nom. f.)
strī = woman (nom. f.) [= nectar/śakti]
bhujyate = is consumed, enjoyed (pass. √bhuj)
dvābhyām = by two (inst. dual) [= iḍā and piṅgalā]
āgatā = having come (nom. f. ppp. ā-√gam)
soma-maṇḍalāt = from the lunar sphere (abl. m.)
tṛtīyaḥ = the third one (nom. m.) [= yogi]
ramate = delights, enjoys (3rd sg. √ram)
yaḥ atra = whoever here
saḥ bhavet = he would become (opt.)
ajara-amaraḥ = without old age and death, immortal (nom. m.)		 One woman (ekā strī) [the nectar], having come (āgatā) from the lunar sphere (somamaṇḍalāt), is consumed (bhujyate) by two (dvābhyām) [channels]. The third one (tṛtīyaḥ) who delights (ramate) here (atra) — he (saḥ) would become (bhavet) ageless and deathless (ajarāmaraḥ). Esoteric allegory: The "one woman" (nectar) consumed by "two" (iḍā/piṅgalā), but the "third" who delights in it becomes immortal
[strī = "woman" symbolizing śakti/amṛta; dvābhyām = the dual channels that normally waste it; tṛtīyaḥ = the third = yogi who captures nectar; ramate from √ram = "delights/enjoys"; ajara-amara = "without aging, without death" = immortal; classic Tantric encoding]		 The one woman [the nectar] who has come from the lunar sphere is consumed by two [the side channels]. The third [the yogi] who delights here becomes ageless and deathless.
Verse 113:

nābhideśe vasaty eva bhāskaro dahanātmakaḥ |
amṛtātmā sthito nityaṃ tālumadhye ca candramāḥ ||113||

De-sandhi:
nābhi-deśe vasati eva bhāskaraḥ dahana-ātmakaḥ |
amṛta-ātmā sthitaḥ nityam tālu-madhye ca candramāḥ ||		 nābhi-deśe = in the navel region (loc. m.)
vasati = dwells, resides (3rd sg. √vas)
eva = indeed, verily
bhāskaraḥ = the sun (nom. m.)
dahana-ātmakaḥ = having fire as its nature (nom. m., bahuvrīhi)
amṛta-ātmā = whose nature is nectar (nom. m., bahuvrīhi)
sthitaḥ = situated, established (nom. m. ppp. √sthā)
nityam = eternally, always (adv.)
tālu-madhye = in the middle of the palate (loc. n.)
ca = and
candramāḥ = the moon (nom. m.)		 The sun (bhāskaraḥ), whose nature is fire (dahanātmakaḥ), dwells (vasati) indeed (eva) in the navel region (nābhideśe). And (ca) the moon (candramāḥ), whose nature is nectar (amṛtātmā), is eternally (nityam) situated (sthitaḥ) in the middle of the palate (tālumadhye). Yogic cosmography: Sun (fire-nature) at navel, Moon (nectar-nature) at palate
[nābhi-deśa = navel region = seat of digestive/solar fire (maṇipūra); dahana-ātmaka = "fire-natured"; amṛta-ātman = "nectar-natured" — parallel bahuvrīhi compounds; tālu-madhya = "middle of the palate" = location of moon/soma; candramāḥ = poetic form of candra]		 The sun, which has fire as its essence, dwells in the navel region. And the moon, whose nature is nectar, is eternally situated in the middle of the palate.
Verse 114:

varṣaty adhomukhaś candro gṛhṇāty ūrdhvamukho raviḥ |
jñātavyaṃ kāraṇaṃ tatra yena pīyūṣam āpyate ||114||

De-sandhi:
varṣati adhaḥ-mukhaḥ candraḥ gṛhṇāti ūrdhva-mukhaḥ raviḥ |
jñātavyam kāraṇam tatra yena pīyūṣam āpyate ||		 varṣati = rains, pours down (3rd sg. √vṛṣ)
adhaḥ-mukhaḥ = facing downward (nom. m.)
candraḥ = the moon (nom. m.)
gṛhṇāti = grasps, takes, receives (3rd sg. √grah)
ūrdhva-mukhaḥ = facing upward (nom. m.)
raviḥ = the sun (nom. m.)
jñātavyam = should be known (n. fut. pass. part. √jñā)
kāraṇam = cause, means, method (nom. n.)
tatra = there, in that matter
yena = by which (inst. n. rel. pron.)
pīyūṣam = nectar, ambrosia (nom. n.)
āpyate = is obtained (pass. √āp)		 The moon (candraḥ), facing downward (adhomukhaḥ), rains down (varṣati); the sun (raviḥ), facing upward (ūrdhvamukhaḥ), grasps (gṛhṇāti) [it]. The means (kāraṇam) by which (yena) the nectar (pīyūṣam) is obtained (āpyate) should be known (jñātavyam) there (tatra). The problem and its solution: The kāraṇa (means) for obtaining pīyūṣa (nectar)
[varṣati from √vṛṣ = to rain/pour; gṛhṇāti from √grah = to grasp/seize (different from grasati √gras "to devour"); kāraṇa = "cause, means, instrument" (neuter); pīyūṣa = nectar/ambrosia (synonym of amṛta); the verse poses the question: what is the method?]		 The moon, facing downwards, rains [nectar]; the sun, facing upwards, grasps [it]. The means by which the nectar is obtained should be understood.
Verse 115:

ūrdhvaṃ nābhir adhas tālur ūrdhvaṃ bhānur adhaḥ śaśī |
karaṇaṃ viparītākhyaṃ guruvākyena labhyate ||115||

De-sandhi:
ūrdhvam nābhiḥ adhaḥ tāluḥ ūrdhvam bhānuḥ adhaḥ śaśī |
karaṇam viparīta-ākhyam guru-vākyena labhyate ||		 ūrdhvam = above, upward (adv.)
nābhiḥ = the navel (nom. f.)
adhaḥ = below, downward (adv.)
tāluḥ = the palate (nom. m.)
ūrdhvam bhānuḥ = the sun above (nom. m.)
adhaḥ śaśī = the moon below (nom. m., śaśin)
karaṇam = practice, technique (nom. n.)
viparīta-ākhyam = called "inverted" (nom. n.)
guru-vākyena = by the word/instruction of the guru (inst. n.)
labhyate = is obtained (pass. √labh)		 The navel (nābhiḥ) [becomes] above (ūrdhvam), the palate (tāluḥ) below (adhaḥ); the sun (bhānuḥ) [becomes] above (ūrdhvam), the moon (śaśī) below (adhaḥ). The practice (karaṇam) called "inverted" (viparītākhyam) is obtained (labhyate) by the instruction of the guru (guruvākyena). Viparītakaraṇa: The inverted practice — obtained through guru's teaching
[The verse describes the reversal: what was above (navel/sun) goes below, what was below (palate/moon) goes above — achieved through headstand/shoulderstand; karaṇa (neut.) = "practice, technique"; viparīta-ākhya = "called inverted"; guru-vākya = "guru's word/instruction" — the practice must be learned from guru's teaching]		 The navel [is now] above, the palate below; the sun above, the moon below. The practice called "inverted" is obtained by the guru's instruction.
💚 ANĀHATA CAKRA AND KHECARĪ MUDRĀ (Verses 116-118) 💚
The heart center, ascending prāṇa, and the tongue-seal for drinking nectar
Verse 116:

tridhā baddho vṛṣo yatra roravīti mahāsvanaḥ |
anāhataṃ ca tac cakraṃ hṛdaye yogino viduḥ ||116||

De-sandhi:
tridhā baddhaḥ vṛṣaḥ yatra roravīti mahā-svanaḥ |
anāhatam ca tat cakram hṛdaye yoginaḥ viduḥ ||		 tridhā = in three ways, threefold (adv.)
baddhaḥ = bound, tied (nom. m. ppp. √bandh)
vṛṣaḥ = bull (nom. m.)
yatra = where (rel. adv.)
roravīti = roars, bellows (3rd sg. intensive √ru)
mahā-svanaḥ = with great sound (nom. m. bahuvrīhi)
anāhatam = unstruck (acc. n., an-āhata)
ca = and
tat cakram = that wheel/center (acc. n.)
hṛdaye = in the heart (loc. n.)
yoginaḥ = yogis (nom. pl.)
viduḥ = know (3rd pl. perf. √vid)		 Where (yatra) the bull (vṛṣaḥ), bound in three ways (tridhā baddhaḥ), roars (roravīti) with great sound (mahāsvanaḥ) — that (tat) wheel (cakram), the "unstruck" (anāhatam), yogis (yoginaḥ) know (viduḥ) [to be] in the heart (hṛdaye). Anāhata = "unstruck" — the heart chakra where the primordial sound resonates
[vṛṣa = "bull" = prāṇa/breath; tridhā baddha = bound by the three granthis or bound in three bandhas; roravīti = intensive/frequentative of √ru "to roar" — the continuous inner sound; anāhata = "un-struck" — sound without percussion, the cosmic hum; this is the mystical etymology of the heart center]		 Yogis know that place in which the bull, bound three times, roars loudly, to be the Anāhata cakra in the heart.
Verse 117:

anāhatam atikramya cākṛṣṭo maṇipūrakāt |
prāptaḥ prāṇo mahāpadmaṃ yoginām amṛtāyate ||117||

De-sandhi:
anāhatam atikramya ca ākṛṣṭaḥ maṇipūrakāt |
prāptaḥ prāṇaḥ mahā-padmam yoginām amṛtāyate ||		 anāhatam = the anāhata [cakra] (acc. n.)
atikramya = having gone beyond, passing (gerund ati-√kram)
ca = and
ākṛṣṭaḥ = drawn up (nom. m. ppp. ā-√kṛṣ)
maṇipūrakāt = from the maṇipūra [cakra] (abl. m.)
prāptaḥ = having reached (nom. m. ppp. pra-√āp)
prāṇaḥ = the breath/vital force (nom. m.)
mahā-padmam = the great lotus (acc. n.) [= sahasrāra]
yoginām = for yogis (gen. pl.)
amṛtāyate = becomes nectar (denom. 3rd sg. amṛta + āyate)		 Having passed beyond (atikramya) the Anāhata (anāhatam), and (ca) drawn up (ākṛṣṭaḥ) from the Maṇipūraka (maṇipūrakāt), the breath (prāṇaḥ), having reached (prāptaḥ) the Great Lotus (mahāpadmam), becomes nectar (amṛtāyate) for yogis (yoginām). Ascending prāṇa: Maṇipūra → Anāhata → Mahāpadma (Sahasrāra) = nectar for yogis
[ākṛṣṭa from ā-√kṛṣ = "drawn toward/up"; maṇipūraka = "jewel city" = navel center; mahāpadma = "great lotus" = thousand-petaled crown center; amṛtāyate = denominative verb "becomes amṛta/nectar" — prāṇa transforms into nectar when it reaches the crown]		 When the breath, drawn [up] from the Maṇipūraka, goes past the Anāhata and reaches the Great Lotus (mahāpadmam), it becomes nectar for yogis.
Verse 118:

mūrdhnaḥ ṣoḍaśapatrapadmagalitaṃ prāṇād avāptaṃ haṭhāt |
ūrdhvāsyo rasanāṃ niyamya vivare śaktiṃ parāṃ cintayet |
utkallolakalājalaṃ ca vimalaṃ dhārākulaṃ yaḥ pibet |
nirdoṣaḥ sa mṛṇālakomalatanur yogī ciraṃ jīvati ||118||

De-sandhi:
mūrdhnaḥ ṣoḍaśa-patra-padma-galitam prāṇāt avāptam haṭhāt |
ūrdhva-āsyaḥ rasanām niyamya vivare śaktim parām cintayet |
ut-kallola-kalā-jalam ca vimalam dhārā-ākulam yaḥ pibet |
nir-doṣaḥ saḥ mṛṇāla-komala-tanuḥ yogī ciram jīvati ||		 mūrdhnaḥ = from/of the head (gen./abl. m.)
ṣoḍaśa-patra-padma = sixteen-petalled lotus (comp.)
galitam = dripped, flowed down (acc. n. ppp. √gal)
prāṇāt = from prāṇa (abl. m.)
avāptam = obtained (acc. n. ppp. ava-√āp)
haṭhāt = forcefully, by haṭha (abl. m.)
ūrdhva-āsyaḥ = face upward (nom. m. bahuvrīhi)
rasanām = the tongue (acc. f.)
niyamya = having controlled/inserted (gerund ni-√yam)
vivare = in the aperture (loc. n.)
śaktim parām = supreme śakti (acc. f.)
cintayet = should visualize (opt. 3rd sg. √cint)
ut-kallola = surging, bubbling up
kalā-jalam = water/liquid of the digits (acc. n.)
vimalam = pure, stainless (acc. n.)
dhārā-ākulam = full of streams (acc. n.)
yaḥ pibet = whoever may drink (opt.)
nir-doṣaḥ = free from faults (nom. m.)
mṛṇāla-komala-tanuḥ = body soft as lotus-stalk (nom. m.)
ciram = for a long time (adv.)
jīvati = lives (3rd sg. √jīv)		 With face upward (ūrdhvāsyaḥ), having controlled (niyamya) the tongue (rasanām) in the aperture (vivare), one should visualize (cintayet) the supreme Śakti (śaktim parām) [and the nectar] which has dripped (galitam) from the sixteen-petalled lotus of the head (mūrdhnaḥ ṣoḍaśapatrapadma), forcefully obtained (haṭhāt avāptam) through prāṇa (prāṇāt). Whoever (yaḥ) drinks (pibet) the pure (vimalam) liquid of the surging lunar digits (utkallolakalājalam), full of streams (dhārākulam) — that yogi (saḥ yogī), free from faults (nirdoṣaḥ), with a body soft as a lotus-stalk (mṛṇālakomalatanuḥ), lives (jīvati) a long time (ciram). Khecarī mudrā: Tongue in cranial aperture, visualizing śakti, drinking lunar nectar
[Śārdūlavikrīḍita meter (19 syllables per pāda); ṣoḍaśapatrapadma = "16-petalled lotus" = viśuddha cakra at throat; vivara = the aperture above soft palate (khecarī target); kalā = lunar digits (16 phases); ut-kallola = "surging up"; mṛṇāla = lotus stalk/fiber — metaphor for supple, youthful body; this is the classic khecarī practice description]		 With his face upwards and his tongue inserted into the aperture [above the palate], the yogi should visualise [the nectar] which has been forcefully obtained from Prāṇa and dripped from the sixteen-petalled lotus [at the throat], [and his tongue] as the supreme Śakti. The yogi who drinks the stream of liquid from the surging digits of the moon becomes free from faults, with a body as supple as a lotus-stalk, and lives a long time.
Verse 119:

kākacañcuvad āsyena śītalaṃ śītalaṃ pibet |
prāṇaṃ prāṇavidhānajño yogī bhavati nirjaraḥ ||119||

De-sandhi:
kāka-cañcu-vat āsyena śītalam śītalam pibet |
prāṇam prāṇa-vidhāna-jñaḥ yogī bhavati nir-jaraḥ ||		 kāka-cañcu-vat = like a crow's beak (adv., -vat suffix)
āsyena = with the mouth (inst. n. āsya)
śītalam śītalam = very cool, extremely cool (emphatic repetition)
pibet = should drink (opt. 3rd sg. √pā)
prāṇam = the breath/prāṇa (acc. m.)
prāṇa-vidhāna-jñaḥ = knower of the method of prāṇa (nom. m.)
yogī = the yogi (nom. m.)
bhavati = becomes (3rd sg. √bhū)
nir-jaraḥ = free from decay/aging, immortal (nom. m.)		 The yogi (yogī) who knows the method of prāṇa (prāṇavidhānajñaḥ) [and] drinks (pibet) the very cool (śītalaṃ śītalam) prāṇa (prāṇam) with his mouth (āsyena) like a crow's beak (kākacañcuvat) becomes (bhavati) free from decay (nirjaraḥ). Kākī mudrā: Crow's beak breathing — cooling prāṇāyāma for immortality
[kāka = crow; cañcu = beak; -vat = "like"; the lips are pursed into a tube like a bird's beak to draw in cool air; śītala repeated for emphasis = "extremely cool"; vidhāna = method, procedure, rule; nir-jara = "without old age" (jara = decay/aging); this is the kākī or "crow" mudrā, a cooling breath technique]		 The yogi who knows what to do with Prāṇa and drinks the very cool Prāṇa with his mouth like a crow's beak becomes free from disease.
Verse 120:

rasanāṃ tālumūle yaḥ kṛtvā prāṇānilaṃ pibet |
abdārdhena bhavet tasya sarvarogaparikṣayaḥ ||120||

De-sandhi:
rasanām tālu-mūle yaḥ kṛtvā prāṇa-anilam pibet |
abda-ardhena bhavet tasya sarva-roga-parikṣayaḥ ||		 rasanām = the tongue (acc. f.)
tālu-mūle = at the root of the palate (loc. n.)
yaḥ = whoever (rel. pron. nom. m.)
kṛtvā = having placed (gerund √kṛ)
prāṇa-anilam = prāṇa-breath, vital wind (acc. m.)
pibet = should drink (opt. 3rd sg. √pā)
abda-ardhena = in half a year (inst. n., abda = year)
bhavet = there would be (opt. 3rd sg. √bhū)
tasya = of/for him (gen. m.)
sarva-roga-parikṣayaḥ = complete destruction of all diseases (nom. m.)		 Whoever (yaḥ), having placed (kṛtvā) the tongue (rasanām) at the root of the palate (tālumūle), drinks (pibet) the prāṇa-breath (prāṇānilam) — in half a year (abdārdhena), for him (tasya) there would be (bhavet) complete destruction of all diseases (sarvarogaparikṣayaḥ). Tongue at palate root + prāṇa drinking = all diseases destroyed in 6 months
[yaḥ...tasya = "whoever...for him" correlative construction; tālu-mūla = "root of the palate" = soft palate area, the khecarī target zone; rasanā = tongue; abda = year; ardha = half; pari-kṣaya = "complete destruction" (pari- intensifier + kṣaya from √kṣi)]		 All diseases are destroyed in six months for him who puts his tongue at the base of his palate and draws in the Prāṇa breath.
💎 VIŚUDDHA CAKRA: THE THROAT CENTER (Verses 121-123) 💎
Storing nectar, etymology of "pure", and the upward path
Verse 121:

viśuddhau parame cakre dhṛtvā somakalāmṛtam |
ūrdhvamārgeṇa hi yāti vañcayitvā raver mukham ||121||

De-sandhi:
viśuddhau parame cakre dhṛtvā soma-kalā-amṛtam |
ūrdhva-mārgeṇa hi yāti vañcayitvā raveḥ mukham ||		 viśuddhau = in the viśuddha [cakra] (loc. f.)
parame cakre = in the supreme wheel/center (loc. n.)
dhṛtvā = having held, having retained (gerund √dhṛ)
soma-kalā-amṛtam = nectar of the lunar digits (acc. n.)
ūrdhva-mārgeṇa = by the upward path (inst. m.)
hi = indeed, verily
yāti = goes (3rd sg. √yā)
vañcayitvā = having deceived/cheated (gerund caus. √vañc)
raveḥ mukham = the mouth of the sun (acc. n. + gen.)		 Having held (dhṛtvā) the nectar of the lunar digits (somakalāmṛtam) in the supreme (parame) Viśuddha cakra (viśuddhau cakre), it goes (yāti) indeed (hi) by the upward path (ūrdhvamārgeṇa), having cheated (vañcayitvā) the mouth of the sun (ravermukham). Viśuddha as nectar reservoir — holding amṛta bypasses the solar fire
[soma-kalā = "lunar digits" = the 16 phases of the moon, source of nectar; dhṛtvā from √dhṛ = "having held/retained"; vañcayitvā = causative gerund of √vañc "to deceive" — the nectar "cheats" the sun by not falling into its consuming fire; ūrdhva-mārga = the ascending path through suṣumnā]		 When the nectar of immortality from the digits of the moon is held at the supreme Viśuddhi cakra, it goes by an upper path (ūrdhvamārgeṇa), having cheated the mouth of the sun.
Verse 122:

viśadena smṛto haṃso nairmalyaṃ śuddhir ucyate |
ataḥ kaṇṭhe viśuddhākhyaṃ cakraṃ cakravido viduḥ ||122||

De-sandhi:
viśadena smṛtaḥ haṃsaḥ nairmalyam śuddhiḥ ucyate |
ataḥ kaṇṭhe viśuddha-ākhyam cakram cakra-vidaḥ viduḥ ||		 viśadena = by purity/clarity (inst. n., viśada)
smṛtaḥ = known as, remembered (nom. m. ppp. √smṛ)
haṃsaḥ = the haṃsa/breath (nom. m.)
nairmalyam = stainlessness, purity (nom. n.)
śuddhiḥ = purification (nom. f.)
ucyate = is called (pass. √vac)
ataḥ = therefore, because of this
kaṇṭhe = in the throat (loc. m.)
viśuddha-ākhyam = called "viśuddha" (acc. n.)
cakram = wheel/center (acc. n.)
cakra-vidaḥ = knowers of cakras (nom. pl.)
viduḥ = know (3rd pl. perf. √vid)		 The haṃsa (haṃsaḥ) [breath] is known as (smṛtaḥ) "vi" [i.e. purity] (viśadena); stainlessness (nairmalyam) is called (ucyate) purification (śuddhiḥ). Therefore (ataḥ), those who know cakras (cakravidaḥ) know (viduḥ) the cakra (cakram) in the throat (kaṇṭhe) [as] called "Viśuddha" (viśuddhākhyam). Mystical etymology: vi = haṃsa (breath), śuddhi = purity → viśuddha
[Folk etymology: vi- is interpreted as haṃsa (the cosmic breath), śuddhi as purification; haṃsa is "known as" (smṛta) vi (purity/clarity), and śuddhi = purification → viśuddha; nairmalya from nir-mala = "without impurity"; this is nirukti-style esoteric word-analysis typical of yoga texts]		 "Vi" means Haṃsa [the breath] and "Śuddhi" means purity. Because of this, those who know about cakras know the cakra in the throat as Viśuddhi.
Verse 123:

amṛtaṃ kandare kṛtvā nāsāntaḥsuṣire kramāt |
svayam askhalitaṃ yāti vañcayitvā mukhaṃ raveḥ ||123||

De-sandhi:
amṛtam kandare kṛtvā nāsā-antaḥ-suṣire kramāt |
svayam a-skhalitam yāti vañcayitvā mukham raveḥ ||		 amṛtam = nectar (acc. n.)
kandare = in the hollow/cavity (loc. n.)
kṛtvā = having placed (gerund √kṛ)
nāsā-antaḥ-suṣire = into the channel inside the nose (loc. n.)
kramāt = gradually, step by step (abl. m.)
svayam = of its own accord, spontaneously
a-skhalitam = without stumbling, directly (acc. n.)
yāti = goes (3rd sg. √yā)
vañcayitvā = having cheated (gerund caus. √vañc)
mukham raveḥ = the mouth of the sun (acc. + gen.)		 Having placed (kṛtvā) the nectar (amṛtam) in the hollow (kandare) [of the Viśuddha], it gradually (kramāt) goes (yāti) of its own accord (svayam), without obstruction (askhalitam), into the channel inside the nose (nāsāntaḥsuṣire), having cheated (vañcayitvā) the mouth (mukham) of the sun (raveḥ). Nectar stored in viśuddha flows spontaneously upward through nasal channel
[kandara = hollow, cavity (the viśuddha's reservoir); nāsā-antaḥ-suṣira = "channel within the nose" = the subtle pathway; a-skhalita = "un-stumbling" = without obstruction, directly; svayam = spontaneously — once stored properly, nectar naturally rises; same vañcayitvā motif — deceiving the solar fire]		 After the nectar of immortality has been placed in the hollow [in the Viśuddhi cakra] it gradually enters of its own accord directly into the channel inside the nose, having cheated the mouth of the sun.
Verse 124:

baddhaṃ somakalājalaṃ yad amalaṃ kaṇṭhasthālād ūrdhvato
nāsāntaḥsuṣireṇa yāti gaganadvāraṃ tataḥ sarvataḥ |
ūrdhvāsyo bhuvi sannipaty niyatas tenaṅgam āplāvayet
evaṃ yaḥ kurute jitendriyacayo naivāsti tasya kṣayaḥ ||124||

De-sandhi:
baddham soma-kalā-jalam yat amalam kaṇṭha-sthālāt ūrdhvataḥ
nāsā-antaḥ-suṣireṇa yāti gagana-dvāram tataḥ sarvataḥ |
ūrdhva-āsyaḥ bhuvi sannipati niyataḥ tena aṅgam āplāvayet
evam yaḥ kurute jita-indriya-cayaḥ na eva asti tasya kṣayaḥ ||		 baddham = held, bound (acc. n. ppp. √bandh)
soma-kalā-jalam = fluid of the lunar digits (acc. n.)
yat amalam = which is pure (rel. + acc. n.)
kaṇṭha-sthālāt = from the region of the throat (abl. n.)
ūrdhvataḥ = upward (adv.)
nāsā-antaḥ-suṣireṇa = through the channel inside the nose (inst. n.)
yāti = goes (3rd sg. √yā)
gagana-dvāram = to the gateway of the sky/skull (acc. n.)
tataḥ sarvataḥ = then entirely, in its entirety
ūrdhva-āsyaḥ = face upward (nom. m. bahuvrīhi)
bhuvi = on the ground (loc. f.)
sannipati = comes down, descends (3rd sg. sam-ni-√pat)
niyataḥ = the ascetic, one who is controlled (nom. m.)
tena = with it (inst. n.)
aṅgam = the body (acc. n.)
āplāvayet = should flood, bathe (caus. opt. ā-√plu)
evam = thus
yaḥ kurute = whoever does (rel. + 3rd sg. √kṛ)
jita-indriya-cayaḥ = with senses mastered (nom. m. bahuvrīhi)
na eva asti = there is not at all
tasya kṣayaḥ = his destruction/perishing (gen. + nom.)		 Having been held (baddham), the pure (amalam) fluid of the lunar digits (somakalājalam) goes (yāti) upward (ūrdhvataḥ) from the throat region (kaṇṭhasthālāt) through the channel inside the nose (nāsāntaḥsuṣireṇa) to the gateway of the skull (gaganadvāram), then entirely (tataḥ sarvataḥ). When [the ascetic] comes down (sannipati) onto the ground (bhuvi) with face upward (ūrdhvāsyaḥ), the controlled one (niyataḥ) should flood (āplāvayet) his body (aṅgam) with it (tena). For him (tasya) who practises thus (evaṃ yaḥ kurute) with all senses mastered (jitendriyacayaḥ), there is no (na eva asti) destruction (kṣayaḥ). Complete viparītakaraṇī: nectar rises to skull gateway, then floods body on descent — immortality
[Śārdūlavikrīḍita meter (19 syllables); gagana-dvāra = "sky-door" = brahmarandhra at crown; sannipati from sam-ni-√pat = "falls together" = comes back down; āplāvayet = causative optative of ā-√plu "to flood/bathe"; jita-indriya-caya = "heap of conquered senses" = one with all senses mastered; kṣaya = destruction, death — the practitioner becomes immortal]		 Having been held [there], the pure fluid from the digits of the moon then goes in its entirety upwards from the region of the throat via the channel in the nose to the gateway to the skull (gaganadvāram). When he comes down onto the ground, with his face [still] upwards, the ascetic (niyataḥ) floods his body with it. He who practises thus with all his senses mastered cannot perish.
KHECARĪ MUDRĀ: SOMA DRINKING AND ITS FRUITS - somapānaphalam (VERSES 125-131)
Verse 125:

ūrdhvajihvaḥ sthito bhūtvā somapānaṃ karoti yaḥ |
māsārdhena na sandeho mṛtyuṃ jayati yogavit ||125||

De-sandhi:
ūrdhva-jihvaḥ sthitaḥ bhūtvā soma-pānam karoti yaḥ |
māsa-ardhena na sandehaḥ mṛtyum jayati yoga-vit ||		 ūrdhva-jihvaḥ = with tongue [turned] upward (nom. m. bahuvrīhi)
sthitaḥ = remaining, stationed (nom. m. ppp √sthā)
bhūtvā = having become (abs. √bhū)
soma-pānam = the drinking of Soma (acc. n.)
karoti = performs (3rd sg. √kṛ)
yaḥ = who (rel. nom. m.)
māsa-ardhena = within half a month (inst. n.)
na sandehaḥ = no doubt (neg. + nom. m.)
mṛtyum = death (acc. m.)
jayati = conquers (3rd sg. √ji)
yoga-vit = knower of yoga (nom. m.)		 He who (yaḥ), having remained (sthito bhūtvā) with tongue turned upward (ūrdhvajihvaḥ), performs (karoti) the drinking of Soma (somapānam) — within half a month (māsārdhena), without doubt (na sandehaḥ), the knower of yoga (yogavit) conquers (jayati) death (mṛtyum). Khecarī + somapāna → conquest of death in half a month
[Anuṣṭubh meter; ūrdhva-jihva = tongue in khecarī position, inserted into nasopharyngeal cavity; soma-pāna = "moon-drinking" = consuming the nectar that drips from the cranial moon (soma/candra); māsa-ardha = half a month = 15 days; yoga-vit from √vid = "one who knows yoga"]		 The knower of yoga who remains with his tongue upwards and drinks Soma is sure to conquer death within half a month.
Verse 126:

sampīḍya rasanāgreṇa rājadantabilaṃ mahat |
dhyātvāmṛtamayīṃ devīṃ ṣaṇmāsena kavirbhavet ||126||

De-sandhi:
sampīḍya rasanā-agreṇa rāja-danta-bilam mahat |
dhyātvā amṛta-mayīm devīm ṣaṭ-māsena kaviḥ bhavet ||		 sampīḍya = having pressed (abs. sam-√pīḍ)
rasanā-agreṇa = with the tip of the tongue (inst. n.)
rāja-danta-bilam = the opening at the uvula (acc. n.; rājadanta = "king-tooth" = uvula)
mahat = great (acc. n.)
dhyātvā = having meditated (abs. √dhyai)
amṛta-mayīm = consisting of the nectar of immortality (acc. f. -maya)
devīm = the goddess (acc. f.)
ṣaṭ-māsena = in six months (inst. m.)
kaviḥ = a poet, seer (nom. m.)
bhavet = becomes (opt. 3rd sg. √bhū)		 Having pressed (sampīḍya) the great (mahat) opening at the uvula (rājadantabilam) with the tip of the tongue (rasanāgreṇa), and having meditated (dhyātvā) on the goddess (devīm) as consisting of the nectar of immortality (amṛtamayīm), one becomes (bhavet) a poet (kaviḥ) within six months (ṣaṇmāsena). Tongue presses uvula + meditation on amṛta-goddess → kavi in 6 months
[Anuṣṭubh meter; rāja-danta = "king-tooth" = uvula (the soft palate's pendant); bila = opening, cavity; amṛta-mayī = adjectival -maya suffix in feminine = "made of/consisting of nectar"; kavi = not merely "poet" but inspired seer with spontaneous eloquent speech — a siddhi of khecarī practice; the goddess (devī) here = kuṇḍalinī or the nectar-source personified]		 Pressing the special opening at the uvula with the tip of the tongue and meditating on the goddess as made of the nectar of immortality, [the yogi] becomes a poet (kavi) in six months.
Verse 127:

sarvadvārāṇi badhnāti tadūrdhvaṃ dhāritaṃ mahat |
baddhamūrdhvabilaṃ yena nava baddhāni tena hi ||
ajarāmaramāpnoti yathā pañcamukho haraḥ |
na muñcantyamṛtaṃ kvāpi tripathācandramākalāḥ ||127||

De-sandhi:
sarva-dvārāṇi badhnāti tat ūrdhvam dhāritam mahat |
baddha-mūrdhva-bilam yena nava baddhāni tena hi ||
ajara-amaram āpnoti yathā pañca-mukhaḥ haraḥ |
na muñcanti amṛtam kva api tripathā-candramā-kalāḥ ||		 sarva-dvārāṇi = all the openings (acc. n. pl.)
badhnāti = closes, seals (3rd sg. √bandh)
tat = that (nom. n.)
ūrdhvam = above (adv.)
dhāritam = held (ppp √dhṛ, nom. n.)
mahat = great (nom. n.)
baddha-mūrdhva-bilam = the upper opening having been sealed (acc. n.)
yena = by whom (inst. rel. m.)
nava = nine
baddhāni = sealed (nom. n. pl. ppp √bandh)
tena = by him (inst. m.)
hi = indeed
ajara-amaram = freedom from old age and death (acc. n.)
āpnoti = attains (3rd sg. ā-√āp)
yathā = like, as
pañca-mukhaḥ = five-faced (nom. m.)
haraḥ = Hara/Śiva (nom. m.)
na muñcanti = do not release (3rd pl. √muc)
amṛtam = nectar (acc. n.)
kva api = anywhere
tripathā = at the triple pathway (= iḍā/piṅgalā/suṣumnā junction)
candramā-kalāḥ = digits of the moon (nom. f. pl.)		 The great (mahat) [nectar] held (dhāritam) above (ūrdhvam) closes (badhnāti) all the openings (sarvadvārāṇi). He by whom (yena) the upper opening (mūrdhvabilam) has been sealed (baddham) — by him (tena) the nine (nava) [others] are sealed (baddhāni) too (hi). He attains (āpnoti) freedom from ageing and death (ajarāmaram), like (yathā) five-faced (pañcamukhaḥ) Hara (haraḥ). The digits of the moon (candramākalāḥ) at the triple pathway (tripathā) never (na … kvāpi) release (muñcanti) the nectar (amṛtam). Sealing the upper opening seals all nine → ajarāmara like Śiva; moon-digits retain nectar
[Double śloka (8 pādas); Jim's edition numbers the sub-verses differently: 148ab, 127, 146cd — suggesting textual conflation across recensions; mūrdhva-bila = "upper opening" = brahmarandhra; nava dvārāṇi = the nine bodily openings (2 eyes, 2 ears, 2 nostrils, mouth, anus, urethra); pañca-mukha = Sadāśiva's five faces (Tatpuruṣa, Aghora, Sadyojāta, Vāmadeva, Īśāna); tri-patha = "triple path" = the three-channel junction at ājñā; candramā-kalāḥ = the 16 digits (kalā) of the internal moon]		 (148ab) The great [nectar] held above her closes all the openings. (127) He who has sealed the upper opening seals the nine [other] openings [too]. (146cd) [And] he attains freedom from old age and death, like five-faced Śiva. [Then] the digits of the moon at the triple pathway never release the nectar of immortality.
Verse 128:

cumbantī yadi lambikāgramaniśaṃ jihvā rasasyandinī
sakṣārā kaṭukātha dugdhasadṛśā madhvājyatulyāthavā |
vyādhīnāṃ haraṇaṃ jaropaśamanaṃ śāstrāgamodīraṇaṃ
tasya syādamaratvamaṣṭaguṇitaṃ siddhāṅganākarṣaṇam ||128||

De-sandhi:
cumbantī yadi lambikā-agram aniśam jihvā rasa-syandinī
sa-kṣārā kaṭukā atha dugdha-sadṛśā madhu-ājya-tulyā athavā |
vyādhīnām haraṇam jarā-upaśamanam śāstra-āgama-udīraṇam
tasya syāt amaratvam aṣṭa-guṇitam siddha-aṅganā-ākarṣaṇam ||		 cumbantī = caressing, kissing (pres. part. f. nom. √cumb)
yadi = if, when
lambikā-agram = the tip of the uvula (acc. n.)
aniśam = continually, ceaselessly (adv.)
jihvā = the tongue (nom. f.)
rasa-syandinī = oozing liquid/juice (nom. f. pres. part. √syand)
sa-kṣārā = salty (nom. f.; sa- = "with")
kaṭukā = pungent, bitter (nom. f.)
atha = then
dugdha-sadṛśā = like milk (nom. f.)
madhu-ājya-tulyā = like honey and ghee (nom. f.)
athavā = or
vyādhīnām = of diseases (gen. m. pl.)
haraṇam = removal (nom. n.)
jarā-upaśamanam = alleviation of old age (nom. n.)
śāstra-āgama-udīraṇam = spontaneous recitation of learned and sacred texts (nom. n.)
tasya = for him (gen. m.)
syāt = there would be (opt. 3rd sg. √as)
amaratvam = immortality (nom. n.)
aṣṭa-guṇitam = the eightfold powers (nom. n.)
siddha-aṅganā-ākarṣaṇam = the attraction of Siddha women (nom. n.)		 If (yadi) the tongue (jihvā), oozing liquid (rasasyandinī), continually (aniśam) caresses (cumbantī) the tip of the uvula (lambikāgram) — [first] salty (sakṣārā), pungent (kaṭukā), then (atha) milky (dugdhasadṛśā), or (athavā) like honey and ghee (madhvājyatulyā) — there arises (syāt) for him (tasya) the removal (haraṇam) of diseases (vyādhīnām), the alleviation (upaśamanam) of old age (jarā), spontaneous recitation (udīraṇam) of the learned and sacred texts (śāstrāgama), immortality (amaratvam), the eightfold powers (aṣṭaguṇitam), and the attraction of Siddha women (siddhāṅganākarṣaṇam). Progressive taste sequence at uvula → catalogue of six siddhis culminating in amaratva
[Śārdūlavikrīḍita meter (19 syllables per pāda); the taste progression (salty → pungent → milky → honey/ghee) marks deepening khecarī mastery — each stage indicates increasing nectar flow; lambikā = uvula; rasa-syandinī from √syand = "to flow/ooze"; aṣṭa-guṇita = the eight classical siddhis (aṇimā, laghimā, mahimā, garīmā, prāpti, prākāmya, īśitva, vaśitva); siddha-aṅganā = female Siddhas/yoginīs; udīraṇa = spontaneous utterance, not ordinary recitation — implies inspired speech]		 If the tongue, oozing liquid, continually caresses the tip of the uvula and is salty, pungent, then milky or like ghee and honey, there is the removal of disease, the alleviation of old age, [spontaneous] recitation of the scholarly and religious texts, immortality, the eight powers and the ability to attract Siddha women.
Verse 129:

amṛtāpūrṇadehasya yogino dvitrivatsarāt |
ūrdhvaṃ pravartate retastvaṇimādiguṇodayaḥ ||129||

De-sandhi:
amṛta-āpūrṇa-dehasya yoginaḥ dvi-tri-vatsarāt |
ūrdhvam pravartate retaḥ tu aṇimā-ādi-guṇa-udayaḥ ||		 amṛta-āpūrṇa-dehasya = of [the yogin] whose body is filled with nectar (gen. m. bahuvrīhi)
yoginaḥ = of the yogin (gen. m.)
dvi-tri-vatsarāt = after two or three years (abl. n.)
ūrdhvam = upward (adv.)
pravartate = moves, proceeds (3rd sg. pra-√vṛt)
retaḥ = semen (nom. n.)
tu = and, moreover
aṇimā-ādi-guṇa-udayaḥ = the arising of powers beginning with aṇimā (nom. m.)		 After two or three years (dvitrivatsarāt), the semen (retaḥ) of the yogin (yoginaḥ) whose body is filled with the nectar of immortality (amṛtāpūrṇadehasya) moves upward (ūrdhvaṃ pravartate), and the powers (guṇa) beginning with aṇimā (aṇimādi) arise (udayaḥ). Nectar-filled body → ūrdhvaretas (upward semen) + aṇimādi siddhis after 2–3 years
[Anuṣṭubh meter; āpūrṇa = ā-√pṝ "completely filled"; ūrdhvam pravartate retaḥ = semen moves upward — the classical ūrdhvaretas doctrine: sublimation of sexual energy; aṇimā = the power of becoming as small as an atom, first of the aṣṭasiddhi; dvi-tri = dvandva "two-three" = approximate; the verse links khecarī's nectar practice to both bindu-preservation and siddhi-attainment]		 After two or three years the semen of the yogi whose body is filled with the nectar of immortality moves upwards and the powers of becoming as small as an atom and so forth arise.
Verse 130:

nityaṃ somakalāpūrṇaṃ śarīraṃ yasya yoginaḥ |
takṣakeṇāpi daṣṭasya viṣaṃ tasya na pīḍayet ||130||

De-sandhi:
nityam soma-kalā-pūrṇam śarīram yasya yoginaḥ |
takṣakeṇa api daṣṭasya viṣam tasya na pīḍayet ||		 nityam = constantly (adv.)
soma-kalā-pūrṇam = filled with the digits of the moon (acc. n.)
śarīram = body (nom. n.)
yasya = whose (gen. rel. m.)
yoginaḥ = of the yogin (gen. m.)
takṣakeṇa = by Takṣaka (inst. m.; the serpent-king)
api = even
