daṣṭasya = of one bitten (gen. m. ppp √daṃś)
viṣam = poison (nom. n.)
tasya = his (gen. m.)
na pīḍayet = would not afflict (neg. + caus. opt. √pīḍ)		 The yogin (yoginaḥ) whose (yasya) body (śarīram) is constantly (nityam) filled with the digits of the moon (somakalāpūrṇam) — even if (api) bitten (daṣṭasya) by Takṣaka (takṣakeṇa), poison (viṣam) would not afflict (na pīḍayet) him (tasya). Soma-saturated body → immunity to poison, even from Takṣaka
[Anuṣṭubh meter; Takṣaka = king of the nāgas, whose bite killed King Parīkṣit (Mahābhārata 1.36–53) — the deadliest possible venom; pīḍayet = causative optative of √pīḍ "to press, afflict"; soma-kalā = the 16 digits of the internal moon; the claim is that the nectar-saturated body becomes a universal antidote]		 When the body of the yogi is constantly filled with the [nectar from the] digits of the moon, poison does not trouble him even if he were to be bitten by Takṣaka.
Verse 131:

indhanāni yathā vahnistailavartīva dīpakaḥ |
tathā somakalāpūrṇaṃ dehī dehaṃ na muñcati ||131||

De-sandhi:
indhanāni yathā vahniḥ taila-vartī iva dīpakaḥ |
tathā soma-kalā-pūrṇam dehī deham na muñcati ||		 indhanāni = fuel (acc. n. pl.)
yathā = just as
vahniḥ = fire (nom. m.)
taila-vartī = a wick in oil (acc. f.)
iva = like, as
dīpakaḥ = a lamp (nom. m.)
tathā = so, likewise
soma-kalā-pūrṇam = full of the digits of the moon (acc. n.)
dehī = the embodied self (nom. m.)
deham = body (acc. m.)
na muñcati = does not leave (neg. + 3rd sg. √muc)		 Just as (yathā) fire (vahniḥ) does not leave fuel (indhanāni), nor (iva) a lamp (dīpakaḥ) [leave] a wick in oil (tailavartī), so (tathā) the embodied self (dehī) does not leave (na muñcati) a body (deham) that is full of the digits of the moon (somakalāpūrṇam). Double simile: fire/fuel + lamp/wick → the self will not abandon a soma-filled body
[Anuṣṭubh meter; two parallel similes for the same point: (1) fire clings to fuel, (2) a lamp's flame clings to the oiled wick — so the ātman/jīva remains bound to a body saturated with lunar nectar; dehī = "the embodied one" = ātman as possessor of a body; muñcati from √muc = "to release, abandon"; this concludes the somapāna section: the body becomes so nourishing to the self that death (= the self's departure) cannot occur]		 Just as fire does not leave fuel, nor [the flame of] a lamp [leave] a wick in oil, so the embodied self does not leave a body which is full of the [nectar from the] digits of the moon.
FIXATION - dhāraṇā (VERSES 132-133)
Verse 132:

āsanena samāyuktaḥ prāṇāyāmena saṃyutaḥ |
pratyāhāreṇa saṃyukto dhāraṇāṃ ca sadābhyset ||132||

De-sandhi:
āsanena samāyuktaḥ prāṇāyāmena saṃyutaḥ |
pratyāhāreṇa saṃyuktaḥ dhāraṇām ca sadā abhyset ||		 āsanena = with posture (inst. n.)
samāyuktaḥ = accomplished, furnished (nom. m. ppp sam-ā-√yuj)
prāṇāyāmena = with breath-control (inst. m.)
saṃyutaḥ = joined, equipped (nom. m. ppp sam-√yuj)
pratyāhāreṇa = with sense-withdrawal (inst. m.)
saṃyuktaḥ = equipped (nom. m. ppp sam-√yuj)
dhāraṇām = fixation (acc. f.)
ca = and
sadā = always, regularly (adv.)
abhyset = should practise (opt. 3rd sg. abhi-√as)		 Accomplished (samāyuktaḥ) in posture (āsanena), equipped (saṃyutaḥ) with breath-control (prāṇāyāmena), furnished (saṃyuktaḥ) with sense-withdrawal (pratyāhāreṇa), one should regularly (sadā) practise (abhyset) fixation (dhāraṇām). Prerequisites for dhāraṇā: āsana → prāṇāyāma → pratyāhāra → then dhāraṇā
[Anuṣṭubh meter; the verse establishes the sequential progression of aṅgas: āsana (3rd limb), prāṇāyāma (4th), pratyāhāra (5th) must all be mastered before dhāraṇā (6th); three near-synonyms for "equipped" (samāyukta, saṃyuta, saṃyukta) — all from √yuj, rhetorically reinforcing the idea of cumulative yoking; abhyset = optative of abhi-√as "to practise diligently"]		 When the yogi is accomplished in posture, breath-control and withdrawal, he should regularly practise fixation.
Verse 133:

hṛdaye pañcabhūtānāṃ dhāraṇaṃ ca pṛthakpṛthak |
manaso niścalatvena dhāraṇetyabhidhīyate ||133||

De-sandhi:
hṛdaye pañca-bhūtānām dhāraṇam ca pṛthak-pṛthak |
manasaḥ niścalatvena dhāraṇā iti abhidhīyate ||		 hṛdaye = in the heart (loc. n.)
pañca-bhūtānām = of the five elements (gen. n. pl.)
dhāraṇam = fixing, holding (nom./acc. n.)
ca = and
pṛthak-pṛthak = one by one, separately (adv., reduplicated)
manasaḥ = of the mind (gen. n.)
niścalatvena = by means of steadiness/immobility (inst. n.; niścala + -tva)
dhāraṇā = fixation (nom. f.)
iti = thus
abhidhīyate = is called, is defined as (pass. 3rd sg. abhi-√dhā)		 The fixing (dhāraṇam) of the five elements (pañcabhūtānām) in the heart (hṛdaye), one by one (pṛthakpṛthak), by means of mental steadiness (manaso niścalatvena) — that is called (iti abhidhīyate) fixation (dhāraṇā). Definition of dhāraṇā: fixing each of the 5 bhūtas in the heart through mental immobility
[Anuṣṭubh meter; pañca-bhūta = earth, water, fire, wind, space — the five gross elements; pṛthak-pṛthak = iterative reduplication "one after another, each separately" — implies sequential elemental meditation; hṛdaye = heart as the seat of concentration (not anāhata cakra specifically, but the inner locus of dhāraṇā); niścalatva = "the state of being unmoved" — the defining quality of dhāraṇā vs. mere attention; abhidhīyate = passive of abhi-√dhā "to name, designate" — a formal definitional statement (lakṣaṇa)]		 Fixing the five elements in the heart one by one by means of mental focus: that is called fixation.
Verse 134:

yā pṛthvī haritālahemarucirā tattvārthamūlāśritā
saṃyuktā kamalāsanena hi catuḥkoṇe hṛdi sthāyinī |
prāṇaṃ tatra vilīya pañcaghaṭikaṃ cittānvitaṃ dhārayed
eṣā stambhakarī sadā kṣitijayaṃ kuryādbhuvo dhāraṇā ||134||

De-sandhi:
yā pṛthvī haritāla-hema-rucirā tattva-artha-mūla-āśritā
saṃyuktā kamala-āsanena hi catuḥ-koṇe hṛdi sthāyinī |
prāṇam tatra vilīya pañca-ghaṭikam citta-anvitam dhārayet
eṣā stambha-karī sadā kṣiti-jayam kuryāt bhuvaḥ dhāraṇā ||		 yā = which (rel. nom. f.)
pṛthvī = the earth [element] (nom. f.)
haritāla-hema-rucirā = shining like yellow orpiment and gold (nom. f. bahuvrīhi)
tattva-artha-mūla-āśritā = dwelling at the base of the true elements (nom. f. ppp ā-√śri)
saṃyuktā = joined, associated (nom. f. ppp sam-√yuj)
kamala-āsanena = with the lotus-seated one [Brahmā] (inst. m.)
hi = indeed
catuḥ-koṇe = in a square / four-cornered [figure] (loc. n.)
hṛdi = in the heart (loc. n.)
sthāyinī = situated, abiding (nom. f. pres. part. √sthā)
prāṇam = the breath (acc. m.)
tatra = there (adv.)
vilīya = having dissolved/absorbed (abs. vi-√lī)
pañca-ghaṭikam = for five ghaṭikās [= two hours] (acc. n.)
citta-anvitam = accompanied by the mind (acc. n.)
dhārayet = should fix, hold (caus. opt. 3rd sg. √dhṛ)
eṣā = this (nom. f.)
stambha-karī = bringing about steadiness/paralysis (nom. f.)
sadā = always (adv.)
kṣiti-jayam = conquest of earth (acc. m.)
kuryāt = would bring about (opt. 3rd sg. √kṛ)
bhuvaḥ = of earth (gen. f.)
dhāraṇā = fixation (nom. f.)		 The earth [element] (pṛthvī), which (yā) shines like yellow orpiment and gold (haritālahemarucirā), dwells at the base of the true elements (tattvārthamūlāśritā). It is joined (saṃyuktā) with the lotus-seated one [Brahmā] (kamalāsanena) and abides (sthāyinī) in a square (catuḥkoṇe) in the heart (hṛdi). Having dissolved (vilīya) the breath (prāṇam) there (tatra), one should fix (dhārayet) [it] together with the mind (cittānvitam) for five ghaṭikās (pañcaghaṭikam) [= two hours]. This (eṣā) is the fixation of earth (bhuvo dhāraṇā); it always (sadā) brings about steadiness (stambhakarī) and would effect (kuryāt) the conquest of earth (kṣitijayam). Pṛthvī-dhāraṇā: earth element visualised as golden square with Brahmā → stambha-siddhi
[Śārdūlavikrīḍita meter (19 syllables); haritāla = orpiment (yellow arsenic sulphide) — the standard colour-code for pṛthvī in tantric elemental schemes; catuḥ-koṇa = square = earth's yantra shape (cf. Tattvacintāmaṇi, Ṣaṭcakranirūpaṇa); kamalāsana = "lotus-seated" = Brahmā, the presiding deity of earth; pañca-ghaṭika = 5 × 24 minutes = 120 minutes = 2 hours; vilīya from vi-√lī = dissolving the breath into the locus — prāṇa and citta fixed together; stambha = "paralysis/immobilisation" — one of the ṣaṭkarmāṇi (six magical acts); kṣiti-jaya = "conquest of earth" = mastery over the earth element, immunity to its effects; this is the first of five sequential bhūta-dhāraṇās (earth, water, fire, wind, space)]		 The earth element shines like yellow orpiment and gold [and] dwells at the base of the true elements. It is joined with Brahmā [and] is situated in a square in the heart. The yogi should fix his breath and his mind there for two hours (pañca ghaṭikam). This is the fixation of earth. It always brings about steadiness and conquers the earth.
Verse 135:

ardhendupratimaṃ ca kundadhavalaṃ kaṇṭhe 'mbutattvam sthitam
yat pīyūṣayakārabījasahitaṃ yuktaṃ sadā viṣṇunā |
prāṇaṃ tatra vilīya pañcaghaṭikaṃ cittānvitaṃ dhārayet
eṣā duḥsahakālakūṭajaraṇā syādvāruṇī dhāraṇā ||135||

De-sandhi:
ardha-indu-pratimam ca kunda-dhavalam kaṇṭhe ambu-tattvam sthitam
yat pīyūṣa-ya-kāra-bīja-sahitam yuktam sadā viṣṇunā |
prāṇam tatra vilīya pañca-ghaṭikam citta-anvitam dhārayet
eṣā duḥsaha-kālakūṭa-jaraṇā syāt vāruṇī dhāraṇā ||		 ardha-indu-pratimam = resembling a half-moon (acc. n.)
ca = and
kunda-dhavalam = white as jasmine (acc. n.)
kaṇṭhe = in the throat (loc. m.)
ambu-tattvam = the water element (nom. n.)
sthitam = situated (ppp √sthā, nom. n.)
yat = which (rel. nom. n.)
pīyūṣa = nectarine, ambrosia-like (adj.)
ya-kāra-bīja-sahitam = accompanied by the seed-syllable ya (acc. n.)
yuktam = joined (ppp √yuj, nom. n.)
sadā = always (adv.)
viṣṇunā = with Viṣṇu (inst. m.)
prāṇam = the breath (acc. m.)
tatra = there (adv.)
vilīya = having dissolved (abs. vi-√lī)
pañca-ghaṭikam = for five ghaṭikās [= two hours] (acc. n.)
citta-anvitam = accompanied by the mind (acc. n.)
dhārayet = should fix (caus. opt. √dhṛ)
eṣā = this (nom. f.)
duḥsaha-kālakūṭa-jaraṇā = digesting unbearable kālakūṭa poison (nom. f.)
syāt = would be (opt. √as)
vāruṇī dhāraṇā = the Vāruṇī (water) fixation (nom. f.)		 The water element (ambutattvam) is situated (sthitam) in the throat (kaṇṭhe). It resembles a half-moon (ardhendupratimaṃ) and is white as jasmine (kundadhavalam). It is accompanied (sahitam) by the nectarine (pīyūṣa) seed-syllable ya (yakārabīja) and is always (sadā) joined (yuktam) with Viṣṇu (viṣṇunā). Having dissolved (vilīya) the breath (prāṇam) there (tatra), one should fix (dhārayet) [it] together with the mind (cittānvitam) for five ghaṭikās (pañcaghaṭikam) [= two hours]. This (eṣā) would be (syāt) the Vāruṇī fixation (vāruṇī dhāraṇā); it digests (jaraṇā) unbearable (duḥsaha) kālakūṭa poison (kālakūṭa). Ap-dhāraṇā (water): half-moon, jasmine-white, at throat, with Viṣṇu → digests kālakūṭa
[Śārdūlavikrīḍita meter; ardha-indu = half-moon = water's yantra shape (crescent); kunda = jasmine — white is water's colour; kaṇṭha = viśuddha region; ya-kāra = the syllable ya; pīyūṣa = ambrosia (lit. "the drink of immortals"); Viṣṇu = presiding deity of water; kālakūṭa = the terrible poison that emerged at the churning of the ocean (Śiva swallowed it → Nīlakaṇṭha); jaraṇā from √jṝ "to digest" — the water fixation neutralises even this cosmic poison; second of five bhūta-dhāraṇās]		 The water element is situated in the throat. It resembles a half-moon and is as white as a jasmine flower. It is accompanied by the nectarine seed-syllable ya and is always with Vishnu. The yogi should fix his breath and his mind there for two hours. This is the Vāruṇī fixation. It digests unbearable poison.
Verse 136:

yattālusthitamindrabījasahitaṃ tattvaṃ trikoṇe jvalat
tejo rephayutaṃ pravālarucinā rudreṇa yatsaṃyutam |
prāṇaṃ tatra vilīya pañcaghaṭikaṃ cittānvitaṃ dhārayed
eṣā vahnijayaṃ sadā vidadhatī vaiśvānarī dhāraṇā ||136||

De-sandhi:
yat tālu-sthitam indra-bīja-sahitam tattvam tri-koṇe jvalat
tejaḥ repha-yutam pravāla-rucinā rudreṇa yat saṃyutam |
prāṇam tatra vilīya pañca-ghaṭikam citta-anvitam dhārayet
eṣā vahni-jayam sadā vidadhatī vaiśvānarī dhāraṇā ||		 yat = which (rel. nom. n.)
tālu-sthitam = situated at the palate (nom. n.)
indra-bīja-sahitam = accompanied by the seed-syllable of Indra [= ra] (nom. n.)
tattvam = element (nom. n.)
tri-koṇe = in a triangle (loc. n.)
jvalat = blazing (pres. part. nom. n. √jval)
tejaḥ = fire, lustre (nom. n.)
repha-yutam = endowed with the syllable ra (nom. n.)
pravāla-rucinā = with coral-coloured lustre (inst. f.)
rudreṇa = with Rudra (inst. m.)
yat saṃyutam = which is joined (rel. + ppp sam-√yuj)
prāṇam tatra vilīya = having dissolved the breath there
pañca-ghaṭikam = for five ghaṭikās [= two hours]
citta-anvitam = accompanied by the mind
dhārayet = should fix
eṣā = this (nom. f.)
vahni-jayam = conquest of fire (acc. m.)
sadā = always
vidadhatī = effecting, producing (pres. part. f. vi-√dhā)
vaiśvānarī dhāraṇā = the Vaiśvānarī (fire) fixation (nom. f.)		 The fire element (tejaḥ tattvam), situated at the palate (tālusthitam), blazing (jvalat) in a triangle (trikoṇe), accompanied (sahitam) by the seed-syllable of Indra (indrabīja) [= ra], endowed with repha (rephayutam), joined (saṃyutam) with coral-hued (pravālarucinā) Rudra (rudreṇa) — having dissolved (vilīya) the breath (prāṇam) there (tatra), one should fix (dhārayet) [it] together with the mind (cittānvitam) for five ghaṭikās (pañcaghaṭikam) [= two hours]. This (eṣā) is the Vaiśvānarī fixation (vaiśvānarī dhāraṇā); it always (sadā) effects (vidadhatī) the conquest of fire (vahnijayam). Tejas-dhāraṇā (fire): triangle at palate, coral-red, with Rudra, syllable ra → vahni-jaya
[Śārdūlavikrīḍita meter; tri-koṇa = triangle = fire's yantra shape; tālu = palate (maṇipūra region in some systems); indra-bīja = ra, the seed of fire (Indra as lord of fire in Vedic context); repha = the consonant ra (technical grammatical term); pravāla = coral — red/orange is fire's colour; Rudra = presiding deity of fire; vaiśvānara = "belonging to all men" = the universal fire (Agni); vidadhatī from vi-√dhā = "bestowing, effecting"; third of five bhūta-dhāraṇās; NB: Jim's translation has "water (vaiśvānarī)" — a clear typographical error for "fire"]		 The fire element is blazing in a triangle at the palate, together with the seed-syllable of Indra, ra, and coral-coloured Rudra. The yogi should fix his breath and his mind there for two hours. This water (vaiśvānarī) fixation always overcomes fire.
Verse 137:

yadbhinnāñjanapuñjasannibhamidam vṛttaṃ bhruvorantare
tattvaṃ vāyumayaṃ vakārasahitaṃ yatreśvaro devatā |
prāṇaṃ tatra vilīya pañcaghaṭikaṃ cittānvitaṃ dhārayed
eṣā khe gamanaṃ karoti yaminaṃ syādvāyavī dhāraṇā ||137||

De-sandhi:
yat bhinna-añjana-puñja-sannibham idam vṛttam bhruvoḥ antare
tattvam vāyu-mayam va-kāra-sahitam yatra īśvaraḥ devatā |
prāṇam tatra vilīya pañca-ghaṭikam citta-anvitam dhārayet
eṣā khe gamanam karoti yaminam syāt vāyavī dhāraṇā ||		 yat = which (rel. nom. n.)
bhinna-añjana-puñja-sannibham = resembling a mass of dark collyrium (nom. n.)
idam = this (nom. n.)
vṛttam = circular (nom. n.)
bhruvoḥ antare = between the eyebrows (gen. dual + loc.)
tattvam = element (nom. n.)
vāyu-mayam = consisting of air (nom. n. -maya)
va-kāra-sahitam = accompanied by the syllable va (nom. n.)
yatra = in which (rel. adv.)
īśvaraḥ = Īśvara (nom. m.)
devatā = the deity (nom. f.)
prāṇam tatra vilīya = having dissolved the breath there
pañca-ghaṭikam = for five ghaṭikās [= two hours]
citta-anvitam = accompanied by the mind
dhārayet = should fix
eṣā = this (nom. f.)
khe = in/through space (loc. n.)
gamanam = movement, travel (acc. n.)
karoti = effects, grants (3rd sg. √kṛ)
yaminam = for the disciplined one (acc. m.)
syāt = would be (opt. √as)
vāyavī dhāraṇā = the Vāyavī (air) fixation (nom. f.)		 This (idam) element (tattvam) consisting of air (vāyumayam), which (yat) resembles a mass of dark collyrium (bhinnāñjanapuñjasannibham), is circular (vṛttam) [and located] between the eyebrows (bhruvorantare), accompanied (sahitam) by the syllable va (vakāra), in which (yatra) the deity (devatā) is Īśvara (īśvaraḥ). Having dissolved (vilīya) the breath (prāṇam) there (tatra), one should fix (dhārayet) [it] together with the mind (cittānvitam) for five ghaṭikās (pañcaghaṭikam) [= two hours]. This (eṣā) would be (syāt) the Vāyavī fixation (vāyavī dhāraṇā); it grants (karoti) the disciplined one (yaminam) movement through space (khe gamanam). Vāyu-dhāraṇā (air): dark circle between eyebrows, with Īśvara, syllable va → khe-gamanam
[Śārdūlavikrīḍita meter; bhinna-añjana = "broken/ground collyrium" = dark antimony/lead-glass — smoky-dark is air's colour; puñja-sannibha = "resembling a heap/mass"; vṛtta = circle = air's yantra shape; bhruvor antare = ājñā-cakra region; va-kāra = the syllable va (NB: Jim reads ya — the standard bīja for vāyu is yam; va/ya confusion common in manuscripts); Īśvara = presiding deity of air; khe gamana = "sky-walking" = the levitation siddhi; yamin from √yam = one who practises yama/self-discipline; fourth of five bhūta-dhāraṇās]		 The element consisting of air is a circle between the eyebrows resembling a mass of lead glass (bhinnāñjana°), accompanied by the syllable ya, in which the deity is Īśvara. The yogi should fix his breath and his mind there for two hours. This is the air (vāyavī) fixation, which grants the ability to move through space to the disciplined.
Verse 138:

ākāśaṃ ca marīcivārisadṛśaṃ yadbrahmarandhre sthitam
yannāthena sadāśivena sahitaṃ śāntaṃ hakārākṣaram |
prāṇaṃ tatra vilīya pañcaghaṭikaṃ cittānvitaṃ dhārayed
eṣā mokṣakapāṭapāṭanapaṭuḥ proktā nabhodhāraṇā ||138||

De-sandhi:
ākāśam ca marīci-vāri-sadṛśam yat brahma-randhre sthitam
yat nāthena sadāśivena sahitam śāntam ha-kāra-akṣaram |
prāṇam tatra vilīya pañca-ghaṭikam citta-anvitam dhārayet
eṣā mokṣa-kapāṭa-pāṭana-paṭuḥ proktā nabhaḥ-dhāraṇā ||		 ākāśam = space [element] (nom. n.)
ca = and
marīci-vāri-sadṛśam = resembling mirage-water (nom. n.)
yat = which (rel. nom. n.)
brahma-randhre = at the aperture of Brahman (loc. n.)
sthitam = situated (ppp √sthā)
yat = which (rel. nom. n.)
nāthena = with the Lord (inst. m.)
sadāśivena = Sadāśiva (inst. m.)
sahitam = together with (nom. n.)
śāntam = peaceful (nom. n.)
ha-kāra-akṣaram = the syllable ha (nom. n.)
prāṇam tatra vilīya = having dissolved the breath there
pañca-ghaṭikam = for five ghaṭikās [= two hours]
citta-anvitam = accompanied by the mind
dhārayet = should fix
eṣā = this (nom. f.)
mokṣa-kapāṭa-pāṭana-paṭuḥ = skilled at breaking open the door of liberation (nom. f.)
proktā = is said to be, is called (ppp pra-√vac, nom. f.)
nabhaḥ-dhāraṇā = the space fixation (nom. f.)		 The space [element] (ākāśam), which (yat) resembles mirage-water (marīcivārisadṛśam), is situated (sthitam) at the aperture of Brahman (brahmarandhre). It is together (sahitam) with the Lord (nāthena) Sadāśiva (sadāśivena), is peaceful (śāntam), and [has] the syllable ha (hakārākṣaram). Having dissolved (vilīya) the breath (prāṇam) there (tatra), one should fix (dhārayet) [it] together with the mind (cittānvitam) for five ghaṭikās (pañcaghaṭikam) [= two hours]. This (eṣā) is called (proktā) the space fixation (nabhodhāraṇā); it is skilled (paṭuḥ) at breaking open (pāṭana) the door (kapāṭa) of liberation (mokṣa). Ākāśa-dhāraṇā (space): mirage-like, at brahmarandhra, with Sadāśiva, syllable ha → mokṣa
[Śārdūlavikrīḍita meter; marīci-vāri = "mirage-water" = shimmering, translucent — space's visual quality; brahma-randhra = the "aperture of Brahman" at the crown of the skull; Sadāśiva = the highest form of Śiva, presiding deity of ākāśa; śānta = peaceful — space is the subtlest, most quiescent element; ha-kāra = the syllable ha = the bīja of ākāśa (also = visarga, the outbreath); mokṣa-kapāṭa-pāṭana-paṭu = a magnificent compound: kapāṭa = door-panel, pāṭana = splitting/breaking (√paṭ caus.), paṭu = skilled/sharp — "expert at smashing the door-bolt of liberation"; proktā = passive ppp of pra-√vac "is declared"; fifth and final bhūta-dhāraṇā — the series ascends: heart → throat → palate → brow → crown, from gross to subtle, culminating in mokṣa]		 The space [element] is located at the aperture of Brahman and looks like a mirage. It is together with Lord Sadāśiva, is peaceful, [and] is the syllable ha. The yogi should fix his breath and his mind there for two hours. This space (nabhas) fixation is said to be able to break down the doorway to liberation (mokṣakavāṭapāṭanapaṭuḥ).

✓ Verses up to 138 completed
Sections: Invocation, Postures, Chakras, Channels, Prāṇa Vāyus, Kuṇḍalinī, Mudrās, Bandhas, Bindu-Rajas, Mahāmudrā, Meditation on Om (praṇavajapaḥ), Advanced Breath Control (prāṇāyāmaḥ) with Progressive Techniques, Kuṇḍalinī Awakening, Benefits of the Six Limbs (ṣaḍaṅgaphalam), Signs of Progress with Warnings (siddhilakṣaṇam), Pratyāhāra (Sense Withdrawal), Esoteric Pratyāhāra (Sun/Moon/Nectar/Viparītakaraṇī), Anāhata Cakra with Khecarī Mudrā, Cooling Breath Techniques, Viśuddha Cakra, Khecarī Mudrā: Soma Drinking (somapānaphalam), and Fixation with Five Elemental Dhāraṇās (dhāraṇā).

Vijñānabhairava Tantra - Scholarly Analysis

Verse-by-Verse Translation with Linguistic Analysis

Sanskrit Text Word-by-Word Analysis Literal Translation Verse Summary Mark's Translation
DEVĪ'S QUESTIONS - devīpraśnaḥ (VERSES 1-7)
śrī devy uvāca |
śrutaṃ deva mayā sarvaṃ rudrayāmalasambhavam |
trikabhedam aśeṣeṇa sārāt sāravibhāgaśaḥ ||1||

De-sandhi:
śrī devī uvāca |
śrutam deva mayā sarvam rudra-yāmala-sambhavam |
trika-bhedam aśeṣeṇa sārāt sāra-vibhāgaśaḥ ||		 śrī = glorious
devī = goddess (nom.)
uvāca = said (3rd sg. perf. √vac)
śrutam = heard (PPP nom.)
deva = O God (voc.)
mayā = by me (inst.)
sarvam = all/completely (nom.)
rudra-yāmala-sambhavam = originating from Rudrayāmala (acc.)
trika-bhedam = the divisions of Trika (acc.)
aśeṣeṇa = without remainder (inst.)
sārāt = from essence (abl.)
sāra-vibhāgaśaḥ = by division of essence (adv.)		 The glorious (śrī) Goddess (devī) said (uvāca): O God (deva), by me (mayā) has been heard (śrutam) completely (sarvam) that which originates from Rudrayāmala (rudrayāmalasambhavam) — the divisions of Trika (trikabhedam) without remainder (aśeṣeṇa), the essence from essence (sārāt), part by part (sāravibhāgaśaḥ). Devī introduces her question
[She has heard the Trika teachings from Rudrayāmala completely, essence by essence]		 The goddess said: O God, I have heard completely the essence of the Trika school that has originated from the Rudrayāmala (Tantra). (But although I have heard) the essence of its essence, part by part...
adyāpi na nivṛtto me saṃśayaḥ parameśvara |
kiṃ rūpaṃ tattvato deva śabdarāśikalāmayam ||2||

De-sandhi:
adya api na nivṛttaḥ me saṃśayaḥ parama-īśvara |
kim rūpam tattvataḥ deva śabda-rāśi-kalā-mayam ||		 adya = now/today
api = even
na = not
nivṛttaḥ = ceased (PPP nom.)
me = my (gen.)
saṃśayaḥ = doubt (nom.)
parama-īśvara = O Supreme Lord (voc.)
kim = what (interrog.)
rūpam = form (nom.)
tattvataḥ = in reality/essentially (adv.)
deva = O God (voc.)
śabda-rāśi-kalā-mayam = consisting of the parts of the phonemic aggregate (nom.)		 ...even now (adyāpi) my (me) doubt (saṃśayaḥ) has not (na) ceased (nivṛttaḥ), O Supreme Lord (parameśvara). What (kim) is the form (rūpam) in reality (tattvataḥ), O God (deva)? Does it consist of the parts of the aggregate of sounds (śabdarāśikalāmayam)? First question: Is Bhairava's form phonemic?
[Despite complete teaching, doubt remains about the ultimate nature of Bhairava]		 ...even now, O Supreme Lord, my doubt has not ceased. O God, what, in actual fact, is (Bhairava's) form (rūpa)? (Does) it consist of the energies of the Aggregate of (phonemic) Sounds?
kiṃ vā navātmabhedena bhairave bhairavākṛtau |
triśirobhedabhinnaṃ vā kiṃ vā śaktitrayātmakam ||3||

De-sandhi:
kim vā nava-ātma-bhedena bhairave bhairava-ākṛtau |
tri-śiraḥ-bheda-bhinnam vā kim vā śakti-traya-ātmakam ||		 kim vā = or what
nava-ātma-bhedena = by the ninefold division (inst.)
bhairave = in Bhairava (loc.)
bhairava-ākṛtau = in the fearsome form (loc.)
tri-śiraḥ-bheda-bhinnam = differentiated by three-headed division (nom.)
vā = or
kim vā = or what
śakti-traya-ātmakam = having the nature of three śaktis (nom.)		 Or (vā) is it by the ninefold division (navātmabhedena) in Bhairava (bhairave), in the fearsome form (bhairavākṛtau)? Or (vā) differentiated by the three-headed division (triśirobhedabhinnam)? Or (vā) having the nature of the three śaktis (śaktitrayātmakam)? Questions: Navātman mantra? Triśirobhairava? Three śaktis?
[Multiple tantric formulations: 9-fold, 3-headed, or icchā-jñāna-kriyā triad?]		 Or else is it within Bhairava's fearsome form as the (Mantra) Navātman? Or is it differentiated into the three-headed (god)? Or is it the three energies?
nādabindumayaṃ vāpi kiṃ candrārdhanirodhikāḥ |
cakrārūḍham anackaṃ vā kiṃ vā śaktisvarūpakam ||4||

De-sandhi:
nāda-bindu-mayam vā api kim candra-ardha-nirodhikāḥ |
cakra-ārūḍham anackam vā kim vā śakti-sva-rūpakam ||		 nāda-bindu-mayam = made of nāda and bindu (nom.)
vā api = or also
kim = what/is it
candra-ardha = half-moon (ardhacandra)
nirodhikāḥ = obstructress (nom.)
cakra-ārūḍham = mounted on the wheel (nom.)
anackam = unstruck/without 'ack' (nom.)
vā = or
śakti-sva-rūpakam = having śakti as its own nature (nom.)		 Or also (vāpi) is it made of nāda and bindu (nādabindumayam)? Is it the half-moon and obstructress (candrārdhanirodhikāḥ)? Or (vā) the unstruck sound (anackam) mounted on the wheel (cakrārūḍham)? Or (vā) having śakti as its essential nature (śaktisvarūpakam)? Questions: Nāda-bindu? Subtle phonemes? Anāhata on cakra?
[Technical terms of mantra-śāstra: subtle sound stages, cakra-mounted consciousness]		 Is it made of Sound (nāda) and the Point (bindu)? Is it the Half Moon (ardhacandra) and the Obstructress (nirodhikā)? Or is it Unstruck Sound, the nature of which is śakti, mounted on the Wheel (of Mantra)?
parāparāyāḥ sakalam aparāyāś ca vā punaḥ |
parāyā yadi tadvat syāt paratvaṃ tad virudhyate ||5||

De-sandhi:
parā-aparāyāḥ sakalam aparāyāḥ ca vā punaḥ |
parāyāḥ yadi tadvat syāt paratvam tat virudhyate ||		 parā-aparāyāḥ = of Parāparā (gen.)
sakalam = with parts/differentiated (nom.)
aparāyāḥ = of Aparā (gen.)
ca = and
vā = or
punaḥ = again/moreover
parāyāḥ = of Parā (gen.)
yadi = if
tadvat = like that
syāt = would be (opt.)
paratvam = supremacy (nom.)
tat = that
virudhyate = is contradicted (pass.)		 Or (vā) is it the differentiated form (sakalam) of Parāparā (parāparāyāḥ), and (ca) again (punaḥ) of Aparā (aparāyāḥ)? If (yadi) Parā (parāyāḥ) were like that (tadvat syāt), that (tat) supremacy (paratvam) would be contradicted (virudhyate). Logical problem: If Parā has parts, she's not supreme
[Parā-Parāparā-Aparā triad; if ultimate has divisions, it's not ultimate]		 Or is it the (differentiated form) of Parāparā with parts (sakala) and, again, of Aparā? If Parā were to be like that, it would be incompatible with her supremacy.
na hi varṇavibhedena dehabhedena vā bhavet |
paratvaṃ niṣkalatvena sakalatve na tad bhavet ||6||

De-sandhi:
na hi varṇa-vibhedena deha-bhedena vā bhavet |
paratvam niṣkalatvena sakalatve na tat bhavet ||		 na = not
hi = indeed
varṇa-vibhedena = by differentiation of phonemes (inst.)
deha-bhedena = by bodily differentiation (inst.)
vā = or
bhavet = would be (opt.)
paratvam = supremacy (nom.)
niṣkalatvena = by undifferentiated nature (inst.)
sakalatve = in the state of having parts (loc.)
na = not
tat = that
bhavet = would be (opt.)		 Indeed (hi) supremacy (paratvam) cannot (na bhavet) be by differentiation of phonemes (varṇavibhedena) or (vā) by bodily differentiation (dehabhedena). Being undifferentiated by nature (niṣkalatvena), that (tat) cannot be (na bhavet) in the state of having parts (sakalatve). The paradox: Ultimate cannot be differentiated yet must be known
[niṣkala (partless) vs sakala (with parts) — core Trika dialectic]		 The supreme state cannot be differentiated into the phonemes (of mantras) by a bodily form; (essentially) undifferentiated by nature, it cannot be made of parts.
prasādaṃ kuru me nātha niḥśeṣaṃ chindhi saṃśayam ||7ab||

De-sandhi:
prasādam kuru me nātha niḥśeṣam chindhi saṃśayam ||		 prasādam = grace (acc.)
kuru = make/grant (2nd sg. imper. √kṛ)
me = to me (dat.)
nātha = O Lord (voc.)
niḥśeṣam = completely/without remainder (adv.)
chindhi = cut/dispel (2nd sg. imper. √chid)
saṃśayam = doubt (acc.)		 O Lord (nātha), grant (kuru) grace (prasādam) to me (me)! Completely (niḥśeṣam) cut away (chindhi) my doubt (saṃśayam)! Devī's plea: Grant grace, dispel all doubt
[Imperative forms; sets stage for Bhairava's response with 112 dhāraṇās]		 O Lord, be gracious and dispel my doubts entirely.
BHAIRAVA'S RESPONSE - bhairavauvāca (VERSES 7cd-23)
śrībhairava uvāca |
sādhu sādhu tvayā pṛṣṭaṃ tantrasāram idaṃ priye ||7cd||

De-sandhi:
śrī-bhairavaḥ uvāca |
sādhu sādhu tvayā pṛṣṭam tantra-sāram idam priye ||		 śrī-bhairavaḥ = the venerable Bhairava (nom.)
uvāca = said (3rd sg. perf. √vac)
sādhu sādhu = well done! well done! (adv.)
tvayā = by you (inst.)
pṛṣṭam = asked (PPP √prach, nom. n.)
tantra-sāram = essence of the tantras (nom. n.)
idam = this (nom. n.)
priye = O beloved (voc. f.)		 The venerable Bhairava (śrībhairavaḥ) said (uvāca): Well done, well done (sādhu sādhu)! This (idam) essence of the tantras (tantrasāram) has been asked (pṛṣṭam) by you (tvayā), O beloved (priye)! Bhairava's approval: The question touches the heart of tantra
[sādhu repeated for emphasis; sāra = essence/core; priye = intimate address to Devī]		 Well done! Well done! O dear one, you have asked about this, the essence of the Tantras.
gūhanīyatamaṃ bhadre tathāpi kathayāmi te |
yat kiṃcit sakalaṃ rūpaṃ bhairavasya prakīrtitam ||8||

De-sandhi:
gūhanīya-tamam bhadre tathā api kathayāmi te |
yat kiṃcit sakalam rūpam bhairavasya prakīrtitam ||		 gūhanīya-tamam = most to be concealed (superlative gerundive √guh)
bhadre = O auspicious one (voc. f.)
tathā api = nevertheless, even so
kathayāmi = I will tell (1st sg. pres. √kath)
te = to you (dat.)
yat kiṃcit = whatever
sakalam = differentiated/with parts (nom. n.)
rūpam = form (nom. n.)
bhairavasya = of Bhairava (gen.)
prakīrtitam = proclaimed/described (PPP pra-√kīrt)		 O auspicious one (bhadre), this is most to be concealed (gūhanīyatamam), yet even so (tathāpi) I will tell (kathayāmi) you (te). Whatever (yat kiṃcit) differentiated (sakalam) form (rūpam) of Bhairava (bhairavasya) has been proclaimed (prakīrtitam)... The supreme secret will be revealed; sakala forms are merely provisional
[gūhanīyatama = superlative of gerundive; sakala = with kalā/parts, i.e. limited/differentiated]		 O auspicious one! this (knowledge) should be kept most secret, but even so I will tell it to you. O Goddess, whatever is said to be Bhairava's differentiated (sakala) form...
tad asāratayā devi vijñeyaṃ śakrajālavat |
māyāsvapnopamaṃ caiva gandharvanagarabhramam ||9||

De-sandhi:
tat asāratayā devi vijñeyam śakra-jāla-vat |
māyā-svapna-upamam ca eva gandharva-nagara-bhramam ||		 tat = that (nom. n.)
asāratayā = by reason of being essenceless (inst. f. abstract)
devi = O Goddess (voc.)
vijñeyam = should be known (gerundive vi-√jñā)
śakra-jāla-vat = like Indra's net/magic (adv.)
māyā-svapna-upamam = comparable to māyā's dream (nom. n.)
ca eva = and indeed
gandharva-nagara-bhramam = the delusion of a gandharva city (nom. n.)		 That (tat), O Goddess (devi), should be known (vijñeyam) as essenceless (asāratayā), like Indra's magical net (śakrajālavat), and indeed (caiva) comparable to māyā's dream (māyāsvapnopamam), the delusion of a gandharva city (gandharvanagarabhramam). Three similes for unreality: Indra's net, dream of māyā, city of gandharvas
[Classical examples of illusion; gandharvapura = mirage city in sky seen by travelers]		 ...it has no essence and so should be known (to be deceptive) like Indra's magic. It is like the dream of Māyā, the mistaken belief in the (non-existent) city of Gandharvas (in the sky).
dhyānārthaṃ bhrāntabuddhīnāṃ kriyāḍambaravartinām |
kevalaṃ varṇitaṃ puṃsāṃ vikalpanihatātmanām ||10||

De-sandhi:
dhyāna-artham bhrānta-buddhīnām kriyā-āḍambara-vartinām |
kevalam varṇitam puṃsām vikalpa-nihata-ātmanām ||		 dhyāna-artham = for the purpose of meditation (acc. n.)
bhrānta-buddhīnām = of those with deluded understanding (gen. pl.)
kriyā-āḍambara-vartinām = of those engaged in ritual ostentation (gen. pl.)
kevalam = merely, only (adv.)
varṇitam = described (PPP √varṇ)
puṃsām = of men (gen. pl.)
vikalpa-nihata-ātmanām = of those whose minds are struck down by conceptualization (gen. pl.)		 [These forms] are described (varṇitam) merely (kevalam) for the purpose of meditation (dhyānārtham), for those of deluded understanding (bhrāntabuddhīnām), those engaged in ritual ostentation (kriyāḍambaravartinām), for men (puṃsām) whose minds are struck down by conceptualization (vikalpanihatātmanām). Provisional teachings for the unready: deluded, ritualistic, conceptualizing minds
[āḍambara = pomp, ostentation; vikalpa = mental construction/duality; nihata = struck down]		 The description of (this form of Bhairava) is given to those people whose mind is confused, those who are engaged in (worldly) activities and immersed in (worldly) thoughts so (they may) meditate (on Him).
tattvato na navātmāsau śabdarāśir na bhairavaḥ |
na cāsau triśirā devo na ca śaktitrayātmakaḥ ||11||

De-sandhi:
tattvataḥ na nava-ātmā asau śabda-rāśiḥ na bhairavaḥ |
na ca asau tri-śirāḥ devaḥ na ca śakti-traya-ātmakaḥ ||		 tattvataḥ = in reality, in truth (abl. adv.)
na = not
nava-ātmā = ninefold self (nom.)
asau = that one, he (nom.)
śabda-rāśiḥ = aggregate of sounds/phonemes (nom.)
bhairavaḥ = Bhairava (nom.)
na ca = nor
tri-śirāḥ = three-headed (nom.)
devaḥ = god (nom.)
śakti-traya-ātmakaḥ = consisting of three śaktis (nom.)		 In reality (tattvataḥ), that Bhairava (asau bhairavaḥ) is not (na) the ninefold self (navātmā), nor (na) the aggregate of phonemes (śabdarāśiḥ). Nor (na ca) is he (asau) the three-headed god (triśirā devaḥ), nor (na ca) consisting of three śaktis (śaktitrayātmakaḥ). Negation of all limited forms: not ninefold, not phonemic, not three-headed, not triadic śakti
[tattvataḥ = ablative used adverbially "from/according to truth"; systematic negation via neti-neti]		 In reality, Bhairava is neither ninefold nor is he the aggregate of (phonemic) sounds; he is not the three-headed God nor is he the three energies.
nādabindumayo vāpi na candrārdhanirodhikāḥ |
na cakrakramasaṃbhinno na ca śaktisvarūpakaḥ ||12||

De-sandhi:
nāda-bindu-mayaḥ vā api na candra-ardha-nirodhikāḥ |
na cakra-krama-saṃbhinnaḥ na ca śakti-sva-rūpakaḥ ||		 nāda-bindu-mayaḥ = made of nāda and bindu (nom.)
vā api = or even
na = not
candra-ardha = half-moon (ardhacandra)
nirodhikāḥ = obstructress (nom.)
na = not
cakra-krama-saṃbhinnaḥ = differentiated by sequence of cakras (nom.)
na ca = nor
śakti-sva-rūpakaḥ = having śakti as his essential nature (nom.)		 [He is] not (na) even (vāpi) made of nāda and bindu (nādabindumayaḥ), nor (na) the half-moon and obstructress (candrārdhanirodhikāḥ). Nor (na) is he differentiated by the sequence of cakras (cakrakramasaṃbhinnaḥ), nor (na ca) is śakti his essential nature (śaktisvarūpakaḥ). More negations: not subtle phonemes, not cakra-based, not śakti-defined
[Technical terms: nāda-bindu-ardhacandra-nirodhikā = stages of mantra dissolution]		 He neither consists of Sound (nāda) and the Point (bindu), nor the Half Moon and Obstructress; nor is he differentiated by (and linked to) the sequence of cakras, nor is śakti His nature.
aprabuddhamatīnāṃ hi etā bālavibhīṣikāḥ |
mātṛmodakavat sarvaṃ pravṛttyartham udāhṛtam ||13||

De-sandhi:
aprabuddha-matīnām hi etāḥ bāla-vibhīṣikāḥ |
mātṛ-modaka-vat sarvam pravṛtti-artham udāhṛtam ||		 aprabuddha-matīnām = of those with unawakened minds (gen. pl.)
hi = indeed, for
etāḥ = these (nom. f. pl.)
bāla-vibhīṣikāḥ = bogeymen for children (nom. f. pl.)
mātṛ-modaka-vat = like a mother's sweet (adv.)
sarvam = all this (nom. n.)
pravṛtti-artham = for the purpose of engagement/practice (acc. n.)
udāhṛtam = is declared/stated (PPP ud-ā-√hṛ)		 For (hi) those with unawakened minds (aprabuddhamatīnām), these (etāḥ) are bogeymen for children (bālavibhīṣikāḥ). All this (sarvam) is declared (udāhṛtam) for the purpose of [spiritual] engagement (pravṛttyartham), like a mother's sweet (mātṛmodakavat). Pedagogical purpose: scary stories and sweets to motivate the spiritually immature
[vibhīṣikā = frightening specter; modaka = sweet ball; upāya = skillful means]		 These (notions of His nature) are (like ghosts) that frighten children, (they are) for those whose intellect has not awakened. It is said that the purpose of all (these teachings) is to impell (them) to act (for their own advancement), like a sweet a mother (gives a child).
dikkālakalanonmuktā deśoddeśāviśeṣiṇī |
vyapadeṣṭum aśakyāsāv akathyā paramārthataḥ ||14||

De-sandhi:
dik-kāla-kalana-unmuktā deśa-uddeśa-aviśeṣiṇī |
vyapadeṣṭum aśakyā asau akathyā parama-arthataḥ ||		 dik-kāla-kalana-unmuktā = free from calculation of direction and time (nom. f.)
deśa-uddeśa-aviśeṣiṇī = without distinction of place or locality (nom. f.)
vyapadeṣṭum = to designate (inf. vi-apa-√diś)
aśakyā = impossible, incapable (nom. f.)
asau = that one, she (nom. f.)
akathyā = inexpressible (nom. f.)
parama-arthataḥ = in the ultimate sense (abl. adv.)		 [That state] is free from calculation of direction and time (dikkālakalanonmuktā), without distinction of place or locality (deśoddeśāviśeṣiṇī). That (asau) is impossible to designate (vyapadeṣṭum aśakyā), inexpressible (akathyā) in the ultimate sense (paramārthataḥ). Beyond all designations: spaceless, timeless, placeless, unspeakable
[kalana = calculation/limitation; feminine forms describe the state (avasthā); paramārthataḥ = "according to highest meaning"]		 (This state) is free of the differentiation of space and time. It is not specified by the designation of (some particular) place. It cannot be described. (Indeed) In reality, it is inexpressible.
antaḥsvānubhavānandā vikalponmuktagocarā |
yāvasthā bharitākārā bhairavī bhairavātmanaḥ ||15||

De-sandhi:
antaḥ-sva-anubhava-ānandā vikalpa-unmukta-gocarā |
yā avasthā bharita-ākārā bhairavī bhairava-ātmanaḥ ||		 antaḥ-sva-anubhava-ānandā = whose bliss is inner self-experience (nom. f.)
vikalpa-unmukta-gocarā = whose domain is free from conceptualization (nom. f.)
yā = which (nom. f. rel.)
avasthā = state, condition (nom. f.)
bharita-ākārā = whose form is fullness (nom. f.)
bhairavī = Bhairavī (nom. f.)
bhairava-ātmanaḥ = of the one whose nature is Bhairava (gen.)		 That (yā) state (avasthā) which is Bhairavī (bhairavī), belonging to one whose nature is Bhairava (bhairavātmanaḥ) — whose bliss is inner self-experience (antaḥsvānubhavānandā), whose domain is free from conceptualization (vikalponmuktagocarā), whose form is fullness (bharitākārā)... The true Bhairavī state: inner bliss, concept-free, full/complete
[bharita = filled/full; gocara = range/domain; bahuvrīhi compounds describing avasthā; sentence continues in v.16]		 [That state of Bhairavī, of the nature of Bhairava, which is the bliss of inner self-experience, whose domain is free from conceptualization, whose form is fullness...]
tad vapus tattvato jñeyaṃ vimalaṃ viśvapūraṇam |
evaṃvidhe pare tattve kaḥ pūjyaḥ kaś ca tṛpyati ||16||

De-sandhi:
tat vapuḥ tattvataḥ jñeyam vimalam viśva-pūraṇam |
evam-vidhe pare tattve kaḥ pūjyaḥ kaḥ ca tṛpyati ||		 tat = that (nom. n.)
vapuḥ = body, form (nom. n.)
tattvataḥ = in truth, according to reality (abl. adv.)
jñeyam = should be known (gerundive √jñā)
vimalam = stainless, immaculate (nom. n.)
viśva-pūraṇam = fulfilling/pervading all (nom. n.)
evam-vidhe = in such a (loc.)
pare = supreme (loc.)
tattve = reality, principle (loc.)
kaḥ = who? what? (nom.)
pūjyaḥ = to be worshipped (gerundive √pūj)
ca = and
tṛpyati = is satisfied (3rd sg. pres. √tṛp)		 That (tat) form (vapuḥ) should be known (jñeyam) in truth (tattvataḥ) as stainless (vimalam) and fulfilling all (viśvapūraṇam). In such (evaṃvidhe) a supreme (pare) reality (tattve), who (kaḥ) is to be worshipped (pūjyaḥ) and who (kaḥ) is satisfied (tṛpyati)? Completes v.15: That body is pure, all-fulfilling — who worships whom?
[Rhetorical question dissolves duality of worshipper/worshipped; viśvapūraṇa = cosmically fulfilling]		 One should know His body as it truly is, immaculate and fulfiling all things. In such a supreme reality, what (can be) the object of worship and who satisfied (thereby)?
evaṃvidhā bhairavasya yāvasthā parigīyate |
sā parā pararūpeṇa parā devī prakīrtitā ||17||

De-sandhi:
evam-vidhā bhairavasya yā avasthā parigīyate |
sā parā para-rūpeṇa parā devī prakīrtitā ||		 evam-vidhā = of such a kind (nom. f.)
bhairavasya = of Bhairava (gen.)
yā = which (nom. f. rel.)
avasthā = state (nom. f.)
parigīyate = is celebrated/lauded (3rd sg. pass. pari-√gai)
sā = she, that (nom. f.)
parā = supreme (nom. f.)
para-rūpeṇa = by reason of supreme nature (inst.)
parā devī = supreme goddess (nom. f.)
prakīrtitā = is proclaimed (PPP pra-√kīrt)		 That (yā) state (avasthā) of Bhairava (bhairavasya) which is of such a kind (evaṃvidhā), which is celebrated (parigīyate) — she (sā) is supreme (parā), and by reason of her supreme nature (pararūpeṇa), is proclaimed (prakīrtitā) as the supreme goddess (parā devī). This state = Parā Devī (Supreme Goddess)
[parigīyate = "is sung about" in scriptures; triple parā emphasizes absolute supremacy]		 That state which is such, lauded (in the scriptures) of that of Bhairava, is supreme and as its nature is supreme (para), it is called the goddess Parā.
śaktiśaktimator yadvad abhedaḥ sarvadā sthitaḥ |
atas taddharmadharmitvāt parā śaktiḥ parātmanaḥ ||18||

De-sandhi:
śakti-śakti-matoḥ yadvat abhedaḥ sarvadā sthitaḥ |
ataḥ tat-dharma-dharmitvāt parā śaktiḥ para-ātmanaḥ ||		 śakti-śakti-matoḥ = of śakti and the possessor of śakti (gen. dual)
yadvat = just as (adv.)
abhedaḥ = non-difference (nom.)
sarvadā = always (adv.)
sthitaḥ = abides (PPP √sthā)
ataḥ = therefore (adv.)
tat-dharma-dharmitvāt = because of being attribute and possessor of attribute (abl.)
parā śaktiḥ = supreme śakti (nom. f.)
para-ātmanaḥ = of the supreme Self (gen.)		 Just as (yadvat) non-difference (abhedaḥ) always (sarvadā) abides (sthitaḥ) between śakti and the possessor of śakti (śaktiśaktimatoḥ), therefore (ataḥ), because of being attribute and its possessor (taddharmadharmitvāt), the supreme śakti (parā śaktiḥ) [is not different] from the supreme Self (parātmanaḥ). Śakti and Śaktimān are non-different, like attribute and substance
[dharma-dharmin = quality and its possessor; core Trika doctrine of Śiva-Śakti identity]		 Just as there is never (any) difference between Śakti and its possessor (or) the attribute and its possessor, thus the supreme power is (not different) from the supreme Self (parātman).
na vahner dāhikā śaktir vyatiriktā vibhāvyate |
kevalaṃ jñānasattāyāṃ prārambho 'yam praveśane ||19||

De-sandhi:
na vahneḥ dāhikā śaktiḥ vyatiriktā vibhāvyate |
kevalam jñāna-sattāyām prārambhaḥ ayam praveśane ||		 na = not
vahneḥ = from/of fire (gen./abl.)
dāhikā = burning (nom. f.)
śaktiḥ = power (nom. f.)
vyatiriktā = separate, distinct (nom. f.)
vibhāvyate = is perceived/conceived (3rd sg. pass. vi-√bhū caus.)
kevalam = only, merely (adv.)
jñāna-sattāyām = in the being/reality of knowledge (loc.)
prārambhaḥ = beginning (nom.)
ayam = this (nom.)
praveśane = for entering (loc. gerund pra-√viś)		 The power (śaktiḥ) of burning (dāhikā) is not (na) perceived (vibhāvyate) as separate (vyatiriktā) from fire (vahneḥ). This (ayam) [distinction] is only (kevalam) a beginning (prārambhaḥ) for entering (praveśane) into the being of knowledge (jñānasattāyām). Fire analogy: burning power is not separate from fire; distinction is only pedagogical entry point
[vahni = fire; dāhikā śakti = capacity to burn; jñānasattā = essential being of awareness]		 The power to burn is not perceived to be different from fire. This (distinction) is only the beginning that serves as an entry into the (essential) being of insight.
śaktyavasthāpraviṣṭasya nirvibhāgena bhāvanā |
tadāsau śivarūpī syāt śaivī mukham ihocyate ||20||

De-sandhi:
śakti-avasthā-praviṣṭasya nirvibhāgena bhāvanā |
tadā asau śiva-rūpī syāt śaivī mukham iha ucyate ||		 śakti-avasthā-praviṣṭasya = of one who has entered the state of śakti (gen.)
nirvibhāgena = without division (inst.)
bhāvanā = contemplation, creative awareness (nom. f.)
tadā = then (adv.)
asau = that one, he (nom.)
śiva-rūpī = having the form of Śiva (nom.)
syāt = would become (opt. √as)
śaivī = Śakti/Śiva's consort (nom. f.)
mukham = face, entrance (nom. n.)
iha = here, in this teaching (adv.)
ucyate = is said (3rd sg. pass. √vac)		 For one who has entered the state of śakti (śaktyavasthāpraviṣṭasya), contemplation (bhāvanā) is without division (nirvibhāgena). Then (tadā) that one (asau) becomes (syāt) Śiva-formed (śivarūpī). Here (iha) Śaivī [śakti] is said to be (ucyate) the entrance (mukham). Entering śakti's state = undivided bhāvanā = becoming Śiva; śakti is the doorway
[mukha = face/mouth/entrance; śaivī = śakti of Śiva; bhāvanā = creative contemplative realization]		 The way of thinking (and being) (bhāvanā) of one who has entered Śakti's state is (one with it) without division. Then he is Śiva. (This) is said to be the entrance which is Śakti here (in this world to the realisation of one's own Śiva nature).
yathālokena dīpasya kiraṇair bhāskarasya ca |
jñāyate digvibhāgādi tadvac chaktyā śivaḥ priye ||21||

De-sandhi:
yathā ālokena dīpasya kiraṇaiḥ bhāskarasya ca |
jñāyate dik-vibhāga-ādi tadvat śaktyā śivaḥ priye ||		 yathā = just as (adv.)
ālokena = by the light (inst.)
dīpasya = of a lamp (gen.)
kiraṇaiḥ = by the rays (inst. pl.)
bhāskarasya = of the sun (gen.)
ca = and
jñāyate = is known (3rd sg. pass. √jñā)
dik-vibhāga-ādi = directions, divisions, etc. (nom.)
tadvat = in the same way (adv.)
śaktyā = by śakti (inst.)
śivaḥ = Śiva (nom.)
priye = O beloved (voc.)		 Just as (yathā) directions and their divisions, etc. (digvibhāgādi) are known (jñāyate) by the light (ālokena) of a lamp (dīpasya) and (ca) by the rays (kiraṇaiḥ) of the sun (bhāskarasya), in the same way (tadvat), O beloved (priye), Śiva (śivaḥ) [is known] by śakti (śaktyā). Light analogy: as lamp/sun reveals directions, so śakti reveals Śiva
[Epistemological point: śakti is the means of knowing Śiva; bhāskara = sun/"light-maker"]		 Just as one knows the directions and (their) divisions etc by the light of the lamp or the rays of the sun, in the same way, O beloved, Śiva (is known) by (His) power (Śakti).
DEVĪ'S SECOND QUESTION - THE MEANS (UPĀYA) (VERSES 22-23)
śrī devy uvāca |
devadeva triśūlāṅka kapālakṛtabhūṣaṇa |
digdeśakālaśūnyā ca vyapadeśavivarjitā ||22||

De-sandhi:
śrī devī uvāca |
deva-deva tri-śūla-aṅka kapāla-kṛta-bhūṣaṇa |
dik-deśa-kāla-śūnyā ca vyapadeśa-vivarjitā ||		 śrī devī uvāca = the blessed Goddess said
deva-deva = O God of gods (voc.)
tri-śūla-aṅka = whose emblem is the trident (voc.)
kapāla-kṛta-bhūṣaṇa = who has made a skull his ornament (voc.)
dik-deśa-kāla-śūnyā = void of direction, place and time (nom. f.)
ca = and
vyapadeśa-vivarjitā = devoid of designation (nom. f.)		 The blessed Goddess (śrī devī) said (uvāca): O God of gods (devadeva), whose emblem is the trident (triśūlāṅka), who has made a skull his ornament (kapālakṛtabhūṣaṇa)! [That state which is] void of direction, place and time (digdeśakālaśūnyā), and (ca) devoid of designation (vyapadeśavivarjitā)... Devī addresses Bhairava with epithets and begins describing the state
[triśūlāṅka = marked with trident; kapālakṛtabhūṣaṇa = skull as ornament — iconographic attributes of Bhairava]		 The Goddess said: O God of the gods, whose emblem is the trident and who has made a skull (his) ornament, [that state which is] free from any sense of direction, space and time and which cannot be described...
yāvasthā bharitākārā bhairavasyopalabhyate |
kair upāyair mukhaṃ tasya parā devi kathaṃ bhavet |
yathā samyag ahaṃ vedmi tathā me brūhi bhairava ||23||

De-sandhi:
yā avasthā bharita-ākārā bhairavasya upalabhyate |
kaiḥ upāyaiḥ mukham tasya parā devī katham bhavet |
yathā samyak aham vedmi tathā me brūhi bhairava ||		 yā avasthā = which state (nom. f.)
bharita-ākārā = whose form is fullness (nom. f.)
bhairavasya = of Bhairava (gen.)
upalabhyate = is attained/perceived (3rd sg. pass. upa-√labh)
kaiḥ upāyaiḥ = by what means? (inst. pl.)
mukham = entrance (nom. n.)
tasya = of that/him (gen.)
parā devī = supreme goddess (nom. f.)
katham = how?
bhavet = would become (opt.)
yathā = so that
samyak = properly, completely (adv.)
aham = I
vedmi = may know (1st sg. pres. √vid)
tathā = thus, in such a way
me = to me (dat.)
brūhi = tell! (2nd sg. imper. √brū)
bhairava = O Bhairava (voc.)		 ...that (yā) state (avasthā) whose form is fullness (bharitākārā), of Bhairava (bhairavasya) — [how] is it attained (upalabhyate)? By what means (kaiḥ upāyaiḥ) would the supreme Goddess (parā devī) become (bhavet) the entrance (mukham) to that (tasya)? Tell (brūhi) me (me), O Bhairava (bhairava), so that (yathā... tathā) I may know (vedmi) properly (samyak)! Devī's crucial question: By what means (upāyaiḥ) can this state be realized?
[bharitākārā = form of fullness; This question prompts Bhairava's revelation of the 112 dhāraṇās; mukha = entrance/doorway via Parā Devī]		 ...and is Bhairava's state of plenitude — by what means can it be realized? How is the supreme Goddess his mouth (through which to enter into him)? O Bhairava, pray tell me (this), so that I may know it properly.
BHAIRAVA'S 112 DHĀRAṆĀS - THE BREATH (VERSES 24-27)
bhairava uvāca |
ūrdhve prāṇo hy adho jīvo visargātmā paroccaret |
utpattidvitayasthāne bharaṇād bharitā sthitiḥ ||24||

De-sandhi:
bhairavaḥ uvāca |
ūrdhve prāṇaḥ hi adhaḥ jīvaḥ visarga-ātmā parā uccaret |
utpatti-dvitaya-sthāne bharaṇāt bharitā sthitiḥ ||		 bhairavaḥ uvāca = Bhairava said
ūrdhve = above (loc.)
prāṇaḥ = the exhaled breath (nom.)
hi = indeed
adhaḥ = below (adv.)
jīvaḥ = the inhaled breath/life (nom.)
visarga-ātmā = whose nature is emission (nom. f.)
parā = the Supreme [Goddess] (nom. f.)
uccaret = utters forth (3rd sg. opt. ud-√car)
utpatti-dvitaya-sthāne = in the two places of origination (loc.)
bharaṇāt = from/by filling (abl.)
bharitā = filled, full (nom. f.)
sthitiḥ = state, condition (nom. f.)		 Bhairava (bhairavaḥ) said (uvāca): The exhaled breath (prāṇaḥ) [rises] above (ūrdhve), indeed (hi), the inhaled breath (jīvaḥ) [descends] below (adhaḥ). The Supreme [Goddess Parā] (parā), whose nature is emission (visargātmā), utters forth (uccaret) [in the form of haṃsa]. In the two places of origination (utpattidvitayasthāne), [one attains] the state of plenitude (bharitā sthitiḥ) by filling (bharaṇāt). First dhāraṇā: Breath awareness at two turning points
[prāṇa = exhalation (saḥ); jīva = inhalation (haṃ); visarga = the emission ḥ; utpattidvitaya = the two places where breath originates/turns — heart and dvādaśānta; bharaṇa = filling with awareness]		 The exhaled breath (prāṇa) (saḥ) is above and the inhaled (jīva) (haṃ) below; (the goddess) Parā who is emission (visarga) is uttering forth (and manifesting in this way) within the two places where they originate. (The yogi attains) the state of plenitude by filling (them).
maruto 'ntar bahir vāpi viyadyugmānivartanāt |
bhairavyā bhairavasyetthaṃ bhairavi vyajyate vapuḥ ||25||

De-sandhi:
marutaḥ antaḥ bahiḥ vā api viyat-yugma-anivartanāt |
bhairavyā bhairavasya ittham bhairavi vyajyate vapuḥ ||		 marutaḥ = of the breath/wind (gen.)
antaḥ = internal (adv.)
bahiḥ = external (adv.)
vā api = or also
viyat-yugma = the pair of voids/spaces (compound)
anivartanāt = from not returning (abl.)
bhairavyā = by Bhairavī (inst. f.)
bhairavasya = of Bhairava (gen.)
ittham = in this way (adv.)
bhairavi = O Bhairavī (voc. f.)
vyajyate = is revealed/manifested (3rd sg. pass. vi-√añj)
vapuḥ = body, form (nom. n.)		 O Bhairavī (bhairavi)! By not returning (anivartanāt) from the two voids (viyadyugma) of the breath (marutaḥ), whether internal (antaḥ) or (vā) also (api) external (bahiḥ), Bhairavī (bhairavyā) reveals (vyajyate) in this way (ittham) the body (vapuḥ) of Bhairava (bhairavasya). Second dhāraṇā: Resting in the void at breath's turning points
[viyadyugma = the two voids/spaces where breath pauses — at the heart (antaḥ) and at dvādaśānta (bahiḥ); anivartana = not returning, remaining still; vapuḥ = body of consciousness]		 O Bhairavī! by not returning out from the two voids of the breath, whether internal or external (where it rests at the beginning and end), Bhairavī reveals in this way Bhairava's body (of consciousness).
na vrajen na viśec chaktir marudrūpā vikāsite |
nirvikalpatayā madhye tayā bhairavarūpatā ||26||

De-sandhi:
na vrajet na viśet śaktiḥ marut-rūpā vikāsite |
nirvikalpatayā madhye tayā bhairava-rūpatā ||		 na = not
vrajet = should go out (3rd sg. opt. √vraj)
na = not
viśet = should enter (3rd sg. opt. √viś)
śaktiḥ = the energy/power (nom. f.)
marut-rūpā = in the form of breath (nom. f.)
vikāsite = when [the center] has unfolded (loc. abs.)
nirvikalpatayā = by the state free of thought (inst. f.)
madhye = in the middle/center (loc.)
tayā = by that [power] (inst. f.)
bhairava-rūpatā = Bhairava-nature, identity with Bhairava (nom. f.)		 The energy (śaktiḥ) in the form of breath (marudrūpā) should neither exit (na vrajet) nor (na) enter (viśet) when the center has unfolded (vikāsite). In the middle (madhye), by the state free of thought (nirvikalpatayā), by that [power] (tayā), [arises] Bhairava-nature (bhairavarūpatā). Third dhāraṇā: Suspending breath in the center without thought
[vikāsite = locative absolute "when unfolded"; madhya = the center (suṣumnā or heart); nirvikalpa = beyond thought-constructs; bhairavarūpatā = abstract noun "Bhairava-ness"]		 The energy in the form of the vital breath should neither exit nor enter when the center has unfolded by the (one pointed) state free of thought. (There), by that (same power), Bhairava's nature (is made manifest).
kumbhitā recitā vāpi pūritā vā yadā bhavet |
tadante śāntanāmāsau śaktyā śāntaḥ prakāśate ||27||

De-sandhi:
kumbhitā recitā vā api pūritā vā yadā bhavet |
tat-ante śānta-nāmā asau śaktyā śāntaḥ prakāśate ||		 kumbhitā = retained (nom. f. PPP √kumbh)
recitā = ejected/exhaled (nom. f. PPP √ric)
vā api = or also
pūritā = filled/inhaled (nom. f. PPP √pūr)
vā = or
yadā = when (adv.)
bhavet = it becomes (3rd sg. opt.)
tat-ante = at the end of that (loc.)
śānta-nāmā = named "Tranquil" (nom. f.)
asau = that [power] (nom. f.)
śaktyā = by [that] power (inst. f.)
śāntaḥ = the Tranquil One (nom. m.)
prakāśate = manifests, shines forth (3rd sg. pres.)		 When (yadā) [the breath] is retained (kumbhitā), or (vāpi) has been exhaled (recitā), or (vā) has been inhaled (pūritā) — at the end of that (tadante), that [power] (asau) named "Tranquil" (śāntanāmā), by [that] power (śaktyā), the Tranquil One (śāntaḥ) manifests (prakāśate). Fourth dhāraṇā: At the end of any breath phase, the Tranquil manifests
[kumbhaka = retention; recaka = exhalation; pūraka = inhalation; śānta = tranquil (epithet of Śiva); śakti here = the tranquil power of breath at rest]		 If (the power of the vital breath) called 'Tranquil' is retained, whether it has been ejected (in the course of exhalation) or filled (in the course of inhalation), in the end of that (practice) the Tranquil One manifests by means of (that same) power.
KUṆḌALINĪ (VERSES 28-29)
āmūlāt kiraṇābhāsāṃ sūkṣmāt sūkṣmatarātmikām |
cintayet tāṃ dviṣaṭkānte śyāmyantīṃ bhairavodayaḥ ||28||

De-sandhi:
ā-mūlāt kiraṇa-ābhāsām sūkṣmāt sūkṣma-tara-ātmikām |
cintayet tām dvi-ṣaṭka-ante śāmyantīm bhairava-udayaḥ ||		 ā-mūlāt = from the root (abl.)
kiraṇa-ābhāsām = having the appearance of rays (acc. f.)
sūkṣmāt = than subtle (abl. comparison)
sūkṣma-tara-ātmikām = whose nature is more subtle (acc. f.)
cintayet = one should contemplate (3rd sg. opt. √cint)
tām = her/that [energy] (acc. f.)
dvi-ṣaṭka-ante = at the end of the twelve (loc.)
śāmyantīm = coming to rest (acc. f. pres. part. √śam)
bhairava-udayaḥ = the arising of Bhairava (nom.)		 One should contemplate (cintayet) her (tām) — having the appearance of rays (kiraṇābhāsām) from the root (āmūlāt), whose nature is more subtle than subtle (sūkṣmāt sūkṣmatarātmikām) — coming to rest (śāmyantīm) at the end of the twelve (dviṣaṭkānte): [this is] the arising of Bhairava (bhairavodayaḥ). Fifth dhāraṇā: Contemplating kuṇḍalinī rising from root to dvādaśānta
[mūla = root (mūlādhāra); dviṣaṭka = "two sixes" = 12 (dvādaśānta, 12 finger-widths above head); kiraṇābhāsa = ray-like appearance; sūkṣmatara = comparative "more subtle"]		 One should think of the light of the rays (of the energy of the vital breath that shine) from the Root, more subtle than subtle, coming to rest within the End of the Twelve (wherein) Bhairava emerges.
udgacchantīṃ taḍitrūpāṃ praticakraṃ kramāt kramam |
ūrdhvaṃ muṣṭitrayaṃ yāvat tāvad ante mahodayaḥ ||29||

De-sandhi:
udgacchantīm taḍit-rūpām prati-cakram kramāt kramam |
ūrdhvam muṣṭi-trayam yāvat tāvat ante mahā-udayaḥ ||		 udgacchantīm = ascending (acc. f. pres. part. ud-√gam)
taḍit-rūpām = having the form of lightning (acc. f.)
prati-cakram = through each wheel/cakra (adv.)
kramāt kramam = in due order, step by step (adv.)
ūrdhvam = upward (adv.)
muṣṭi-trayam = three fists [= 12 finger-widths] (acc. n.)
yāvat = as far as, until (adv.)
tāvat = then, at that point (adv.)
ante = in the end (loc.)
mahā-udayaḥ = the great arising/awakening (nom.)		 [Contemplate her] ascending (udgacchantīm), in the form of lightning (taḍitrūpām), through each cakra (praticakram) step by step (kramāt kramam), upward (ūrdhvam) as far as (yāvat) three fists [above the head] (muṣṭitrayam) — then (tāvat), in the end (ante), the Great Awakening (mahodayaḥ)! Sixth dhāraṇā: Kuṇḍalinī as lightning through the cakras
[taḍit = lightning; praticakram = "at each cakra"; muṣṭitraya = 3 fists = 12 aṅgulas = dvādaśānta; mahodaya = the great arising = liberation/enlightenment]		 (Contemplate Kuṇḍalinī, the power of the vital breath) in the form of lightning, ascending through each Wheel (one after the other) in due order up to the upper End of the Twelve until, in the end, the Great Awakening!
kramadvādaśakaṃ samyag dvādaśākṣarabheditam |
sthūlasūkṣmaparasthityā muktvā muktvāntataḥ śivaḥ ||30||

De-sandhi:
krama-dvādaśakam samyak dvādaśa-akṣara-bheditam |
sthūla-sūkṣma-para-sthityā muktvā muktvā antataḥ śivaḥ ||		 krama-dvādaśakam = the sequence of twelve (nom. n.)
samyak = rightly, properly (adv.)
dvādaśa-akṣara-bheditam = differentiated by the twelve vowels (nom. n.)
sthūla = gross (adj.)
sūkṣma = subtle (adj.)
para = supreme (adj.)
sthityā = by the states (inst. f.)
muktvā muktvā = abandoning again and again (gerund √muc)
antataḥ = in the end, finally (adv.)
śivaḥ = Śiva (nom.)		 The sequence of twelve [centers] (kramadvādaśakam) is rightly (samyak) differentiated by the twelve vowels (dvādaśākṣarabheditam). Abandoning (muktvā muktvā) [their] gross, subtle, and supreme states (sthūlasūkṣmaparasthityā), in the end (antataḥ) [one attains] Śiva (śivaḥ). Seventh dhāraṇā: The twelve centers linked to twelve vowels
[dvādaśaka = set of 12 (cakras/centers); dvādaśākṣara = 12 vowels (a ā i ī u ū ṛ ṝ ḷ ḹ e o); sthūla-sūkṣma-para = gross-subtle-supreme triad; muktvā muktvā = iterative gerund "abandoning each in turn"]		 The sequence of twelve (centres within the body) is rightly linked to the twelve vowels. Abandoning (their) gross, subtle and supreme states (one after another), in the end (the yogi attains) Śiva.
tayāpūryāśu mūrdhāntaṃ bhaṅktvā bhrūkṣepasetunā |
nirvikalpaṃ manaḥ kṛtvā sarvordhve sarvagodgamaḥ ||31||

De-sandhi:
tayā āpūrya āśu mūrdha-antam bhaṅktvā bhrū-kṣepa-setunā |
nirvikalpam manaḥ kṛtvā sarva-ūrdhve sarva-ga-udgamaḥ ||		 tayā = by that [energy] (inst. f.)
āpūrya = having filled (gerund ā-√pṛ)
āśu = quickly (adv.)
mūrdha-antam = up to the summit of the head (acc.)
bhaṅktvā = having crossed over (gerund √bhañj)
bhrū-kṣepa-setunā = by the bridge of eyebrow contraction (inst.)
nirvikalpam = free from thought (acc. n.)
manaḥ = mind (acc. n.)
kṛtvā = having made (gerund √kṛ)
sarva-ūrdhve = above all (loc.)
sarva-ga = all-pervading, omnipresent (adj.)
udgamaḥ = emergence, arising (nom.)		 Having quickly (āśu) filled (āpūrya) up to the summit of the head (mūrdhāntam) with that [energy] (tayā), and having crossed over (bhaṅktvā) by the bridge of eyebrow contraction (bhrūkṣepasetunā), having made (kṛtvā) the mind (manaḥ) free from thought (nirvikalpam), [there is] the emergence (udgamaḥ) of the omnipresent one (sarvaga) above all (sarvordhve). Eighth dhāraṇā: Energy to crown, crossing via bhrūmadhya
[mūrdhānta = brahmarandhra at crown; bhrūkṣepasetu = "bridge of eyebrow-throw" = bhrūmadhya contraction; sarvaga = omnipresent Śiva; multiple gerunds show sequence of practice]		 Having quickly filled (the body up to the Cavity of Brahmā) at the summit of the head with that (same energy of the vital breath) and having crossed over (it) by the bridge (formed by) the contraction of the eyebrows, freeing one's mind from thought, the omnipresent one emerges above all (things).
śikhipakṣaiś citrarūpair maṇḍalaiḥ śūnyapañcakam |
dhyāyato 'nuttare śūnye praveśo hṛdaye bhavet ||32||

De-sandhi:
śikhi-pakṣaiḥ citra-rūpaiḥ maṇḍalaiḥ śūnya-pañcakam |
dhyāyataḥ anuttare śūnye praveśaḥ hṛdaye bhavet ||		 śikhi-pakṣaiḥ = like peacock feathers (inst. pl.)
citra-rūpaiḥ = variegated in form (inst. pl.)
maṇḍalaiḥ = by means of the spheres/circles (inst. pl.)
śūnya-pañcakam = the five voids (acc. n.)
dhyāyataḥ = of one meditating (gen. sg. pres. part.)
anuttare = in the most excellent, unsurpassed (loc.)
śūnye = in the void (loc.)
praveśaḥ = entry (nom.)
hṛdaye = in the heart (loc.)
bhavet = would occur (3rd sg. opt.)		 Meditating (dhyāyataḥ) on the five voids (śūnyapañcakam) by means of the spheres [of the senses] (maṇḍalaiḥ), variegated in form (citrarūpaiḥ) like peacock feathers (śikhipakṣaiḥ), entry (praveśaḥ) into the most excellent void (anuttare śūnye) would occur (bhavet) in the heart (hṛdaye). Ninth dhāraṇā: Five sense-voids like peacock feathers
[śikhipakṣa = peacock feather (with iridescent "eyes"); maṇḍala = sphere/circle (of sense experience); śūnyapañcaka = five voids (of five senses); anuttara = unsurpassed (supreme reality); hṛdaya = heart-space of consciousness]		 Meditating on the five voids (of the sensations) by means of the spheres (of the senses), variegated in form (like) the feathers of a peacock, (the yogi experiences) entry into the most excellent (anuttara) Void within the Heart (of consciousness).
WHERE THE MIND GOES (VERSES 33-35)
īdṛśena krameṇaiva yatra kutrāpi cintanā |
śūnye kuḍye pare pātre svayaṃ līnā varapradā ||33||

De-sandhi:
īdṛśena krameṇa eva yatra kutra api cintanā |
śūnye kuḍye pare pātre svayam līnā vara-pradā ||		 īdṛśena = by this kind of (inst.)
krameṇa = by method/process (inst.)
eva = indeed, same (emphatic)
yatra kutra api = wherever (adv.)
cintanā = thought, attention (nom. f.)
śūnye = in an empty space (loc.)
kuḍye = on a wall (loc.)
pare pātre = on a worthy vessel/disciple (loc.)
svayam = spontaneously, of itself (adv.)
līnā = dissolved (nom. f. PPP √lī)
vara-pradā = boon-bestowing (nom. f.)		 By this same (eva) method (īdṛśena krameṇa), wherever (yatra kutrāpi) one's attention (cintanā) [settles] — in an empty space (śūnye), on a wall (kuḍye), or on a worthy vessel (pare pātre) — it spontaneously (svayam) dissolves (līnā) [and becomes] boon-bestowing (varapradā). Tenth dhāraṇā: Attention dissolving wherever it rests
[cintanā = attentive thought; kuḍya = wall (neutral object); para pātra = "supreme vessel" = worthy disciple; līnā = dissolved/absorbed; varapradā = "boon-giver" = bestowing realization]		 Wherever one's (attentive) thought (cintanā) (settles) by this same process, be it in an empty space, on a wall, or on a worthy disciple, it spontaneously dissolves away into it and (so) bestows the boon (of pure consciousness).
kapālāntar mano nyasya tiṣṭhan mīlitalocanaḥ |
krameṇa manaso dārḍhyāt lakṣayet lakṣyam uttamam ||34||

De-sandhi:
kapāla-antaḥ manaḥ nyasya tiṣṭhan mīlita-locanaḥ |
krameṇa manasaḥ dārḍhyāt lakṣayet lakṣyam uttamam ||		 kapāla-antaḥ = within the skull (loc.)
manaḥ = mind (acc.)
nyasya = having placed (gerund ni-√as)
tiṣṭhan = sitting, remaining (pres. part. √sthā)
mīlita-locanaḥ = with eyes closed (nom.)
krameṇa = progressively, gradually (inst.)
manasaḥ = of the mind (gen.)
dārḍhyāt = from firmness/stability (abl.)
lakṣayet = one should perceive (3rd sg. opt. √lakṣ)
lakṣyam = the goal/target (acc.)
uttamam = supreme (acc.)		 Having placed (nyasya) the mind (manaḥ) within the skull (kapālāntaḥ), sitting (tiṣṭhan) with eyes closed (mīlitalocanaḥ), progressively (krameṇa) from the stability (dārḍhyāt) of the mind (manasaḥ), one should perceive (lakṣayet) the supreme (uttamam) goal (lakṣyam). Eleventh dhāraṇā: Mind placed within the skull, eyes closed
[kapālāntar = inside the skull (cranial space); mīlitalocana = "closed-eyed" (bahuvrīhi); dārḍhya = firmness/stability (from dṛḍha); lakṣya = target/goal (what is aimed at)]		 Having placed one's mind within (the space of the) skull, sitting (motionless) with the eyes closed, by progressively (increasing) the stability of the mind, one perceives the supreme goal.
madhyanāḍī madhyasaṃsthā bisasūtrābharūpayā |
dhyātāntarvyomayā devyā tayā devaḥ prakāśate ||35||

De-sandhi:
madhya-nāḍī madhya-saṃsthā bisa-sūtra-ābha-rūpayā |
dhyātā antar-vyomayā devyā tayā devaḥ prakāśate ||		 madhya-nāḍī = the middle channel (nom. f.)
madhya-saṃsthā = situated in the middle (nom. f.)
bisa-sūtra-ābha-rūpayā = in the form resembling a lotus fiber (inst. f.)
dhyātā = meditated upon (nom. f. PPP √dhyai)
antar-vyomayā = as the inner void/space (inst. f.)
devyā = as the Goddess (inst. f.)
tayā = by her (inst. f.)
devaḥ = the God (nom.)
prakāśate = is illumined/manifests (3rd sg. pres.)		 The middle channel (madhyanāḍī), situated in the middle (madhyasaṃsthā), in a form resembling a lotus fiber (bisasūtrābharūpayā) — meditated upon (dhyātā) as the Goddess (devyā) who is the inner void (antarvyomayā), by her (tayā) the God (devaḥ) is illumined (prakāśate). Twelfth dhāraṇā: Suṣumnā as lotus fiber, Goddess as inner void
[madhyanāḍī = suṣumnā (central channel); bisasūtra = lotus fiber (extremely subtle); antarvyoma = inner space/void; devī illuminates deva — Śakti reveals Śiva]		 The Middle Channel is situated in the middle (between the two breaths) in (an extremely subtle) form like a lotus fibre. Having meditated on that as the Goddess who is the inner Void, God is illumined by Her.
BINDU AND LIGHT (VERSES 36-37)
kararuddhadṛgastreṇa bhrūbhedād dvārarodhanāt |
dṛṣṭe bindau kramāl līne tanmadhye paramā sthitiḥ ||36||

De-sandhi:
kara-ruddha-dṛk-astreṇa bhrū-bhedāt dvāra-rodhanāt |
dṛṣṭe bindau kramāt līne tat-madhye paramā sthitiḥ ||		 kara-ruddha = blocked by the hands (compound)
dṛk-astreṇa = by the weapon of sight (inst.)
bhrū-bhedāt = from piercing the eyebrows (abl.)
dvāra-rodhanāt = from closing the doors [of senses] (abl.)
dṛṣṭe = when perceived (loc. abs.)
bindau = the point [of light] (loc.)
kramāt = gradually (abl.)
līne = when dissolved (loc. abs.)
tat-madhye = in the middle of that (loc.)
paramā = supreme (nom. f.)
sthitiḥ = state (nom. f.)		 By the weapon of blocking perception with the hands (kararuddhadṛgastreṇa), from piercing [the center between] the eyebrows (bhrūbhedāt), from closing the doors [of the senses] (dvārarodhanāt) — when the point [of light] (bindau) is perceived (dṛṣṭe) and gradually (kramāt) dissolves (līne), in the middle of that (tanmadhye) [arises] the supreme state (paramā sthitiḥ). Thirteenth dhāraṇā: Closing senses, piercing bhrūmadhya, bindu dissolves
[kararuddha = hands blocking eyes/ears; dṛgastra = "eye-weapon" = perception; bhrūbheda = piercing between brows; dvāra = doors (sense organs); bindu = point of light seen in meditation]		 By closing the doors (of the senses) with the weapon, that is, blocking (their) perception with the hands, and by piercing (the center between) the eyebrows, when the Point (bindu) (of light) is perceived and gradually dissolves away, (the yogi attains) the supreme state in the center (of the Void of consciousness).
dhāmāntaḥkṣobhasambhūtasūkṣmāgnitilakākṛtim |
binduṃ śikhānte hṛdaye layānte dhyāyato layaḥ ||37||

De-sandhi:
dhāma-antaḥ-kṣobha-sambhūta-sūkṣma-agni-tilaka-ākṛtim |
bindum śikhā-ante hṛdaye laya-ante dhyāyataḥ layaḥ ||		 dhāma-antaḥ-kṣobha-sambhūta = arisen from inner agitation of the abode [of sight] (compound)
sūkṣma-agni-tilaka-ākṛtim = in the form of a subtle fire-mark (acc.)
bindum = the point [of light] (acc.)
śikhā-ante = at the end of the topknot (loc.)
hṛdaye = in the heart (loc.)
laya-ante = when dissolution ends (loc.)
dhyāyataḥ = of one meditating (gen. pres. part.)
layaḥ = dissolution (nom.)		 A point [of light] (bindum) in the form of a subtle fire-mark (sūkṣmāgnitilakākṛtim) arises from the inner agitation of the abode [of sight] (dhāmāntaḥkṣobhasambhūta). When it dissolves (layānte) while meditating (dhyāyataḥ) at the end of the topknot (śikhānte) and in the heart (hṛdaye), [there is complete] dissolution (layaḥ). Fourteenth dhāraṇā: Fire-tilaka from eye agitation, dissolving at śikhānta and heart
[dhāman = abode (eyes); kṣobha = agitation; tilaka = mark/line; śikhānta = end of topknot (dvādaśānta); laya = dissolution/absorption; double laya = when bindu dissolves, mind dissolves]		 A Point (of light) in the form of a subtle line (tilaka) of fire (between the eyebrows) arises by the inner agitation of (the eyeballs,) the abode (dhāman) (of sight). When it has dissolved away whilst meditating (on it) (within the upper End of the Twelve-finger Space) in the extremity of the topknot and within the heart, (the mind) dissolves away.
SOUND PRACTICES - ŚABDA (VERSES 38-42)
anāhate pātrakarṇe 'bhagnaśabde sariddrute |
śabdabrahmaṇi niṣṇātaḥ paraṃ brahmādhigacchati ||38||

De-sandhi:
anāhate pātra-karṇe abhagna-śabde sarit-drute |
śabda-brahmaṇi niṣṇātaḥ param brahma adhigacchati ||		 anāhate = in the unstruck [sound] (loc.)
pātra-karṇe = in the cavity of the ear (loc.)
abhagna-śabde = in the uninterrupted sound (loc.)
sarit-drute = of a fast-flowing river (loc.)
śabda-brahmaṇi = in the Brahman that is sound (loc.)
niṣṇātaḥ = deeply merged/immersed (nom. PPP ni-√snā)
param = supreme (acc.)
brahma = Brahman (acc.)
adhigacchati = attains (3rd sg. pres. adhi-√gam)		 He who is deeply merged (niṣṇātaḥ) in the Brahman that is sound (śabdabrahmaṇi) attains (adhigacchati) the supreme Brahman (param brahma). [It is] the unstruck sound (anāhate) [perceived] in the cavity of the ear (pātrakarṇe), the uninterrupted sound (abhagnaśabde) of a fast-flowing river (sariddrute). Fifteenth dhāraṇā: Anāhata nāda — unstruck sound like a river
[anāhata = "unstruck" = inner sound not produced by contact; pātrakarṇa = "vessel-ear" = ear cavity; sariddruta = swift river; śabdabrahman = sonic absolute; niṣṇāta = deeply bathed/immersed]		 He attains the supreme Brahman who is deeply merged into the Brahman that is sound (śabdabrahman). It is the Unstruck Sound (perceived) within the cavity of the ear (that is like) the uninterrupted sound of a fast-flowing river.
praṇavādisamuccārāt plutānte śūnyabhāvanāt |
śūnyayā parayā śaktyā śūnyatām eti bhairavi ||39||

De-sandhi:
praṇava-ādi-samuccārāt pluta-ante śūnya-bhāvanāt |
śūnyayā parayā śaktyā śūnyatām eti bhairavi ||		 praṇava-ādi-samuccārāt = from uttering praṇava (OṂ) etc. (abl.)
pluta-ante = at the end of the prolated [sound] (loc.)
śūnya-bhāvanāt = from meditating on the void (abl.)
śūnyayā = by the void (inst. f.)
parayā = supreme (inst. f.)
śaktyā = by the power (inst. f.)
śūnyatām = emptiness (acc.)
eti = attains (3rd sg. pres. √i)
bhairavi = O Bhairavī (voc.)		 O Bhairavī (bhairavi)! By uttering praṇava (OṂ) or the like (praṇavādisamuccārāt), and by meditating on the void (śūnyabhāvanāt) at the end of the prolated sound (plutānte), [the yogi] attains (eti) emptiness (śūnyatām) by means of the void (śūnyayā) which is the supreme power (parayā śaktyā). Sixteenth dhāraṇā: OṂ prolonged, void at the end
[praṇava = OṂ; pluta = prolonged (3 mātrās); śūnyabhāvana = contemplation of void; śūnyatā = emptiness/voidness; parā śakti = supreme power of Śiva]		 O Bhairavī, by uttering a praṇava or the like and by meditating on the Void at the end of (its) prolated sound, (the yogi) attains Emptiness by means of the Void which is (Śiva's) supreme power.
yasya kasyāpi varṇasya pūrvāntāv anubhāvayet |
śūnyayā śūnyabhūto 'sau śūnyākāraḥ pumān bhavet ||40||

De-sandhi:
yasya kasya api varṇasya pūrva-antau anubhāvayet |
śūnyayā śūnya-bhūtaḥ asau śūnya-ākāraḥ pumān bhavet ||		 yasya kasya api = of any whatever (gen.)
varṇasya = of a phoneme (gen.)
pūrva-antau = the beginning and end (acc. dual)
anubhāvayet = one should experience mindfully (3rd sg. caus. opt.)
śūnyayā = by the void (inst.)
śūnya-bhūtaḥ = having become void (nom.)
asau = that one (nom.)
śūnya-ākāraḥ = having the form of void (nom.)
pumān = person (nom.)
bhavet = becomes (3rd sg. opt.)		 One should mindfully experience (anubhāvayet) the beginning and end (pūrvāntau) of any (yasya kasyāpi) phoneme (varṇasya). Having become void (śūnyabhūtaḥ) by the void (śūnyayā), that (asau) person (pumān) becomes (bhavet) the form of void (śūnyākāraḥ). Seventeenth dhāraṇā: Beginning and end of any phoneme
[varṇa = phoneme/letter; pūrvānta = beginning-and-end (dvandva dual); anubhāvayet = causative "cause to experience" = experience mindfully; śūnyākāra = "void-formed"]		 One should experience (mindfully) the beginning and end of (the utterance of) any phoneme (varṇa). Having become the Void by (the power of) the Void (there), the person (free of identification with embodied subjectivity becomes) the form of the Void.
tantryādivādyaśabdeṣu dīrgheṣu kramasaṃsthiteḥ |
ananyacetāḥ pratyante paravyomavapur bhavet ||41||

De-sandhi:
tantrī-ādi-vādya-śabdeṣu dīrgheṣu krama-saṃsthiteḥ |
ananya-cetāḥ prati-ante para-vyoma-vapuḥ bhavet ||		 tantrī-ādi-vādya-śabdeṣu = in sounds of stringed and other instruments (loc. pl.)
dīrgheṣu = prolonged (loc. pl.)
krama-saṃsthiteḥ = situated in due order (gen.)
ananya-cetāḥ = with undivided attention (nom.)
prati-ante = at the end (loc.)
para-vyoma-vapuḥ = whose body is the supreme void (nom.)
bhavet = becomes (3rd sg. opt.)		 [He who listens] with undivided attention (ananyacetāḥ) to the prolonged (dīrgheṣu) sounds of stringed and other instruments (tantryādivādyaśabdeṣu), [the notes] situated in due order (kramasaṃsthiteḥ) — at the end (pratyante) [his] body becomes the supreme void (paravyomavapur bhavet). Eighteenth dhāraṇā: Listening to music with undivided attention
[tantrī = stringed instrument (vīṇā); vādya = musical instrument; krama = sequence (of notes/rāga); ananyacetas = "not-other-minded" = one-pointed; paravyomavapus = "supreme-space-body"]		 He who (listens) with undivided attention to the prolonged sounds of stringed and other instruments of (the notes played) in their due order (to make music), when (their resonance) comes to an end, (his) body is the Supreme Void.
piṇḍamantrasya sarvasya sthūlavarṇakrameṇa tu |
ardhendubindunādāntaḥśūnyoccārād bhavec chivaḥ ||42||

De-sandhi:
piṇḍa-mantrasya sarvasya sthūla-varṇa-krameṇa tu |
ardha-indu-bindu-nāda-anta-śūnya-uccārāt bhavet śivaḥ ||		 piṇḍa-mantrasya = of a syllabic mantra (gen.)
sarvasya = entire (gen.)
sthūla-varṇa-krameṇa = in accord with the sequence of gross letters (inst.)
tu = indeed
ardha-indu = half-moon (ardhacandra)
bindu = point
nāda = sound
anta = end
śūnya = void
uccārāt = from uttering (abl.)
bhavet = becomes (3rd sg. opt.)
śivaḥ = Śiva (nom.)		 By uttering (uccārāt) the void (śūnya) [at the end of] the half-moon, point, sound, and end (ardhendubindunādāntaḥ) of an entire (sarvasya) syllabic mantra (piṇḍamantrasya), in accord with the sequence of gross letters (sthūlavarṇakrameṇa), [one] becomes (bhavet) Śiva (śivaḥ). Nineteenth dhāraṇā: Mantra dissolution through subtle stages
[piṇḍamantra = "compact/seed mantra" (like OṂ); sthūlavarṇa = gross letters; ardhendubindunādānta = stages of mantra dissolution: half-moon → point → resonance → end → void; uccāra = utterance leading to silence]		 By uttering the Void (at the end) of the Half Moon (ardhacandra), the Point (bindu), Sound (nāda), and the End (of Sound) of an entire syllabic (piṇḍa) mantra in accord with the sequence of (its) gross letters, (the yogi) becomes Śiva.
VOID AND SPACE — ŚŪNYA / VYOMAN (VERSES 43-49)
nijadehe sarvadikkaṃ yugapad bhāvayed viyat |
nirvikalpamanās tasya viyat sarvam pravartate ||43||

De-sandhi:
nija-dehe sarva-dikkam yugapad bhāvayet viyat |
nirvikalpa-manāḥ tasya viyat sarvam pravartate ||		 nija-dehe = in one's own body (loc.)
sarva-dikkam = in all directions (acc. adv.)
yugapad = simultaneously
bhāvayet = one should meditate on (3rd sg. caus. opt. √bhū)
viyat = space/void (acc.)
nirvikalpa-manāḥ = whose mind is free of thought-constructs (nom.)
tasya = for him (gen.)
viyat = space/void (nom.)
sarvam = everything (nom.)
pravartate = manifests/becomes (3rd sg. pres. pra-√vṛt)		 One should meditate (bhāvayet) on space (viyat) simultaneously (yugapad) in all directions (sarvadikkam) within one's own body (nijadehe). For him (tasya) whose mind is free of thought-constructs (nirvikalpamanāḥ), everything (sarvam) becomes (pravartate) void (viyat). Twentieth dhāraṇā: Simultaneous void in all directions within the body
[nija = one's own; dikkam = directions (acc. adv.); yugapad = simultaneously; viyat = sky/space/void; nirvikalpamanas = "thought-free-minded"; sarvam pravartate = "everything manifests [as]"]		 (The yogi) should meditate on the Void within his own body in all directions (at once) simultaneously. (When his) mind (has thus become) free of thought, everything becomes void for him.
pṛṣṭaśūnyaṃ mūlaśūnyaṃ yugapad bhāvayec ca yaḥ |
śarīranirapekṣiṇyā śaktyā śūnyamanā bhavet ||44||

De-sandhi:
pṛṣṭha-śūnyam mūla-śūnyam yugapad bhāvayet ca yaḥ |
śarīra-nirapekṣiṇyā śaktyā śūnya-manāḥ bhavet ||		 pṛṣṭha-śūnyam = the void above/behind (acc.)
mūla-śūnyam = the void at the base (acc.)
yugapad = simultaneously
bhāvayet = should contemplate (3rd sg. caus. opt.)
ca = and
yaḥ = he who (nom.)
śarīra-nirapekṣiṇyā = by [the power] independent of the body (inst. f.)
śaktyā = by the power (inst. f.)
śūnya-manāḥ = void-minded (nom.)
bhavet = becomes (3rd sg. opt.)		 He who (yaḥ) simultaneously (yugapad) contemplates (bhāvayet) the void above (pṛṣṭhaśūnyam) and the void at the base (mūlaśūnyam) — by the power (śaktyā) [of consciousness] independent of the body (śarīranirapekṣiṇyā), becomes void-minded (śūnyamanā bhavet). Twenty-first dhāraṇā: Void above and void below, simultaneously
[pṛṣṭha = back/upper part; mūla = root/base; nirapekṣiṇī = independent (f. adj.); śūnyamanas = "empty-minded" = mind absorbed in void; śakti here = power of consciousness transcending the body]		 He who simultaneously contemplates the void above and the void in the root (foundation below) is, by the power (of consciousness) which is independent of the body, devoid of mind.
pṛṣṭaśūnyaṃ mūlaśūnyaṃ hṛcchūnyam bhāvayet sthiram |
yugapan nirvikalpatvān nirvikalpodayas tataḥ ||45||

De-sandhi:
pṛṣṭha-śūnyam mūla-śūnyam hṛt-śūnyam bhāvayet sthiram |
yugapat nirvikalpatvāt nirvikalpa-udayaḥ tataḥ ||		 pṛṣṭha-śūnyam = the void above (acc.)
mūla-śūnyam = the void at the base (acc.)
hṛt-śūnyam = the void in the heart (acc.)
bhāvayet = should contemplate (3rd sg. caus. opt.)
sthiram = firmly (adv.)
yugapat = simultaneously
nirvikalpatvāt = from being free of thought-constructs (abl.)
nirvikalpa-udayaḥ = the arising of the thought-free state (nom.)
