tataḥ = then / from that		 One should firmly (sthiram) contemplate (bhāvayet) the void above (pṛṣṭhaśūnyam), the void at the base (mūlaśūnyam), and the void in the heart (hṛcchūnyam) simultaneously (yugapat). From [this] freedom from thought-constructs (nirvikalpatvāt), then (tataḥ) the arising of the thought-free state (nirvikalpodayaḥ) [dawns]. Twenty-second dhāraṇā: Triple void — above, below, and heart — simultaneously
[hṛcchūnya = hṛd + śūnya (heart-void); sthira = firm/steady; nirvikalpatva = state of being free from vikalpa; nirvikalpodaya = "arising of the thought-free" = dawn of pure awareness; progressive expansion from v.44: two → three voids]		 One should meditate firmly simultaneously on the void above, the void in the root (foundation below) and the void in the heart, thus being free of thought, (the liberated state) free of thought dawns.
tanūdeśe śūnyataiva kṣaṇamātraṃ vibhāvayet |
nirvikalpaṃ nirvikalpo nirvikalpasvarūpabhāk ||46||

De-sandhi:
tanū-deśe śūnyatā eva kṣaṇa-mātram vibhāvayet |
nirvikalpam nirvikalpaḥ nirvikalpa-svarūpa-bhāk ||		 tanū-deśe = in [any] place in the body (loc.)
śūnyatā = emptiness/voidness (nom.)
eva = only/just
kṣaṇa-mātram = for a mere instant (acc.)
vibhāvayet = should perceive/reflect (3rd sg. caus. opt. vi-√bhū)
nirvikalpam = free of thought-constructs (acc./adv.)
nirvikalpaḥ = [one who is] free of thought-constructs (nom.)
nirvikalpa-svarūpa-bhāk = partaking of the nature of the thought-free state (nom.)		 One should perceive (vibhāvayet), even for a mere instant (kṣaṇamātram), that in [any] place in the body (tanūdeśe) there is only emptiness (śūnyataiva). [Perceiving this] free of thought-constructs (nirvikalpam), [one becomes] free of thought-constructs (nirvikalpaḥ), partaking of the nature of the thought-free state (nirvikalpasvarūpabhāk). Twenty-third dhāraṇā: Momentary perception of emptiness in any body-part
[tanū = body; deśa = place/region; śūnyatā = voidness; kṣaṇamātra = "moment-only" = even a single instant; vibhāvayet = caus. "perceive clearly"; triple nirvikalpa = emphatic threefold assertion of the thought-free state]		 If one reflects (and perceives) in a manner free of thought (even) for just a moment that within (any) place in the body there is only emptiness, the one who is (thus) free of thought possesses the nature of (that reality) free of thought.
sarvaṃ dehagataṃ dravyaṃ viyadvyāptaṃ mṛgekṣaṇe |
vibhāvayet tatas tasya bhāvanā sā sthirā bhavet ||47||

De-sandhi:
sarvam deha-gatam dravyam viyat-vyāptam mṛga-īkṣaṇe |
vibhāvayet tataḥ tasya bhāvanā sā sthirā bhavet ||		 sarvam = all (acc.)
deha-gatam = present in the body (acc.)
dravyam = substance/matter (acc.)
viyat-vyāptam = pervaded by space (acc.)
mṛga-īkṣaṇe = O gazelle-eyed one (voc. f.)
vibhāvayet = should perceive (3rd sg. caus. opt.)
tataḥ = then
tasya = of him (gen.)
bhāvanā = contemplation (nom. f.)
sā = that (nom. f.)
sthirā = firm/stable (nom. f.)
bhavet = becomes (3rd sg. opt.)		 O gazelle-eyed one (mṛgekṣaṇe)! One should perceive (vibhāvayet) that all (sarvam) substance (dravyam) present in the body (dehagataṃ) is pervaded by space (viyadvyāptam). Then (tataḥ) that (sā) contemplation (bhāvanā) of his (tasya) becomes (bhavet) firm (sthirā). Twenty-fourth dhāraṇā: All bodily substance pervaded by space
[dehagata = "gone-into-body" = present in the body; dravya = substance/matter; viyadvyāpta = "space-pervaded"; mṛgekṣaṇa = "deer-eyed" = beautiful-eyed (epithet of Devī); sthirā bhāvanā = firm contemplation — an easier approach for those unable to do the prior practices]		 O gazelle-eyed one! If (one is unable to do this), one should reflect that all the substance present in the body is pervaded by the Void; then one's contemplation (of the Void) will be firm.
dehāntare tvagvibhāgam bhittibhūtaṃ vicintayet |
na kiṃcid antare tasya dhyāyann adhyeyabhāg bhavet ||48||

De-sandhi:
deha-antare tvak-vibhāgam bhitti-bhūtam vicintayet |
na kiñcit antare tasya dhyāyan adhyeya-bhāk bhavet ||		 deha-antare = within the body (loc.)
tvak-vibhāgam = the skin portion (acc.)
bhitti-bhūtam = being [merely] a wall (acc.)
vicintayet = should contemplate (3rd sg. caus. opt. vi-√cint)
na kiñcit = nothing at all
antare = within (loc.)
tasya = of it / his (gen.)
dhyāyan = meditating (pres. part. nom. √dhyai)
adhyeya-bhāk = partaking of that which transcends meditation (nom.)		 One should contemplate (vicintayet) that the skin portion (tvagvibhāgam) within the body (dehāntare) is [merely] a wall (bhittibhūtam), [within which there is] nothing at all (na kiñcit) inside (antare). Meditating (dhyāyan) thus, one becomes (bhavet) a partaker of that which transcends meditation (adhyeyabhāk). Twenty-fifth dhāraṇā: Skin as wall, nothing inside
[tvak = skin; vibhāga = part/section; bhitti = wall; adhyeya = "to be meditated upon" — paradox: meditating on emptiness, one attains that which is beyond the grasp of meditation; -bhāk = partaking of (√bhaj)]		 One should think that the skin part of the body is (only an insentient) wall within which there is nothing at all. Meditating in this way, one participates in that which cannot be an object of meditation.
hṛdyākāśe nilīnākṣaḥ padmasampuṭamadhyagaḥ |
ananyacetāḥ subhage paraṃ saubhāgyam āpnuyāt ||49||

De-sandhi:
hṛdi-ākāśe nilīna-akṣaḥ padma-sampuṭa-madhya-gaḥ |
ananya-cetāḥ su-bhage param saubhāgyam āpnuyāt ||		 hṛdi-ākāśe = in the space of the heart (loc.)
nilīna-akṣaḥ = whose senses have dissolved/merged (nom.)
padma-sampuṭa-madhya-gaḥ = abiding in the centre of the lotus-cup (nom.)
ananya-cetāḥ = with undivided mind (nom.)
su-bhage = O blessed one (voc. f.)
param = supreme (acc.)
saubhāgyam = auspiciousness / good fortune (acc.)
āpnuyāt = attains (3rd sg. opt. √āp)		 O blessed one (subhage)! [The yogī] whose senses have merged (nilīnākṣaḥ) into the space of the heart (hṛdyākāśe), abiding in the centre of the lotus-cup (padmasampuṭamadhyagaḥ), with undivided mind (ananyacetāḥ), attains (āpnuyāt) supreme (param) auspiciousness (saubhāgyam). Twenty-sixth dhāraṇā: Senses dissolved in heart-space, within the lotus
[hṛdyākāśa = hṛdaya + ākāśa (heart-space); nilīnākṣa = "dissolved-senses" (ni-√lī + akṣa); padmasampuṭa = "lotus-casket" = two halves of the heart-lotus; ananyacetas = "not-other-minded" = one-pointed; saubhāgya = supreme felicity]		 O Blessed One, (the yogi) whose senses have merged into the space within the heart and abides (there) with an undistracted mind in the center between the two halves of the lotus, attains supreme good fortune.
DVĀDAŚĀNTA — END OF TWELVE (VERSES 50-51)
sarvataḥ svaśarīrasya dvādaśānte manolayāt |
dṛḍhabuddher dṛḍhībhūtaṃ tattvalakṣyam pravartate ||50||

De-sandhi:
sarvataḥ sva-śarīrasya dvādaśa-ante manaḥ-layāt |
dṛḍha-buddheḥ dṛḍhī-bhūtam tattva-lakṣyam pravartate ||		 sarvataḥ = from all sides / in every respect
sva-śarīrasya = of one's own body (gen.)
dvādaśa-ante = at the end of twelve [finger-breadths] (loc.)
manaḥ-layāt = from dissolution of the mind (abl.)
dṛḍha-buddheḥ = of one whose intellect is firm (gen.)
dṛḍhī-bhūtam = having become firmly established (nom.)
tattva-lakṣyam = the goal which is [ultimate] reality (nom.)
pravartate = manifests (3rd sg. pres. pra-√vṛt)		 By dissolving the mind (manolayāt) at the end of twelve [finger-breadths] (dvādaśānte) of one's own body (svaśarīrasya) in every respect (sarvataḥ), for one whose intellect is firm (dṛḍhabuddheḥ), the firmly established (dṛḍhībhūtam) goal which is [ultimate] reality (tattvalakṣyam) manifests (pravartate). Twenty-seventh dhāraṇā: Mind dissolution at dvādaśānta of one's own body
[dvādaśānta = "end of twelve" = 12 finger-breadths above the crown; manolaya = mind-dissolution; dṛḍhabuddhi = firm-intellected; tattvalakṣya = "reality-goal" = the one reality (Śiva) as target; sarvataḥ = "in every respect" — at every dvādaśānta point of the body]		 By dissolving away the mind in the End of the Twelve of one's own body (everywhere in all its locations and) in all respects, the goal which is (Śiva Who is the one ultimate) reality manifests well established for (the adept whose) mind is (thus made) firm (and unwavering).
yathā tathā yatra tatra dvādaśānte manaḥ kṣipet |
pratikṣaṇaṃ kṣīṇavṛtter vailakṣaṇyaṃ dinair bhavet ||51||

De-sandhi:
yathā tathā yatra tatra dvādaśa-ante manaḥ kṣipet |
prati-kṣaṇam kṣīṇa-vṛtteḥ vailakṣaṇyam dinaiḥ bhavet ||		 yathā tathā = in whatever way
yatra tatra = wherever
dvādaśa-ante = at the end of twelve (loc.)
manaḥ = mind (acc.)
kṣipet = one should cast/throw (3rd sg. opt. √kṣip)
prati-kṣaṇam = every moment
kṣīṇa-vṛtteḥ = of one whose mental activity has ceased (gen.)
vailakṣaṇyam = extraordinary state / uniqueness (nom.)
dinaiḥ = within [a few] days (inst. pl.)
bhavet = arises (3rd sg. opt.)		 One should cast (kṣipet) the mind (manaḥ) into the end of twelve (dvādaśānte) in whatever way (yathā tathā) and wherever [one may be] (yatra tatra), every moment (pratikṣaṇam). For one whose mental activity has ceased (kṣīṇavṛtteḥ), an extraordinary state (vailakṣaṇyam) arises (bhavet) within [a few] days (dinaiḥ). Twenty-eighth dhāraṇā: Casting the mind into dvādaśānta constantly
[yathā tathā yatra tatra = "however, wherever" = freely, without restriction; kṣipet = "should throw/project"; kṣīṇavṛtti = "exhausted-activity" (cf. Yogasūtra cittavṛttinirodha); vailakṣaṇya = "distinctiveness" = extraordinary state; practical emphasis: any time, any place]		 One should cast one's mind into the End of the Twelve in any way and wherever (one may be) every moment and so for one (whose mental) activity (has) ceased (by this practice, he experiences) an extraordinary (state of consciousness) within (a few) days.
FIRE OF TIME — KĀLĀGNI (VERSES 52-53)
kālāgninā kālapadād utthitena svakam puram |
pluṣṭam vicintayed ante śāntābhāsas tadā bhavet ||52||

De-sandhi:
kāla-agninā kāla-padāt utthitena svakam puram |
pluṣṭam vicintayet ante śānta-ābhāsaḥ tadā bhavet ||		 kāla-agninā = by the fire of time (inst.)
kāla-padāt = from the foot/step of time (abl.)
utthitena = which has arisen (inst. PPP ud-√sthā)
svakam = one's own (acc.)
puram = body/city (acc.)
pluṣṭam = burned/scorched (acc. PPP √pluṣ)
vicintayet = should contemplate (3rd sg. caus. opt. vi-√cint)
ante = in the end
śānta-ābhāsaḥ = manifestation of peace (nom.)
tadā = then
bhavet = arises (3rd sg. opt.)		 One should contemplate (vicintayet) that one's own body (svakam puram) has been burned (pluṣṭam) by the fire of time (kālāgninā) which has arisen (utthitena) from the foot of time (kālapadāt). Then (tadā), in the end (ante), the manifestation of peace (śāntābhāsaḥ) arises (bhavet). Twenty-ninth dhāraṇā: Body burned by the fire of time
[kālāgni = "time-fire" = cosmic fire of dissolution; kālapada = "time-foot" = the left big toe (where the fire originates); utthita = "arisen"; pura = "city" = body; pluṣṭa = burned/scorched; śāntābhāsa = "peace-appearance" = manifestation of tranquility; visualization of total bodily dissolution]		 One should imagine that one's own the body has been burnt (to ashes) by the Fire of Time that has arisen from the (toe of the left) foot. Then, in the end, the peaceful state will manifest.
evam eva jagat sarvaṃ dagdhaṃ dhyātvā vikalpataḥ |
ananyacetasaḥ puṃsaḥ pumbhāvaḥ paramo bhavet ||53||

De-sandhi:
evam eva jagat sarvam dagdham dhyātvā vikalpataḥ |
ananya-cetasaḥ puṃsaḥ pum-bhāvaḥ paramaḥ bhavet ||		 evam eva = in this very way / just so
jagat = the world (acc.)
sarvam = entire (acc.)
dagdham = burned (acc. PPP √dah)
dhyātvā = having meditated (abs. √dhyai)
vikalpataḥ = through imagination (abl.)
ananya-cetasaḥ = of one with undivided mind (gen.)
puṃsaḥ = of a person (gen.)
pum-bhāvaḥ = the state of pure being (nom.)
paramaḥ = supreme (nom.)
bhavet = arises (3rd sg. opt.)		 In this very way (evam eva), having meditated (dhyātvā) through imagination (vikalpataḥ) that the entire (sarvam) world (jagat) is burned (dagdham), for a person (puṃsaḥ) of undivided mind (ananyacetasaḥ), the supreme (paramaḥ) state of pure being (pumbhāvaḥ) arises (bhavet). Thirtieth dhāraṇā: Imagining the whole world burned
[evam eva = "just so" = in the same manner as v.52; jagat = world/universe; dagdha = burned (√dah); vikalpataḥ = "from/through imagination" — vikalpa used positively as a tool; pumbhāva = "man-nature" = supreme masculine principle = Śiva's essential nature; extends v.52 from one's body to the entire world]		 Having meditating in this way by imagining that the entire universe has been burnt away, a person whose mind is undistracted possesses the soul's supreme state.
TATTVA AND ŚAKTI (VERSES 54-55)
svadehe jagato vāpi sūkṣmasūkṣmatarāṇi ca |
tattvāni yāni nilayaṃ dhyātvānte vyajyate parā ||54||

De-sandhi:
sva-dehe jagataḥ vā api sūkṣma-sūkṣmatarāṇi ca |
tattvāni yāni nilayam dhyātvā ante vyajyate parā ||		 sva-dehe = in one's own body (loc.)
jagataḥ = of the world (gen.)
vā api = or also
sūkṣma-sūkṣmatarāṇi = subtle and ever subtler (acc. pl.)
ca = and
tattvāni = principles/realities (acc. pl.)
yāni = which (acc. pl.)
nilayam = to [their] place of rest / dissolution (acc.)
dhyātvā = having meditated (abs.)
ante = at the end
vyajyate = is revealed (3rd sg. pass. vi-√añj)
parā = the supreme [śakti] (nom. f.)		 Having meditated (dhyātvā) on whichever (yāni) principles (tattvāni), subtle and ever subtler (sūkṣmasūkṣmatarāṇi), in one's own body (svadehe) or also (vāpi) of the world (jagataḥ), [tracing them] to [their] dissolution (nilayam), at the end (ante) the supreme [śakti] (parā) is revealed (vyajyate). Thirty-first dhāraṇā: Meditating on tattvas from subtle to subtler, to their dissolution
[sūkṣmasūkṣmatara = comparative "subtle-subtler" = progressively more refined; tattva = principle/reality (36 tattvas in Śaiva cosmology); nilaya = resting place / dissolution; vyajyate = passive vi-√añj "is manifested/revealed"; parā = the supreme Śakti; microcosm (svadehe) and macrocosm (jagataḥ) treated as equivalent]		 Having meditated on the merger of the subtle and (then progressively) most subtle principles within one's own body or the world (back into one another), in the end (the goddess) Parā is revealed.
pināṃ ca durbalāṃ śaktiṃ dhyātvā dvādaśagocare |
praviśya hṛdaye dhyāyan muktaḥ svātantryam āpnuyāt ||55||

De-sandhi:
pīnām ca durbalām śaktim dhyātvā dvādaśa-gocare |
praviśya hṛdaye dhyāyan muktaḥ svātantryam āpnuyāt ||		 pīnām = full/expanded (acc. f.)
ca = and
durbalām = weak/subtle (acc. f.)
śaktim = power/energy (acc. f.)
dhyātvā = having meditated on (abs.)
dvādaśa-gocare = in the domain of the twelve (loc.)
praviśya = having entered (abs. pra-√viś)
hṛdaye = in the heart (loc.)
dhyāyan = meditating (pres. part. nom.)
muktaḥ = liberated (nom.)
svātantryam = freedom / independence (acc.)
āpnuyāt = attains (3rd sg. opt. √āp)		 Having meditated (dhyātvā) on the power (śaktim) [that is both] full (pīnām) and subtle (durbalām) in the domain of the twelve (dvādaśagocare), having entered (praviśya) the heart (hṛdaye) and meditating (dhyāyan), the liberated one (muktaḥ) attains (āpnuyāt) freedom (svātantryam). Thirty-second dhāraṇā: Meditating on śakti — full and subtle — at dvādaśānta, then entering the heart
[pīnā = full/expanded; durbalā = weak/thin/subtle — śakti contemplated in both extremes; dvādaśagocara = "twelve-domain" = sphere of dvādaśānta; svātantrya = "self-dependence" = absolute freedom; two stages: first contemplate śakti at dvādaśānta, then enter heart]		 Having meditated on the energy (of the breath) thick (with its natural sound) as well as weak (and lengthy) in the region of the twelve (finger space, then) once entered the heart (when falling asleep) engaged (in this) meditation, one attains mastery over one's dreams.
COSMIC PATHS — ADHVAN (VERSES 56-57)
bhuvanādhvādirūpeṇa cintayet kramaśo 'khilam |
sthūlasūkṣmaparasthityā yāvad ante manolayaḥ ||56||

De-sandhi:
bhuvana-adhva-ādi-rūpeṇa cintayet kramaśaḥ akhilam |
sthūla-sūkṣma-para-sthityā yāvat ante manaḥ-layaḥ ||		 bhuvana-adhva-ādi-rūpeṇa = in the form of the path of worlds etc. (inst.)
cintayet = should contemplate (3rd sg. caus. opt. √cint)
kramaśaḥ = gradually / in sequence
akhilam = the whole / entirely
sthūla-sūkṣma-para-sthityā = through the stages of gross, subtle, and supreme (inst.)
yāvat = until
ante = at the end
manaḥ-layaḥ = dissolution of mind (nom.)		 One should contemplate (cintayet) the entirety (akhilam) gradually (kramaśaḥ) in the form of the path of worlds and so forth (bhuvanādhvādirūpeṇa), through the stages of gross, subtle, and supreme (sthūlasūkṣmaparasthityā), until (yāvat) at the end (ante) [there is] dissolution of the mind (manolayaḥ). Thirty-third dhāraṇā: Contemplating the cosmic paths from gross to supreme
[bhuvanādhvan = "world-path" = one of the six adhvans in Śaiva cosmology; kramaśaḥ = in sequence; sthūla-sūkṣma-para = three levels: gross, subtle, supreme (transcendent); manolaya = mind-dissolution; the six adhvans: kalā, tattva, bhuvana (objective) + varṇa, mantra, pada (subjective)]		 One should think successively of every in the form of the path (adhvan) of worlds (bhuvana) and the rest (of the paths), in their gross, subtle and supreme condition until, in the end, the mind dissolves away.
asya sarvasya viśvasya paryanteṣu samantataḥ |
adhvaprakriyayā tattvaṃ śaivaṃ dhyatvā mahodayaḥ ||57||

De-sandhi:
asya sarvasya viśvasya pari-anteṣu samantataḥ |
adhva-prakriyayā tattvam śaivam dhyātvā mahā-udayaḥ ||		 asya sarvasya viśvasya = of this entire universe (gen.)
pari-anteṣu = at the extremities / borders (loc. pl.)
samantataḥ = on all sides / everywhere
adhva-prakriyayā = through the method of the [cosmic] paths (inst.)
tattvam = reality / principle (acc.)
śaivam = pertaining to Śiva (acc.)
dhyātvā = having meditated on (abs.)
mahā-udayaḥ = the great arising / great dawn (nom.)		 Having meditated (dhyātvā) through the method of the [cosmic] paths (adhvaprakriyayā) on the Śaiva reality (tattvaṃ śaivam) at the extremities (paryanteṣu) of this entire universe (asya sarvasya viśvasya) on all sides (samantataḥ), [there is] the great arising (mahodayaḥ). Thirty-fourth dhāraṇā: Śiva-tattva at the edges of the universe through the adhvan method
[paryanta = boundary/extremity; samantataḥ = on all sides; adhvaprakriyā = "path-procedure" = the method of the six adhvans; śaivatattva = the reality of Śiva; mahodaya = "great-arising" = supreme awakening; extends v.56 — now focus is on the cosmic periphery where Śiva is found]		 Having meditated of the reality of all this universe, all around and up to the ultimate limits by the method of the Paths (adhvan), (one will experience) the great awakening!
DISSOLUTION PRACTICES — LAYA (VERSES 58-62)
viśvam etan mahādevi śūnyabhūtaṃ vicintayet |
tatraiva ca mano līnaṃ tatas tallayabhājanam ||58||

De-sandhi:
viśvam etat mahā-devi śūnya-bhūtam vicintayet |
tatra eva ca manaḥ līnam tataḥ tat-laya-bhājanam ||		 viśvam = the universe (acc.)
etat = this (acc.)
mahā-devi = O great goddess (voc.)
śūnya-bhūtam = as having become void (acc.)
vicintayet = should contemplate (3rd sg. caus. opt.)
tatra eva = right there
ca = and
manaḥ = mind (nom.)
līnam = dissolved (nom. PPP √lī)
tataḥ = then
tat-laya-bhājanam = a receptacle for that dissolution (nom.)		 O great goddess (mahādevi)! One should contemplate (vicintayet) this (etat) universe (viśvam) as having become void (śūnyabhūtam). And (ca) the mind (manaḥ) dissolved (līnam) right there (tatraiva), then (tataḥ) [becomes] a receptacle for that dissolution (tallayabhājanam). Thirty-fifth dhāraṇā: The universe as void, mind dissolved therein
[śūnyabhūta = "become-void"; līna = dissolved/absorbed; tallayabhājana = "that-dissolution-vessel" — the mind, dissolved in the void universe, becomes the very receptacle of absorption; mahādevī = vocative address to Pārvatī]		 O great Goddess! one should think that this universe is Void and that the mind is dissolved away just there itself. Thus one becomes worthy of merging into that (Emptiness).
ghaṭādibhājane dṛṣṭim bhittis tyaktvā vinikṣipet |
tallayaṃ tatkṣaṇād gatvā tallayāt tanmayo bhavet ||59||

De-sandhi:
ghaṭa-ādi-bhājane dṛṣṭim bhittīḥ tyaktvā vinikṣipet |
tat-layam tat-kṣaṇāt gatvā tat-layāt tat-mayaḥ bhavet ||		 ghaṭa-ādi-bhājane = in a pot or other vessel (loc.)
dṛṣṭim = the gaze (acc.)
bhittīḥ = the walls (acc. pl.)
tyaktvā = having abandoned (abs. √tyaj)
vinikṣipet = should cast (3rd sg. opt. vi-ni-√kṣip)
tat-layam = dissolution into that [space] (acc.)
tat-kṣaṇāt = from that very moment (abl.)
gatvā = having attained (abs. √gam)
tat-layāt = from that dissolution (abl.)
tat-mayaḥ = identified with that (nom.)
bhavet = becomes (3rd sg. opt.)		 One should cast (vinikṣipet) the gaze (dṛṣṭim) into a pot or other vessel (ghaṭādibhājane), abandoning (tyaktvā) the walls (bhittīḥ). Having attained (gatvā) dissolution into that [space] (tallayam) from that very moment (tatkṣaṇāt), from that dissolution (tallayāt) one becomes (bhavet) identified with that (tanmayaḥ). Thirty-sixth dhāraṇā: Gazing into the empty interior of a vessel, ignoring the walls
[ghaṭa = pot; bhājana = vessel/container; dṛṣṭi = gaze; bhitti = wall; vinikṣipet = "should cast into"; tallaya = "that-dissolution" = absorption into that [space]; tanmaya = "consisting of that" = total identification; fourfold tat-: tat-laya, tat-kṣaṇa, tat-laya, tan-maya — emphatic repetition]		 One should cast (one's) sight into (the empty space of), for example, a jar (or any other vessel), leaving aside the enclosing walls (containing it). Having dissolved away that very moment into it, one becomes of that nature by virtue of that merger.
nirvṛkṣagiribhittyādideśe dṛṣṭiṃ vinikṣipet |
vilīne mānase bhāve vṛttikṣīṇaḥ prajāyate ||60||

De-sandhi:
nir-vṛkṣa-giri-bhitti-ādi-deśe dṛṣṭim vinikṣipet |
vilīne mānase bhāve vṛtti-kṣīṇaḥ prajāyate ||		 nir-vṛkṣa-giri-bhitti-ādi-deśe = in a place devoid of trees, mountains, walls, etc. (loc.)
dṛṣṭim = the gaze (acc.)
vinikṣipet = should cast (3rd sg. opt.)
vilīne = dissolved (loc. PPP vi-√lī)
mānase = mental (loc.)
bhāve = state (loc.)
vṛtti-kṣīṇaḥ = one whose mental activities are exhausted (nom.)
prajāyate = arises / is born (3rd sg. pass. pra-√jan)		 One should cast (vinikṣipet) the gaze (dṛṣṭim) into a place devoid of trees, mountains, walls, and the like (nirvṛkṣagiribhittyādideśe). When the mental state (mānase bhāve) has dissolved (vilīne), one arises (prajāyate) with mental activities exhausted (vṛttikṣīṇaḥ). Thirty-seventh dhāraṇā: Gazing into featureless open space
[nirvṛkṣa = treeless; giri = mountain; bhitti = wall; deśa = place/region; vilīna = dissolved/vanished; mānasa bhāva = mental state; vṛttikṣīṇa = "activity-exhausted" = one whose mental fluctuations have ceased; open space provides nothing for the mind to grasp — cf. v.59 (enclosed space) vs v.60 (open space)]		 One should cast one's gaze on (an open stretch of) land devoid of trees, mountains, walls and the like. When the mind is (thus) absorbed (and dissolves away), one becomes free of the fluctuations (of thought).
ubhayor bhāvayor jñāne dhyātvā madhyaṃ samāśrayet |
yugapac ca dvayaṃ tyaktvā madhye tattvam prakāśate ||61||

De-sandhi:
ubhayoḥ bhāvayoḥ jñāne dhyātvā madhyam samāśrayet |
yugapat ca dvayam tyaktvā madhye tattvam prakāśate ||		 ubhayoḥ = of both (gen. dual)
bhāvayoḥ = of [two] states (gen. dual)
jñāne = in the knowledge (loc.)
dhyātvā = having meditated (abs.)
madhyam = the middle (acc.)
samāśrayet = should resort to (3rd sg. caus. opt. sam-ā-√śri)
yugapat = simultaneously
ca = and
dvayam = both / the pair (acc.)
tyaktvā = having abandoned (abs. √tyaj)
madhye = in the middle (loc.)
tattvam = reality / truth (nom.)
prakāśate = shines forth (3rd sg. pres. pra-√kāś)		 In the knowledge (jñāne) of both (ubhayoḥ) states (bhāvayoḥ), having meditated (dhyātvā), one should resort to (samāśrayet) the middle (madhyam). And (ca) having simultaneously (yugapat) abandoned (tyaktvā) both (dvayam), in the middle (madhye) reality (tattvam) shines forth (prakāśate). Thirty-eighth dhāraṇā: The middle between two states
[ubhayoḥ bhāvayoḥ = "of both states" — any pair of opposites (pleasure/pain, subject/object, etc.); madhya = middle/centre; samāśrayet = "should take refuge in"; dvaya = the pair/duality; tattvam prakāśate = "reality shines forth" — in the gap between two cognitions, pure awareness is revealed]		 When perceiving two entities (or states), having (perceived and) meditated (on them), rest in the center between them. Having abandoned the two together all at once, Reality shines in the center.
bhāve tyakte niruddhā cin naiva bhāvāntaraṃ vrajet |
tadā tanmadhyabhāvena vikasatyati bhāvanā ||62||

De-sandhi:
bhāve tyakte niruddhā cit na eva bhāva-antaram vrajet |
tadā tat-madhya-bhāvena vikasati ati-bhāvanā ||		 bhāve = when a thing/state (loc.)
tyakte = has been abandoned (loc. abs. PPP √tyaj)
niruddhā = checked/restrained (nom. f.)
cit = consciousness (nom. f.)
na eva = not at all
bhāva-antaram = another thing/state (acc.)
vrajet = should move on (3rd sg. opt. √vraj)
tadā = then
tat-madhya-bhāvena = by the state in the middle of that (inst.)
vikasati = blossoms forth (3rd sg. pres. vi-√kas)
ati-bhāvanā = transcendent meditation (nom. f.)		 When one thing (bhāve) has been abandoned (tyakte), consciousness (cit), checked (niruddhā), should not at all (naiva) move on (vrajet) to another thing (bhāvāntaram). Then (tadā), by the state in the middle of that (tanmadhyabhāvena), transcendent meditation (atibhāvanā) blossoms forth (vikasati). Thirty-ninth dhāraṇā: Between two cognitions — transcendent meditation
[tyakte = abandoned (loc. abs.); niruddhā = checked/restrained; bhāvāntara = "another state/object"; tanmadhyabhāva = "that-middle-state" = the gap between two cognitions; atibhāvanā = "trans-meditation" = meditation that transcends ordinary contemplation; vi-√kas = to blossom/expand]		 When one thing has been abandoned, and consciousness has been (firmly) checked, it should not move on to something else. Then (a higher) transcendental meditation (atibhāvanā) blossoms forth, by virtue of the state (free of thought) in the center (between them) (tanmadhyabhāvena).
CONSCIOUSNESS AND EQUALITY (VERSES 63-64)
sarvaṃ dehaṃ cinmayaṃ hi jagad vā paribhāvayet |
yugapan nirvikalpena manasā paramodayaḥ ||63||

De-sandhi:
sarvam deham cit-mayam hi jagat vā paribhāvayet |
yugapat nirvikalpena manasā parama-udayaḥ ||		 sarvam = all/entire (acc.)
deham = the body (acc.)
cit-mayam = consisting of consciousness (acc.)
hi = indeed
jagat = the world (acc.)
vā = or
paribhāvayet = should reflect on (3rd sg. caus. opt. pari-√bhū)
yugapat = all at once
nirvikalpena = free of thought-constructs (inst.)
manasā = by the mind (inst.)
parama-udayaḥ = the supreme awakening (nom.)		 One should reflect (paribhāvayet) on the entire (sarvam) body (deham) or (vā) the world (jagat) as consisting of nothing but consciousness (cinmayam), all at once (yugapat) by the mind (manasā) free of thought-constructs (nirvikalpena) — [this is] the supreme awakening (paramodayaḥ). Fortieth dhāraṇā: Body or world as pure consciousness
[cinmaya = "consciousness-made" = consisting entirely of cit; paribhāvayet = "should thoroughly reflect on"; nirvikalpena manasā = "by the thought-free mind"; paramodaya = "supreme-arising" = the great awakening; microcosm (deham) or macrocosm (jagat) — both equally cinmaya]		 One should reflect all at once on all the body or the universe (that is is) nothing but consciousness, by the mind (thus) free of thought - the supreme Awakening!
vāyudvayasya saṃghaṭṭād antar vā bahir antataḥ |
yogī samatvavijñānasamudgamanabhājanam ||64||

De-sandhi:
vāyu-dvayasya saṃghaṭṭāt antar vā bahiḥ antataḥ |
yogī samatva-vijñāna-samudgamana-bhājanam ||		 vāyu-dvayasya = of the two breaths (gen.)
saṃghaṭṭāt = from the coming together / collision (abl.)
antar = within
vā = or
bahiḥ = outside
antataḥ = finally / at the end
yogī = the yogī (nom.)
samatva-vijñāna = knowledge of equality (compound)
samudgamana = emergence / arising (compound)
bhājanam = vessel / receptacle (nom.)		 From the coming together (saṃghaṭṭāt) of the two breaths (vāyudvayasya), either within (antar) or outside (bahiḥ), finally (antataḥ), the yogī (yogī) [becomes] a receptacle (bhājanam) for the emergence (samudgamana) of the knowledge (vijñāna) of equality (samatva). Forty-first dhāraṇā: Union of the two breaths — prāṇa and apāna
[vāyudvaya = "breath-pair" = prāṇa + apāna; saṃghaṭṭa = collision/junction; antar = within (heart); bahiḥ = outside (dvādaśānta); samatvavijñāna = "equality-knowledge" = knowledge of the sameness of all things; samudgamana = "rising up/emergence"; bhājana = vessel/receptacle — the yogī becomes a container for awakening]		 By the coming together of the two breaths, either within (the heart) or outside (in the End of the Twelve), finally (when they come to a halt), the yogī (experiences the reality which) is the vessel of the emergence of the knowledge of the equality (of all things).
BLISS PRACTICES — ĀNANDA (VERSES 65-74)
sarvaṃ jagat svadehaṃ vā svānandabharitaṃ smaret |
yugapat svāmṛtenaiva parānandamayo bhavet ||65||

De-sandhi:
sarvam jagat sva-deham vā sva-ānanda-bharitam smaret |
yugapat sva-amṛtena eva para-ānanda-mayaḥ bhavet ||		 sarvam = the entire (acc.)
jagat = world (acc.)
sva-deham = one's own body (acc.)
vā = or
sva-ānanda-bharitam = filled with one's own bliss (acc.)
smaret = should recollect (3rd sg. opt. √smṛ)
yugapat = all at once
sva-amṛtena = by one's own nectar (inst.)
eva = alone
para-ānanda-mayaḥ = consisting of supreme bliss (nom.)
bhavet = becomes (3rd sg. opt.)		 One should recollect (smaret) that the entire world (sarvam jagat) or (vā) one's own body (svadeham) is filled with one's own bliss (svānandabharitam), all at once (yugapat). By the nectar of one's own [nature] (svāmṛtena) alone (eva), one becomes (bhavet) pervaded by supreme bliss (parānandamayaḥ). Forty-second dhāraṇā: World or body filled with one's own bliss
[svānandabharita = "own-bliss-filled"; smaret = "should recollect" — not create, but recognize innate bliss; svāmṛta = "own-nectar" = innate amṛta; parānandamaya = "supreme-bliss-consisting"; choice of world OR body — same principle as v.63]		 (The yogī) should recollect that the whole universe or his own body is filled all at once with his own (innate) bliss, (then) by the nectar of his own (innate nature) alone, he becomes supreme bliss.
kuhanena prayogeṇa sadya eva mṛgekṣaṇe |
samudeti mahānando yena tattvaṃ prakāśate ||66||

De-sandhi:
kuhanena prayogeṇa sadyaḥ eva mṛga-īkṣaṇe |
samudeti mahā-ānandaḥ yena tattvam prakāśate ||		 kuhanena = by the trick (inst.)
prayogeṇa = by the application/practice (inst.)
sadyaḥ = immediately / all of a sudden
eva = indeed
mṛga-īkṣaṇe = O gazelle-eyed one (voc.)
samudeti = arises (3rd sg. pres. sam-ud-√i)
mahā-ānandaḥ = great bliss (nom.)
yena = by which (inst.)
tattvam = reality (nom.)
prakāśate = shines forth (3rd sg. pres. pra-√kāś)		 O gazelle-eyed one (mṛgekṣaṇe)! By the application (prayogeṇa) of the trick (kuhanena), great bliss (mahānandaḥ) arises (samudeti) immediately (sadya eva), by which (yena) reality (tattvam) shines forth (prakāśate). Forty-third dhāraṇā: The trick (kuhana) — tickling — producing sudden bliss
[kuhana = trick/tickling (specifically: tickling under the armpits); prayoga = application/practice; sadyas = immediately; mahānanda = great bliss; the sudden, unexpected sensation disrupts mental constructs, revealing pure awareness]		 O gazelle-eyed (goddess), by applying the trick (of tickling under the armpits) great bliss arises all of a sudden (sadyas) due to which (the supreme) reality manifests.
sarvasrotonibandhena prāṇaśaktyordhvayā śanaiḥ |
pipīlasparśavelāyām prathate paramaṃ sukham ||67||

De-sandhi:
sarva-srotaḥ-nibandhena prāṇa-śaktyā ūrdhvayā śanaiḥ |
pipīla-sparśa-velāyām prathate paramam sukham ||		 sarva-srotaḥ-nibandhena = by checking all the currents (inst.)
prāṇa-śaktyā = by the energy of vital breath (inst.)
ūrdhvayā = upward (inst. f.)
śanaiḥ = slowly / gradually
pipīla-sparśa-velāyām = at the time of the ant-crawling sensation (loc.)
prathate = manifests / expands (3rd sg. pres. √prath)
paramam = supreme (nom.)
sukham = bliss (nom.)		 By checking all the currents [of the senses] (sarvasrotonibandhena), when the energy of the vital breath (prāṇaśaktyā) [rises] upward (ūrdhvayā) slowly (śanaiḥ), at the time of the sensation [like the crawling] of ants (pipīlasparśavelāyām), supreme (paramam) bliss (sukham) manifests (prathate). Forty-fourth dhāraṇā: Upward prāṇa-śakti, ant-crawling sensation
[sarvasrotas = "all-currents" = channels of sensory activity; nibandha = checking/blocking; prāṇaśakti = energy of vital breath; ūrdhva = upward; pipīlasparśa = "ant-touch" = tingling/crawling sensation (kuṇḍalinī symptom); velā = time/moment; √prath = to expand/manifest]		 When (the yogi) feels a tactile sensation (like the crawling) of ants due to the slow upward rise of the energy of vital breath brought about by checking all the currents (of the activity of the senses), he experiences supreme bliss.
vahner viṣasya madhye tu cittaṃ sukhamayaṃ kṣipet |
kevalaṃ vāyupūrṇaṃ vā smarānandena yujyate ||68||

De-sandhi:
vahneḥ viṣasya madhye tu cittam sukha-mayam kṣipet |
kevalam vāyu-pūrṇam vā smara-ānandena yujyate ||		 vahneḥ = of fire (gen.)
viṣasya = of poison (gen.)
madhye = in the middle (loc.)
tu = indeed
cittam = the mind (acc.)
sukha-mayam = made of bliss (acc.)
kṣipet = should place/cast (3rd sg. opt. √kṣip)
kevalam = only/solely
vāyu-pūrṇam = filled with breath (acc.)
vā = or
smara-ānandena = with the bliss of passion (inst.)
yujyate = is united (3rd sg. pass. √yuj)		 One should place (kṣipet) the mind (cittam) made of bliss (sukhamayam) in the middle (madhye) between the fire (vahneḥ) and the poison (viṣasya), or else (vā) filled only with breath (kevalaṃ vāyupūrṇam), [one] is united (yujyate) with the bliss of passion (smarānandena). Forty-fifth dhāraṇā: Mind between fire and poison, united with the bliss of passion
[vahni = fire (= initial arousal/desire); viṣa = poison (= appeasement/dissolution at the end); madhya = middle point between arising and cessation; sukhamaya = "bliss-made"; smarānanda = "passion-bliss" = the bliss of Kāma; sexual yoga: the erotic is transmuted into spiritual awareness]		 (When making love) one should place one's mind made of bliss in the middle (between the) 'Fire' (of the arousal of desire at the beginning) and the 'Poison' (of the pervasive consciousness of its appeasement at the end), or else filled only with the vital breath, (the mind) should be united with the bliss of passion (smarānanda) (which is the bliss of consciousness generated by the union of Śiva and Śakti).
śaktisaṃgamasaṃkṣubdhaśaktyāveśāvasānikam |
yat sukham brahmatattvasya tat sukhaṃ svākyam ucyate ||69||

De-sandhi:
śakti-saṃgama-saṃkṣubdha-śakti-āveśa-avasānikam |
yat sukham brahma-tattvasya tat sukham sva-ākyam ucyate ||		 śakti-saṃgama = union with a woman (śakti) (compound)
saṃkṣubdha = agitated/aroused (PPP sam-√kṣubh)
śakti-āveśa = penetration/possession by Śakti (compound)
avasānikam = culminating in (acc.)
yat = which (nom.)
sukham = bliss (nom.)
brahma-tattvasya = of the reality of Brahman (gen.)
tat = that (nom.)
sukham = bliss (nom.)
sva-ākyam = one's own / inherent (nom.)
ucyate = is said (3rd sg. pass. √vac)		 The bliss (sukham) which (yat) culminates (avasānikam) in the penetration by Śakti (śaktyāveśa) aroused (saṃkṣubdha) by union with a woman (śaktisaṃgama) — that (tat) bliss (sukham) of the reality of Brahman (brahmatattvasya) is said (ucyate) to be one's own [inherent bliss] (svākyam). Forty-sixth dhāraṇā: Bliss of sexual union as Brahman's own bliss
[śaktisaṃgama = "śakti-union" = sexual union (śakti = woman/power); saṃkṣubdha = aroused/agitated; śaktyāveśa = "śakti-penetration" = being possessed by the cosmic Śakti; avasānika = "that which comes at the end" = culminating; svākya = "one's own" = inherent; sexual bliss = Brahman's bliss = one's own nature]		 The bliss (sukha) that culminates (during orgasm) with penetration into Śakti aroused by union with a woman (śakti) is that of the Brahman which is said to be one's own (inherent) bliss.
lehanāmanthanākoṭaiḥ strīsukhasya bharāt smṛteḥ |
śaktyabhāve 'pi deveśi bhaved ānandasamplavaḥ ||70||

De-sandhi:
lehana-āmanthana-ākoṭaiḥ strī-sukhasya bharāt smṛteḥ |
śakti-abhāve api deva-īśi bhavet ānanda-samplavaḥ ||		 lehana = licking (compound element)
āmanthana = churning/stirring (compound element)
ākoṭaiḥ = by pressing/embracing (inst. pl.)
strī-sukhasya = of the pleasure with a woman (gen.)
bharāt = from the fullness/intensity (abl.)
smṛteḥ = from recollection (abl.)
śakti-abhāve = in the absence of a partner (loc.)
api = even
deva-īśi = O goddess of the gods (voc.)
bhavet = arises (3rd sg. opt.)
ānanda-samplavaḥ = a flood of bliss (nom.)		 O goddess of the gods (deveśi)! Even (api) in the absence of a [female] partner (śaktyabhāve), from the recollection (smṛteḥ) of the fullness (bharāt) of pleasure with a woman (strīsukhasya) through [the experiences of] licking, churning, and pressing (lehanāmanthanākoṭaiḥ), a flood of bliss (ānandasamplavaḥ) arises (bhavet). Forty-seventh dhāraṇā: Recollection of sexual pleasure producing bliss without a partner
[lehana = licking; āmanthana = churning; ākoṭa = pressing/embracing — types of erotic play; strīsukha = "woman-pleasure"; bharāt smṛteḥ = "from the intensity of recollection"; śaktyabhāva = "absence of śakti/woman"; ānandasamplava = "bliss-flood"; memory alone suffices — physical partner not required]		 O goddess, even in the absence of a woman (śakti) there is a flood of bliss by (merely) filling one's memory with the joy (of sex with a) woman had by licking, churning, squeezing and the like.
ānande mahati prāpte dṛṣṭe vā bāndhave cirāt |
ānandam udgataṃ dhyātvā tallayas tanmanā bhavet ||71||

De-sandhi:
ānande mahati prāpte dṛṣṭe vā bāndhave cirāt |
ānandam udgatam dhyātvā tat-layaḥ tat-manāḥ bhavet ||		 ānande mahati = when great bliss (loc.)
prāpte = has been attained (loc. abs.)
dṛṣṭe = upon seeing (loc. abs.)
vā = or
bāndhave = a relative/friend (loc.)
cirāt = after a long time (abl.)
ānandam = the bliss (acc.)
udgatam = arisen (acc. PPP ud-√gam)
dhyātvā = having meditated on (abs.)
tat-layaḥ = dissolved into that (nom.)
tat-manāḥ = whose mind is that (nom.)
bhavet = becomes (3rd sg. opt.)		 When great bliss (ānande mahati) has been attained (prāpte), or (vā) upon seeing (dṛṣṭe) a relative (bāndhave) after a long time (cirāt), having meditated on (dhyātvā) the bliss (ānandam) as it arises (udgatam), one becomes (bhavet) dissolved into that (tallayaḥ), [one's] mind becoming that [bliss] (tanmanāḥ). Forty-eighth dhāraṇā: Meditating on bliss as it arises — great joy or reunion
[ānande mahati prāpte = loc. abs. "when great bliss is obtained"; bāndhava = relative/friend; cirāt = "from long" = after a long time; udgata = arisen; tallaya = "that-dissolution"; tanmanas = "that-minded" = mind absorbed in that; any strong natural emotion can serve as entry point]		 When there is occasion for great bliss (such as love making), or when seeing a (dear) relative (or friend) after a long time, meditate on the bliss (as it) arises (just then); the mind of one that is that (bliss) dissolves into it.
jagdhipānakṛtollāsarasānandavijṛmbhaṇāt |
bhāvayed bharitāvasthāṃ mahānandas tato bhavet ||72||

De-sandhi:
jagdhi-pāna-kṛta-ullāsa-rasa-ānanda-vijṛmbhaṇāt |
bhāvayet bharita-avasthām mahā-ānandaḥ tataḥ bhavet ||		 jagdhi = eating (compound element)
pāna = drinking (compound element)
kṛta = produced by (compound element)
ullāsa = outpouring / exuberance (compound element)
rasa = savor / aesthetic delight (compound element)
ānanda = joy (compound element)
vijṛmbhaṇāt = from the unfolding / expansion (abl.)
bhāvayet = should contemplate (3rd sg. caus. opt.)
bharita-avasthām = the state of fullness / plenitude (acc.)
mahā-ānandaḥ = great bliss (nom.)
tataḥ = from that
bhavet = arises (3rd sg. opt.)		 From the unfolding (vijṛmbhaṇāt) of the joy (ānanda) that is the savor (rasa) of the exuberance (ullāsa) produced by (kṛta) eating (jagdhi) and drinking (pāna), one should contemplate (bhāvayet) the state of fullness (bharitāvasthām). From that (tataḥ), great bliss (mahānandaḥ) arises (bhavet). Forty-ninth dhāraṇā: Contemplating the state of fullness from eating and drinking
[jagdhi = eating (√ghas/√jakṣ); pāna = drinking; ullāsa = outpouring/exuberance; rasa = savor/aesthetic delight; vijṛmbhaṇa = unfolding/expansion (vi-√jṛmbh); bharitāvasthā = "filled-state" = spiritual plenitude; everyday sensory pleasure as gateway to mahānanda]		 (The yogi) should contemplate the state (of spiritual) plenitude (he experiences brought about) by the unfolding of the joy which is the savor (of the aesthetic delight) of the outpouring (of consciousness) brought about by eating and drinking. From that there is great bliss.
gītādiviṣayāsvādāsamasaukhyaikatātmanaḥ |
yoginas tanmayatvena manorūḍhes tadātmatā ||73||

De-sandhi:
gītā-ādi-viṣaya-āsvāda-asama-saukhya-ekatā-ātmanaḥ |
yoginaḥ tat-mayatvena manaḥ-rūḍheḥ tat-ātmatā ||		 gītā-ādi = music and the like (compound)
viṣaya = sense objects (compound)
āsvāda = savoring / tasting (compound)
asama = unparalleled / incomparable (compound)
saukhya = happiness / joy (compound)
ekatā = oneness (compound)
ātmanaḥ = of the self (gen.)
yoginaḥ = of the yogī (gen.)
tat-mayatvena = by identification with that (inst.)
manaḥ-rūḍheḥ = whose mind is fixed (gen.)
tat-ātmatā = identity with that [bliss] (nom.)		 For the yogī (yoginaḥ) whose self is one with the unparalleled happiness (asamasaukhyaikatātmanaḥ) of savoring (āsvāda) sense objects (viṣaya) like music and the like (gītādi), whose mind is fixed [on that] (manorūḍheḥ), by identification with it (tanmayatvena), [there arises] identity with that [bliss] (tadātmatā). Fiftieth dhāraṇā: Savoring music — identification with aesthetic bliss
[gītādi = "song etc." = music and other arts; viṣaya = sense object; āsvāda = savoring/tasting; asamasaukhya = "unparalleled happiness"; ekatā = oneness; tanmayatva = "that-consisting-ness" = complete identification; manorūḍha = "mind-mounted/fixed"; tadātmatā = "that-selfness" = becoming that; aesthetic absorption (rasa) as yoga]		 The yogi who is one with the unparalleled joy of savoring music and other such (beautiful) objects (of sense, his) mind fixed (and well concentrated on that) becomes of that same (blissful) nature by identifying with it (tanmayatvena).
yatra yatra manas tuṣṭir manas tatraiva dhārayet |
tatra tatra parānandasvarūpaṃ saṃpravartate ||74||

De-sandhi:
yatra yatra manaḥ tuṣṭiḥ manaḥ tatra eva dhārayet |
tatra tatra para-ānanda-svarūpam sampravartate ||		 yatra yatra = wherever
manaḥ = the mind (nom./gen.)
tuṣṭiḥ = satisfaction / contentment (nom.)
manaḥ = the mind (acc.)
tatra eva = right there
dhārayet = should fix / maintain (3rd sg. caus. opt. √dhṛ)
tatra tatra = in each such place
para-ānanda-svarūpam = the inherent nature of supreme bliss (nom.)
sampravartate = arises / manifests (3rd sg. pres. sam-pra-√vṛt)		 Wherever (yatra yatra) the mind (manaḥ) finds satisfaction (tuṣṭiḥ), one should fix (dhārayet) the mind (manaḥ) right there (tatraiva). In each such place (tatra tatra), the inherent nature (svarūpam) of supreme bliss (parānanda) arises (sampravartate). Fifty-first dhāraṇā: Fixing the mind wherever it finds satisfaction
[yatra yatra...tatra tatra = "wherever...there" — universal principle; tuṣṭi = satisfaction/contentment; dhārayet = "should hold/fix" (dhāraṇā in its root sense); parānandasvarūpa = "supreme-bliss-own-form" = the essential nature of supreme bliss; the most accessible dhāraṇā — any moment of natural contentment is the gateway]		 One should maintain one's attention wherever the mind finds satisfaction. It is there that the inherent nature of Supreme Bliss arises.
LIMINAL AWARENESS AND LIGHT (VERSES 75-76)
anāgatāyāṃ nidrāyām praṇaṣṭe bāhyagocare |
sāvasthā manasā gamyā parā devī prakāśate ||75||

De-sandhi:
anāgatāyām nidrāyām praṇaṣṭe bāhya-gocare |
sā avasthā manasā gamyā parā devī prakāśate ||		 anāgatāyām = not yet arrived (loc. f.)
nidrāyām = sleep (loc.)
praṇaṣṭe = has disappeared (loc. abs. PPP pra-√naś)
bāhya-gocare = the external field [of awareness] (loc.)
sā = that (nom. f.)
avasthā = state (nom. f.)
manasā = by the mind (inst.)
gamyā = should be grasped / accessible (nom. f. gerundive √gam)
parā = supreme (nom. f.)
devī = goddess (nom. f.)
prakāśate = manifests (3rd sg. pres. pra-√kāś)		 When sleep (nidrāyām) has not yet arrived (anāgatāyām) [but] the external field [of awareness] (bāhyagocare) has disappeared (praṇaṣṭe), that (sā) state (avasthā), graspable by the mind (manasā gamyā), is the goddess Parā (parā devī) [who] manifests (prakāśate). Fifty-second dhāraṇā: The liminal state between waking and sleep — Parā Devī
[anāgatā nidrā = "not-yet-come sleep" = at the threshold of sleep; bāhyagocara = "external-field" = domain of outer awareness; praṇaṣṭa = vanished; gamyā = gerundive "to be grasped/reached"; parā devī = the supreme goddess — the transitional state IS the goddess herself; highly relevant to svapna practices]		 The state that manifests which should be grasped by the (attentive) mind when, (as one falls asleep), the external field (of awareness) has disappeared (but) sleep has not yet come, is the goddess Parā.
tejasā sūryadīpāder ākāśe śabalīkṛte |
dṛṣṭir niveśyā tatraiva svātmarūpam prakāśate ||76||

De-sandhi:
tejasā sūrya-dīpa-ādeḥ ākāśe śabalī-kṛte |
dṛṣṭiḥ niveśyā tatra eva sva-ātma-rūpam prakāśate ||		 tejasā = by the radiance / light (inst.)
sūrya-dīpa-ādeḥ = of the sun, a lamp, etc. (gen.)
ākāśe = in space (loc.)
śabalī-kṛte = made variegated / dappled (loc. abs.)
dṛṣṭiḥ = the gaze (nom.)
niveśyā = should be directed / placed (nom. f. gerundive ni-√viś)
tatra eva = right there
sva-ātma-rūpam = one's own essential nature (nom.)
prakāśate = manifests (3rd sg. pres. pra-√kāś)		 When space (ākāśe) has been made variegated (śabalīkṛte) by the radiance (tejasā) of the sun, a lamp, or the like (sūryadīpādeḥ), the gaze (dṛṣṭiḥ) should be directed (niveśyā) right there (tatraiva). One's own essential nature (svātmarūpam) manifests (prakāśate). Fifty-third dhāraṇā: Gazing into light-dappled space
[tejas = radiance/light; sūrya = sun; dīpa = lamp; ādi = etc.; śabalīkṛta = "made variegated/dappled" = mottled with light patterns; dṛṣṭi = gaze; niveśyā = gerundive "should be placed/directed"; svātmarūpa = "own-self-form" = essential nature; natural light phenomena as meditation support]		 One should direct one's gaze into (an empty) space variegated by the light of the sun or a lamp etc. There itself one's own essential nature manifests.
MUDRĀ AND POSTURE — MUDRĀ / ĀSANA (VERSES 77-79)
karaṅkiṇyā krodhanayā bhairavyā lelihānayā |
khecaryā dṛṣṭikāle ca parāvāptiḥ prakāśate ||77||

De-sandhi:
karaṅkiṇyā krodhanayā bhairavyā lelihānayā |
khecaryā dṛṣṭi-kāle ca parā-āvāptiḥ prakāśate ||		 karaṅkiṇyā = by [the mudrā] Karaṅkiṇī (inst. f.)
krodhanayā = by [the mudrā] Krodhanā (inst. f.)
bhairavyā = by [the mudrā] Bhairavī (inst. f.)
lelihānayā = by [the mudrā] Lelihānā (inst. f.)
khecaryā = by [the mudrā] Khecarī (inst. f.)
dṛṣṭi-kāle = at the time of perception (loc.)
ca = and
parā-āvāptiḥ = the supreme pervasion / attainment (nom.)
prakāśate = manifests (3rd sg. pres. pra-√kāś)		 By [the mudrās] Karaṅkiṇī (karaṅkiṇyā), Krodhanā (krodhanayā), Bhairavī (bhairavyā), Lelihānā (lelihānayā), and Khecarī (khecaryā), at the time of perception (dṛṣṭikāle), the supreme pervasion (parāvāptiḥ) manifests (prakāśate). Fifty-fourth dhāraṇā: The five mudrās — at the moment of perception
[karaṅkiṇī = "skull-bearer" mudrā; krodhanā = "wrathful" mudrā; bhairavī = "Bhairava's" mudrā; lelihānā = "licking/flickering" mudrā; khecarī = "sky-moving" mudrā; dṛṣṭikāla = "perception-time" = the moment sensory awareness arises; parāvāpti = "supreme-attainment/pervasion"]		 By (practicing the mudrās) Karaṅkinī, Krodhanā, Bhairavī, Lelihānā and Khecarī, when perception takes place the supreme pervasion (of consciousness) is revealed.
mṛdvāsane sphijaikena hastapādau nirāśrayam |
nidhāya tatprasaṅgena parā pūrṇā matir bhavet ||78||

De-sandhi:
mṛdu-āsane sphik-ekena hasta-pādau nirāśrayam |
nidhāya tat-prasaṅgena parā pūrṇā matiḥ bhavet ||		 mṛdu-āsane = on a soft seat (loc.)
sphik-ekena = on one buttock (inst.)
hasta-pādau = hands and feet (acc. dual)
nirāśrayam = without support (acc.)
nidhāya = having placed / kept (abs. ni-√dhā)
tat-prasaṅgena = by maintaining that [posture] (inst.)
parā = supreme (nom. f.)
pūrṇā = full (nom. f.)
matiḥ = intuition / understanding (nom.)
bhavet = becomes (3rd sg. opt.)		 On a soft seat (mṛdvāsane), on one buttock (sphijaikena), having kept (nidhāya) hands and feet (hastapādau) without support (nirāśrayam) — by maintaining that [posture] (tatprasaṅgena), intuition (matiḥ) becomes (bhavet) supreme (parā) and full (pūrṇā). Fifty-fifth dhāraṇā: Sitting on one buttock, limbs unsupported
[mṛdvāsana = "soft-seat"; sphij = buttock; nirāśraya = "without support/refuge"; nidhāya = abs. "having placed"; tatprasaṅga = "that-connection" = by maintaining that posture; mati = intuition/understanding; physical instability creates mental alertness — precarious balance focuses the mind]		 (The yogi should sit) on a soft seat, on (just) one buttock, keeping (his) hands and feet without support. By maintaining (this posture, his) intuition (mati) becomes supreme and full.
upaviśyāsane samyag bāhū kṛtvārdhakuñcitau |
kakṣavyomni manaḥ kurvan śamam āyāti tallayāt ||79||

De-sandhi:
upaviśya āsane samyak bāhū kṛtvā ardha-kuñcitau |
kakṣa-vyomni manaḥ kurvan śamam āyāti tat-layāt ||		 upaviśya = having sat down (abs. upa-√viś)
āsane = on a seat (loc.)
samyak = properly
bāhū = the two arms (acc. dual)
kṛtvā = having made (abs.)
ardha-kuñcitau = half-bent (acc. dual)
kakṣa-vyomni = in the space of the armpits (loc.)
manaḥ = mind (acc.)
kurvan = fixing / placing (pres. part. nom. √kṛ)
śamam = peace (acc.)
āyāti = attains (3rd sg. pres. ā-√yā)
tat-layāt = from merging in that (abl.)		 Having sat down (upaviśya) on a seat (āsane) properly (samyak), having made (kṛtvā) the two arms (bāhū) half-bent (ardhakuñcitau), fixing (kurvan) the mind (manaḥ) in the space of the armpits (kakṣavyomni) — from merging in that (tallayāt), one attains (āyāti) peace (śamam). Fifty-sixth dhāraṇā: Fixing the mind in the armpit-space, arms half-bent
[upaviśya = abs. "having sat"; samyak = properly; bāhu = arm; ardhakuñcita = "half-bent"; kakṣavyoman = "armpit-space" = the hollow space under the arms; tallaya = "that-dissolution" = merging into that space; the subtle inner space of the body as meditation focus]		 Sat on a seat, half bending the arms as required, fixing the mind on the space under the armpits, by merging in that, (the yogi) becomes peaceful.
GAZE AND UTTERANCE — DṚṢṬI / UCCĀRA (VERSES 80-81)
sthūlarūpasya bhāvasya stabdhāṃ dṛṣṭiṃ nipātya ca |
acireṇa nirādhāraṃ manaḥ kṛtvā śivaṃ vrajet ||80||

De-sandhi:
sthūla-rūpasya bhāvasya stabdhām dṛṣṭim nipātya ca |
acireṇa nirādhāram manaḥ kṛtvā śivam vrajet ||		 sthūla-rūpasya = of gross form (gen.)
bhāvasya = of a thing / object (gen.)
stabdhām = fixed / unblinking (acc. f.)
dṛṣṭim = the gaze (acc.)
nipātya = having directed (abs. caus. ni-√pat)
ca = and
acireṇa = in a short time (inst.)
nirādhāram = without support (acc.)
manaḥ = mind (acc.)
kṛtvā = having made (abs.)
śivam = Śiva / tranquility (acc.)
vrajet = one attains (3rd sg. opt. √vraj)		 Having directed (nipātya) an unblinking (stabdhām) gaze (dṛṣṭim) on a thing (bhāvasya) of gross form (sthūlarūpasya), and (ca) having freed (kṛtvā) the mind (manaḥ) of [all] support (nirādhāram) in a short time (acireṇa), one attains (vrajet) Śiva (śivam). Fifty-seventh dhāraṇā: Fixed gaze on a gross object, then freeing the mind
[sthūlarūpa = "gross-form" = a tangible visible object; stabdha = fixed/unblinking; nipātya = caus. abs. "having directed/cast"; acireṇa = "not-long" = quickly; nirādhāra = "without support" = the mind released from its object; two stages: first fix the gaze, then release — the object is a springboard to the supportless]		 Fixing one's gaze without blinking on something with a gross (and beautiful) form, freeing the mind of (all) support, in a short time, one attains tranquility (Śiva).
madhyajihve sphāritāsye madhye nikṣipya cetanām |
hoccāraṃ manasā kurvaṃs tataḥ śānte pralīyate ||81||

De-sandhi:
madhya-jihve sphārita-āsye madhye nikṣipya cetanām |
ha-uccāram manasā kurvan tataḥ śānte pralīyate ||		 madhya-jihve = the tongue [raised] into the centre (loc. abs.)
sphārita-āsye = the mouth wide open (loc. abs.)
madhye = in the centre (loc.)
nikṣipya = having placed / cast (abs. ni-√kṣip)
cetanām = awareness / attention (acc.)
ha-uccāram = the utterance of 'Ha' (acc.)
manasā = mentally (inst.)
kurvan = performing (pres. part. nom. √kṛ)
tataḥ = then
śānte = in the Peaceful One (loc.)
pralīyate = dissolves (3rd sg. pres. pra-√lī)		 The tongue [raised] into the centre [of the palate] (madhyajihve), the mouth wide open (sphāritāsye), having placed (nikṣipya) the attention (cetanām) in the centre (madhye), performing (kurvan) the utterance of 'Ha' (hoccāram) mentally (manasā) — then (tataḥ) one dissolves (pralīyate) into the Peaceful One (śānte). Fifty-eighth dhāraṇā: Tongue raised, mouth open, mental utterance of Ha
[madhyajihva = tongue at the middle (palate/nasal cavity); sphāritāsya = "wide-open-mouth"; cetanā = awareness/attention; hoccāra = "utterance of Ha" = the sound of exhalation/visarga; śānta = the Peaceful One = Śiva; khecarī-like tongue position combined with Ha-mantra]		 The tongue (turned up) into the center (of the nasal cavity), the mouth wide open and the attention fixed on the center (there), uttering the letter H mentally, then (the yogi) dissolves into the Tranquil One.
BODY AND MOVEMENT (VERSES 82-83)
āsane śayane sthitvā nirādhāraṃ vibhāvayan |
svadehaṃ manasi kṣīṇe kṣaṇāt kṣīṇāśayo bhavet ||82||

De-sandhi:
āsane śayane sthitvā nirādhāram vibhāvayan |
sva-deham manasi kṣīṇe kṣaṇāt kṣīṇa-āśayaḥ bhavet ||		 āsane = on a seat (loc.)
śayane = on a bed (loc.)
sthitvā = having remained (abs. √sthā)
nirādhāram = without support (acc.)
vibhāvayan = meditating on (pres. part. nom. vi-√bhū caus.)
sva-deham = one's own body (acc.)
manasi = when the mind (loc.)
kṣīṇe = has ceased (loc. abs. PPP √kṣi)
kṣaṇāt = in an instant (abl.)
kṣīṇa-āśayaḥ = one whose dispositions are destroyed (nom.)
bhavet = becomes (3rd sg. opt.)		 Either sitting (āsane) or lying down (śayane), having remained (sthitvā) and meditating (vibhāvayan) on one's own body (svadeham) as being without support (nirādhāram) — when the mind (manasi) has ceased (kṣīṇe), in an instant (kṣaṇāt) one becomes (bhavet) free of [all binding] dispositions (kṣīṇāśayaḥ). Fifty-ninth dhāraṇā: Body without support — sitting or lying
[āsana = seat; śayana = bed; nirādhāra = "without support" = visualize body floating/unsupported; vibhāvayan = "clearly perceiving/meditating"; kṣīṇa = wasted/ceased; āśaya = dispositions/latent tendencies; triple kṣ- alliteration: kṣīṇe, kṣaṇāt, kṣīṇāśaya — poetic emphasis on dissolution]		 Either (sitting) on a seat or (lying) on a bed, meditating on his own body as being without support, when the mind ceases, within a moment (the yogi) is free of (all his binding) dispositions (āśaya).
calāsane sthitasyātha śanair vā dehacālanāt |
praśānte mānase bhāve devi divyaugham āpnuyāt ||83||

De-sandhi:
cala-āsane sthitasya atha śanaiḥ vā deha-cālanāt |
praśānte mānase bhāve devi divya-ogham āpnuyāt ||		 cala-āsane = on a moving seat / vehicle (loc.)
sthitasya = of one who is situated (gen.)
atha = then / or
śanaiḥ = slowly
vā = or
deha-cālanāt = from moving the body (abl.)
praśānte = becoming peaceful (loc. abs.)
mānase = mental (loc.)
bhāve = state (loc.)
devi = O goddess (voc.)
divya-ogham = the divine flood (acc.)
āpnuyāt = attains (3rd sg. opt. √āp)		 O goddess (devi)! For one seated (sthitasya) on a moving vehicle (calāsane), or (vā) from slowly (śanaiḥ) moving (cālanāt) the body (deha), when the mental state (mānase bhāve) becomes peaceful (praśānte), one attains (āpnuyāt) the divine flood [of consciousness] (divyaugham). Sixtieth dhāraṇā: Moving vehicle or gentle swaying — the divine flood
[calāsana = "moving-seat" = vehicle/swing/palanquin; sthitasya = gen. "of one situated"; dehacālana = "body-moving" = swaying/rocking; praśānta = pacified/peaceful; divyaugha = "divine-flood" = torrent of transcendent consciousness; rhythmic movement pacifies the mind]		 O Goddess, whether seated on a moving vehicle or by moving (one's own) body slowly, when the mind becomes peaceful, one then attains the divine flood (of consciousness).
SKY AND THE THREE STATES — ĀKĀŚA / AVASTHĀ (VERSES 84-86)
ākāśaṃ vimalam paśyan kṛtvā dṛṣṭiṃ nirantarām |
stabdhātmā tatkṣaṇād devi bhairavaṃ vapur āpnuyāt ||84||

De-sandhi:
ākāśam vimalam paśyan kṛtvā dṛṣṭim nirantarām |
stabdha-ātmā tat-kṣaṇāt devi bhairavam vapuḥ āpnuyāt ||		 ākāśam = space / sky (acc.)
vimalam = clear / pure (acc.)
paśyan = beholding (pres. part. nom. √paś)
kṛtvā = having made (abs.)
dṛṣṭim = the gaze (acc.)
nirantarām = uninterrupted / without break (acc. f.)
stabdha-ātmā = one whose self is immobile (nom.)
tat-kṣaṇāt = from that very moment (abl.)
devi = O goddess (voc.)
bhairavam = Bhairava's (acc.)
vapuḥ = nature / body (acc.)
āpnuyāt = attains (3rd sg. opt. √āp)		 Beholding (paśyan) the clear (vimalam) sky (ākāśam), having made (kṛtvā) the gaze (dṛṣṭim) uninterrupted (nirantarām), one whose self is immobile (stabdhātmā), O goddess (devi), from that very moment (tatkṣaṇāt), attains (āpnuyāt) the nature (vapuḥ) of Bhairava (bhairavam). Sixty-first dhāraṇā: Beholding the clear sky with unbroken gaze
[ākāśa = sky/space; vimala = clear/pure/stainless; nirantara = "without gap" = unbroken/continuous; stabdhātman = "immobile-self" = body, senses, and mind all still; tatkṣaṇāt = "from that very instant" = immediate realization; bhairavavapus = "Bhairava-body/nature"]		 Beholding the clear sky, having made (his) gaze (constant) without a break (or blinking), one (whose body, senses and mind) are immobile attains, O Goddess, that very instant, Bhairava's nature.
līnaṃ mūrdhni viyat sarvam bhairavatvena bhāvayet |
tat sarvam bhairavākāratejastattvaṃ samāviśet ||85||

De-sandhi:
līnam mūrdhni viyat sarvam bhairavatvena bhāvayet |
tat sarvam bhairava-ākāra-tejas-tattvam samāviśet ||		 līnam = merged / dissolved (acc.)
mūrdhni = in the head / crown (loc.)
viyat = sky / space (acc.)
sarvam = entire (acc.)
bhairavatvena = as Bhairava's state (inst.)
bhāvayet = should meditate (3rd sg. caus. opt.)
tat = that (nom.)
sarvam = all (nom.)
bhairava-ākāra = of Bhairava's form (compound)
tejas = radiant energy (compound)
tattvam = reality / principle (acc.)
samāviśet = should penetrate / be absorbed into (3rd sg. opt. sam-ā-√viś)		 One should meditate (bhāvayet) that the entire (sarvam) sky (viyat) is merged (līnam) within the head (mūrdhni) as Bhairava's state (bhairavatvena). All that (tat sarvam) should penetrate (samāviśet) the reality (tattvam) [which is] the radiant energy (tejas) of Bhairava's form (bhairavākāra). Sixty-second dhāraṇā: Entire sky merged in the head as Bhairava
[līna = dissolved/merged; mūrdhan = crown of head; viyat = sky/space; bhairavatvena = "as Bhairava-ness"; bhairavākāra = "Bhairava-form"; tejas = radiant energy/lustre; tattva = reality; samāviśet = "should enter/penetrate" — mutual absorption: yogi enters tattva AND tattva enters yogi]		 (The yogi) should imagine that the entire (expanse of the) sky is merged within (his) head as Bhairava's state. (Then he and) all that (exists) penetrates (and is absorbed) into the reality (which is) the radiant energy (tejas) of Bhairava's form.
kiñcij jñātaṃ dvaitadāyi bāhyālokas tamaḥ punaḥ |
viśvādi bhairavaṃ rūpaṃ jñātvānantaprakāśabhṛt ||86||

De-sandhi:
kiñcit jñātam dvaita-dāyi bāhya-ālokaḥ tamaḥ punaḥ |
viśva-ādi bhairavam rūpam jñātvā ananta-prakāśa-bhṛt ||		 kiñcit = something / a little (acc.)
jñātam = known (nom. PPP √jñā)
dvaita-dāyi = producing duality (nom.)
bāhya-ālokaḥ = external light (nom.)
tamaḥ = darkness (nom.)
punaḥ = again / moreover
viśva-ādi = waking and other [states] (acc.)
bhairavam = Bhairava's (acc.)
rūpam = form / nature (acc.)
jñātvā = having known (abs. √jñā)
ananta-prakāśa-bhṛt = bearer of infinite light (nom.)		 [Waking is that in which only] something (kiñcit) is known (jñātam) — [it] produces duality (dvaitadāyi). [Dream is] the light (ālokaḥ) of the external [world] (bāhya). [Deep sleep is] darkness (tamaḥ). Having known (jñātvā) waking and the other [states] (viśvādi) as Bhairava's form (bhairavaṃ rūpam), one becomes a bearer of infinite light (anantaprakāśabhṛt). Sixty-third dhāraṇā: Waking, dream, and deep sleep as Bhairava's form
[kiñcijjñāta = "something-known" = partial knowledge (= jāgrat/waking); dvaitadāyin = "duality-giving"; bāhyāloka = "external-light" = light of dream (illumines traces of outer world); tamas = darkness (= suṣupti/deep sleep); viśvādi = "waking etc." = the three states; anantaprakāśabhṛt = "infinite-light-bearer" — the three avasthās recognized as Bhairava → turīya]		 (The yogi) possesses infinite light once known Bhairava's nature as the waking and other states, namely, (waking in which only) something is known that generates duality, (dreaming in which) the light (shines that illumines the mental traces of) the outer (world) and (deep sleep which is) darkness.
DARKNESS MEDITATION (VERSES 87–88)
evam eva durniśāyāṃ kṛṣṇapakṣāgame ciram | taimiraṃ bhāvayan rūpaṃ bhairavaṃ rūpam eṣyati || 87 ||

De-sandhi:
evam eva durniśāyām kṛṣṇa-pakṣa-āgame ciram | taimiram bhāvayan rūpam bhairavam rūpam eṣyati ||		 evam eva = in the very same way (adv.)
durniśāyām = on a dark/stormy night (f. loc. sg.)
kṛṣṇa-pakṣa-āgame = at the onset of the dark lunar fortnight (m. loc. sg., tatpuruṣa)
ciram = for a long time (adv.)
taimiram = dark, of darkness (adj. acc. sg. n.)
bhāvayan = contemplating, meditating on (pres. act. ptc. nom. sg. m., causative √bhū)
rūpam = form (n. acc. sg.)
bhairavam = of Bhairava (adj. acc. sg. n.)
rūpam = form, nature (n. acc. sg.)
eṣyati = will attain, will reach (fut. 3 sg., √i)		 In exactly the same way, contemplating for a long time the dark (taimiram) form on a terrible night at the onset of the (kṛṣṇa-pakṣa) dark lunar fortnight, one will attain the (bhairavam rūpam) form of Bhairava. Dhāraṇā 64: Darkness of the moonless fortnight
[Parallel to verse 86: substitutes cosmic dark-fortnight night for the dark sky; same contemplative mechanism — form of darkness as the form of Bhairava]
[durniśā: lit. "evil night," commentators gloss as stormy or pitch-dark night]
[eṣyati: future tense, indicating the soteriological result of sustained practice]		 In the very same way, (the yogi) engaged in contemplating for a long time the (awesome) dark form (of the sky) on a dark (stormy) night when the dark lunar fortnight begins, attains Bhairava's nature. (87) (86)
evam eva nimīlyādau netre kṛṣṇābham agrataḥ | prasārya bhairavaṃ rūpaṃ bhāvayaṃs tanmayo bhavet || 88 ||

De-sandhi:
evam eva nimīlya ādau netre kṛṣṇa-ābham agrataḥ | prasārya bhairavam rūpam bhāvayan tat-mayaḥ bhavet ||		 evam eva = in the very same way (adv.)
nimīlya = having closed (ger., √mīl)
ādau = initially, first (adv.)
netre = the two eyes (n. acc. dual)
kṛṣṇa-ābham = dark-gleaming, the darkness (n. acc. sg.)
agrataḥ = in front, before (adv.)
prasārya = having extended, spreading out (ger., pra-√sṛ)
bhairavam = of Bhairava (adj. acc. sg. n.)
rūpam = form (n. acc. sg.)
bhāvayan = contemplating (pres. act. ptc. nom. sg. m.)
tat-mayaḥ = consisting of that, identified with that (adj. nom. sg. m.)
bhavet = one becomes (opt. 3 sg., √bhū)		 In exactly the same way, initially closing the eyes and perceiving the (kṛṣṇa-ābham) darkness in front, then extending it outward and contemplating that (bhairavam rūpam) form of Bhairava, one becomes absorbed in that (tanmayaḥ). Dhāraṇā 65: Closed-eye darkness extended outward
[Two-phase practice: (1) eyes closed — inner darkness perceived; (2) prasārya — darkness "spread out" into external space]
[Abhinavagupta's commentary: the darkness is not mere absence of light but the luminous void of Bhairava]
[tanmayaḥ bhavet: standard result formula — ontological identification with the object of contemplation]		 In the very same way, initially closing the eyes (and meditating on) the darkness in front (and then opening them) extending out, contemplating (bhāvayan) (that dark) form of Bhairava, one becomes of that same nature. (88) (87)
SENSORY BLOCKAGE (VERSE 89)
yasya kasyendriyasyāpi vyāghātāc ca nirodhataḥ | praviṣṭasyādvaye śūnye tatraivātmā prakāśate || 89 ||

De-sandhi:
yasya kasya indriyasya api vyāghātāt ca nirodhataḥ | praviṣṭasya advaye śūnye tatra eva ātmā prakāśate ||		 yasya kasya = of any whatever (gen. sg., relative indef.)
indriyasya = of a sense organ (n. gen. sg.)
api = also, even (part.)
vyāghātāt = by a blow, by obstruction from without (m. abl. sg.)
ca = and (conj.)
nirodhataḥ = by (voluntary) blocking, restraint (m. abl. sg.)
praviṣṭasya = of one who has entered (gen. sg. m., pf. ptc. pra-√viś)
advaye = in the non-dual (adj. loc. sg. n.)
śūnye = in the void (n. loc. sg.)
tatra eva = just there, in that very place (adv.)
ātmā = the Self (m. nom. sg.)
prakāśate = shines forth, manifests (pres. 3 sg., pra-√kāś)		 By the obstruction and blocking of any one of the sense organs — through a blow (from without) or through voluntary restraint — upon entering the (advaye śūnye) non-dual Void, the (ātmā) Self manifests right there. Dhāraṇā 66: Blocking any single sense organ
[vyāghāta: accidental blow; nirodha: deliberate restraint — both produce the same result]
[yasya kasya: the generality is significant — any sense, not a specific one, suffices]
[advaye śūnye: the void produced by sensory suspension is already the non-dual absolute]
[prakāśate: intransitive, the Self "self-luminates" — not an object perceived but the subject revealing itself]		 (By blocking the function) of any one of the senses by a blow (from an external object) and by blocking (it voluntarily by striking oneself), the Self (ātman) (of the yogi) who has (thereby) entered the non-dual Void manifests there. (89) (88)
PHONEMIC MEDITATION: THE LETTER A AND VISARGA (VERSES 90–91)
abindum avisargaṃ ca akāraṃ japato mahān | udeti devi sahasā jñānaughaḥ parameśvaraḥ || 90 ||

De-sandhi:
a-bindum a-visargam ca a-kāram japataḥ mahān | udeti devi sahasā jñāna-oghaḥ parama-īśvaraḥ ||		 a-bindum = without bindu (anusvāra, .M) (adj. acc. sg. m.)
a-visargam = without visarga (.H) (adj. acc. sg. m.)
ca = and (conj.)
a-kāram = the syllable/letter A (m. acc. sg.)
japataḥ = of one who is reciting/repeating (gen. sg. m., pres. ptc. √jap)
mahān = great (adj. nom. sg. m.)
udeti = arises, rises up (pres. 3 sg., ud-√i)
devi = O Goddess (voc. sg. f.)
sahasā = suddenly, all at once (adv.)
jñāna-oghaḥ = torrent/flood of knowledge (m. nom. sg., tatpuruṣa)
parama-īśvaraḥ = the Supreme Lord (m. nom. sg.)		 O (Devi) Goddess, for one repeating the syllable (akāram) A — without (a-bindum) bindu and without (a-visargam) visarga — the Supreme Lord (parama-īśvaraḥ), a great torrent of knowledge (jñānaughaḥ), arises suddenly and at once. Dhāraṇā 67: Japa of the bare vowel A
[A = the first vowel, associated with Śiva/prakāśa; stripped of its nasal (bindu) and aspirate (visarga) modifications it is pure undifferentiated sound]
[jñānaugha: characteristic Kashmir Śaiva image — knowledge not as epistemic act but as flooding self-revelation of consciousness]
[sahasā: the instantaneous character of the awakening is doctrinally significant — not gradual accumulation]
[parameśvara = jñānaugha: apposition — the Lord IS the torrent of gnosis]		 O goddess, by repeating the great letter 'A' without bindu (.M) and visarga (.H), the Supreme Lord, who is a torrent of knowledge, arises (spontaneously all) at once. (90)
varṇasya savisargasya visargāntaṃ citiṃ kuru | nirādhāreṇa cittena spṛśed brahma sanātanam || 91 ||

De-sandhi:
varṇasya sa-visargasya visarga-antam citim kuru | nirādhāreṇa cittena spṛśet brahma sanātanam ||		 varṇasya = of a phoneme/letter (m. gen. sg.)
sa-visargasya = that which has visarga (adj. gen. sg. m.)
visarga-antam = ending in / at the end of visarga (adj. acc. sg. m.)
citim = awareness, consciousness-power (f. acc. sg.)
kuru = fix, place, direct (impv. 2 sg., √kṛ)
nirādhāreṇa = free of (objective) support (adj. inst. sg. m.)
cittena = with the mind (n. inst. sg.)
spṛśet = one may touch, make contact with (opt. 3 sg., √spṛś)
brahma = Brahman, the Absolute (n. acc. sg.)
sanātanam = eternal, primordial (adj. acc. sg. n.)		 Fix the power of (citim) awareness at the end of the (visarga-antam) visarga of a phoneme bearing visarga, with a mind (nirādhāreṇa cittena) free of objective support; one may thereby touch the (brahma sanātanam) eternal Brahman. Dhāraṇā 68: Awareness at the terminal point of visarga
[Complement to v. 90: there the vowel A is stripped of visarga; here the visarga itself is the locus of meditation]
[visargānta: the aspirate release-point of visarga (.H) — phonetically the final breath-expulsion — taken as threshold between manifest sound and silence]
[citi vs. citta: citi = the consciousness-power (Śakti); citta = the empirical mind — the instruction is to direct the latter via the former]
[nirādhāreṇa: the mind must be unsupported by any object, i.e., turned toward its own ground]		 Fix (the power of your) awareness (citi), with the mind (citta) free of (objective) support, (within or) on the end of visarga (.H) of a phoneme with visarga (.H) and make contact with the eternal Brahman. (91) (90)
SKY-SELF MEDITATION (VERSE 92)
vyomākāraṃ svam ātmānaṃ dhyāyed digbhir anāvṛtam | nirāśrayā citiḥ śaktiḥ svarūpaṃ darśayet tadā || 92 ||

De-sandhi:
vyoma-ākāram svam ātmānam dhyāyet digbhiḥ anāvṛtam | nirāśrayā citiḥ śaktiḥ sva-rūpam darśayet tadā ||		 vyoma-ākāram = sky-formed, in the form of the sky/space (adj. acc. sg. m.)
svam = one's own (adj. acc. sg. m.)
ātmānam = Self (m. acc. sg.)
dhyāyet = one should meditate on (opt. 3 sg., √dhyai)
digbhiḥ = by the directions (f. inst. pl.)
anāvṛtam = unenclosed, unconfined (adj. acc. sg. m., a-ā-√vṛ)
nirāśrayā = free of support, unsupported (adj. nom. sg. f.)
citiḥ = consciousness-power (f. nom. sg.)
śaktiḥ = the Power, Śakti (f. nom. sg.)
sva-rūpam = own nature, own form (n. acc. sg.)
darśayet = reveals, makes visible (opt. caus. 3 sg., √dṛś)
tadā = then (adv.)		 One should meditate on one's own (svam ātmānam) Self as (vyomākāram) sky-formed, unconfined by the directions; then (citiḥ śaktiḥ) the power of consciousness, free of support (nirāśrayā), reveals her own nature (svarūpam). Dhāraṇā 69: Meditation on the Self as unbounded sky
[vyomākāra: the sky as image of consciousness — boundless, undivided, neither grasped nor rejecting]
[digbhiḥ anāvṛtam: the directions (the ten-directional space) do not enclose the ātman — its "location" is not spatial]
[nirāśrayā citiḥ śaktiḥ: three terms in apposition — the power of awareness has no external prop; self-subsisting]
[svarūpam darśayet: the Śakti "self-displays" — not the yogin who perceives her but she who reveals herself through his practice]		 One should meditate on one's own Self in the form of the sky, unconfined by the directions, then (the Goddess who is) the power of consciousness, free of (all) support, displays (her) own nature. (92) (91)
PAIN AS MEDITATIVE OBJECT (VERSE 93)
kiñcid aṅgaṃ vibhidyādau tīkṣṇasūcyādinā tataḥ | tatraiva cetanāṃ yuktvā bhairave nirmalā gatiḥ || 93 ||

De-sandhi:
kiñcit aṅgam vibhidya ādau tīkṣṇa-sūcyā-ādinā tataḥ | tatra eva cetanām yuktvā bhairave nirmalā gatiḥ ||		 kiñcit = some, a certain (pron. adj. acc. sg. n.)
aṅgam = limb, part of the body (n. acc. sg.)
vibhidya = having pierced (ger., vi-√bhid)
ādau = first, initially (adv.)
tīkṣṇa-sūcyā = with a sharp needle (f. inst. sg.)
ādinā = and so forth, or the like (inst. sg.)
tataḥ = then, thereafter (adv.)
tatra eva = just there (adv.)
cetanām = awareness, consciousness (f. acc. sg.)
yuktvā = having joined, having focused (ger., √yuj)
bhairave = in Bhairava (m. loc. sg.)
nirmalā = pure, stainless (adj. nom. sg. f.)
gatiḥ = movement, state, attainment (f. nom. sg.)		 Having first pierced some (kiñcit aṅgam) limb with a sharp needle (tīkṣṇa-sūcyā) or the like, and then focusing (cetanām yuktvā) awareness right there, one attains pure (nirmalā) movement into (bhairave) Bhairava. Dhāraṇā 70: Focused attention on the site of pain
[Extreme instance of the general principle at v. 89: forcible disruption of ordinary sensory flow creates a gap in which pure awareness is accessible]
[cetanām yuktvā: the piercing is merely instrumental; the practice is the turning of awareness, not the injury]
[bhairave nirmalā gatiḥ: gati as "way of going" into Bhairava — the pain site becomes a threshold]
[ādinā: commentators mention hot coal, caustic substances — any sharp sudden sensation serves]		 Having first pierced some limb (of the body) with a sharp needle or the like and then focused attention just there (on the painful sensation, one attains) the pure state within Bhairava. (93) (92)
MENTAL NEGATION AND TATTVA DISCERNMENT (VERSES 94–95)
cittādyantaḥkṛtir nāsti mamāntar bhāvayed iti | vikalpānām abhāvena vikalpair ujjhito bhavet || 94 ||

De-sandhi:
citta-ādi-antaḥ-kṛtiḥ na asti mama antaḥ bhāvayet iti | vikalpānām abhāvena vikalpaiḥ ujjhitaḥ bhavet ||		 citta-ādi = mind (citta) and the rest (antaḥkaraṇa, ahaṃkāra, buddhi) (comp.)
antaḥ-kṛtiḥ = inner faculty/organ (f. nom. sg.)
na asti = does not exist (neg. pres. 3 sg., √as)
mama = within me, of me (gen./loc. sg.)
antaḥ = within (adv./prefix)
bhāvayet = one should reflect, meditate (opt. caus. 3 sg., √bhū)
iti = thus (quot. part.)
vikalpānām = of conceptual constructs/thoughts (m. gen. pl.)
abhāvena = by the non-existence, by absence (m. inst. sg.)
vikalpaiḥ = by/from thoughts (m. inst. pl.)
ujjhitaḥ = abandoned, freed from (adj. nom. sg. m., √ujjh)
bhavet = one becomes (opt. 3 sg., √bhū)		 One should reflect: "there is no inner organ (antaḥkṛtiḥ) — mind and the rest (cittādi) — within me." Through the very absence of (vikalpānām abhāvena) conceptual constructs, one becomes freed from (vikalpaiḥ ujjhitaḥ) those constructs. Dhāraṇā 71: Negation of the inner organ
[cittādi: the fourfold antaḥkaraṇa (manas, buddhi, ahaṃkāra, citta) of Sāṃkhya — their denial is denial of the empirical subject-apparatus]
[Self-undermining structure: the vikalpa "there are no vikalpas" dissolves itself in the act of its own performance]
[vikalpānām abhāvena: instrumental of means — the absence of thoughts IS the instrument of liberation from thoughts]
[bhāvayet iti: the quotation marks (iti) signal that this is a prescribed internal speech-act, not a metaphysical assertion]		 (The yogi) should reflect (bhāvayet) that "there is no inner (mental) organ within me, whether mind (intellect or ego)." (Thus) because there are no thoughts (also), (the yogi) is devoid of them. (94) (93)
māyā vimohinī nāma kalāyāḥ kalanaṃ sthitam | ityādidharmaṃ tattvānāṃ kalayan na pṛthag bhavet || 95 ||

De-sandhi:
māyā vimohinī nāma kalāyāḥ kalanam sthitam | iti-ādi-dharmam tattvānām kalayan na pṛthak bhavet ||		 māyā = Māyā, the power of illusion (f. nom. sg.)
vimohinī = deluding, bewildering (adj. nom. sg. f.)
nāma = by name, indeed (adv.)
kalāyāḥ = of Kalā (the tattva of limited agency) (f. gen. sg.)
kalanam = operation, functioning (n. nom. sg.)
sthitam = is established, goes on (adj./ppp nom. sg. n.)
iti-ādi-dharmam = this and other properties (acc. sg. m., dvandva comp.)
tattvānām = of the reality-principles/tattvas (n. gen. pl.)
kalayan = discerning, reckoning (pres. ptc. nom. sg. m., √kal)
na = not (neg.)
pṛthak = separate, distinct (adv./adj.)
bhavet = one becomes (opt. 3 sg., √bhū)		 (Māyā) is indeed bewildering; the functioning (kalanam) of (kalāyāḥ) Kalā is established — discerning (kalayan) this and the other properties (ityādi-dharmam) of the (tattvānām) tattvas, one no longer becomes separate (na pṛthak). Dhāraṇā 72: Discernment of the properties of the tattvas
[kalā: the fifth kañcuka (limiting adjunct) in Kashmir Śaiva cosmology, source of the sense of limited authorship]
[kalayan: wordplay — the same root (√kal) names both the tattva kalā and the act of "discerning/calculating" its nature]
[na pṛthag bhavet: liberation framed as non-separation rather than union — the yogin never was separate; discernment removes the illusion of difference]
[ityādi: instruction to extend the analysis beyond māyā and kalā to all 36 tattvas]		 Māyā is deluding. The operation (kalana) of the Force (of limited agency) (kalā) (and the other kañcukas) goes on (functioning) – discerning (kalayan) this and the other properties of the reality principles (tattva) (with detachment) the individual soul (nā) is separated (from them). (95) (94)
OBSERVING DESIRE AND KNOWLEDGE AT THEIR ORIGIN (VERSES 96–98)
jhagitīcchāṃ samutpannām avalokya śamaṃ nayet | yata eva samudbhūtā tatas tatraiva līyate || 96 ||

De-sandhi:
jhagiti icchām samutpannām avalokya śamam nayet | yataḥ eva samudbhūtā tataḥ tatra eva līyate ||		 jhagiti = immediately, in a flash (onomatopoetic adv.)
icchām = desire, will (f. acc. sg.)
samutpannām = that has arisen, that has just come forth (adj. acc. sg. f., ppp sam-ud-√pad)
avalokya = having observed, having seen (ger., ava-√lok)
śamam = to rest, to quietude (m. acc. sg.)
nayet = one should lead, bring (opt. 3 sg., √nī)
yataḥ eva = from just that (place/source) (rel. abl.)
samudbhūtā = it has arisen, come forth (ppp nom. sg. f.)
tataḥ = from there, thence (abl. adv.)
tatra eva = right there (adv.)
līyate = it dissolves, merges (pres. pass. 3 sg., √lī)		 Having observed a desire (icchām) that has arisen instantly (jhagiti), one should bring it to rest (śamam nayet); it dissolves (līyate) right there into the very source from which it arose. Dhāraṇā 73: Instantaneous arrest of nascent desire
[jhagiti: a Prakrit-inflected adverb conveying the suddenness and momentariness of desire's arising — the practice must be equally sudden]
[avalokya + nayet: two-step: (1) bare witnessing of desire without suppression; (2) return to source — not repression but reversal]
[yata eva ... tatra eva: source and dissolution-point are identical — desire returns to the consciousness-ground from which it differentiated]
[śamam nayet: "lead to peace" — the idiom of directing rather than destroying]		 Having observed that a desire (has just) arisen, it should be put to rest immediately. Thus, it dissolves away there from whence it has come forth. (96) (95)
yadā mamecchā notpannā jñānaṃ vā kas tadāsmi vai | tattvato 'haṃ tathābhūtas tallīnas tanmanā bhavet || 97 ||

De-sandhi:
yadā mama icchā na utpannā jñānam vā kaḥ tadā asmi vai | tattvataḥ aham tathā-bhūtaḥ tat-līnaḥ tat-manāḥ bhavet ||		 yadā = when (conj.)
mama = my (gen. sg.)
icchā = will, desire (f. nom. sg.)
na utpannā = has not arisen (neg. ppp nom. sg. f.)
jñānam = knowledge, cognition (n. nom. sg.)
vā = or (conj.)
kaḥ = who (pron. nom. sg. m.)
tadā = then (adv.)
asmi = I am (pres. 1 sg., √as)
vai = indeed, truly (emph. part.)
tattvataḥ = in truth, truly (adv., abl.)
aham = I (nom. sg.)
tathā-bhūtaḥ = being such, having become that (adj. nom. sg. m.)
tat-līnaḥ = merged/absorbed in that (adj. nom. sg. m.)
tat-manāḥ = of that mind, with that as one's mind (adj. nom. sg. m.)
bhavet = one becomes (opt. 3 sg., √bhū)		 Reflecting: "when my (mama) will (icchā) or knowledge (jñānam) have not arisen — who am I then? — truly, I am as I truly am (tattvataḥ aham)!" — being such, merged in that (tallīnaḥ), one becomes of that mind (tanmanāḥ). Dhāraṇā 74: Self-inquiry in the interval before desire or knowledge arises
