[Complement to v. 96: there desire is caught as it arises; here the interrogative turns to the interval before arising]
[kaḥ tadā asmi: the famous inquiry into the witness of the gap — in the absence of all mental content, what remains is pure "I am"]
[tattvataḥ: "in truth/in reality" — the answer to the question is not a proposition but a recognition of what already is]
[tathābhūtas tallīnas tanmanāḥ: triple compound forming a climactic progression — being such → merging → becoming of that mind]		 (Reflecting:) "when my will or knowledge have not arisen, who am I then? – Indeed, I am as I truly am!" being such (i.e. consciousness and bliss), merged in that, he becomes of that mind. (97)
icchāyām athavā jñāne jāte cittaṃ niveśayet | ātmabuddhyānanyacetās tatas tattvārthadarśanam || 98 ||

De-sandhi:
icchāyām athavā jñāne jāte cittam niveśayet | ātma-buddhyā ananya-cetāḥ tataḥ tattva-artha-darśanam ||		 icchāyām = in desire, at the moment of desire (f. loc. sg.)
athavā = or (conj.)
jñāne = in knowledge/perception (n. loc. sg.)
jāte = when it has (just) arisen (loc. abs., ppp √jan)
cittam = the mind, mental attention (n. acc. sg.)
niveśayet = one should place, direct (opt. caus. 3 sg., ni-√viś)
ātma-buddhyā = with the notion/understanding of (it as) the Self (f. inst. sg.)
ananya-cetāḥ = whose mind is not directed toward anything else (bahuvrihi adj. nom. sg. m.)
tataḥ = from this, then (adv.)
tattva-artha-darśanam = vision of the true meaning of reality (n. nom. sg., tatpuruṣa)		 At the moment when desire (icchāyām) or knowledge (jñāne) has just arisen, one whose mind is not directed elsewhere (ananya-cetāḥ) should place awareness there with the understanding (buddhyā) that this is the (ātma) Self; from this arises a vision of what truly is (tattvārthadarśanam). Dhāraṇā 75: Attending to the nascent moment of desire or knowledge as Self
[Triad vv. 96–98: three approaches to the same threshold — (96) desire quelled at source; (97) gap before arising interrogated; (98) arising moment itself inhabited as Self]
[jāte: locative absolute — the "just arisen" state before the object has been fixed; the brief pre-intentional moment]
[ātmabuddhyā: the instrumental of "Self-cognition" — reframing the mental event as Śiva's own self-manifestation]
[tattvārthadarśanam: not intellectual knowledge but darśana — direct vision; the phrase resonates with Pratyabhijñā terminology]		 (At the very instant) when a desire or perception has (just) arisen, (but is as yet without an object), one whose attention is not directed towards anything else, should place the mind (where it emerges, cultivating) the notion (buddhi) that (this is) the Self. (Thus he) then (has) a vision of what truly exists (tattvārtha). (98) (97)
NATURE OF CONSCIOUSNESS (VERSES 99–100)
nirnimittaṃ bhavej jñānaṃ nirādhāraṃ bhramātmakam | tattvataḥ kasyacin naitad evambhāvī śivaḥ priye || 99 ||

De-sandhi:
nir-nimittam bhavet jñānam nir-ādhāram bhrama-ātmakam | tattvataḥ kasyacit na etat evam-bhāvī śivaḥ priye ||		 nir-nimittam = without (outer, independent) cause/reason (adj. nom. sg. n.)
bhavet = is, arises (opt. 3 sg., √bhū)
jñānam = knowledge, cognition (n. nom. sg.)
nir-ādhāram = without support, baseless (adj. nom. sg. n.)
bhrama-ātmakam = consisting of/characterized by illusion (adj. nom. sg. n.)
tattvataḥ = in reality, truly (adv.)
kasyacit = of anyone, someone's (gen. sg., indef.)
na = not (neg.)
etat = this (pron. nom. sg. n.)
evam-bhāvī = one who abides in this way, holding this attitude (adj. nom. sg. m.)
śivaḥ = Śiva (m. nom. sg.)
priye = O beloved one (voc. sg. f.)		 All (jñānam) knowledge arises without independent cause (nirnimittam), without support (nirādhāram), consisting of illusion (bhramātmakam); in reality (tattvataḥ) this belongs to no one. One who abides in this way (evambhāvī), O beloved (priye), is (śivaḥ) Śiva. Dhāraṇā 76: Knowledge as causeless, ownerless appearance
[nirnimitta + nirādhāra: double negation strips knowledge of both its objective cause and its subjective owner]
[bhramātmakam: "illusion" here not in a nihilistic sense but as abhāsavāda — knowledge is appearance (ābhāsa) in consciousness, not independent reality]
[kasyacit na etat: the culminating move — no empirical subject owns this cognition; it belongs to consciousness-as-Śiva]
[evambhāvī śivaḥ: the direct identification formula — the attitude of understanding IS the identity with Śiva, not its precondition]		 (All) knowledge is without (outer, independent) cause, a baseless illusion. In reality this (knowledge) does not belong to anybody. One who abides in this way (with this attitude), O dear one, is Śiva. (99) (98)
ciddharmā sarvadeheṣu viśeṣo nāsti kutracit | ataś ca tanmayaṃ sarvaṃ bhāvayan bhavajij janaḥ || 100 ||

De-sandhi:
cit-dharmā sarva-deheṣu viśeṣaḥ na asti kutracit | ataḥ ca tat-mayam sarvam bhāvayan bhava-jit janaḥ ||		 cit-dharmā = whose nature/property is consciousness (adj. nom. sg. m., bahuvrihi)
sarva-deheṣu = in all bodies (m. loc. pl.)
viśeṣaḥ = distinction, difference (m. nom. sg.)
na asti = does not exist (neg. pres. 3 sg.)
kutracit = anywhere, in any respect (adv., indef.)
ataḥ = therefore, from this (adv.)
ca = and (conj.)
tat-mayam = consisting of that, permeated by that (adj. acc. sg. n.)
sarvam = everything, all (adj. acc. sg. n.)
bhāvayan = contemplating, meditating on (pres. ptc. nom. sg. m.)
bhava-jit = conqueror of phenomenal existence (m. nom. sg., tatpuruṣa)
janaḥ = a person (m. nom. sg.)		 The one whose nature is consciousness (ciddharmā) is present in all bodies (sarvadeheṣu); there is no distinction (viśeṣaḥ na asti) anywhere. Therefore, a person (janaḥ) who contemplates (bhāvayan) everything as consisting of that (tanmayam) becomes the conqueror of phenomenal existence (bhavajit). Dhāraṇā 77: Contemplation of all as consciousness-nature
[ciddharmā: subject is not "consciousness" but "the one whose dharma is cit" — Śiva/Bhairava as omnipresent substratum of all embodied beings]
[sarva-deheṣu viśeṣo nāsti: the non-difference of consciousness across all bodies is the metaphysical premise; bhāvayan performs the recognition]
[tat-mayam sarvam: universal identification — "all as consisting of that" — echoes the opening teaching of the VBT on the nature of Bhairava]
[bhavajit: "conqueror of saṃsāra/bhava" — not liberation after death but victory over conditioned existence here and now]
[This verse forms a structural closure: the universality of consciousness-nature (ciddharmā) answers the opening question about Bhairava's nature]		 The one whose nature (dharma) is consciousness is present in all bodies; there is no difference anywhere. Thus, a person engaged in contemplating (bhāvayan) everything as being of that nature conquers phenomenal existence. (100) (99)

✓ Verses 1-100 complete
112 dhāraṇās (meditation techniques) from the Trika tradition.

Upaniṣads - Scholarly Analysis

Selected passages with detailed linguistic and philosophical analysis

Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad
Śvetāśvatara Upaniṣad

Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 1.5.2-3

With Śaṅkara's Commentary (Bhāṣya) - The Seven Foods

Sanskrit Text Word-by-Word Analysis Literal Translation Grammar/Commentary Key Terms
BṚHADĀRAṆYAKA 1.5.2 - ŚAṄKARA'S COMMENTARY ON THE SEVEN FOODS
'yat saptānnāni medhayā
tapasā janayat pitā' iti
medhayā hi tapasā janayat pitā		 • yat = that which (rel. pron.)
• sapta-annāni = seven foods
• medhayā = through meditation, wisdom (inst. f. from medhā)
• tapasā = through austerity (inst. n. from tapas)
• janayat (ajanayat) = created (3sg. imperf. caus. from √jan)
• pitā = Father, Prajāpati (nom. m.)
• iti = thus (quotative)
• hi = indeed, for (emphatic particle) 		[Regarding] "That (yat) the Father (pitā) created (janayat) seven foods (saptānnāni) through wisdom (medhayā) and austerity (tapasā)" - indeed (hi) the Father (pitā) created (janayat) [them] through meditation (medhayā) and austerity (tapasā). Śaṅkara quotes the Upaniṣadic verse and affirms it. The dual means (medhā + tapas) are necessary for creation. Standard bhāṣya format: quote then explain. medhā - mental vigor, wisdom
tapas - heat, austerity
√jan (caus.) - to generate
Bhāṣya structure
THE COMMON FOOD
'ekamasya sādhāraṇam' iti
idam eva asya tat sādhāraṇam annam
yad idam adyate		 • ekam = one (nom./acc. n.)
• asya = of this/his (gen.)
• sādhāraṇam = common, shared (adj. from sā-dhāraṇa)
• idam = this (nom./acc. n.)
• eva = indeed, exactly (particle)
• tat = that (nom./acc. n.)
• annam = food
• yat = which (relative)
• adyate = is eaten (3sg. pres. pass. from √ad) 		[Regarding] "One (ekam) of these (asya) is common (sādhāraṇam)" - this (idam) very (eva) [food] that (yat) is eaten (adyate) is that (tat) common (sādhāraṇam) food (annam) of his (asya). First food identified: the common food (sādhāraṇam) shared by all. This is ordinary food that everyone eats. √dhṛ with sa- prefix = "held in common". sādhāraṇa - held in common
√ad - to eat
√dhṛ - to hold
Common sustenance
sa ya etad upāste
na sa pāpmano vyāvartate
miśraṃ hy etat		 • saḥ = he (nom. m.)
• yaḥ = who (nom. m. relative)
• etat = this (acc. n.)
• upāste = worships, meditates upon (3sg. pres. mid. from upa-√ās)
• na = not
• pāpmanaḥ = from evil, sin (abl. m. from pāpman)
• vyāvartate = turns away (3sg. pres. mid. from vi-ā-√vṛt)
• miśram = mixed, impure (PPP from √miś)
• hy (hi) = for, indeed 		He (saḥ) who (yaḥ) worships (upāste) this (etat) does not (na) turn away (vyāvartate) from evil (pāpmanaḥ), for (hi) this (etat) is mixed (miśram). Warning: worshipping the common food doesn't purify because it's miśra (mixed/impure). This food is morally neutral - both good and bad partake. √vṛt with vi-ā- = "to turn completely away from". upa-√ās - to sit near, worship
pāpman - evil, sin
vi-ā-√vṛt - to turn away
√miś - to mix
Mixed nature
TWO FOODS FOR THE GODS
'dve devān abhājayat' iti
hutaṃ ca prahutaṃ ca		 • dve = two (nom./acc. n. dual)
• devān = to the gods (acc. pl. m.)
• abhājayat = distributed, apportioned (3sg. imperf. caus. from ā-√bhaj)
• iti = thus
• hutam = oblation, what is offered in fire (PPP from √hu)
• ca = and
• prahutam = offering, present (PPP from pra-√hu) 		[Regarding] "Two (dve) he apportioned (abhājayat) to the gods (devān)" - [these are] the oblation (hutam) and (ca) the offering (prahutam). Two foods for gods: huta (burnt oblation in fire) and prahuta (offered food/gifts). √bhaj with ā- = "to distribute, apportion". Both are sacrificial offerings but different types. √bhaj - to share, divide
√hu - to offer in fire
pra-√hu - to offer forth
Two types of sacrifice
tasmād devebhyo juhvati ca
pra ca juhvanti		 • tasmāt = therefore (abl. from tat)
• devebhyaḥ = to the gods (dat. pl.)
• juhvati = they offer, sacrifice (3pl. pres. from √hu)
• ca = and
• pra = forth (prefix)
• pra juhvanti = they offer forth (3pl. pres. from pra-√hu) 		Therefore (tasmāt) [people] make oblations (juhvati) to the gods (devebhyaḥ) and (ca) also offer forth (pra juhvanti) [presents]. Explanation of ritual practice: people perform both huta (fire oblations) and prahuta (presenting offerings). This explains the dual nature of Vedic sacrifice. √hu - to sacrifice
Ritual practice: two types
Fire + presentation
atho āhur darśa-pūrṇamāsau iti • atho (atha + u) = but, moreover
• āhuḥ = they say (3pl. perf. from √ah/√brū)
• darśa = new moon (m. from √dṛś)
• pūrṇamāsa = full moon (m.)
• -au = dual ending
• iti = thus 		But (atho) some say (āhuḥ) these are the new moon (darśa) and full moon (pūrṇamāsau) [sacrifices]. Alternative interpretation: the two foods are the darśa (new moon) and pūrṇamāsa (full moon) sacrifices. These are major Vedic rituals performed monthly. darśa - new moon
pūrṇamāsa - full moon
√dṛś - to see
Monthly sacrifices
tasmān na iṣṭi-yājukaḥ syāt • tasmāt = therefore
• na = not
• iṣṭi = sacrifice, offering (f. from √yaj)
• yājuka = performer, sacrificer (agent noun from √yaj)
• syāt = should be (3sg. opt. from √as) 		Therefore (tasmāt) one should (syāt) not (na) be a performer of desire-motivated sacrifices (iṣṭi-yājukaḥ). Ethical teaching: avoid iṣṭi sacrifices motivated by selfish desires. These two foods for gods are pure offerings, not for personal gain. Optative syāt = obligation/recommendation. iṣṭi - sacrifice
√yaj - to sacrifice
√as (opt.) - should be
Avoid selfish rituals
MILK - THE FOOD FOR ANIMALS
'paśubhya ekaṃ prāyacchat' iti
tat payaḥ		 • paśubhyaḥ = to animals (dat. pl. m. from paśu)
• ekam = one (acc. n.)
• prāyacchat = gave (3sg. imperf. from pra-√yam/dā)
• tat = that
• payaḥ = milk (nom./acc. n. from payas) 		[Regarding] "One (ekam) he gave (prāyacchat) to animals (paśubhyaḥ)" - that (tat) is milk (payaḥ). Identification: the one food for animals (paśu) is milk (payas). This sustains both humans and animals in infancy. Central to Vedic cosmology. paśu - animal, cattle
payas - milk, fluid
pra-√dā - to give forth
Universal nourishment
payo hy eva agre
manuṣyāḥ ca paśavaḥ ca upajīvanti		 • payaḥ = milk (nom. n.)
• hy (hi) = for, indeed
• eva = exactly, indeed
• agre = in the beginning, at first (loc. from agra)
• manuṣyāḥ = humans (nom. pl. m.)
• ca = and
• paśavaḥ = animals (nom. pl. m.)
• upajīvanti = they live on, subsist on (3pl. pres. from upa-√jīv) 		For (hi) indeed (eva) in the beginning (agre) both humans (manuṣyāḥ) and (ca) animals (paśavaḥ) live on (upajīvanti) milk (payaḥ). Universal truth: milk is the first food (agre) for all mammals. Both manuṣya and paśu begin life sustained by payas. √jīv with upa- = "to live upon, subsist by". agra - beginning, front
manuṣya - human
upa-√jīv - to live on
First food of life
tasmāt kumāram jātam
ghṛtaṃ vai vā agre pratilehayanti
stanam vā anudhāpayanti		 • tasmāt = therefore
• kumāram = child, boy (acc. m.)
• jātam = born (acc. m. PPP from √jan)
• ghṛtam = ghee, clarified butter (acc. n.)
• vai = indeed, truly
• vā = or
• agre = at first
• pratilehayanti = they make lick (3pl. pres. caus. from prati-√lih)
• stanam = breast, nipple (acc. m.)
• anudhāpayanti = they make suckle (caus. from anu-√dhe) 		Therefore (tasmāt), the born (jātam) child (kumāram) is first (agre) made to lick (pratilehayanti) ghee (ghṛtam), or (vā) they are made to suckle (anudhāpayanti) the breast (stanam). Cultural practice: newborns (kumāra jāta) receive either ghee (ghṛta) or breast milk (stana) as first food. √lih = "to lick", √dhe = "to suck". This confirms milk's primacy. kumāra - child
ghṛta - ghee
√lih - to lick
√dhe - to suck
First feeding ritual
atha vatsam jātam āhur
atṛṇādaḥ iti		 • atha = now, then, and
• vatsam = calf (acc. m.)
• jātam = born (acc. m. PPP)
• āhuḥ = they say (3pl. perf.)
• atṛṇādaḥ = not eating grass (nom. m. compound: a-tṛṇa-ada)
• a = not (negative prefix)
• tṛṇa = grass, straw
• ada = eating (from √ad)
• iti = thus 		And (atha) of a calf (vatsam) that is born (jātam) they say (āhuḥ) "it does not yet eat (atṛṇādaḥ) grass (tṛṇa)". Parallel example: newborn calves (vatsa jāta) are called atṛṇāda "non-grass-eater" because they survive only on milk initially. Confirms milk's universality as first food. vatsa - calf
tṛṇa - grass
√ad - to eat
Before solid food
'tasmin sarvaṃ pratiṣṭhitam
yacca prāṇiti yacca na' iti
payasi hī idam sarvam pratiṣṭhitam
yacca prāṇiti yacca na		 • tasmin = in/on that (loc. n.)
• sarvam = all, everything (nom./acc. n.)
• pratiṣṭhitam = established, resting (PPP from prati-√sthā)
• yat = what, which (relative)
• ca = and
• prāṇiti = breathes, lives (3sg. pres. from √an with prāṇa)
• na = not
• payasi = in/on milk (loc. n.)
• hi = indeed
• idam = this 		[Regarding] "On it (tasmin) everything (sarvam) rests (pratiṣṭhitam), what (yat) breathes (prāṇiti) and (ca) what does not (na)" - indeed (hi) on milk (payasi) all (sarvam) this (idam) rests (pratiṣṭhitam), what (yat) breathes (prāṇiti) and (ca) what does not (na). Cosmic significance: ALL existence (sarvam) - both animate (prāṇiti "what breathes") and inanimate (na prāṇiti) - depends on milk. √sthā with prati- = "to stand upon, be established in". Milk as cosmic foundation. prati-√sthā - to be established
√an - to breathe
Animate vs inanimate
Milk = foundation
CONQUERING REPEATED DEATH
tad yad idam āhuḥ
saṃvatsaram payasā juhvat
apa punaḥ-mṛtyum jayati iti
na tathā vidyāt		 • tat = that, now
• yat = what, which
• idam = this
• āhuḥ = they say (3pl. perf.)
• saṃvatsaram = year (acc. m. from saṃvatsara)
• payasā = with milk (inst. n.)
• juhvat = offering (pres. part. from √hu)
• apa = away (prefix)
• punaḥ-mṛtyum = repeated death (acc. m. compound)
• jayati = conquers, wins (3sg. pres. from √ji)
• na = not
• tathā = thus, in that way
• vidyāt = should know (3sg. opt. from √vid) 		Now (tat) regarding what (yat) they say (āhuḥ) "By [one] offering (juhvat) milk (payasā) for a year (saṃvatsaram) one conquers (jayati) repeated death (punaḥ-mṛtyum)" - one should (vidyāt) not (na) understand it thus (tathā). Refutation of literal interpretation: offering milk sacrifices for a full saṃvatsara (year) doesn't literally conquer punaḥ-mṛtyu (re-death/rebirth) through duration alone. Optative vidyāt = "should/must know". Higher understanding needed. saṃvatsara - year
punaḥ-mṛtyu - repeated death
√ji - to conquer
√vid (opt.) - should know
Not literal duration
yat ahaḥ eva juhoti
tat ahaḥ punaḥ-mṛtyum apajayati
evam vidvān		 • yat = which (relative)
• ahaḥ = day (nom./acc. n. from ahan)
• eva = indeed, very
• juhoti = offers (3sg. pres. from √hu)
• tat = that
• punaḥ-mṛtyum = repeated death (acc. m.)
• apajayati = conquers, overcomes (3sg. pres. from apa-√ji)
• evam = thus, in this way
• vidvān = knowing, knower (nom. m. perf. part. from √vid) 		On whatever (yat) day (ahaḥ) one offers (juhoti), on that (tat) very (eva) day (ahaḥ) the knower (vidvān) conquers (apajayati) repeated death (punaḥ-mṛtyum). Correct understanding: conquest of punaḥ-mṛtyu happens immediately (yat ahaḥ = "on that very day") for the vidvān (knower/one who understands). Knowledge, not mere ritual duration, liberates. √ji with apa- = "to conquer away". ahan - day
apa-√ji - to conquer away
vidvān - knower (perf. part.)
Immediate liberation
Knowledge key
sarvaṃ hi devebhyaḥ anna-ādyam prayacchati • sarvam = all (nom./acc. n.)
• hi = for, indeed
• devebhyaḥ = to the gods (dat. pl. m.)
• annādyam (anna-ādyam) = edible food (acc. n. compound)
• anna = food
• ādya = to be eaten, edible (gerundive from √ad)
• prayacchati = offers, gives (3sg. pres. from pra-√yam/dā) 		For (hi) he offers (prayacchati) all (sarvam) edible food (annādyam) to the gods (devebhyaḥ). The knower offers ALL food (sarva anna) to the gods (devebhyaḥ). Every meal becomes an offering. Gerundive ādya = "to be eaten, edible". This transforms eating into sacrifice. ādya - edible (gerundive)
pra-√dā - to give forth
All eating = sacrifice
Constant offering
THE IMPERISHABLE PERSON AS CAUSE
'kasmāt tāni na kṣīyante
adyamānāni sarvadā' iti
puruṣo vā akṣitiḥ
sa hī idam annam punaḥ punaḥ janayate		 • kasmāt = why, from what (abl. interrogative)
• tāni = those, they (nom./acc. n. pl.)
• na = not
• kṣīyante = are exhausted, decay (3pl. pres. pass. from √kṣi)
• adyamānāni = being eaten (nom./acc. n. pl. pres. pass. part. from √ad)
• sarvadā = always (adv. sarva + dā)
• puruṣaḥ = person, Self (nom. m.)
• vā = indeed
• akṣitiḥ = imperishable, indestructible (f. from a-kṣiti)
• sa = he
• hi = for, indeed
• idam = this
• annam = food
• punaḥ punaḥ = again and again
• janayate = produces, generates (3sg. pres. caus. from √jan) 		[Regarding] "Why (kasmāt) are they (tāni) not (na) exhausted (kṣīyante) though always (sarvadā) being eaten (adyamānāni)?" - The person (puruṣaḥ) indeed (vā) is the imperishable (akṣitiḥ) [cause], for (hi) he (sa) produces (janayate) this (idam) food (annam) again and again (punaḥ punaḥ). Philosophical key: food never runs out because puruṣa (the cosmic Person/Self) is akṣiti (imperishable) and continuously regenerates (punaḥ punaḥ janayate) food through consciousness and action. √kṣi = "to decay, perish". The unchanging Self perpetually manifests as changing food. puruṣa - Person, Self
akṣiti - imperishable
√kṣi - to perish
√jan (caus.) - to produce
Infinite regeneration
'yo vai tām akṣitim veda' iti
puruṣo vā akṣitiḥ		 • yaḥ = who (nom. m. relative)
• vai = indeed, truly
• etām = this (acc. f. from idam)
• akṣitim = imperishability (acc. f.)
• veda = knows (3sg. perf. from √vid, used as present)
• puruṣaḥ = person, Self
• vā = indeed
• akṣitiḥ = imperishable (f. nom.) 		[Regarding] "He who (yaḥ) knows (veda) this (etām) imperishability (akṣitim)" - the person (puruṣaḥ) indeed (vā) is the imperishable (akṣitiḥ) [cause]. Reiteration: knowing (veda) the akṣiti (imperishability) means understanding that puruṣa IS the imperishable source. Perfect tense veda used as present = "knows" (completed knowledge existing now). Self-knowledge is key. √vid (perf.) - knows
Knowledge of Self
Imperishable nature
Identity realized
sa hī idam annam dhiyā dhiyā
janayate karmabhiḥ		 • sa = he
• hi = for, indeed
• idam = this
• annam = food
• dhiyā dhiyā = through thought after thought (inst. f. from dhī, repeated)
• janayate = produces (3sg. pres. caus.)
• karmabhiḥ = through/from actions (inst. pl. n. from karman) 		For (hi) he (sa) produces (janayate) this (idam) food (annam) through thought after thought (dhiyā dhiyā), through actions (karmabhiḥ). Mechanism of regeneration: puruṣa creates food through repeated dhī (thought/intelligence) and karman (actions). The repetition "dhiyā dhiyā" emphasizes continuous mental activity. Mind (dhī) + action (karman) = manifestation of food. √dhī = "to think, meditate". dhī - thought, intelligence
√dhī - to think
karman - action, deed
Mind + action = creation
Continuous process
yat hi etat na karma kuryāt
kṣīyeta ha		 • yat = if (conditional use of relative)
• hi = indeed
• etat = this
• na = not
• karma = action (acc. n.)
• kuryāt = were to do, should do (3sg. opt. from √kṛ)
• kṣīyeta = would be exhausted (3sg. opt. pass. from √kṣi)
• ha = indeed, certainly (emphatic particle) 		If (yat) he (etat) were not (na) to do (kuryāt) this action (karma), [the food] would indeed (ha) be exhausted (kṣīyeta). Counterfactual: without continued karma (action) by puruṣa, food would kṣīyeta (be depleted/exhausted). Optatives kuryāt + kṣīyeta express hypothetical condition. Action sustains existence. Cessation of conscious action = cessation of manifestation. √kṛ (opt.) - were to do
√kṣi (opt. pass.) - would be exhausted
Necessity of action
Counterfactual
EATING WITH PRATĪKA (THE MOUTH)
'so 'nnam atti pratīkena' iti
mukham pratīkam
mukhena ity etat		 • saḥ = he
• annam = food (acc. n.)
• atti = eats (3sg. pres. from √ad)
• pratīkena = with pratīka; facing (inst. n. from pratīka)
• mukham = mouth, face (nom./acc. n.)
• pratīkam = facing, pre-eminence (nom./acc. n.)
• mukhena = through/with the mouth (inst. n.)
• ity (iti) = thus
• etat = this 		[Regarding] "He (saḥ) eats (atti) food (annam) with pratīka (pratīkena)" - the mouth (mukham) is pratīka (pratīkam); this (etat) means "through the mouth (mukhena)". Etymological explanation: pratīka literally means "facing, appearance, pre-eminent part" but here means mukha (mouth/face). The Self eats through the mouth as instrument. Pratīka = prati (towards) + √ī (to go) + ka = "what goes towards/faces". The mouth faces food. pratīka - face, appearance, counterpart
mukha - mouth, face
√ad - to eat
Mouth = interface
Etymology explained
'sa devān apigacchati
sa ūrjam upajīvati' iti
praśaṃsā		 • sa = he
• devān = gods (acc. pl. m.)
• apigacchati = attains, goes to (3sg. pres. from api-√gam)
• ūrjam = vigor, strength, sap (acc. f. from ūrj)
• upajīvati = lives on, subsists on (3sg. pres. from upa-√jīv)
• iti = thus
• praśaṃsā = praise, eulogy (f. from pra-√śaṃs) 		[Regarding] "He (sa) attains (apigacchati) the gods (devān), he (sa) lives on (upajīvati) vigor (ūrjam)" - this is praise (praśaṃsā). Concluding eulogy (praśaṃsā): the knower who understands this teaching attains the gods (devān apigacchati) and lives sustained by ūrja (vigor/vital sap). √gam with api- = "to go to, attain, reach". √śaṃs with pra- = "to praise, recite". The commentary ends with praise formula. api-√gam - to attain
ūrj - vigor, strength, sap
upa-√jīv - to live on
praśaṃsā - eulogy
Concluding praise
BṚHADĀRAṆYAKA 1.5.3 - THE THREE FOODS FOR THE SELF
'trīṇy ātmane akuruta' iti
manaḥ vācam prāṇam
tāni ātmane akuruta		 • trīṇi = three (nom./acc. n. pl.)
• ātmane = for himself, for the Self (dat. m. from ātman)
• akuruta = made, did (3sg. imperf. Ātm. from √kṛ)
• iti = thus
• manaḥ = mind (nom./acc. n. from manas)
• vācam = speech (acc. f. from vāc)
• prāṇam = breath, life (acc. m. from prāṇa)
• tāni = those, these (nom./acc. n. pl.) 		[Regarding] "Three (trīṇi) he made (akuruta) for himself (ātmane)" - [these are] mind (manaḥ), speech (vācam), and breath (prāṇam); these (tāni) he made (akuruta) for himself (ātmane). The three "self-foods" (ātmane = "for the Self"): manas (mind), vāc (speech), prāṇa (breath). These are internal faculties, unlike external foods. Ātmanepada akuruta = reflexive "made for himself". The Self's own instruments of manifestation. ātman - self, soul
manas - mind (√man - to think)
vāc - speech (√vac - to speak)
prāṇa - breath (pra-√an - to breathe forth)
Three internal faculties
THE PRIMACY OF MIND
anyatra manā abhūvam na adarśam
anyatra manā abhūvam na aśrauṣam iti		 • anyatra = elsewhere, in another place (adv.)
• manāḥ = with mind (inst. n. from manas)
• abhūvam = I was (1sg. root aorist from √bhū)
• na = not
• adarśam = I saw (1sg. imperf. from √dṛś)
• na = not
• aśrauṣam = I heard (aorist from √śru)
• iti = thus 		"When my mind (manāḥ) was (abhūvam) elsewhere (anyatra), I did not (na) see (adarśam); when my mind (manāḥ) was (abhūvam) elsewhere (anyatra), I did not (na) hear (aśrauṣam)" - thus. Common experience proving mind's primacy: without mental attention (manāḥ), sense organs don't function. "Anyatra manāḥ" = "mind elsewhere" = inattention. Even though eyes/ears work physically, perception requires manas. Mind coordinates all sensing. anyatra - elsewhere
√bhū (aorist) - I was
√dṛś - to see
√śru - to hear
Mind = prerequisite for perception
manasā hy eva paśyati
manasā śṛṇoti		 • manasā = with/through the mind (inst. n.)
• hy (hi) = for, indeed
• eva = indeed, exactly
• paśyati = sees, perceives (3sg. pres. from √paś/dṛś)
• śṛṇoti = hears (3sg. pres. from √śru) 		For (hi) indeed (eva) one sees (paśyati) with the mind (manasā), one hears (śṛṇoti) with the mind (manasā). Philosophical principle: all perception (paśyati, śṛṇoti) is ultimately mental (manasā). Eyes and ears are mere instruments; manas is the true perceiver. Eva emphasizes exclusivity - ONLY through mind does perception occur. √paś/√dṛś - to see
√śru - to hear
Mind = true perceiver
Senses = instruments
MENTAL FUNCTIONS - ALL ARE MIND
kāmaḥ saṅkalpaḥ vicikitsā
śraddhā aśraddhā
dhṛtiḥ adhṛtiḥ
hrīḥ dhīḥ bhīḥ iti
etat sarvam manaḥ eva		 • kāmaḥ = desire (nom. m. from √kam)
• saṅkalpaḥ = resolve, intention (nom. m. from sam-√kḷp)
• vicikitsā = doubt (f. from vi-√cit intensive)
• śraddhā = faith (f. from śrat-√dhā)
• aśraddhā = lack of faith (f.)
• dhṛtiḥ = firmness (f. from √dhṛ)
• adhṛtiḥ = lack of firmness (f.)
• hrīḥ = shame, modesty (f. from √hrī)
• dhīḥ = intelligence (f. from √dhī)
• bhīḥ = fear (f. from √bhī)
• iti = thus
• etat = this
• sarvam = all
• manaḥ = mind
• eva = indeed, only 		Desire (kāmaḥ), resolve (saṅkalpaḥ), doubt (vicikitsā), faith (śraddhā), lack of faith (aśraddhā), firmness (dhṛtiḥ), lack of firmness (adhṛtiḥ), shame (hrīḥ), intelligence (dhīḥ), fear (bhīḥ) - all (sarvam) this (etat) is indeed (eva) just the mind (manaḥ). Comprehensive list of mental states/functions, all identified as manas itself. Includes: 1) kāma (desire), 2) saṅkalpa (will/resolve), 3) vicikitsā (doubt), 4) śraddhā (faith) and its opposite, 5) dhṛti (steadfastness) and its opposite, 6) hrī (shame/modesty), 7) dhī (wisdom/understanding), 8) bhī (fear). All psychological phenomena reduce to mind. Eva = "only/just" mind. √kam - to desire
sam-√kḷp - to resolve
vi-√cit - to doubt
√dhṛ - to hold
√hrī - to be ashamed
√dhī - to think
√bhī - to fear
All mental states = mind
tasmād api pṛṣṭhataḥ upaspṛṣṭaḥ
manasā vijānāti		 • tasmāt = therefore
• api = even, also
• pṛṣṭhataḥ = from behind (adv. from pṛṣṭha + -tas)
• upaspṛṣṭaḥ = touched, contacted (nom. m. PPP from upa-√spṛś)
• manasā = through the mind (inst. n.)
• vijānāti = perceives, knows distinctly (3sg. pres. from vi-√jñā) 		Therefore (tasmāt) even (api) when touched (upaspṛṣṭaḥ) from behind (pṛṣṭhataḥ), one perceives (vijānāti) it through the mind (manasā). Even tactile perception from pṛṣṭhataḥ (behind = unseen location) requires manas. Physical contact (upaspṛṣṭa) is processed by mind. √jñā with vi- = "to know distinctly, discern". Mind perceives even without visual confirmation. Touch needs mental awareness. pṛṣṭha - back, rear
upa-√spṛś - to touch
vi-√jñā - to perceive distinctly
Even touch needs mind
SPEECH - ALL SOUND IS SPEECH
yaḥ kaḥ ca śabdaḥ
vāk eva sā eṣā hi
antam āyattā
eṣā hi na		 • yaḥ = whatever, who (nom. m.)
• kaḥ = who, what (interrogative as indefinite)
• ca = and (= whatever, any)
• śabdaḥ = sound, word (nom. m. from √śabd)
• vāk = speech (nom. f.)
• eva = indeed, alone
• sā = that, she (nom. f.)
• eṣā = this (nom. f.)
• hi = for, indeed
• antam = end, goal (acc. n. from anta)
• āyattā = dependent on, aiming at (nom. f. PPP from ā-√yat)
• na = not 		Whatever (yaḥ kaḥ ca) sound (śabdaḥ) there is, that (sā) is indeed (eva) speech (vāk) alone, for (hi) it (eṣā) aims at (āyattā) an end (antam); it does not (na) [exist independently]. All śabda (sound/word) is fundamentally vāc (speech). Speech is purposive - āyattā "aiming at" anta (an end/goal). √yat with ā- = "to depend on, aim at, strive for". Speech is not random noise but intentional communication towards meaning. No sound exists independently (na) of communicative intent. √śabd - to make sound
vāc - speech, voice
anta - end, goal
ā-√yat - to aim at, depend on
Sound = purposive speech
PRĀṆA - THE FIVE VITAL AIRS
prāṇaḥ apānaḥ vyānaḥ
udānaḥ samānaḥ anaḥ iti
etat sarvam prāṇaḥ eva		 • prāṇaḥ = the upward/forward breath (nom. m. from pra-√an)
• apānaḥ = the downward breath (nom. m. from apa-√an)
• vyānaḥ = the diffused breath (nom. m. from vi-√an)
• udānaḥ = the ascending breath (nom. m. from ud-√an)
• samānaḥ = the equalizing breath (nom. m. from sam-√an)
• anaḥ = respiration, breathing (nom. m. from √an)
• iti = thus
• etat = this
• sarvam = all
• prāṇaḥ = breath, life-force
• eva = indeed, only 		Prāṇa (prāṇaḥ), apāna (apānaḥ), vyāna (vyānaḥ), udāna (udānaḥ), samāna (samānaḥ), and ana (anaḥ) - all (sarvam) this (etat) is indeed (eva) just breath (prāṇaḥ). The five vital airs (pañca-prāṇa) plus generic breath (ana): 1) prāṇa (forward/upward - chest/throat), 2) apāna (downward - lower body), 3) vyāna (diffused - throughout body), 4) udāna (ascending - head/upward), 5) samāna (equalizing - navel/digestion), 6) ana (generic breathing). All are manifestations of one life-force (prāṇa eva). √an = "to breathe, live". Each prefix modifies direction/function. pra-√an - forward breath
apa-√an - downward breath
vi-√an - pervasive breath
ud-√an - upward breath
sam-√an - equalizing breath
√an - to breathe
Five vital airs
THE SELF MADE OF THREE
etat-mayaḥ vā ayam ātmā
vāṅ-mayaḥ manaḥ-mayaḥ
prāṇa-mayaḥ		 • etat-mayaḥ = made of these, consisting of these (nom. m. compound)
• vā = indeed
• ayam = this (nom. m. from idam)
• ātmā = Self, soul (nom. m.)
• vāṅ-mayaḥ (vāk-mayaḥ) = made of speech (nom. m.)
• manaḥ-mayaḥ = made of mind (nom. m. compound manas-maya)
• prāṇa-mayaḥ = made of breath (nom. m.)
• -maya = made of, consisting of (suffix from √mā - to measure, form) 		This (ayam) Self (ātmā) indeed (vā) is made of these (etat-mayaḥ) [three], made of speech (vāṅ-mayaḥ), made of mind (manaḥ-mayaḥ), made of breath (prāṇa-mayaḥ). Concluding identification: the ātman (Self) consists of (maya) the three internal faculties: vāc (speech), manas (mind), prāṇa (breath). These three "foods made for himself" (1.5.3 opening) constitute the Self's manifestation. Suffix -maya = "consisting of, formed by". The Self IS these three, just as it IS the seven foods. Upaniṣadic identity formula. ātman - Self, soul
-maya - made of, consisting of
√mā - to measure, form
Self = mind + speech + breath
Complete identity

✓ BAU 1.5.2-3 with Śaṅkara's Commentary
Focus: The Seven Foods of Prajāpati and Cosmic Distribution

Śvetāśvatara Upaniṣad - Chapter 1

Verses 1.1-12 - Philosophical Inquiry into Brahman

Sanskrit Text Word-by-Word Analysis Literal Translation Grammar Notes Key Terms
VERSE 1.1 - OPENING QUESTIONS ON BRAHMAN
oṃ / kiṃkāraṇaṃ brahma kutaḥ sma jātā jīvāmaḥ kena / kva ca saṃpratiṣṭhāḥ / adhiṣṭhitāḥ kena sukhetareṣu vartāmahe / brahmavido vyavasthām // SvetUp_1.1 // • oṃ = sacred syllable
• kim-kāraṇam = what-cause (nom. sg. n.)
• brahma = Brahman (nom. sg. n.)
• kutaḥ = from where? whence? (adv.)
• sma = particle (past emphasis)
• jātāḥ = born (nom. pl. m. PPP from √jan)
• jīvāmaḥ = we live (1st pl. pres. from √jīv)
• kena = by what? (instr. sg. n. interr.)
• kva = where? (adv.)
• ca = and
• saṃpratiṣṭhāḥ = established, resting (nom. pl. m. from sam-prati-√sthā)
• adhiṣṭhitāḥ = ruled over, governed (nom. pl. m. PPP from adhi-√sthā)
• kena = by what/whom? (instr. sg. n./m. interr.)
• sukha-itareṣu = in pleasure and its opposite (loc. pl. n.)
• vartāmahe = we exist, abide (1st pl. mid. pres. from √vṛt)
• brahma-vidaḥ = O knowers of Brahman (voc. pl. m.)
• vyavasthām = determination, settled state (acc. sg. f.) 		Om (oṃ). What (kim) is the cause (kāraṇam), what is Brahman (brahma)? From where (kutaḥ) were we born (jātāḥ)? By what (kena) do we live (jīvāmaḥ)? And (ca) where (kva) are we established (saṃpratiṣṭhāḥ)? Governed (adhiṣṭhitāḥ) by what (kena) do we exist (vartāmahe) in pleasure (sukha) and its opposite (itareṣu) [pain]? O knowers (vidaḥ) of Brahman (brahma), [tell us] the determination (vyavasthām) [of these matters]. Opens with sacred syllable Om, the primordial sound representing Brahman. This foundational verse poses five essential questions to the knowers of Brahman: (1) What is the ultimate cause/Brahman? (2) What is our origin? (3) What sustains us? (4) What is our foundation/support? (5) Who governs our experience of opposites? Particle sma adds past emphasis. Interrogatives kim, kutaḥ, kena probe cause, origin, means. Compound sam-prati-sthā = "well-established, firmly based"—where do we rest/stand? What is our ontological ground? PPP adhiṣṭhita = "presided over, ruled". Compound sukha-itara = "pleasure-and-other" = pleasure and pain. Middle voice vartāmahe = "we revolve/exist" (from √vṛt "to turn"). What power rules our existence in duality? Vocative address brahma-vid = "Brahman-knower". Vyavasthā = determination, settled conclusion, definitive answer. Appeal to those who know to resolve these questions. Рассуждающие о Брахмане рассуждают: В чем причина? В Брахмане? Откуда мы родились? Чем живем? И где основаны? Знающие Брахмана, [поведайте] – кем ведомые существуем мы в смене счастья и иных [обстоятельств]?
VERSE 1.2 - ANALYSIS OF PROPOSED CAUSES
kālaḥ svabhāvo niyatiḥ yadṛcchā bhūtāni yoniḥ puruṣa iti / cintyam etad api ca ātmasaṃyogānandahetutve // SvetUp_1.2 // • kālaḥ = time (nom. sg. m.)
• svabhāvaḥ = inherent nature (nom. sg. m.)
• niyatiḥ = necessity, fate (nom. sg. f.)
• yadṛcchā = chance, accident (nom. sg. f.)
• bhūtāni = elements (nom. pl. n.)
• yoniḥ = womb, source (nom. sg. f.)
• puruṣaḥ = person, spirit (nom. sg. m.)
• iti = thus (quotative)
• cintyam = to be thought about (gerundive from √cit)
• etat = this (nom. sg. n.)
• api ca = moreover, also
• ātma-saṃyoga = conjunction with the Self
• ānanda-hetutve = in being the cause of bliss (loc. sg.) 		Time (kālaḥ), inherent nature (svabhāvaḥ), necessity (niyatiḥ), chance (yadṛcchā), elements (bhūtāni), the womb (yoniḥ) [Prakṛti], the person (puruṣaḥ)—thus (iti) [say some]. Moreover (api ca), this (etat) should be contemplated (cintyam): [whether these are] the cause (hetutve) of the bliss (ānanda) that comes from conjunction (saṃyoga) with the Self (ātma). Lists rival philosophical theories of causation. Each represents a different school: Time (Kālavāda), Nature (Svabhāvavāda), Fate (Niyativāda), Chance (Yadṛcchāvāda), Elements (Bhūtavāda), Yoni (Sāṃkhya's Prakṛti/material cause), Puruṣa (conscious principle). The 'iti' marks these as views to be examined. Gerundive cintyam = "must be thought about" (obligation). Question: can these theories explain spiritual bliss (ānanda) from Self-realization? Locative absolute construction hetutve addresses whether any of these proposed causes can account for the bliss experienced in union with the Self. The verse challenges these reductionist explanations. Время, собственная природа, необходимость, случайность [первичные] элементы, пуруша – считать ли их источником? Но сочетание их не [является этим источником] из-за природы Атмана – ведь и Атман бессилен перед причиной счастья и несчастья.
VERSE 1.3 - YOGIC VISION OF THE DIVINE POWER
te dhyānayogānugatā apaśyan devātmaśaktiṃ svaguṇair nigūḍhām / yaḥ kāraṇāni nikhilāni tāni kālātmayuktāny adhitiṣṭhaty ekaḥ // SvetUp_1.3 // • te = they (nom. pl. m.)
• dhyāna-yoga-anugatāḥ = devoted to meditation-yoga (nom. pl. m. PPP from anu-√gam)
• apaśyan = they saw, perceived (3rd pl. impf. from √paś)
• deva-ātma-śaktim = the divine Self-power (acc. sg. f.)
• sva-guṇaiḥ = by its own qualities (instr. pl. m.)
• nigūḍhām = hidden, concealed (acc. sg. f. PPP from ni-√guh)
• yaḥ = who (nom. sg. m. rel. pron.)
• kāraṇāni = causes (acc. pl. n.)
• nikhilāni = all, entire (acc. pl. n.)
• tāni = those (acc. pl. n. demons.)
• kāla-ātma-yuktāni = united with time and self (acc. pl. n.)
• adhitiṣṭhati = rules over, presides (3rd sg. pres. from adhi-√sthā)
• ekaḥ = the One (nom. sg. m.) 		They (te), devoted to (anugatāḥ) the yoga (yoga) of meditation (dhyāna), saw (apaśyan) the divine (deva) Self-power (ātmaśaktim) hidden (nigūḍhām) by its own (sva) qualities (guṇaiḥ)—[the power] of the One (ekaḥ) who (yaḥ) alone rules over (adhitiṣṭhati) all (nikhilāni) those (tāni) causes (kāraṇāni) united (yuktāni) with time (kāla) and self (ātma). This verse presents the yogic answer to the questions posed in verses 1.1-1.2. Yogins as seers: PPP anugata from anu-√gam = "devoted to, following". Compound dhyāna-yoga = meditative practice. Imperfect apaśyan = completed vision. Object: deva-ātma-śakti = divine Self-power (God's Śakti). PPP nigūḍhā = concealed by its own guṇas (qualities/modes of prakṛti). Instrumental svaguṇaiḥ = means of concealment. Relative clause describing the Supreme One: Verb adhi-√sthā = "to stand over, preside, govern". Nikhila = all, entire (comprehensive). Demonstrative tāni refers back to causes from verse 1.2 (time, nature, fate, etc.). Compound kāla-ātma-yukta = "united/connected with time-and-self"—all temporal and conscious causes. Eka = the singular divine principle that transcends and rules all multiplicity. The yogins through meditation perceived the hidden divine power behind all apparent causes. Следовавшие размышлению и йоге видели силу божественной сущности, скрытую [ее] собственными свойствами, Что одна правит всеми этими причинами, связанными с временем и Атманом.
VERSE 1.6 - THE SWAN SOUL IN BRAHMAN'S WHEEL
sarvājīve sarvasaṃsthe bṛhante tasmin haṃso bhrāmyate brahmacakre / pṛthag ātmānaṃ preritāraṃ ca matvā juṣṭas tatas tenāmṛtatvam eti // SvetUp_1.6 // • sarva-ājīve = life of all (loc. sg. m.)
• sarva-saṃsthe = support of all (loc. sg. m.)
• bṛhante = vast, great (loc. sg. m.)
• tasmin = in that (loc. sg. m.)
• haṃsaḥ = swan, soul (nom. sg. m.)
• bhrāmyate = wanders (3rd sg. pres. pass. from √bhram)
• brahma-cakre = wheel of Brahman (loc. sg. n.)
• pṛthak = separately, differently (adv.)
• ātmānam = self (acc. sg. m.)
• preritāram = mover, impeller (acc. sg. m. from pra-√īr)
• ca = and
• matvā = thinking, having understood (abs. from √man)
• juṣṭaḥ = favored, pleased (nom. sg. m. PPP from √juṣ)
• tataḥ = then, from that (adv.)
• tena = by Him (instr. sg. m.)
• amṛtatvam = immortality (acc. sg. n.)
• eti = goes (3rd sg. pres. from √i) 		In that (tasmin) vast (bṛhante) [wheel] which is the life of all (sarvājīve), the support of all (sarvasaṃsthe)—in that wheel of Brahman (brahmacakre) the swan (haṃsaḥ) wanders (bhrāmyate). Having understood (matvā) the self (ātmānam) and (ca) the mover (preritāram) as separate (pṛthak)—[when] favored (juṣṭaḥ) by Him (tena), then (tataḥ) [the soul] goes (eti) to immortality (amṛtatvam). The haṃsa (swan) is a metaphor for the individual soul (jīvātman). The locative compounds describe the brahma-cakra (wheel of Brahman/universe). Passive voice bhrāmyate suggests the soul's involuntary wandering in saṃsāra. Path to liberation: Absolutive matvā = "having understood/thought". Pṛthak = separately (initially seeing duality between self and God). PPP juṣṭa from √juṣ = "favored, graced, blessed". When the soul, having first seen itself as separate from the divine mover/controller, is then blessed by Him, it attains amṛtatva (immortality/liberation). Instrumental tena = means of grace. The verse describes both bondage (wandering in the wheel) and liberation (grace leading to immortality). В этом великом колесе Брахмана, всеоживляющем, всеохватывающем, блуждает "лебедь". Мысля Атмана и Движущего различными; возлюбленный [же] им, он идет к бессмертию.
VERSE 1.7 - THE SUPREME BRAHMAN
udgītam etat paramaṃ tu brahma tasmiṃs trayaṃ supratiṣṭhākṣaraṃ ca / atrāntaraṃ brahmavido viditvā līnā brahmaṇi tatparā yonimuktāḥ // SvetUp_1.7 // • udgītam = sung, chanted (nom. sg. n. PPP from ud-√gai)
• etat = this (nom. sg. n.)
• paramam = supreme, highest (nom. sg. n.)
• tu = but, indeed
• brahma = Brahman (nom. sg. n.)
• tasmin = in it (loc. sg. n.)
• trayam = triad, three (nom. sg. n.)
• su-pratiṣṭhā = well-established
• akṣaram = imperishable (nom. sg. n.)
• atra = here (adv.)
• antaram = difference, interior (acc. sg. n.)
• brahma-vidaḥ = Brahman-knowers (nom. pl. m.)
• viditvā = having known (abs. from √vid)
• līnāḥ = absorbed, dissolved (nom. pl. m. PPP from √lī)
• brahmaṇi = in Brahman (loc. sg. n.)
• tat-parāḥ = intent on That (nom. pl. m.)
• yoni-muktāḥ = freed from rebirth (nom. pl. m.) 		This (etat) is sung (udgītam) as the supreme (paramam) Brahman (brahma); in it (tasmin) [is] the triad (trayam), well-established (supratiṣṭhā) and imperishable (akṣaram). Having known (viditvā) the distinction (antaram) here (atra), the knowers of Brahman (brahmavidaḥ), absorbed (līnāḥ) in Brahman (brahmaṇi), intent on That (tatparāḥ), are freed from rebirth (yonimuktāḥ). Udgīta refers to the sacred Vedic chant. The trayam (triad) refers to: enjoyer, enjoyed, and impeller (or knower-known-knowledge). Compound su-pratiṣṭhā = "well-established". Akṣara = "imperishable syllable/reality". Antara = "distinction/difference" between the triad and Brahman. Absolutive viditvā shows prior knowledge. Līnāḥ indicates complete absorption/dissolution. Yoni-mukta compound = "freed from the womb" (rebirth). The verse describes the supreme Brahman containing the cosmic triad, and how knowers who discern the relationship between multiplicity and unity attain liberation through absorption in Brahman. Это воспето как высший Брахман, в нем – триада, [он] твердо основанный и нетленный. Знающие Брахмана, узнав, что [содержится] в нем, погружаются в Брахмана; преданные ему, [они] освобождаются от рождения.
VERSE 1.8 - THE LORD AND THE BOUND SOUL
saṃyuktam etat kṣaram akṣaraṃ ca vyaktāvyaktaṃ bharate viśvam īśaḥ / anīśaś cātmā badhyate bhoktṛbhāvāj jñātvā devaṃ mucyate sarvapāśaiḥ // SvetUp_1.8 // • saṃyuktam = united, joined (acc. sg. n. PPP from sam-√yuj)
• etat = this (acc. sg. n.)
• kṣaram = perishable (acc. sg. n.)
• akṣaram = imperishable (acc. sg. n.)
• ca = and
• vyakta-avyaktam = manifest-unmanifest (acc. sg. n. dvandva)
• bharate = supports (3rd sg. pres. Ātm. from √bhṛ)
• viśvam = universe (acc. sg. n.)
• īśaḥ = Lord (nom. sg. m.)
• anīśaḥ = powerless, not-lord (nom. sg. m.)
• ca = and
• ātmā = soul, self (nom. sg. m.)
• badhyate = is bound (3rd sg. pres. pass. from √bandh)
• bhoktṛ-bhāvāt = due to enjoyer-nature (abl. sg. m.)
• jñātvā = having known (abs. from √jñā)
• devam = God (acc. sg. m.)
• mucyate = is freed (3rd sg. pres. pass. from √muc)
• sarva-pāśaiḥ = from all bonds (inst. pl. m.) 		The Lord (īśaḥ) supports (bharate) this (etat) united (saṃyuktam) perishable (kṣaram) and (ca) imperishable (akṣaram), manifest-unmanifest (vyaktāvyaktam) universe (viśvam). And (ca) the powerless (anīśaḥ) soul (ātmā) is bound (badhyate) due to (bhāvāt) its enjoyer-nature (bhoktṛ); having known (jñātvā) God (devam), it is freed (mucyate) from all (sarva) bonds (pāśaiḥ). Dvandva compounds showing paired opposites: kṣara-akṣara (perishable-imperishable), vyakta-avyakta (manifest-unmanifest). Bharate (Ātmanepada) suggests the Lord's self-sustained support. The universe includes both changing and unchanging aspects. Contrasts īśa (Lord) with anīśa (powerless soul). Passive verbs: badhyate (is bound), mucyate (is freed). Ablative bhāvāt shows cause: "due to being an enjoyer (bhoktṛ)". Absolutive jñātvā precedes liberation. Instrumental pāśaiḥ = "from bonds". The verse contrasts the Lord who supports all cosmic dualities with the bound soul who, through ignorance, identifies with enjoyership, yet can be freed through knowledge of God. Владыка поддерживает все это сочетание тленного и нетленного, проявленного и непроявленного. Не будучи владыкой, [индивидуальный] Атман связан, ибо по природе [он] – воспринимающий. Познав божество, он освобождается от всех уз.
VERSE 1.9 - THE THREE UNBORN PRINCIPLES
jñājñau dvāv ajāv īśanīśāv ajā hy ekā bhoktṛbhogārthayuktā / anantaś cātmā viśvarūpo hy akartā trayaṃ yadā vindate brahmam etat // SvetUp_1.9 // • jña-ajñau = knowing-unknowing (nom. dual m. dvandva)
• dvau = two (nom. dual m.)
• ajau = unborn (nom. dual m.)
• īśa-anīśau = powerful-powerless (nom. dual m. dvandva)
• ajā = unborn (nom. sg. f.)
• hi = indeed
• ekā = one (nom. sg. f.)
• bhoktṛ-bhoga-artha-yuktā = connected with enjoyer-enjoyed-purpose (nom. sg. f.)
• anantaḥ = infinite (nom. sg. m.)
• ca = and
• ātmā = Self (nom. sg. m.)
• viśva-rūpaḥ = universal form (nom. sg. m. Bahuvrīhi)
• hi = indeed
• akartā = non-doer (nom. sg. m.)
• trayam = three (acc. sg. n.)
• yadā = when
• vindate = finds (3rd sg. pres. Ātm. from √vid)
• brahma = Brahman (nom. sg. n.)
• etat = this (nom. sg. n.) 		Two (dvau) unborn ones (ajau)—knowing and unknowing (jñājñau), powerful and powerless (īśanīśau); and (hi) one (ekā) unborn (ajā) [prakṛti] connected (yuktā) with enjoyer (bhoktṛ), enjoyed (bhoga), and purpose (artha). And (ca) the infinite (anantaḥ) Self (ātmā), of universal form (viśvarūpaḥ), indeed (hi) the non-doer (akartā)—when (yadā) one finds (vindate) [these] three (trayam), this (etat) is Brahman (brahma). Three unborn principles: (1) jña-ajña dvandva = jīva (knowing) and īśvara (unknowing of limitation) or puruṣa in bondage/freedom, (2) ajā ekā = prakṛti (feminine, one), (3) implied ātman. Complex compound bhoktṛ-bhoga-artha shows the material nature serving experience. The ātman is characterized by three qualities: ananta (infinite), viśva-rūpa (all-forms), akartā (non-agent). When the seeker comprehends (vindate) all three principles (trayam), this realization constitutes knowing Brahman. Ātmanepada vindate emphasizes personal realization. The verse presents the fundamental metaphysical structure of reality. [Существуют] двое нерожденных – знающих и незнающих, владыка и не владыка, ибо один, нерожденный, связан с воспринимающим и воспринимаемым предметом. И [еще есть] бесконечный Атман, принимающий все образы, недеятельный. Когда [человек] находит триаду, это Брахман.
VERSE 1.10 - PROGRESSIVE LIBERATION
kṣaraṃ pradhānam amṛtākṣaraṃ haraḥ kṣarātmānāv īśate deva ekaḥ / tasyābhidhyānād yojanāt tattvabhāvād bhūyaś cānte viśvamāyānivṛttiḥ // SvetUp_1.10 // • kṣaram = perishable (nom. sg. n.)
• pradhānam = primary matter, prakṛti (nom. sg. n.)
• amṛta-akṣaram = immortal-imperishable (nom. sg. m.)
• haraḥ = Hara, Śiva (nom. sg. m.)
• kṣara-ātmānau = perishable and soul (acc. dual m.)
• īśate = rules (3rd sg. pres. Ātm. from √īś)
• devaḥ = God (nom. sg. m.)
• ekaḥ = one (nom. sg. m.)
• tasya = His (gen. sg. m.)
• abhidhyānāt = from meditation (abl. sg. n. from abhi-√dhyai)
• yojanāt = from union (abl. sg. n. from √yuj)
• tattva-bhāvāt = from reality-state (abl. sg. m.)
• bhūyaḥ = progressively, further (adv.)
• ca = and
• ante = at the end (loc. sg. m.)
• viśva-māyā-nivṛttiḥ = cessation of universal illusion (nom. sg. f.) 		Matter (pradhānam) is perishable (kṣaram); Hara (haraḥ) is immortal-imperishable (amṛtākṣaram); the one (ekaḥ) God (devaḥ) rules (īśate) over the perishable and the soul (kṣarātmānau). From meditation (abhidhyānāt) on Him (tasya), from union (yojanāt), from reality-state (tattvabhāvāt), progressively (bhūyaḥ) and (ca) at the end (ante)—cessation (nivṛttiḥ) of universal illusion (viśvamāyā). Pradhāna = Sāṃkhya term for primordial matter/prakṛti. Hara = name of Śiva ("the destroyer/taker away"). Dual kṣarātmānau = "the perishable and the soul". Īśate (Ātmanepada) emphasizes the Lord's inherent sovereignty over both matter and individual souls. Three ablatives showing progressive stages: abhidhyānāt (from meditation), yojanāt (from yoga/union), tattvabhāvāt (from abiding in reality). Bhūyaḥ = "increasingly, further". Result: viśva-māyā-nivṛtti = complete cessation of cosmic illusion. Ante = "ultimately, at the end". The verse describes the metaphysical structure and the progressive path to liberation through meditation, union, and abiding in the truth of Śiva. Тленное – прадхана, бессмертное и нетленное – Хара. Тленным и Атманом правит один бог. Размышлением о нем, соединением [с ним], пребыванием в [его] сущности постепенно [прекращается] и в конце исчезает всякое заблуждение.
VERSE 1.11 - THREE STAGES OF LIBERATION
jñātvā devaṃ sarvapāśāpahāniḥ kṣīṇaiḥ kleśair janmamṛtyuprahāṇiḥ / tasyābhidhyānāt tṛtīyaṃ dehabhede viśvaiśvaryaṃ kevala āptakāmaḥ // SvetUp_1.11 // • jñātvā = having known (abs. from √jñā)
• devam = God (acc. sg. m.)
• sarva-pāśa-apahāniḥ = removal of all bonds (nom. sg. f.)
• kṣīṇaiḥ = exhausted (inst. pl. m. PPP from √kṣi)
• kleśaiḥ = with afflictions (inst. pl. m.)
• janma-mṛtyu-prahāṇiḥ = cessation of birth-death (nom. sg. f.)
• tasya = His (gen. sg. m.)
• abhidhyānāt = from meditation (abl. sg. n.)
• tṛtīyam = third (nom. sg. n.)
• deha-bhede = at dissolution of body (loc. sg. m.)
• viśva-aiśvaryam = universal lordship (nom. sg. n.)
• kevalaḥ = alone, absolute (nom. sg. m.)
• āpta-kāmaḥ = with desires fulfilled (nom. sg. m.) 		Having known (jñātvā) God (devam)—removal (apahāniḥ) of all (sarva) bonds (pāśa); with afflictions (kleśaiḥ) exhausted (kṣīṇaiḥ)—cessation (prahāṇiḥ) of birth and death (janmamṛtyu). From meditation (abhidhyānāt) on Him (tasya), thirdly (tṛtīyam), at the dissolution (bhede) of the body (deha)—universal lordship (viśvaiśvaryam); alone (kevalaḥ), with desires fulfilled (āptakāmaḥ). Three stages of liberation: (1) jñātvā devam leads to sarva-pāśa-apahāni (removal of all fetters), (2) kṣīṇaiḥ kleśaiḥ (instrumental absolute = "afflictions being exhausted") leads to janma-mṛtyu-prahāṇi (end of rebirth cycle). Kleśa = mental afflictions, defilements. (3) Third stage: tṛtīya (third result). Deha-bhede (locative) = "at body-dissolution" (death). Result: viśva-aiśvarya = cosmic lordship, kevala = absolute/alone (kaivalya), āpta-kāma = all desires fulfilled. This describes videha-mukti (liberation after death) with divine powers. The verse maps progressive liberation from initial knowledge through purification to ultimate transcendence. Когда познан бог, спадают все узы, с уничтожением страданий исчезают рождение и смерть. Размышлением о нем [достигается] третье [состояние], с распадом плоти – господство над всем, одинокий достигает [исполнения] желаний.
VERSE 1.12 - THE ULTIMATE KNOWLEDGE
etaj jñeyaṃ nityam evātmasaṃsthaṃ nātaḥ paraṃ veditavyaṃ hi kiṃcit / bhoktā bhogyaṃ preritāraṃ ca matvā sarvaṃ proktaṃ trividhaṃ brahmam etat // SvetUp_1.12 // • etat = this (nom. sg. n.)
• jñeyam = to be known (nom. sg. n. FPP from √jñā)
• nityam = eternally (adv.)
• eva = indeed
• ātma-saṃstham = existing in the Self (nom. sg. n.)
• na = not
• ataḥ = from this, beyond this (abl. adv.)
• param = other, beyond (nom. sg. n.)
• veditavyam = to be known (nom. sg. n. FPP from √vid)
• hi = indeed
• kiṃcit = anything (nom. sg. n. indefinite)
• bhoktā = enjoyer (nom. sg. m. agent noun from √bhuj)
• bhogyam = enjoyed (nom. sg. n. FPP from √bhuj)
• preritāram = impeller, mover (acc. sg. m. from pra-√īr)
• ca = and
• matvā = having understood (abs. from √man)
• sarvam = all (nom. sg. n.)
• proktam = declared (nom. sg. n. PPP from pra-√vac)
• trividham = threefold (nom. sg. n.)
• brahma = Brahman (nom. sg. n.)
• etat = this (nom. sg. n.) 		This (etat) is to be known (jñeyam) as eternally (nityam) existing in the Self (ātmasaṃstham); beyond (param) this (ataḥ), indeed (hi), nothing (kiṃcit) else is to be known (veditavyam). Having understood (matvā) the enjoyer (bhoktā), the enjoyed (bhogyam), and (ca) the impeller (preritāram)—all (sarvam) is declared (proktam): this (etat) threefold (trividham) Brahman (brahma). Two FPPs (future passive participles/gerundives): jñeyam, veditavyam = "must be known". Ātma-saṃstha = "established in the Self". Negative statement: na ataḥ param kiṃcit veditavyam = "nothing beyond this needs to be known". This is the complete knowledge. Summarizes the teaching with three principles: bhoktā (experiencer/puruṣa), bhogyam (experienced/prakṛti), preritāra (mover/īśvara). Absolutive matvā shows completion of understanding. Trividha = "threefold". Proktam = "fully declared/taught". This verse concludes the opening chapter's inquiry by declaring the completeness of the threefold knowledge of Brahman as enjoyer, enjoyed, and impeller. Следует узнать это вечное, пребывающее в Атмане; ничего не следует знать, кроме него. В помышлении о воспринимающем, воспринимаемом в Движущем высказано все. Это тройной Брахман.
VERSE 1.13 - FIRE HIDDEN IN WOOD METAPHOR
yathā yonigatasya mūrtir na dṛśyate naiva ca liṅganāśaḥ / sa bhūya evendhanayonigṛhyas tad vobhayaṃ vai praṇavena dehe // SvetUp_1.13 // • yathā = just as, even as (adv.)
• yoni-gatasya = of that which has gone into the womb/source (gen. sg. n.)
• mūrtiḥ = visible form, shape (nom. sg. f.)
• na = not
• dṛśyate = is seen (3rd sg. pres. pass. from √dṛś)
• na eva = nor indeed
• ca = and
• liṅga-nāśaḥ = destruction of the subtle form (nom. sg. m.)
• saḥ = that, it (nom. sg. m.)
• bhūyas = again (adv.)
• eva = indeed
• indhana-yoni-gṛhyaḥ = grasped by its fuel-source (nom. sg. m.)
• tat = that (nom. sg. n.)
• vā = or
• ubhayam = both (nom. sg. n.)
• vai = indeed, truly
• praṇavena = by means of Om (instr. sg. m.)
• dehe = in the body (loc. sg. m.) 		Just as (yathā) the visible form (mūrtiḥ) of that which has gone into its source (yonigatasya) is not (na) seen (dṛśyate), nor indeed (naiva) is there (ca) destruction of its subtle form (liṅganāśaḥ)—that (saḥ) is again (bhūyas) indeed (eva) grasped (gṛhyaḥ) by its fuel-source (indhanayoni); so (tat) both (ubhayam) [visible and subtle] truly (vai) [is realized] by means of Om (praṇavena) in the body (dehe). Fire metaphor begins. When fire returns to its source (wood), the visible form disappears but the subtle form (liṅga) is not destroyed. Passive verb dṛśyate from √dṛś. Compound yoni-gata = "gone into the womb/source". Liṅga-nāśa = destruction of the subtle/characteristic mark. Fire emerges again (bhūyas) when grasped from its source through friction. Similarly, the Self (both gross and subtle aspects - ubhayam) is realized through praṇava (Om) practiced in the body. Compound indhana-yoni = "fuel-source". Instrumental praṇavena = "by means of Om". The verse teaches that the Self, though hidden, is not destroyed and can be revealed through spiritual practice. Как не виден облик огня, скрытого [в своем] источнике, [но] основа [его] не гибнет И он снова добывается в [своем] источнике [с помощью] дерева, – так, поистине, в обоих случаях [постигается Атман] в теле с помощью пранавы.
VERSE 1.14 - THE PRACTICE OF CHURNING
svadeham araṇiṃ kṛtvā praṇavaṃ cottarāraṇim / dhyānanirmathanābhyāsād devaṃ paśyen nigūḍhavat // SvetUp_1.14 // • sva-deham = one's own body (acc. sg. m.)
• araṇim = lower fire-stick (acc. sg. f.)
• kṛtvā = having made (abs. from √kṛ)
• praṇavam = Om (acc. sg. m.)
• ca = and
• uttara-araṇim = upper fire-stick (acc. sg. f.)
• dhyāna-nirmathana-abhyāsāt = from practice of the churning of meditation (abl. sg. m.)
• devam = the deity, the Self (acc. sg. m.)
• paśyet = one should see (3rd sg. opt. from √paś)
• nigūḍha-vat = like something hidden (adv.) 		Having made (kṛtvā) one's own body (svadeham) the lower fire-stick (araṇim), and (ca) Om (praṇavam) the upper fire-stick (uttarāraṇim), from the practice (abhyāsāt) of churning (nirmathana) through meditation (dhyāna), one should see (paśyet) the deity (devam) like (vat) something hidden (nigūḍha). Meditation technique prescribed. Two fire-sticks (araṇi) needed for friction: body is lower stick, Om is upper stick. Absolutive kṛtvā shows sequential action. Compound uttara-araṇi = "upper churning stick". The body-Om combination generates spiritual fire. Triple compound: dhyāna-nirmathana-abhyāsa = "practice of meditation-churning". Optative paśyet = "one should see" (prescription). The Self/God (deva) is hidden like fire in wood, revealed through sustained practice. Ablative shows cause: "from practice". Nigūḍha-vat = "like hidden [fire]". The verse prescribes a specific yogic technique for realizing the hidden divine presence. Сделав свое тело [верхним] арани и пранаву – нижним арани, Усердным трением размышления [человек] увидит бога, подобного скрытому [огню].
VERSE 1.15 - OIL IN SESAME METAPHOR
tileṣu tailaṃ dadhanīva sarpir āpaḥ srotaḥsv araṇīṣu cāgniḥ / evam ātmā ātmani gṛhyate 'sau satyenainaṃ tapasā yo 'nupaśyati // SvetUp_1.15 // • tileṣu = in sesame seeds (loc. pl. m.)
• tailam = oil (nom. sg. n.)
• dadhani = in milk/curds (loc. sg. n.)
• iva = like, as
• sarpiḥ = butter, ghee (nom. sg. n.)
• āpaḥ = water (nom. pl. f.)
• srotaḥsu = in rivers/streams (loc. pl. n.)
• araṇīṣu = in fire-sticks (loc. pl. f.)
• ca = and
• agniḥ = fire (nom. sg. m.)
• evam = thus, in this way (adv.)
• ātmā = the Self (nom. sg. m.)
• ātmani = in the self (loc. sg. m.)
• gṛhyate = is grasped (3rd sg. pres. pass. from √grah)
• asau = that (nom. sg. m.)
• satyena = by truth (instr. sg. n.)
• enam = this (acc. sg. m.)
• tapasā = by austerity (instr. sg. n.)
• yaḥ = who (nom. sg. m.)
• anupaśyati = perceives, sees (3rd sg. pres. from anu-√paś) 		As (iva) oil (tailam) in sesame seeds (tileṣu), butter (sarpiḥ) in milk (dadhani), water (āpaḥ) in streams (srotaḥsu), and (ca) fire (agniḥ) in fire-sticks (araṇīṣu)—thus (evam) that (asau) Self (ātmā) is grasped (gṛhyate) in the self (ātmani) by the one who (yaḥ) perceives (anupaśyati) this (enam) by truth (satyena) and by austerity (tapasā). Four parallel examples of hidden presence: oil pervades seeds, butter pervades milk, water pervades riverbeds, fire pervades wood. All use locative case for "in/within". Series of natural analogies building to the main point about Self in self. Main point: Ātman (capital S Self) exists within ātman (small s self/individual). Passive gṛhyate = "is grasped/comprehended". Two instrumental means: satya (truth/truthfulness) and tapas (ascetic discipline). Relative clause with yaḥ...anupaśyati = "who perceives". The perceiver must use both truth and austerity. The verse uses powerful natural metaphors to convey the immanence of the Supreme Self within each individual being. Как сезамовое масло в зернах сезама, масло – в сливках, вода – в русле реки и огонь – в кусках дерева, Так постигает в самом себе Атмана тот, кто взирает на него в правде и подвижничестве.
VERSE 1.16 - THE ALL-PERVADING SELF
sarvavyāpinam ātmānaṃ kṣīre sarpir ivārpitam / ātmavidyātapomūlaṃ tad brahmopaniṣatparaṃ tad brahmopaniṣatparam // SvetUp_1.16 // • sarva-vyāpinam = all-pervading (acc. sg. m.)
• ātmānam = the Self (acc. sg. m.)
• kṣīre = in milk (loc. sg. n.)
• sarpiḥ = butter (nom. sg. n.)
• iva = like, as
• arpitam = placed, pervading (acc. sg. n.)
• ātma-vidyā-tapas-mūlam = rooted in Self-knowledge and austerity (nom. sg. n.)
• tat = that (nom. sg. n.)
• brahma-upaniṣat-param = that which has Brahman-Upaniṣad as highest (nom. sg. n.)
• [repeated for emphasis] 		The all-pervading (sarvavyāpinam) Self (ātmānam), like (iva) butter (sarpiḥ) pervading (arpitam) in milk (kṣīre), [is realized as] rooted (mūlam) in Self-knowledge (ātmavidyā) and austerity (tapas). That (tat) is the supreme (param) Brahman-teaching (brahmopaniṣat), that is the supreme Brahman-teaching (tad brahmopaniṣatparam). Compound sarva-vyāpin = "all-pervading" (vi-√āp = to pervade completely). Central image: butter pervades all milk, invisible but present everywhere. Arpita can mean "placed" or "pervading". The Self pervades all individual selves like butter in milk. Triple compound: ātma-vidyā-tapas-mūla = "having Self-knowledge and austerity as root". The means are ātma-vidyā (Self-knowledge) and tapas (discipline). Compound brahma-upaniṣat-para = "having Brahman-teaching as supreme". Final phrase repeated (epizeuxis) for solemn emphasis. This concludes Chapter 1's practical teaching with the declaration that this knowledge of the all-pervading Self, rooted in spiritual discipline, constitutes the supreme Upaniṣadic wisdom. [Он постигает] Атмана, проникающего во все, словно масло, находящееся в сливках, – Корень самопознания и подвижничества. Это высшая упанишада Брахмана! Это высшая упанишада Брахмана!
CHAPTER 3 - RUDRA, THE ONE GOD
VERSE 3.1 - THE ONE RULER WITH HIS POWERS
ya eko jālavān īśata īśanībhiḥ sarvāṃl lokān īśata īśanībhiḥ /
ya evaika udbhave saṃbhave ca ya etad vidur amṛtās te bhavanti // SvetUp_3.1 //		 • yaḥ = who (nom. sg. m. relative pronoun)
• ekaḥ = one, alone (nom. sg. m.)
• jālavān = possessing the net (nom. sg. m. from jāla + vat suffix)
• īśate = rules, governs (3rd sg. pres. Ātm. from √īś)
• īśanībhiḥ = with powers, ruling powers (inst. pl. f.)
• sarvān = all (acc. pl. m.)
• lokān = worlds (acc. pl. m.)
• īśate = rules, governs (3rd sg. pres. Ātm. from √īś) [repeated]
• īśanībhiḥ = with powers (inst. pl. f.) [repeated]
• yaḥ = who (nom. sg. m. relative pronoun)
• eva = indeed, alone (emphatic particle)
• ekaḥ = one (nom. sg. m.)
• udbhave = in origination, creation (loc. sg. m.)
• saṃbhave = in dissolution (loc. sg. m.)
• ca = and
• ye = who (nom. pl. m. relative pronoun)
• etat = this (acc. sg. n. demonstrative pronoun)
• viduḥ = know (3rd pl. perf. from √vid)
• amṛtāḥ = immortal (nom. pl. m.)
• te = they (nom. pl. m. pronoun)
• bhavanti = become (3rd pl. pres. from √bhū) 		He who (yaḥ) alone (ekaḥ), possessing the net (jālavān), rules (īśate) all (sarvān) worlds (lokān) with his ruling powers (īśanībhiḥ); who (yaḥ) alone (eva ekaḥ) [presides] over creation (udbhave) and (ca) dissolution (saṃbhave)—those who (ye) know (viduḥ) this (etat) become (bhavanti) immortal (amṛtāḥ). Opens Chapter 3 with the declaration of the One God who rules all worlds through his powers (īśanī). The metaphor of the net (jāla) suggests divine omnipresence and control. Кто познает того, который один, простирает сеть, владеет с помощью сил владычества, владеет всеми мирами с помощью сил владычества, Который один в возникновении и пребывании [мира], – те становятся бессмертными.
VERSE 3.2 - RUDRA, THE ONE WITHOUT A SECOND
eko hi rudro na dvitīyāya tasthe ya imāṃl lokān īśata īśanībhiḥ /
pratyaṅ janās tiṣṭhati saṃcukocāntakāle saṃsṛjya viśvā bhuvanāni gopāḥ // SvetUp_3.2 //		 • ekaḥ = one (nom. sg. m.)
• hi = indeed, for (emphatic particle)
• rudraḥ = Rudra (nom. sg. m.)
• na = not
• dvitīyāya = for a second (dat. sg. m. from dvitīya)
• tasthe = stood, exists (3rd sg. perf. Ātm. from √sthā)
• yaḥ = who (nom. sg. m. relative pronoun)
• imān = these (acc. pl. m. demonstrative pronoun)
• lokān = worlds (acc. pl. m.)
• īśate = rules (3rd sg. pres. Ātm. from √īś)
• īśanībhiḥ = with powers (inst. pl. f.)
• pratyaṅ = facing, opposite (adv.)
• janān = people, creatures (acc. pl. m.)
• tiṣṭhati = stands (3rd sg. pres. from √sthā)
• saṃcukoca = having withdrawn (3rd sg. perf. from sam + √kuc)
• anta-kāle = at the end of time (loc. sg. m. compound)
• saṃsṛjya = having created (abs. from sam + √sṛj)
• viśvā = all (acc. pl. n.)
• bhuvanāni = worlds (acc. pl. n.)
• gopāḥ = protector, guardian (nom. sg. m.) 		Rudra (rudraḥ) is indeed (hi) one (ekaḥ); for a second (dvitīyāya) does not (na) exist (tasthe); he who (yaḥ) rules (īśate) these (imān) worlds (lokān) with his powers (īśanībhiḥ). He stands (tiṣṭhati) facing (pratyaṅ) creatures (janān); the protector (gopāḥ), having created (saṃsṛjya) all (viśvā) worlds (bhuvanāni), withdraws (saṃcukoca) them at the end of time (anta-kāle). Identifies the One God as Rudra. The phrase "na dvitīyāya tasthe" (no second exists) is a strong statement of non-dualism. Rudra is both creator and destroyer. Ибо Рудра, который владеет этими мирами с помощью сил владычества, один – [мудрецы] не держатся второго. Он стоит перед людьми; сотворив все миры, он, пастырь, свертывает [их] в конце времени.
VERSE 3.3 - THE GOD WITH EYES AND FACES EVERYWHERE
viśvataścakṣur uta viśvatomukho viśvatobāhur uta viśvatospāt /
saṃ bāhubhyāṃ dhamati saṃ patatrair dyāvābhūmī janayan deva ekaḥ // SvetUp_3.3 //		 • viśvataḥ-cakṣuḥ = with eyes everywhere (nom. sg. m. bahuvrīhi compound)
• uta = and, also
• viśvataḥ-mukhaḥ = with faces everywhere (nom. sg. m. bahuvrīhi compound)
• viśvataḥ-bāhuḥ = with arms everywhere (nom. sg. m. bahuvrīhi compound)
• uta = and, also
• viśvataḥ-pāt = with feet everywhere (nom. sg. m. bahuvrīhi compound)
• sam = together (prefix)
• bāhubhyām = with arms (inst. dual m.)
• dhamati = forges, blows (3rd sg. pres. from √dham)
• sam = together (prefix)
• patatraiḥ = with wings (inst. pl. n.)
• dyāvā-bhūmī = heaven and earth (acc. dual f. dvandva compound)
• janayan = generating, creating (pres. part. nom. sg. m. from √jan)
• devaḥ = god (nom. sg. m.)
• ekaḥ = one (nom. sg. m.) 		With eyes everywhere (viśvataḥ-cakṣuḥ), and (uta) with faces everywhere (viśvataḥ-mukhaḥ), with arms everywhere (viśvataḥ-bāhuḥ), and (uta) with feet everywhere (viśvataḥ-pāt); forging together (sam dhamati) with arms (bāhubhyām), with wings (patatraiḥ), the one (ekaḥ) God (devaḥ) generates (janayan) heaven and earth (dyāvā-bhūmī). Universal omnipresence of the divine. The God with all-pervasive senses and limbs creates the cosmos through divine power. The phrase "deva ekaḥ" (the one God) emphasizes monotheism. [Имеющий] повсюду глаз и повсюду лицо, повсюду руку и повсюду ногу, Сотворив небо и землю, единый бог выковывает [их] руками и крыльями.
VERSE 3.4 - RUDRA THE ORIGIN OF GODS
yo devānāṃ prabhavaś codbhavaś ca viśvādhipo rudro maharṣiḥ /
hiraṇyagarbhaṃ janayāmāsa pūrvaṃ sa no buddhyā śubhayā saṃyunaktu // SvetUp_3.4 //		 • yaḥ = who (nom. sg. m. relative pronoun)
• devānām = of gods (gen. pl. m.)
• prabhavaḥ = source, origin (nom. sg. m.)
• ca = and
• udbhavaḥ = arising, origination (nom. sg. m.)
• ca = and
• viśva-adhipaḥ = lord of all (nom. sg. m. tatpuruṣa compound)
• rudraḥ = Rudra (nom. sg. m.)
• mahā-ṛṣiḥ = great seer (nom. sg. m. karmadhāraya compound)
• hiraṇya-garbham = golden womb/embryo (acc. sg. m. karmadhāraya compound)
• janayām āsa = generated (3rd sg. periphrastic perf. from √jan)
• pūrvam = in the beginning (adv.)
• saḥ = he (nom. sg. m. pronoun)
• naḥ = us (acc. pl. pronoun)
• buddhyā = with understanding (inst. sg. f.)
• śubhayā = with auspicious (inst. sg. f.)
• saṃyunaktu = may he unite (3rd sg. opt. from sam + √yuj) 		Who (yaḥ) is the source (prabhavaḥ) and (ca) origin (udbhavaḥ) of the gods (devānām), the lord of all (viśva-adhipaḥ), Rudra (rudraḥ) the great seer (mahā-ṛṣiḥ), who in the beginning (pūrvam) generated (janayām āsa) the golden womb (hiraṇya-garbham) - may he (saḥ) unite (saṃyunaktu) us (naḥ) with auspicious (śubhayā) understanding (buddhyā). Rudra identified as primal creator, source of gods and Hiraṇyagarbha. The verse links Rudra with cosmic creation and spiritual wisdom. [Тот], кто повелитель и творец богов, всеобщий владыка, Рудра, великий мудрец, Породивший вначале золотой зародыш, – да наделит он нас [способностью] ясного постижения!
VERSE 3.5 - PRAYER FOR RUDRA'S BENIGN FORM
yā te rudra śivā tanūr aghorāpāpakāśinī /
tayā nas tanuvā śaṃtamayā giriśantābhicākaśīhi // SvetUp_3.5 //		 • yā = which (nom. sg. f. relative pronoun)
• te = your (gen. sg. pronoun)
• rudra = O Rudra (voc. sg. m.)
• śivā = auspicious, benign (nom. sg. f.)
• tanūḥ = form, body (nom. sg. f.)
• aghorā = not terrible (nom. sg. f. from a-ghora)
• apāpa-kāśinī = not showing evil (nom. sg. f. compound from a-pāpa + kāśin)
• tayā = with that (inst. sg. f. demonstrative pronoun)
• naḥ = to us (dat. pl. pronoun)
• tanuvā = with form (inst. sg. f.)
• śaṃtamayā = most benign (inst. sg. f. superlative from śam)
• giri-śanta = O dweller in mountains (voc. sg. m. compound)
• abhicākaśīhi = appear, shine forth (2nd sg. imper. from abhi + √kāś) 		O Rudra (rudra), that (yā) auspicious (śivā) form (tanūḥ) of yours (te) which is not terrible (aghorā), which does not show evil (apāpa-kāśinī) - with that (tayā) most benign (śaṃtamayā) form (tanuvā), O dweller in mountains (giri-śanta), appear (abhicākaśīhi) to us (naḥ). Prayer for Rudra's benevolent aspect. Contrasts the fierce (ghora) with peaceful (śiva) manifestations. Invokes Rudra as Giriśa (mountain-dweller). Твой благодетельный образ, Рудра, не ужасен, не являет зла, – Воззри на нас этим несущим покой образом, обитатель гор!
VERSE 3.6 - APPEAL TO LOWER THE ARROW
yām iṣuṃ giriśanta haste bibharṣy astave /
śivāṃ giritra tāṃ kuru mā hiṃsīḥ puruṣaṃ jagat // SvetUp_3.6 //		 • yām = which (acc. sg. f. relative pronoun)
• iṣum = arrow (acc. sg. f.)
• giri-śanta = O dweller in mountains (voc. sg. m. compound)
• haste = in hand (loc. sg. m./n.)
• bibharṣi = you hold (2nd sg. pres. from √bhṛ)
• astave = for throwing (dat. sg. n. inf. from √as)
• śivām = auspicious, benign (acc. sg. f.)
• giri-tra = O mountain-protector (voc. sg. m. compound)
• tām = that (acc. sg. f. demonstrative pronoun)
• kuru = make (2nd sg. imper. from √kṛ)
• mā = not (prohibitive particle)
• hiṃsīḥ = you should harm (2nd sg. aor. injunctive from √hiṃs)
• puruṣam = person, man (acc. sg. m.)
• jagat = world (acc. sg. n.) 		That (yām) arrow (iṣum) which you hold (bibharṣi) in your hand (haste) to shoot (astave), O dweller in mountains (giri-śanta), make (kuru) that (tām) auspicious (śivām), O protector of mountains (giri-tra). Do not (mā) harm (hiṃsīḥ) person (puruṣam) or world (jagat). Supplication to Rudra to make his weapon benign. Shows dual nature of divine power - can destroy or protect. Emphasis on non-violence toward beings. Стрелу, которую [ты], обитатель гор, держишь в руке, чтобы метнуть [ее], – Сделай ее благодетельной, хранитель гор, не причиняй вреда человеку и животному!
VERSE 3.7 - THE SUPREME BRAHMAN HIDDEN IN ALL
tataḥ paraṃ brahma paraṃ bṛhantaṃ yathānikāyaṃ sarvabhūteṣu gūḍhaṃ /
viśvasyaikaṃ pariveṣṭitāram īśaṃ taṃ jñātvāmṛtā bhavanti // SvetUp_3.7 //		 • tataḥ = than that (abl. adv.)
• param = higher, supreme (acc. sg. n.)
• brahma = Brahman (acc. sg. n.)
• param = supreme (acc. sg. n.)
• bṛhantam = great, vast (acc. sg. n.)
• yathā-nikāyam = according to each body/form (acc. sg. n. adv. compound)
• sarva-bhūteṣu = in all beings (loc. pl. n. tatpuruṣa compound)
• gūḍham = hidden, concealed (acc. sg. n. PPP from √guh)
• viśvasya = of the universe (gen. sg. n.)
• ekam = one (acc. sg. m.)
• pariveṣṭitāram = enveloper, encompasser (acc. sg. m. agent noun from pari + √veṣṭ)
• īśam = lord, ruler (acc. sg. m.)
• tam = him (acc. sg. m. demonstrative pronoun)
• jñātvā = having known (abs. from √jñā)
• amṛtāḥ = immortal (nom. pl. m.)
• bhavanti = become (3rd pl. pres. from √bhū) 		Higher (param) than that (tataḥ) is Brahman (brahma), supreme (param), vast (bṛhantam), hidden (gūḍham) according to form (yathā-nikāyam) in all beings (sarva-bhūteṣu), the one (ekam) enveloper (pariveṣṭitāram) of the universe (viśvasya), the Lord (īśam) - knowing (jñātvā) him (tam), they become (bhavanti) immortal (amṛtāḥ). Describes the transcendent Brahman hidden within all beings. Knowledge of this supreme reality leads to immortality. Key Upaniṣadic teaching on immanence and transcendence. Выше этого – Брахман, высший, великий, скрытый во всех существах, в теле каждого, Единый, объемлющий вселенную, – познав этого владыку, становятся бессмертными.
VERSE 3.8 - THE GREAT PERSON BEYOND DARKNESS
vedāham etaṃ puruṣaṃ mahāntam ādityavarṇaṃ tamasaḥ parastāt /
tam eva viditvāti mṛtyum eti nānyaḥ panthā vidyate 'yanāya // SvetUp_3.8 //		 • veda = I know (1st sg. perf. from √vid)
• aham = I (nom. sg. pronoun)
• etam = this (acc. sg. m. demonstrative pronoun)
• puruṣam = Person (acc. sg. m.)
• mahāntam = great (acc. sg. m.)
• āditya-varṇam = sun-colored, radiant as sun (acc. sg. m. tatpuruṣa compound)
• tamasaḥ = than darkness (abl. sg. n.)
• parastāt = beyond (abl. adv.)
• tam = him (acc. sg. m. demonstrative pronoun)
• eva = indeed (emphatic particle)
• viditvā = having known (abs. from √vid)
• ati = beyond, over (prefix)
• mṛtyum = death (acc. sg. m.)
• eti = goes (3rd sg. pres. from √i)
• na = not
• anyaḥ = other (nom. sg. m.)
• panthāḥ = path, way (nom. sg. m.)
• vidyate = exists, is found (3rd sg. pres. pass. from √vid)
• ayanāya = for going (dat. sg. n. from √i) 		I (aham) know (veda) this (etam) great (mahāntam) Person (puruṣam), sun-colored (āditya-varṇam), beyond (parastāt) darkness (tamasaḥ). Only by knowing (viditvā) him (tam) indeed (eva) does one pass (eti) beyond (ati) death (mṛtyum); there is (vidyate) no (na) other (anyaḥ) path (panthāḥ) to go (ayanāya). Famous verse on liberation through knowledge. The Person is luminous (āditya-varṇa) and transcends ignorance (tamas). Exclusive path to immortality is knowing this reality. Я знаю этого пурушу, великого, цвета солнца, [находящегося] по ту сторону мрака, – Лишь познав его, идет [человек] за пределы смерти; нет иного пути, [которым можно было бы] следовать.
VERSE 3.9 - NOTHING HIGHER OR SMALLER
yasmāt paraṃ nāparam asti kiṃcid yasmān nāṇīyo na jyāyo 'sti kiṃcit /
vṛkṣa iva stabdho divi tiṣṭhaty ekas tenedaṃ pūrṇaṃ puruṣeṇa sarvam // SvetUp_3.9 //		 • yasmāt = than whom (abl. sg. m. relative pronoun)
• param = higher (nom. sg. n. comparative)
• na = not
• aparam = other, lower (nom. sg. n.)
• asti = exists (3rd sg. pres. from √as)
• kiṃcit = anything (nom. sg. n. indefinite pronoun)
• yasmāt = than whom (abl. sg. m. relative pronoun)
• na = not
• aṇīyaḥ = smaller (nom. sg. m. comparative)
• na = not
• jyāyaḥ = greater (nom. sg. m. comparative)
• asti = exists (3rd sg. pres. from √as)
• kiṃcit = anything (nom. sg. n.)
• vṛkṣaḥ = tree (nom. sg. m.)
• iva = like, as
• stabdhaḥ = fixed, standing firm (nom. sg. m. PPP from √stambh)
• divi = in heaven (loc. sg. m.)
• tiṣṭhati = stands (3rd sg. pres. from √sthā)
• ekaḥ = one (nom. sg. m.)
• tena = by that (inst. sg. m. demonstrative pronoun)
• idam = this (nom. sg. n. demonstrative pronoun)
• pūrṇam = filled (nom. sg. n. PPP from √pṛ)
• puruṣeṇa = by the Person (inst. sg. m.)
• sarvam = all (nom. sg. n.) 		Than whom (yasmāt) nothing (kiṃcit) higher (param) or lower (aparam) exists (asti), than whom (yasmāt) nothing (kiṃcit) smaller (aṇīyaḥ) or (na) greater (jyāyaḥ) exists (asti) - like (iva) a tree (vṛkṣaḥ) firmly rooted (stabdhaḥ) he stands (tiṣṭhati) alone (ekaḥ) in heaven (divi); by that (tena) Person (puruṣeṇa) all (sarvam) this (idam) is filled (pūrṇam). Paradox of supreme reality - nothing beyond it, yet encompasses all. Tree metaphor shows stability and cosmic support. All pervaded by the Person. Выше которого ничего нет, меньше или больше которого ничего нет, Единый, он стоит словно древо, утвержденное в небе; этим пурушей наполнен весь этот [мир].
VERSE 3.10 - THE FORMLESS AND FREEDOM FROM AFFLICTION
tato yad uttarataraṃ yad arūpam anāmayam /
ya etad vidur amṛtās te bhavanti athetare duḥkham evāpiyanti // SvetUp_3.10 //		 • tataḥ = than that (abl. adv.)
• yat = which (nom. sg. n. relative pronoun)
• uttarataram = higher, more supreme (nom. sg. n. comparative)
• yat = which (nom. sg. n. relative pronoun)
• arūpam = formless (nom. sg. n. from a-rūpa)
• anāmayam = free from affliction (nom. sg. n. from an-āmaya)
• ye = who (nom. pl. m. relative pronoun)
• etat = this (acc. sg. n. demonstrative pronoun)
• viduḥ = know (3rd pl. perf. from √vid)
• amṛtāḥ = immortal (nom. pl. m.)
• te = they (nom. pl. m. pronoun)
• bhavanti = become (3rd pl. pres. from √bhū)
• atha = but, however (particle)
• itare = others (nom. pl. m.)
• duḥkham = suffering (acc. sg. n.)
• eva = only, alone (emphatic particle)
• āpiyanti = go to, obtain (3rd pl. pres. from ā + √ap) 		Higher (uttarataram) than that (tataḥ), which (yat) is formless (arūpam), free from affliction (anāmayam) - those who (ye) know (viduḥ) this (etat) become (bhavanti) immortal (amṛtāḥ), but (atha) others (itare) go (āpiyanti) only (eva) to suffering (duḥkham). Describes ultimate reality as formless (arūpa) and beyond suffering (anāmaya). Sharp contrast between liberating knowledge and ignorance leading to suffering. Лишено образа, свободно от страдания то, что выше этого [мира]; Те, кто знает это, становятся бессмертными, прочие достигают лишь несчастья.
VERSE 3.11 - THE ALL-PERVADING ŚIVA
sarvānanaśirogrīvaḥ sarvabhūtaguhāśayaḥ /
sarvavyāpī sa bhagavāṃs tasmāt sarvagataḥ śivaḥ // SvetUp_3.11 //		 • sarva-ānana-śiraḥ-grīvaḥ = with all faces, heads, and necks (nom. sg. m. bahuvrīhi compound)
• sarva-bhūta-guhā-śayaḥ = dwelling in the cave of all beings (nom. sg. m. bahuvrīhi compound)
• sarva-vyāpī = all-pervading (nom. sg. m. from sarva + vi-āp)
• saḥ = he (nom. sg. m. pronoun)
• bhagavān = blessed one, lord (nom. sg. m.)
• tasmāt = therefore (abl. adv.)
• sarva-gataḥ = gone everywhere, omnipresent (nom. sg. m. from sarva + gata PPP √gam)
• śivaḥ = Śiva, auspicious one (nom. sg. m.) 		With all faces, heads, and necks (sarva-ānana-śiraḥ-grīvaḥ), dwelling (śayaḥ) in the cave (guhā) of all beings (sarva-bhūta), all-pervading (sarva-vyāpī) is that (saḥ) blessed one (bhagavān) - therefore (tasmāt) Śiva (śivaḥ) is omnipresent (sarva-gataḥ). Cosmic form with universal features. Immanent presence in the heart-cave (guhā) of all beings. First explicit naming of supreme as Śiva in this Upaniṣad. Пребывающий во всех лицах, головах, шеях, в тайнике [сердца] всех существ, Он – всепроникающий, владыка и потому – вездесущий Шива.
VERSE 3.12 - THE GREAT LORD ACTIVATING EXISTENCE
mahān prabhur vai puruṣaḥ sattvasyaiṣa pravartakaḥ /
sunirmalām imāṃ prāptim īśāno jyotir avyayaḥ // SvetUp_3.12 //		 • mahān = great (nom. sg. m.)
• prabhuḥ = lord, master (nom. sg. m.)
• vai = indeed, truly (emphatic particle)
• puruṣaḥ = Person (nom. sg. m.)
• sattvasya = of existence, of purity (gen. sg. n.)
• eṣaḥ = this one (nom. sg. m. demonstrative pronoun)
• pravartakaḥ = activator, instigator (nom. sg. m. from pra + √vṛt)
• su-nirmalām = supremely pure (acc. sg. f. from su + nir-mala)
• imām = this (acc. sg. f. demonstrative pronoun)
• prāptim = attainment, achievement (acc. sg. f.)
• īśānaḥ = ruler, lord (nom. sg. m.)
• jyotiḥ = light (nom. sg. n.)
• avyayaḥ = imperishable (nom. sg. m. from a-vyaya) 		Great (mahān) indeed (vai) is the Lord (prabhuḥ), the Person (puruṣaḥ), this (eṣaḥ) activator (pravartakaḥ) of sattva (sattvasya) (existence/purity); the ruler (īśānaḥ), the imperishable (avyayaḥ) light (jyotiḥ), granting this (imām) supremely pure (su-nirmalām) attainment (prāptim).
