Lord as activator of sattva (pure being/consciousness). Described as imperishable light (jyotir avyaya) and source of spiritual attainment. Поистине, этот пуруша – великий властитель, движущая сила существующего; Незапятнано это достижение [его, он] – владыка, непреходящий свет.
VERSE 3.13 - THE THUMB-SIZED ĀTMAN IN THE HEART
aṅguṣṭhamātraḥ puruṣo 'ntarātmā sadā janānāṃ hṛdaye saṃniviṣṭaḥ /
hṛdā manīṣā manasābhikḷpto ya etad vidur amṛtās te bhavanti // SvetUp_3.13 //		 • aṅguṣṭha-mātraḥ = thumb-sized (nom. sg. m. tatpuruṣa compound)
• puruṣaḥ = Person (nom. sg. m.)
• antaḥ-ātmā = inner self (nom. sg. m. tatpuruṣa compound)
• sadā = always (adv.)
• janānām = of people (gen. pl. m.)
• hṛdaye = in the heart (loc. sg. n.)
• saṃniviṣṭaḥ = seated, dwelling (nom. sg. m. PPP from sam + ni + √viś)
• hṛdā = by the heart (inst. sg. n.)
• manīṣā = through thought, wisdom (inst. sg. f.)
• manasā = by the mind (inst. sg. n.)
• abhikḷptaḥ = perceived, conceived (nom. sg. m. PPP from abhi + √kḷp)
• ye = who (nom. pl. m. relative pronoun)
• etat = this (acc. sg. n. demonstrative pronoun)
• viduḥ = know (3rd pl. perf. from √vid)
• amṛtāḥ = immortal (nom. pl. m.)
• te = they (nom. pl. m. pronoun)
• bhavanti = become (3rd pl. pres. from √bhū) 		The thumb-sized (aṅguṣṭha-mātraḥ) Person (puruṣaḥ), the inner self (antaḥ-ātmā), always (sadā) dwelling (saṃniviṣṭaḥ) in the hearts (hṛdaye) of people (janānām), is perceived (abhikḷptaḥ) by the heart (hṛdā) through thought (manīṣā) and mind (manasā) - those who (ye) know (viduḥ) this (etat) become (bhavanti) immortal (amṛtāḥ). Famous image of thumb-sized (aṅguṣṭha-mātra) Person in the heart. Requires inner perception through refined consciousness. Knowledge leads to immortality. Пуруша, величиной с большой палец, постоянно пребывающий внутри тела, в сердце людей, Постигнут сердцем, мыслью, разумом. Те, кто знает его, становятся бессмертными.
VERSE 3.14 - THE THOUSAND-HEADED PERSON
sahasraśīrṣā puruṣaḥ sahasrākṣaḥ sahasrapāt /
sa bhūmiṃ viśvato vṛtvā atyatiṣṭhad daśāṅgulam // SvetUp_3.14 //		 • sahasra-śīrṣā = thousand-headed (nom. sg. m. bahuvrīhi compound)
• puruṣaḥ = Person (nom. sg. m.)
• sahasra-akṣaḥ = thousand-eyed (nom. sg. m. bahuvrīhi compound)
• sahasra-pāt = thousand-footed (nom. sg. m. bahuvrīhi compound)
• saḥ = he (nom. sg. m. pronoun)
• bhūmim = earth (acc. sg. f.)
• viśvataḥ = on all sides, everywhere (adv.)
• vṛtvā = having enveloped (abs. from √vṛ)
• ati = beyond (prefix)
• atiṣṭhat = stood, exceeded (3rd sg. impf. from ati + √sthā)
• daśa-aṅgulam = ten fingers (acc. sg. n. tatpuruṣa compound) 		The Person (puruṣaḥ) with a thousand heads (sahasra-śīrṣā), a thousand eyes (sahasra-akṣaḥ), a thousand feet (sahasra-pāt) - having enveloped (vṛtvā) the earth (bhūmim) on all sides (viśvataḥ), he (saḥ) stands (atiṣṭhat) ten fingers (daśa-aṅgulam) beyond (ati). Famous opening verse of Puruṣa Sūkta (RV 10.90). Cosmic Person transcends creation by ten fingers (daśa-aṅgula) - both immanent and transcendent. Тысячеголовый пуруша, тысячеглазый, тысяченогий – Окружив всю землю, он простирается на десять пальцев над ней.
VERSE 3.15 - THE PERSON IS ALL THIS
puruṣa evedaṃ sarvaṃ yad bhūtaṃ yac ca bhavyam /
utāmṛtatvasyeśāno yad annenātirohati // SvetUp_3.15 //		 • puruṣaḥ = Person (nom. sg. m.)
• eva = indeed (emphatic particle)
• idam = this (nom. sg. n. demonstrative pronoun)
• sarvam = all (nom. sg. n.)
• yat = what (nom. sg. n. relative pronoun)
• bhūtam = has been, past (nom. sg. n. PPP from √bhū)
• yat = what (nom. sg. n. relative pronoun)
• ca = and
• bhavyam = will be, future (nom. sg. n. FPP from √bhū)
• uta = and also
• amṛtatvasya = of immortality (gen. sg. n.)
• īśānaḥ = lord, ruler (nom. sg. m.)
• yat = which (nom. sg. n. relative pronoun)
• annena = by food (inst. sg. n.)
• atirohati = grows beyond, transcends (3rd sg. pres. from ati + √ruh) 		The Person (puruṣaḥ) is (eva) all (sarvam) this (idam) - what (yat) has been (bhūtam) and (ca) what will be (bhavyam); and (uta) he is the lord (īśānaḥ) of immortality (amṛtatvasya), who (yat) grows beyond (atirohati) through food (annena). Puruṣa as totality of existence - past, present, future. Lord of immortality yet manifests through material sustenance. Paradox of transcendence and immanence. Этот пуруша – все, что было и что будет, А также владыка бессмертия и того, что возрастает благодаря пище.
VERSE 3.16 - HANDS AND FEET EVERYWHERE
sarvataḥpāṇipādaṃ tat sarvato'kṣiśiromukhaṃ /
sarvataḥśrutimal loke sarvam āvṛtya tiṣṭhati // SvetUp_3.16 //		 • sarvataḥ-pāṇi-pādam = with hands and feet everywhere (nom. sg. n. bahuvrīhi compound)
• tat = that (nom. sg. n. demonstrative pronoun)
• sarvataḥ-akṣi-śiraḥ-mukham = with eyes, heads, faces everywhere (nom. sg. n. bahuvrīhi compound)
• sarvataḥ-śruti-mat = having ears everywhere (nom. sg. n. bahuvrīhi compound)
• loke = in the world (loc. sg. m.)
• sarvam = all (acc. sg. n.)
• āvṛtya = having covered, enveloped (abs. from ā + √vṛ)
• tiṣṭhati = stands (3rd sg. pres. from √sthā) 		With hands and feet everywhere (sarvataḥ-pāṇi-pādam), that (tat) with eyes, heads, and faces everywhere (sarvataḥ-akṣi-śiraḥ-mukham), with ears everywhere (sarvataḥ-śruti-mat) in the world (loke) - stands (tiṣṭhati) enveloping (āvṛtya) all (sarvam). Universal form with sensory and motor organs everywhere. Divine omnipresence expressed through physical imagery. Stands enveloping (āvṛtya) all creation. Со всех сторон у него руки и ноги, со всех сторон – глаза, головы, рты, Со всех сторон – уши; он стоит, охватывая все в мире.
VERSE 3.17 - APPEARING THROUGH SENSES YET BEYOND THEM
sarvendriyaguṇābhāsaṃ sarvendriyavivarjitaṃ /
sarvasya prabhum īśānaṃ sarvasya śaraṇaṃ suhṛt // SvetUp_3.17 //		 • sarva-indriya-guṇa-ābhāsam = appearing with all sense qualities (acc. sg. m. tatpuruṣa compound)
• sarva-indriya-vivarjitam = devoid of all senses (acc. sg. m. tatpuruṣa compound)
• sarvasya = of all (gen. sg. n.)
• prabhum = lord, master (acc. sg. m.)
• īśānam = ruler, controller (acc. sg. m.)
• sarvasya = of all (gen. sg. n.)
• śaraṇam = refuge, shelter (acc. sg. n.)
• su-hṛt = friend, well-wisher (acc. sg. m. from su + hṛd) 		Shining through (ābhāsam) all (sarva) sense qualities (indriya-guṇa) yet devoid (vivarjitam) of all senses (sarva-indriya), the lord (prabhum) and ruler (īśānam) of all (sarvasya), the refuge (śaraṇam) of all (sarvasya), the friend (su-hṛt). Paradox: manifests through sensory experience (guṇa-ābhāsa) yet transcends all senses (indriya-vivarjita). Supreme as lord, refuge, and friend of all beings. Отражающий свойства всех чувств, лишенный всех чувств, [Он – ] повелитель, владыка всего, великое убежище всего.
VERSE 3.18 - THE SWAN IN THE NINE-GATED CITY
navadvāre pure dehī haṃso lelāyate bahiḥ /
vaśī sarvasya lokasya sthāvarasya carasya ca // SvetUp_3.18 //		 • nava-dvāre = in the nine-gated (loc. sg. f. karmadhāraya compound)
• pure = in the city (loc. sg. f.)
• dehī = embodied one, soul (nom. sg. m.)
• haṃsaḥ = swan, soul (nom. sg. m.)
• lelāyate = sports, plays (3rd sg. pres. Ātm. from √lī)
• bahiḥ = outside (adv.)
• vaśī = ruler, controller (nom. sg. m.)
• sarvasya = of all (gen. sg. m.)
• lokasya = of the world (gen. sg. m.)
• sthāvarasya = of the stationary (gen. sg. n. from √sthā)
• carasya = of the moving (gen. sg. n. from √car)
• ca = and 		In the city (pure) with nine gates (nava-dvāre), the embodied one (dehī), the swan (haṃsaḥ), sports (lelāyate) without (bahiḥ); the controller (vaśī) of the whole (sarvasya) world (lokasya), of both the stationary (sthāvarasya) and (ca) the moving (carasya). Body as nine-gated city (two eyes, two ears, two nostrils, mouth, genitals, anus). Soul as haṃsa (swan/spirit) playing externally. Yet remains master of all existence. В граде с девятью вратами наделенный телом "лебедь" устремляется наружу – Властитель всего мира, неподвижного и движущегося.
VERSE 3.19 - WITHOUT ORGANS YET ALL-CAPABLE
apāṇipādo javano grahītā paśyaty acakṣuḥ sa śṛṇoty akarṇaḥ /
sa vetti vedyaṃ na ca tasyāsti vettā tam āhur agryaṃ puruṣaṃ mahāntam // SvetUp_3.19 //		 • a-pāṇi-pādaḥ = without hands or feet (nom. sg. m. bahuvrīhi compound)
• javanaḥ = swift, moving quickly (nom. sg. m.)
• grahītā = grasper, seizer (nom. sg. m. agent noun from √grah)
• paśyati = sees (3rd sg. pres. from √paś)
• a-cakṣuḥ = without eyes (nom. sg. m. from a-cakṣus)
• saḥ = he (nom. sg. m. pronoun)
• śṛṇoti = hears (3rd sg. pres. from √śru)
• a-karṇaḥ = without ears (nom. sg. m. from a-karṇa)
• saḥ = he (nom. sg. m. pronoun)
• vetti = knows (3rd sg. pres. from √vid)
• vedyam = what is to be known (acc. sg. n. FPP from √vid)
• na = not
• ca = and
• tasya = of him (gen. sg. m. pronoun)
• asti = exists (3rd sg. pres. from √as)
• vettā = knower (nom. sg. m. agent noun from √vid)
• tam = him (acc. sg. m. demonstrative pronoun)
• āhuḥ = they call (3rd pl. perf. from √ah)
• agryam = foremost, primordial (acc. sg. m.)
• puruṣam = Person (acc. sg. m.)
• mahāntam = great (acc. sg. m.) 		Without hands or feet (a-pāṇi-pādaḥ) yet swift (javanaḥ), grasping (grahītā); seeing (paśyati) without eyes (a-cakṣuḥ), he (saḥ) hearing (śṛṇoti) without ears (a-karṇaḥ); he (saḥ) knows (vetti) what is to be known (vedyam), but (ca) there is (asti) no (na) knower (vettā) of him (tasya) - they call (āhuḥ) him (tam) the first (agryam), the great (mahāntam) Person (puruṣam). Paradoxical nature of supreme consciousness - acts without organs, knows all yet unknowable. The ultimate subject who cannot become object. The primal great Person. Без ног и рук, он быстрый и хватающий: он видит без глаз, слышает без ушей; Он знает то, что следует знать; его же никто не знает. Его зовут первым великим пурушей.
VERSE 3.20 - SMALLER THAN ATOM, GREATER THAN GREAT
aṇor aṇīyān mahato mahīyān ātmā guhāyāṃ nihito 'sya jantoḥ /
tam akratuṃ paśyati vītaśoko dhātuprasādān mahimānam īśam // SvetUp_3.20 //		 • aṇoḥ = than the atom (abl. sg. m. from aṇu)
• aṇīyān = smaller (nom. sg. m. comparative from aṇu)
• mahataḥ = than the great (abl. sg. m. from mahat)
• mahīyān = greater (nom. sg. m. comparative from mahat)
• ātmā = Self (nom. sg. m.)
• guhāyām = in the cave (loc. sg. f.)
• nihitaḥ = placed, hidden (nom. sg. m. PPP from ni + √dhā)
• asya = of this (gen. sg. m. demonstrative pronoun)
• jantoḥ = of the creature (gen. sg. m.)
• tam = him (acc. sg. m. demonstrative pronoun)
• a-kratum = without desire/will (acc. sg. m. from a-kratu)
• paśyati = sees (3rd sg. pres. from √paś)
• vīta-śokaḥ = free from sorrow (nom. sg. m. from vīta + śoka)
• dhātu-prasādāt = by the grace of the creator (abl. sg. m. tatpuruṣa compound)
• mahimānam = greatness, glory (acc. sg. m.)
• īśam = of the lord (acc. sg. m.) 		Smaller (aṇīyān) than the atom (aṇoḥ), greater (mahīyān) than the great (mahataḥ), the self (ātmā) is set (nihitaḥ) in the cave (guhāyām) of this (asya) creature (jantoḥ); the one without desire (a-kratum), free from sorrow (vīta-śokaḥ), sees (paśyati) him (tam) through the creator's grace (dhātu-prasādāt) - the majesty (mahimānam) of the Lord (īśam). Famous paradox of ātman - infinitesimally small yet infinitely great. Hidden in heart-cave. Vision requires divine grace and freedom from desire/sorrow. Меньше малого, больше большого скрыт Атман в сердце этого существа. Его, лишенного стремлений, великого владыку, видит [человек], свободный от печали, благодаря умиротворению чувств.
VERSE 3.21 - THE UNAGING, ANCIENT, ALL-SELF
vedāham etam ajaraṃ purāṇaṃ sarvātmānaṃ sarvagataṃ vibhutvāt /
janmanirodhaṃ pravadanti yasya brahmavādino hi pravadanti nityam // SvetUp_3.21 //		 • veda = I know (1st sg. perf. from √vid)
• aham = I (nom. sg. pronoun)
• etam = this (acc. sg. m. demonstrative pronoun)
• ajaram = unaging, immortal (acc. sg. m. from a-jara)
• purāṇam = ancient, primeval (acc. sg. m.)
• sarva-ātmānam = self of all (acc. sg. m. tatpuruṣa compound)
• sarva-gatam = gone everywhere, omnipresent (acc. sg. m.)
• vibhutvāt = from pervasiveness (abl. sg. n. from vi-bhū)
• janma-nirodham = cessation of birth (acc. sg. m. tatpuruṣa compound)
• pravadanti = proclaim, declare (3rd pl. pres. from pra + √vad)
• yasya = of whom (gen. sg. m. relative pronoun)
• brahma-vādinaḥ = knowers of Brahman (nom. pl. m. tatpuruṣa compound)
• hi = indeed, for (emphatic particle)
• pravadanti = proclaim (3rd pl. pres. from pra + √vad)
• nityam = eternal (acc. sg. m.) 		I (aham) know (veda) this (etam) unaging (ajaram), ancient one (purāṇam), the self of all (sarva-ātmānam), all-pervading (sarva-gatam) through omnipresence (vibhutvāt); of whom (yasya) the knowers of Brahman (brahma-vādinaḥ) declare (pravadanti) indeed (hi) there is cessation of birth (janma-nirodham), whom they declare (pravadanti) eternal (nityam). Ultimate reality as unaging (ajara), ancient (purāṇa), self of all (sarva-ātman). Knowledge of this ends the cycle of birth. Eternal nature proclaimed by Brahman-knowers. Я знаю этого нестарящегося, изначального, всеобщего Атмана, вошедшего во все благодаря [своей] вездесущности, [Которого] называют прекращением рождений, к которому рассуждающие о Брахмане обращаются как к вечному.

✓ Śvetāśvatara Upaniṣad 1.1-16 & 3.1-21 Complete
Focus: From Philosophical Inquiry to Knowledge of the Supreme Reality

Pātañjalayogaśāstram — Vyāsa's Yogasūtrabhāṣya

Sūtra-by-Sūtra Translation with Linguistic Analysis

Sanskrit Text Word-by-Word Analysis Literal Translation Notes Clean Translation
Maṅgalaśloka

yas tyaktvā rūpam ādyaṃ prabhavati jagato 'nekadhānugrahāya
prakṣīṇakleśarāśir viṣamaviṣadharo 'nekavaktraḥ subhogī /
sarvajñānaprasūtir bhujagaparikaraḥ prītaye yasya nityaṃ
devo 'hīśaḥ sa vo 'vyāt sitavimalatanur yogado yogayuktaḥ //1//

De-sandhi:
yaḥ tyaktvā rūpam ādyam prabhavati jagataḥ anekadhā anugrahāya
prakṣīṇa-kleśa-rāśiḥ viṣama-viṣa-dharaḥ aneka-vaktraḥ su-bhogī /
sarva-jñāna-prasūtiḥ bhujaga-parikaraḥ prītaye yasya nityam
devaḥ ahīśaḥ saḥ vaḥ avyāt sita-vimala-tanuḥ yoga-daḥ yoga-yuktaḥ //		 yaḥ = he who (m. nom. sg., rel. pron.)
tyaktvā = having abandoned (abs. √tyaj)
rūpam = form (n. acc. sg.)
ādyam = primordial, first (n. acc. sg., adj.)
prabhavati = manifests, comes forth (3sg. pres. pra + √bhū)
jagataḥ = of the world (n. gen. sg.)
anekadhā = in many ways (indecl.)
anugrahāya = for the sake of grace (m. dat. sg.)
prakṣīṇa-kleśa-rāśiḥ = one whose heap of afflictions is destroyed (m. nom. sg., bahuvrīhi: pra-kṣīṇa PPP pra + √kṣi; kleśa = affliction; rāśi = heap)
viṣama-viṣa-dharaḥ = bearer of terrible poison (m. nom. sg., tatpuruṣa: viṣama = terrible; viṣa = poison; dhara from √dhṛ)
aneka-vaktraḥ = having many mouths (m. nom. sg., bahuvrīhi)
su-bhogī = having beautiful coils (m. nom. sg., bahuvrīhi: su = beautiful; bhoga = coil of a serpent)
sarva-jñāna-prasūtiḥ = source of all knowledge (m. nom. sg., tatpuruṣa)
bhujaga-parikaraḥ = having serpents as retinue (m. nom. sg., bahuvrīhi: bhujaga = serpent; parikara = retinue)
prītaye = for the delight (f. dat. sg., from prīti)
yasya = of whom (m. gen. sg., rel. pron.)
nityam = always (indecl.)
devaḥ = god (m. nom. sg.)
ahīśaḥ = lord of serpents (m. nom. sg., tatpuruṣa: ahi = serpent; īśa = lord)
saḥ = he, that one (m. nom. sg., dem.)
vaḥ = you (acc. pl., enclitic)
avyāt = may he protect (3sg. opt./precative √av)
sita-vimala-tanuḥ = one whose body is white and pure (m. nom. sg., bahuvrīhi)
yoga-daḥ = bestower of yoga (m. nom. sg., tatpuruṣa: yoga + da from √dā)
yoga-yuktaḥ = united with yoga (m. nom. sg., tatpuruṣa)		 He who (yaḥ), having abandoned (tyaktvā) his primordial form (rūpam ādyam), manifests (prabhavati) for the manifold (anekadhā) grace (anugrahāya) of the world (jagataḥ) — whose mass of afflictions is destroyed (prakṣīṇa-kleśa-rāśiḥ), who bears terrible poison (viṣama-viṣa-dharaḥ), who has many mouths (aneka-vaktraḥ), whose coils are beautiful (su-bhogī), who is the source of all knowledge (sarva-jñāna-prasūtiḥ), who has serpents as his retinue (bhujaga-parikaraḥ) — for whose delight (prītaye yasya) always (nityam) — may that god (devaḥ), the Lord of Serpents (ahīśaḥ), protect (avyāt) you (vaḥ), he whose body is white and pure (sita-vimala-tanuḥ), the bestower of yoga (yoga-daḥ), united with yoga (yoga-yuktaḥ). Invocation to Ādiśeṣa / Patañjali

Syntax: Main clause: saḥ devaḥ ahīśaḥ vaḥ avyāt. yaḥ…prabhavati = relative clause. Nominative bahuvrīhis (prakṣīṇakleśarāśiḥ, viṣamaviṣadharaḥ, etc.) all modify devaḥ as appositional epithets. prītaye yasya nityam = relative dative of purpose (referent likely Viṣṇu, on whom Śeṣa reclines).

Metre: Sragdharā — 21 syllables per pāda.

Context: Vyāsa's invocatory verse to Ādiśeṣa, the primordial serpent who incarnated as Patañjali. He is simultaneously the serpent deity (bearing poison, possessing many hoods, having beautiful coils) and the yogic teacher (source of all knowledge, bestower of yoga, freed from kleśas). The word ahīśa = direct epithet of Śeṣa. prakṣīṇakleśarāśiḥ anticipates the YS programme of kleśa-destruction.		 He who, having abandoned his primordial form, manifests for the manifold grace of the world — whose mass of afflictions is destroyed, who bears terrible poison, who has many mouths, whose coils are beautiful, who is the source of all knowledge, who has serpents as his retinue — for whose delight always — may that god, the Lord of Serpents, protect you, he whose body is white and pure, the bestower of yoga, united with yoga.
Sanskrit Text Word-by-Word Analysis Literal Translation Notes Clean Translation
Sūtra YS 1.1
atha yogānuśāsanam || YS_1.1 ||
De-sandhi:
atha yoga-anuśāsanam ||		 atha = now, here begins (indecl.; an auspicious particle marking the commencement of a śāstra; also implies adhikāra — the formal opening of a subject)
yoga = yoga, union, concentration; here specifically = samādhi (m. stem in cpd.)
anuśāsanam = teaching, instruction, exposition (n. nom. sg.; anu + √śās — lit. "instructing after/following [a tradition]"; implies a systematic re-statement of a received teaching, not a new invention)		 Now (atha), the teaching (anuśāsanam) of yoga (yoga). Meaning: This deceptively simple sūtra packs several layers. Atha signals that the student has fulfilled the prerequisites (adhikāra) and is now qualified to receive instruction. Anuśāsanam (anu + śāsanam) indicates that this is a re-teaching, a systematic exposition of an already-existing tradition — yoga is not being invented here, it is being codified. Vyāsa glosses yogaḥ samādhiḥ — yoga, in this śāstra, means samādhi specifically. The entire Yogasūtra is thus framed from the first word as a practical manual for attaining samādhi.

Syntax: A nominal sentence (no verb). Yogānuśāsanam is a tatpuruṣa compound (ṣaṣṭhī-tatpuruṣa: "teaching of yoga"). Atha stands outside the compound as an introductory particle. The sentence functions as a title-statement: "Here commences the teaching of yoga."

Metre: Prose (sūtra form).		 Now, the teaching of yoga.
Vyāsabhāṣya on YS 1.1
[1] athety ayam adhikārārthaḥ. atha iti = "[the word] atha" — quotative particle iti marks the cited word
ayam = this (m. nom. sg., demonstrative)
adhikāra-arthaḥ = having the meaning of adhikāra, i.e. "commencement" (m. nom. sg., tatpuruṣa cpd.: adhikāra = jurisdiction, commencement of a topic; artha = meaning)		 The word atha here has the sense of adhikāra (commencement of a subject). The word *atha* here has the sense of *adhikāra* (commencement of a subject).
[2] yogānuśāsanaṃ śāstram adhikṛtaṃ veditavyam. yoga-anuśāsanam = the instruction of yoga (n. nom. sg., tatpuruṣa cpd.)
śāstram = the śāstra, teaching-text (n. nom. sg.)
adhikṛtam = commenced, undertaken as the subject (n. nom. sg., PPP adhi + √kṛ)
veditavyam = to be understood, should be known (n. nom. sg., FPP √vid)		 The śāstra on yoga-instruction is what has been commenced and is to be understood. The śāstra on yoga-instruction is what has been commenced and is to be understood.
[3] yogaḥ samādhiḥ. yogaḥ = yoga (m. nom. sg.)
samādhiḥ = samādhi, concentration, absorption (m. nom. sg.; sam + ā + √dhā — lit. "placing together completely")		 Yoga is samādhi. This is arguably the single most consequential definition in the bhāṣya. Vyāsa equates yoga directly with samādhi — not with the ethical precepts, not with āsana, not with prāṇāyāma, but with the cognitive state of absorption itself. Everything else in the system serves this. Yoga is samādhi.
[4] sa ca sārvabhaumaś cittasya dharmaḥ. saḥ = that, he (m. nom. sg.)
ca = and (indecl.)
sārvabhaumaḥ = universal, belonging to all levels (m. nom. sg.; sarva-bhūmi + -a — "pertaining to all grounds")
cittasya = of the mind (n. gen. sg.)
dharmaḥ = property, attribute, characteristic (m. nom. sg.)		 And that [samādhi] is a universal property of the mind. A remarkable claim: samādhi is not reserved for advanced yogins. It is an inherent, natural property of the mind (citta) at every level of its functioning. The mind always has some degree of absorption — the question is what quality and what object that absorption takes. And that [samādhi] is a universal property of the mind.
[5] kṣiptaṃ mūḍhaṃ vikṣiptam ekāgraṃ niruddham iti cittabhūmayaḥ. kṣiptam = scattered, agitated (n. nom. sg., PPP √kṣip)
mūḍham = stupefied, deluded (n. nom. sg., PPP √muh)
vikṣiptam = distracted, intermittently focused (n. nom. sg., PPP vi + √kṣip)
ekāgram = one-pointed, focused (n. nom. sg., bahuvrīhi cpd.: eka = one; agra = point)
niruddham = restrained, fully ceased (n. nom. sg., PPP ni + √rudh)
iti = thus (quotative, closes the list)
citta-bhūmayaḥ = the levels/grounds of the mind (f. nom. pl., tatpuruṣa cpd.: citta = mind; bhūmi = ground, level)		 Scattered, stupefied, distracted, one-pointed, and restrained — these are the levels of the mind. The five cittabhūmi form a hierarchy from rajasic agitation (kṣipta) through tamasic stupor (mūḍha), to the mixed state of distraction (vikṣipta), and upward to sattvic one-pointedness (ekāgra) and complete cessation (niruddha). Only the last two belong to yoga proper. Scattered, stupefied, distracted, one-pointed, and restrained — these are the levels of the mind.
[6] tatra vikṣipte cetasi vikṣepopasarjanībhūtaḥ samādhir na yogapakṣe vartate. tatra = there, in that context (indecl.)
vikṣipte = in the distracted (n. loc. sg., PPP vi + √kṣip; locative absolute)
cetasi = in the mind (n. loc. sg.)
vikṣepa-upasarjanī-bhūtaḥ = having become subordinate to distraction (m. nom. sg., cpd.: vikṣepa = distraction; upasarjanī = subordinate element; bhūta = having become, PPP √bhū)
samādhiḥ = samādhi (m. nom. sg.)
na = not (indecl.)
yoga-pakṣe = in the domain/category of yoga (m. loc. sg., tatpuruṣa cpd.: pakṣa = side, category)
vartate = exists, occurs, belongs (3rd sg. pres. ātm. √vṛt)		 In the case of the distracted mind, samādhi that has become subordinate to distraction does not belong to the domain of yoga. Even the distracted mind experiences moments of absorption — one can be briefly focused amidst distraction. Vyāsa clarifies that such incidental samādhi, where concentration is a mere servant (upasarjanī) of the dominant distraction, does not qualify as yoga. The concentration must be primary, not accidental. In the case of the distracted mind, samādhi that has become subordinate to distraction does not belong to the domain of yoga.
[7] yas tv ekāgre cetasi sadbhūtam arthaṃ pradyotayati kṣiṇoti ca kleśān karmabandhanāni ślathayati nirodham abhimukhaṃ karoti sa saṃprajñāto yoga ity ākhyāyate. yaḥ = which, that which (m. nom. sg., relative pronoun)
tu = but, however (indecl.)
ekāgre = in the one-pointed (n. loc. sg.)
cetasi = in the mind (n. loc. sg.)
sat-bhūtam = real, truly existent (n. acc. sg., cpd.: sat = real; bhūta = being)
artham = object, meaning (m. acc. sg.)
pradyotayati = illuminates, makes shine forth (3rd sg. pres. caus. pra + √dyut)
kṣiṇoti = diminishes, wears away (3rd sg. pres. √kṣi)
ca = and (indecl.)
kleśān = the afflictions (m. acc. pl.; the five kleśas: avidyā, asmitā, rāga, dveṣa, abhiniveśa)
karma-bandhanāni = the bonds of karma (n. acc. pl., tatpuruṣa cpd.)
ślathayati = loosens, slackens (3rd sg. pres. caus. √ślath)
nirodham = cessation, restraint (m. acc. sg., ni + √rudh)
abhimukham = facing towards, directed towards (adv./adj.)
karoti = makes, renders (3rd sg. pres. √kṛ)
saḥ = that (m. nom. sg., correlative of yaḥ)
saṃprajñātaḥ = "with full knowing," cognitive [samādhi] (m. nom. sg., PPP sam + pra + √jñā)
yogaḥ = yoga (m. nom. sg.)
iti = thus (quotative)
ākhyāyate = is called, is designated (3rd sg. pres. pass. ā + √khyā)		 But that [samādhi] which, in the one-pointed mind, illuminates a real object, diminishes the afflictions, loosens the bonds of karma, and turns towards cessation — that is called saṃprajñāta yoga. Vyāsa gives a fourfold functional definition of saṃprajñāta yoga: (1) it illuminates real objects, (2) it wears away the kleśas, (3) it loosens karmic bonds, and (4) it inclines the mind towards nirodha. This is yoga as a self-liquidating process — it accomplishes its work and then turns towards its own cessation. But that [samādhi] which, in the one-pointed mind, illuminates a real object, diminishes the afflictions, loosens the bonds of karma, and turns towards cessation — that is called saṃprajñāta yoga.
[8] sa ca vitarkānugato vicārānugata ānandānugato 'smitānugata ity upariṣṭān nivedayiṣyāmaḥ. saḥ = that (m. nom. sg.)
ca = and (indecl.)
vitarka-anugataḥ = accompanied by gross deliberation (m. nom. sg., tatpuruṣa cpd.: vitarka = deliberation; anugata = followed by, PPP anu + √gam)
vicāra-anugataḥ = accompanied by subtle reflection (m. nom. sg.)
ānanda-anugataḥ = accompanied by bliss (m. nom. sg.)
asmitā-anugataḥ = accompanied by the sense of pure I-ness (m. nom. sg.)
iti = thus (quotative)
upariṣṭāt = further on, later (indecl.)
nivedayiṣyāmaḥ = we shall explain, we shall communicate (1st pl. fut. caus. ni + √vid)		 And that [saṃprajñāta yoga] is accompanied by vitarka, vicāra, ānanda, and asmitā — we shall explain this further on. The four anugata stages correspond to progressively subtler objects of absorption. Vitarka involves gross conceptual engagement with the object; vicāra is subtle, non-discursive reflection; ānanda is absorption in bliss itself; asmitā is absorption in the bare sense of "I am." Each stage leaves behind the coarser engagement of the previous one. Vyāsa references YS 1.17 (savitarkā savicārā sānandā sāsmitā rūpānugamāt saṃprajñātaḥ). And that [saṃprajñāta yoga] is accompanied by vitarka, vicāra, ānanda, and asmitā — we shall explain this further on.
[9] sarvavṛttinirodhe tv asaṃprajñātaḥ samādhiḥ. sarva-vṛtti-nirodhe = in the cessation of all mental modifications (m. loc. sg., tatpuruṣa cpd.: sarva = all; vṛtti = modification, fluctuation, √vṛt; nirodha = cessation, restraint)
tu = but, however (indecl.)
asaṃprajñātaḥ = "without full knowing," non-cognitive, objectless (m. nom. sg.; a + sam + pra + √jñā)
samādhiḥ = samādhi, absorption (m. nom. sg.)		 But when all mental modifications cease, that is asaṃprajñāta samādhi. This is the telos of the entire system. When even the subtle objects of saṃprajñāta are relinquished and all vṛttis whatsoever cease, what remains is asaṃprajñāta samādhi — objectless, contentless absorption. This directly glosses the definition in YS 1.2: yogaś cittavṛttinirodhaḥ. Vyāsa thus links sūtra 1.1 forward to sūtra 1.2, showing that the "yoga" being taught is precisely this cessation. But when all mental modifications cease, that is asaṃprajñāta samādhi.

Summary of the Architecture

Vyāsa's bhāṣya on this single sūtra establishes the entire conceptual framework of the Yogasūtra:

Concept Definition
Yoga = samādhi
Samādhi = a universal property of the mind (sārvabhauma cittadharma)
Five cittabhūmi kṣipta, mūḍha, vikṣipta, ekāgra, niruddha
What is excluded Samādhi subordinate to distraction (vikṣipta level)
Saṃprajñāta yoga Illuminates real objects, diminishes kleśas, loosens karmic bonds, turns towards nirodha
Four stages vitarka, vicāra, ānanda, asmitā
Asaṃprajñāta samādhi Cessation of all vṛttis — the goal

---

---
Sanskrit Text Word-by-Word Analysis Literal Translation Notes Clean Translation
Vyāsa's Introductory Line to YS 1.2
tasya lakṣaṇābhidhitsayedaṃ sūtraṃ pravavṛte —
De-sandhi:
tasya lakṣaṇa-abhidhitsayā idam sūtram pravavṛte —		 tasya = of that [yoga] (m. gen. sg.)
lakṣaṇa = definition, characteristic mark (n. stem in cpd.)
abhidhitsayā = with the desire to state, with the intention of expressing (f. inst. sg.; desiderative abstract noun from abhi + √dhā — "wishing to place forth, wishing to declare")
idam = this (n. nom. sg.)
sūtram = sūtra, aphorism (n. nom. sg.)
pravavṛte = has been set forth, has commenced (3rd sg. perf. ātm. pra + √vṛt)		 With the desire to state (abhidhitsayā) the definition (lakṣaṇa) of that [yoga] (tasya), this (idam) sūtra (sūtram) has been set forth (pravavṛte). Meaning: Vyāsa signals that sūtra 1.1 introduced the subject (yoga) and now sūtra 1.2 provides its formal definition (lakṣaṇa). The desiderative noun abhidhitsā (from abhi + √dhā) conveys deliberate intention — Patañjali composed this sūtra specifically to define what yoga is.

Syntax: Lakṣaṇābhidhitsayā is an instrumental of cause/purpose (a single tatpuruṣa compound: "by the desire-to-state-the-definition"). Pravavṛte is a perfect middle — the sūtra "set itself forth," i.e. arose organically from the need to define.		 With the desire to state the definition of that [yoga], this sūtra has been set forth.
Sūtra YS 1.2
yogaś cittavṛttinirodhaḥ || YS_1.2 ||
De-sandhi:
yogaḥ citta-vṛtti-nirodhaḥ ||		 yogaḥ = yoga (m. nom. sg.)
citta = mind, consciousness-field (n. stem in cpd.; from √cit — "to perceive, to be conscious"; citta encompasses buddhi, ahaṃkāra, and manas)
vṛtti = modification, fluctuation, turning (f. stem in cpd.; from √vṛt — "to turn, to revolve"; the dynamic movements/patternings of the mind)
nirodhaḥ = cessation, restraint, suppression (m. nom. sg.; ni + √rudh — "to block, to hold back"; a complete blocking or stilling)		 Yoga (yogaḥ) is the cessation (nirodhaḥ) of the modifications (vṛtti) of the mind (citta). Meaning: This is the most famous definition in all of yoga literature. Yoga is defined not as a practice, a posture, or a philosophy, but as a state — the state in which the mind's habitual fluctuations have ceased. The compound cittavṛttinirodhaḥ is a ṣaṣṭhī-tatpuruṣa chain: nirodha of vṛttis of citta. Crucially, as Vyāsa will immediately explain, the absence of the word sarva ("all") means this definition encompasses both partial cessation (saṃprajñāta) and total cessation (asaṃprajñāta). Yoga is thus a spectrum, not a single absolute state.

Syntax: A nominal sentence (no verb), functioning as a formal definition (lakṣaṇa). The predicate cittavṛttinirodhaḥ defines the subject yogaḥ. The compound unpacks as: nirodhaḥ [vṛttīnām [cittasya]] — "the restraint [of the modifications [of the mind]]."

Metre: Prose (sūtra form).		 Yoga is the cessation of the modifications of the mind.
Vyāsabhāṣya on YS 1.2
[1] sarvaśabdāgrahaṇāt saṃprajñāto 'pi yoga ity ākhyāyate. sarva-śabda-agrahaṇāt = because of the non-use of the word "all" (n. abl. sg., tatpuruṣa cpd.: sarva = all; śabda = word; agrahaṇa = non-inclusion, non-employment; ablative of reason)
saṃprajñātaḥ = the cognitive [samādhi] (m. nom. sg., PPP sam + pra + √jñā)
api = also, even (indecl.)
yogaḥ = yoga (m. nom. sg.)
iti = thus (quotative)
ākhyāyate = is called, is designated (3rd sg. pres. pass. ā + √khyā)		 Because the word "all" is not used [in the sūtra], even saṃprajñāta is called yoga. A brilliant piece of exegetical logic. Had the sūtra read sarva-citta-vṛtti-nirodhaḥ ("cessation of ALL modifications"), only asaṃprajñāta would qualify as yoga. The deliberate absence of sarva means that even partial cessation — where some refined vṛttis remain — counts as yoga. This is Vyāsa reading Patañjali's silence as meaningful. Because the word "all" is not used [in the sūtra], even saṃprajñāta is called yoga.
[2] cittaṃ hi prakhyāpravṛttisthitiśīlatvāt triguṇam. cittam = the mind (n. nom. sg.)
hi = for, because (indecl., causal particle)
prakhyā-pravṛtti-sthiti-śīlatvāt = because of having the nature of illumination, activity, and stability (n. abl. sg., compound: prakhyā = illumination [= sattva]; pravṛtti = activity [= rajas]; sthiti = stability, inertia [= tamas]; śīlatva = having the character/nature of)
tri-guṇam = three-guṇa-ed, composed of three guṇas (n. nom. sg.)		 For the mind is constituted by the three guṇas, because its nature is illumination, activity, and stability. Vyāsa grounds the discussion in Sāṃkhya metaphysics. The mind is not a simple substance — it is a product of prakṛti, composed of all three guṇas in varying proportions. What follows is a remarkable four-stage analysis of how the mind's sattva-component behaves depending on which guṇa dominates. For the mind is constituted by the three guṇas, because its nature is illumination, activity, and stability.
[3] prakhyārūpaṃ hi cittasattvaṃ rajastamobhyāṃ saṃsṛṣṭam aiśvaryaviṣayapriyaṃ bhavati. prakhyā-rūpam = whose form is illumination (n. nom. sg., bahuvrīhi cpd.)
hi = for (indecl.)
citta-sattvam = the sattva-aspect of the mind (n. nom. sg., tatpuruṣa cpd.)
rajas-tamobhyām = with rajas and tamas (n. inst. du.)
saṃsṛṣṭam = mixed, combined (n. nom. sg., PPP sam + √sṛj)
aiśvarya-viṣaya-priyam = fond of sovereignty and sense-objects (n. nom. sg., compound: aiśvarya = lordly power, supernatural powers; viṣaya = sense-objects; priya = fond of, attracted to)
bhavati = becomes (3rd sg. pres. √bhū)		 For the sattva of the mind, whose form is illumination, when mixed with rajas and tamas, becomes fond of sovereignty and sense-objects. This is the vikṣipta state described in 1.1's bhāṣya. The mind's luminous sattva is present but contaminated by both rajas and tamas. The result: the mind seeks worldly power (aiśvarya — the siddhis, political power, social dominance) and sensory pleasure (viṣaya). The sattva gives enough clarity to pursue these effectively, but the rajasic-tamasic admixture ensures the pursuit is outward, not inward. For the sattva of the mind, whose form is illumination, when mixed with rajas and tamas, becomes fond of sovereignty and sense-objects.
[4] tad eva tamasānuviddham adharmājñānāvairāgyānaiśvaryopagaṃ bhavati. tad = that (n. nom. sg.)
eva = very, same (indecl., emphatic)
tamasā = by tamas (n. inst. sg.)
anuviddham = pervaded, shot through (n. nom. sg., PPP anu + √vyadh — lit. "pierced along")
adharma-ajñāna-avairāgya-anaiśvarya-upagam = inclined towards adharma, ignorance, non-dispassion, and lack of sovereignty (n. nom. sg., compound: adharma = unrighteousness; ajñāna = ignorance; avairāgya = non-detachment, worldly attachment; anaiśvarya = lack of mastery; upaga = inclined towards, approaching, from upa + √gam)
bhavati = becomes (3rd sg. pres. √bhū)		 That same [sattva], pervaded by tamas, becomes inclined towards adharma, ignorance, non-dispassion, and lack of sovereignty. When tamas dominates, sattva is overwhelmed. The result is a four-fold inversion: dharma becomes adharma, jñāna becomes ajñāna, vairāgya becomes avairāgya, aiśvarya becomes anaiśvarya. These four correspond exactly (in inverted form) to the four positive qualities that appear when sattva is purified (sentence 5). Vyāsa is building a systematic schema of the mind's possible states, organised around four cardinal qualities and their negations. That same [sattva], pervaded by tamas, becomes inclined towards adharma, ignorance, non-dispassion, and lack of sovereignty.
[5] tad eva prakṣīṇamohāvaraṇaṃ sarvataḥ pradyotamānam anuviddhaṃ rajomātrayā dharmajñānavairāgyaiśvaryopagaṃ bhavati. tad eva = that same (n. nom. sg.)
prakṣīṇa-moha-āvaraṇam = whose veil of delusion is destroyed (n. nom. sg., bahuvrīhi cpd.: prakṣīṇa = fully destroyed, PPP pra + √kṣi; moha = delusion; āvaraṇa = covering, veil)
sarvataḥ = in all directions, from every side (indecl.)
pradyotamānam = shining forth, becoming luminous (n. nom. sg., pres. mid. part. pra + √dyut)
anuviddham = pervaded (n. nom. sg., PPP anu + √vyadh)
rajaḥ-mātrayā = by a mere trace of rajas (f. inst. sg.; rajas + mātrā = a small measure, a trace)
dharma-jñāna-vairāgya-aiśvarya-upagam = inclined towards dharma, knowledge, dispassion, and sovereignty (n. nom. sg., compound)
bhavati = becomes (3rd sg. pres. √bhū)		 That same [sattva], whose veil of delusion is destroyed, shining forth in all directions, pervaded by a mere trace of rajas, becomes inclined towards dharma, knowledge, dispassion, and sovereignty. Here the sattva shines almost unobstructed — delusion's covering has been destroyed. A mere trace (mātrā) of rajas remains, providing just enough dynamism for the mind to function. This is the ekāgra bhūmi described in 1.1's bhāṣya: the mind is one-pointed, luminous, and oriented towards the four positive qualities (dharma, jñāna, vairāgya, aiśvarya). This corresponds to the stage of saṃprajñāta samādhi. The four qualities are the classical "fruits" of yogic practice — righteousness, wisdom, detachment, and mastery. That same [sattva], whose veil of delusion is destroyed, shining forth in all directions, pervaded by a mere trace of rajas, becomes inclined towards dharma, knowledge, dispassion, and sovereignty.
[6] tad eva rajoleśamalāpetaṃ svarūpapratiṣṭhaṃ sattvapuruṣānyatākhyātimātraṃ dharmameghadhyānopagaṃ bhavati. tad eva = that same (n. nom. sg.)
rajaḥ-leśa-mala-apetam = free from the stain of even a trace of rajas (n. nom. sg., bahuvrīhi cpd.: rajas = rajas; leśa = trace, fragment; mala = stain, impurity; apeta = gone away, freed from, PPP apa + √i)
sva-rūpa-pratiṣṭham = established in its own nature (n. nom. sg., tatpuruṣa cpd.: sva-rūpa = own form/nature; pratiṣṭha = established, grounded)
sattva-puruṣa-anyatā-khyāti-mātram = consisting merely of the discernment of the otherness between sattva and puruṣa (n. nom. sg., compound: sattva = sattva-guṇa/buddhi; puruṣa = the Self, consciousness; anyatā = otherness, distinctness; khyāti = discernment, seeing; mātra = mere, consisting only of)
dharma-megha-dhyāna-upagam = inclined towards dharmamegha meditation (n. nom. sg., compound: dharma-megha = "cloud of dharma," the highest meditative state; dhyāna = meditation; upaga = inclined towards)
bhavati = becomes (3rd sg. pres. √bhū)		 That same [sattva], free from the stain of even a trace of rajas, established in its own nature, consisting merely of the discernment of the otherness between sattva and puruṣa, becomes inclined towards dharmamegha meditation. The pinnacle of the four stages. Every last trace of rajas has departed. The mind (sattva/buddhi) is now so transparent that its sole remaining function is to see the difference between itself and puruṣa. This is vivekakhyāti — discriminative discernment — the ultimate refinement of buddhi. It is called dharmameghadhyāna (the "cloud of dharma" meditation, cf. YS 4.29), where dharma "rains" upon the yogin. Crucially, even this supremely refined state still belongs to prakṛti and must ultimately be transcended. That same [sattva], free from the stain of even a trace of rajas, established in its own nature, consisting merely of the discernment of the otherness between sattva and puruṣa, becomes inclined towards dharmamegha meditation.
[7] tat paraṃ prasaṃkhyānam ity ācakṣate dhyāyinaḥ. tat = that (n. acc. sg.)
param = supreme, highest (n. acc. sg.)
prasaṃkhyānam = meditative discernment, thorough enumeration (n. acc. sg.; pra + sam + √khyā — "complete seeing-through, thorough counting-up" — the culminating insight of Sāṃkhya-Yoga)
iti = thus (quotative)
ācakṣate = they call, they declare (3rd pl. pres. ātm. ā + √cakṣ)
dhyāyinaḥ = meditators, contemplatives (m. nom. pl.; from dhyāyin, one who meditates)		 Meditators call that the supreme meditative discernment. Prasaṃkhyāna is a technical term — the final, complete discernment where buddhi sees everything for what it is. The attribution to dhyāyinaḥ (meditators) grounds this in contemplative tradition rather than mere theory. Meditators call that the supreme meditative discernment.
[8] citiśaktir apariṇāminy apratisaṃkramā darśitaviṣayā śuddhā cānantā ca sattvaguṇātmikā ceyam ato viparītā vivekakhyātir iti. citi-śaktiḥ = the power of pure consciousness (f. nom. sg., tatpuruṣa cpd.: citi = consciousness, awareness; śakti = power — this is puruṣa, the seer)
apariṇāminī = unchanging, not subject to transformation (f. nom. sg.; a + pariṇāmin — unlike prakṛti, puruṣa never transforms)
apratisaṃkramā = non-transitive, not passing over to objects (f. nom. sg.; a + prati + sam + √kram — consciousness does not "go out" to meet objects)
darśita-viṣayā = one to whom objects are [merely] shown (f. nom. sg., bahuvrīhi cpd.: darśita = shown, displayed, PPP caus. √dṛś; viṣaya = object — the objects are brought to puruṣa by buddhi; puruṣa does not reach out)
śuddhā = pure (f. nom. sg.)
ca = and (indecl.)
anantā = infinite, unlimited (f. nom. sg.)
ca = and
sattva-guṇa-ātmikā = having sattva-guṇa as its essence (f. nom. sg.)
ca = and
iyam = this (f. nom. sg., referring to vivekakhyāti)
ataḥ = from that, therefore (indecl.)
viparītā = opposite, contrary (f. nom. sg.)
viveka-khyātiḥ = discriminative discernment (f. nom. sg., tatpuruṣa cpd.: viveka = discrimination; khyāti = seeing, discernment)
iti = thus (quotative)		 The power of pure consciousness is unchanging, non-transitive, [merely] has objects shown to it, is pure, and infinite. But this discriminative discernment, being of the nature of sattva-guṇa, is the opposite of that. A pivotal contrast. Vyāsa lists five attributes of citiśakti (puruṣa): unchanging, non-transitive, passively receiving objects, pure, infinite. Then he observes that vivekakhyāti — however refined — is viparītā (opposite) because it belongs to sattva-guṇa, which is a product of prakṛti and therefore changing, transitive, active, limited. Even the most luminous buddhi is still not puruṣa. This sets up the final act: even vivekakhyāti must be let go. The power of pure consciousness is unchanging, non-transitive, [merely] has objects shown to it, is pure, and infinite. But this discriminative discernment, being of the nature of sattva-guṇa, is the opposite of that.
[9] atas tasyāṃ viraktaṃ cittaṃ tām api khyātiṃ niruṇaddhi. ataḥ = therefore (indecl.)
tasyām = in/towards that [vivekakhyāti] (f. loc. sg.)
viraktam = dispassionate, detached (n. nom. sg., PPP vi + √rañj — "become un-coloured, disenchanted")
cittam = the mind (n. nom. sg.)
tām = that (f. acc. sg.)
api = even (indecl.)
khyātim = discernment (f. acc. sg.)
niruṇaddhi = restrains, blocks, holds back (3rd sg. pres. act. ni + √rudh)		 Therefore, the mind, having become dispassionate towards that [discernment], restrains even that discernment. The supreme paradox of the Yoga system. The mind, having cultivated vivekakhyāti to its highest pitch, must now become dispassionate towards that very discernment and restrain even it. The tool that brought the yogin to the threshold of liberation must itself be relinquished. This is paravairāgya — the highest dispassion (cf. YS 1.16), directed not at worldly objects but at the most refined product of the mind itself. Niruṇaddhi — the same root as nirodha in the sūtra — brings us full circle. Therefore, the mind, having become dispassionate towards that [discernment], restrains even that discernment.
[10] tadavasthaṃ saṃskāropagaṃ bhavati. tad-avastham = in that state, having that condition (n. nom. sg., bahuvrīhi cpd.: tad = that; avasthā = state, condition)
saṃskāra-upagam = inclined towards saṃskāras, tending towards latent impressions (n. nom. sg., tatpuruṣa cpd.: saṃskāra = latent impression, karmic residue; upaga = inclined towards)
bhavati = becomes (3rd sg. pres. √bhū)		 In that state, [the mind] becomes inclined towards [residual] saṃskāras only. When even vivekakhyāti ceases, the mind does not vanish — it subsides into its saṃskāric substrate. The saṃskāras of nirodha themselves gradually overwrite all other saṃskāras (cf. YS 1.50: tajjaḥ saṃskāro 'nyasaṃskārapratibandhī). The mind is, so to speak, "running on fumes" — only latent impressions remain, no active cognition. In that state, [the mind] becomes inclined towards [residual] saṃskāras only.
[11] sa nirbījaḥ samādhiḥ. saḥ = that (m. nom. sg.)
nirbījaḥ = seedless, without seed (m. nom. sg.; niḥ + bīja — no seed for future mental activity remains)
samādhiḥ = samādhi, absorption (m. nom. sg.)		 That is seedless samādhi. Nirbīja ("seedless") means that no latent cause of further cognitive arising remains. This is the ultimate goal — a samādhi that does not plant the seeds of its own dissolution. Contrast with sabīja samādhi (YS 1.46), where refined saṃskāras still operate. That is seedless samādhi.
[12] na tatra kiṃcit saṃprajñāyata ity asaṃprajñātaḥ. na = not (indecl.)
tatra = there, in that [state] (indecl.)
kiṃcit = anything, anything whatsoever (n. nom. sg., indefinite pronoun)
saṃprajñāyate = is cognised, is fully known (3rd sg. pres. pass. sam + pra + √jñā)
iti = therefore, thus (indecl.)
asaṃprajñātaḥ = the non-cognitive, the without-full-knowing [samādhi] (m. nom. sg.)		 Nothing whatsoever is cognised there — therefore [it is called] asaṃprajñāta. Vyāsa provides an elegant etymological gloss: asaṃprajñāta literally means "that in which nothing is saṃprajñāta (fully cognised)." This is not unconsciousness — it is consciousness without any object, awareness beyond all knowing. The etymology itself defines the state. Nothing whatsoever is cognised there — therefore [it is called] asaṃprajñāta.
[13] dvividhaḥ sa yogaś cittavṛttinirodha iti. dvividhaḥ = twofold, of two kinds (m. nom. sg.; dvi = two; vidha = kind)
saḥ = that (m. nom. sg.)
yogaḥ = yoga (m. nom. sg.)
citta-vṛtti-nirodhaḥ = the cessation of mental modifications (m. nom. sg.)
iti = thus (quotative, closing the commentary on this sūtra)		 That yoga, the cessation of mental modifications, is twofold. The conclusion brings the entire bhāṣya full circle. Yoga = cittavṛttinirodha, and that nirodha is twofold: saṃprajñāta (with refined cognition, partial cessation) and asaṃprajñāta (without any cognition, total cessation). The twofold division organises the entire first pāda of the Yogasūtra.

## Vyāsa's Four Stages of Sattva

The bhāṣya's most distinctive contribution is a systematic four-stage model of how the mind's sattva-component behaves depending on guṇa-dominance:

| Stage | Guṇa Mixture | Orientation | Corresponding Bhūmi | |-------|-------------|-------------|---------------------| | 1 | Sattva + rajas + tamas equally | Fond of sovereignty and sense-objects (aiśvarya-viṣaya-priya) | vikṣipta (distracted) | | 2 | Sattva dominated by tamas | Inclined towards adharma, ignorance, non-dispassion, lack of mastery | mūḍha (stupefied) | | 3 | Sattva shining, mere trace of rajas | Inclined towards dharma, knowledge, dispassion, mastery | ekāgra (one-pointed) | | 4 | Sattva free from all rajas | Discernment of sattva-puruṣa distinction only; dharmamegha | niruddha (restrained) |

The four positive qualities — dharma, jñāna, vairāgya, aiśvarya — and their four negations form a symmetrical axis around which the entire model turns.

## The Final Movement

Beyond even Stage 4:

Vivekakhyāti (Stage 4) → recognised as still belonging to sattva-guṇa, therefore viparītā (opposite) to puruṣa → paravairāgya (dispassion towards discernment itself) → nirodha of khyāti → mind subsides into saṃskāras only → nirbīja samādhi → nothing cognised = asaṃprajñāta.

This is the architecture of liberation according to Vyāsa: the mind refines itself to the point of seeing its own distinctness from puruṣa, then lets go of even that seeing.		 That yoga, the cessation of mental modifications, is twofold.
Sanskrit Text Word-by-Word Analysis Literal Translation Notes Clean Translation
Vyāsa's Introductory Line to YS 1.3
tadavasthe cetasi viṣayābhāvād buddhibodhātmā puruṣaḥ kiṃsvabhāva iti —
De-sandhi:
tad-avasthe cetasi viṣaya-abhāvāt buddhi-bodha-ātmā puruṣaḥ kim-svabhāvaḥ iti —		 tad-avasthe = in that state, having that condition (n. loc. sg., bahuvrīhi cpd.: tad = that [nirodha]; avasthā = state; locative absolute)
cetasi = in the mind (n. loc. sg.)
viṣaya-abhāvāt = due to the absence of objects (m. abl. sg., tatpuruṣa cpd.: viṣaya = object; abhāva = absence; ablative of cause)
buddhi-bodha-ātmā = whose essence is the awareness of buddhi (m. nom. sg., bahuvrīhi cpd.: buddhi = intellect; bodha = awareness, cognition; ātman = self, essence)
puruṣaḥ = puruṣa, the Self, the seer (m. nom. sg.)
kim-svabhāvaḥ = of what nature? (m. nom. sg., bahuvrīhi cpd.: kim = what; svabhāva = own nature)
iti = thus (quotative, marking the question)		 When the mind (cetasi) is in that state [of nirodha] (tad-avasthe), in the absence of objects (viṣaya-abhāvāt), what is the nature (kim-svabhāvaḥ) of puruṣa (puruṣaḥ), whose essence is the awareness of buddhi (buddhi-bodha-ātmā)? Meaning: Vyāsa poses the question that sūtra 1.3 will answer. The bhāṣya on 1.2 concluded with asaṃprajñāta samādhi — the cessation of all vṛttis. The natural question arises: if puruṣa's experience is always mediated through buddhi's awareness, what happens to puruṣa when buddhi has no objects? Does puruṣa become "nothing"? The compound buddhibodhātmā is itself philosophically loaded — it frames puruṣa provisionally as "one whose self is the awareness-of-buddhi," precisely the identification that yoga aims to dissolve.

Syntax: Locative absolute construction (tadavasthe cetasi — "the mind being in that state"). The question is structured as a bahuvrīhi predication: puruṣaḥ kim-svabhāvaḥ = "puruṣa is of-what-nature?"		 When the mind is in that state, in the absence of objects, what is the nature of puruṣa, whose essence is the awareness of buddhi?
Sūtra YS 1.3
tadā draṣṭuḥ svarūpe 'vasthānam || YS_1.3 ||
De-sandhi:
tadā draṣṭuḥ sva-rūpe avasthānam ||		 tadā = then, at that time (indecl.; correlative to yogaś cittavṛttinirodhaḥ — "when yoga [= nirodha] obtains")
draṣṭuḥ = of the seer (m. gen. sg.; draṣṭṛ, agent noun from √dṛś — "one who sees"; a technical designation for puruṣa throughout the Yogasūtra)
sva-rūpe = in [its] own form, in [its] own nature (n. loc. sg.; sva = own; rūpa = form, nature)
avasthānam = abiding, resting, establishment (n. nom. sg.; ava + √sthā — "standing in, settling into")		 Then (tadā), [there is] the abiding (avasthānam) of the seer (draṣṭuḥ) in [its] own form (sva-rūpe). Meaning: This is the positive answer to the question posed in the introductory line. When nirodha is achieved, puruṣa does not become nothing — it abides in its own form. The word svarūpe is crucial: puruṣa has a nature of its own, independent of any mental content. The seer is not constituted by what it sees. In nirodha, the seer simply is what it always was — pure awareness, no longer coloured by the mind's fluctuations. Avasthānam suggests not an achievement of something new, but a settling-back into what was always already the case.

Syntax: A nominal sentence (no finite verb). Draṣṭuḥ is a genitive of possession/reference ("the abiding belonging to the seer"). Tadā links back to sūtra 1.2 — "when cittavṛttinirodha obtains, then this."

Metre: Prose (sūtra form).		 Then the seer abides in its own form.
Vyāsabhāṣya on YS 1.3
[1] svarūpapratiṣṭhā tadānīṃ citiśaktir yathā kaivalye. sva-rūpa-pratiṣṭhā = established in its own form (f. nom. sg., tatpuruṣa cpd.: sva-rūpa = own form; pratiṣṭhā = established, grounded)
tadānīm = at that time, then (indecl.)
citi-śaktiḥ = the power of consciousness (f. nom. sg., tatpuruṣa cpd.: citi = pure awareness; śakti = power — Vyāsa's term for puruṣa qua awareness)
yathā = just as, as (indecl.)
kaivalye = in kaivalya, in the state of absolute isolation (n. loc. sg.; kaivalya = aloneness, the final liberation of puruṣa from prakṛti; from kevala = alone)		 At that time, the power of consciousness is established in its own form, just as in kaivalya. A startling equation: the state of nirodha-samādhi is experientially identical to kaivalya, the ultimate liberation described in Yogasūtra 4.34. In both cases, citiśakti rests in its own nature. The difference is ontological: in nirodha, the saṃskāric seeds still exist (the mind can re-emerge); in kaivalya, the separation is permanent. The simile yathā kaivalye ("just as in kaivalya") means the yogin in nirodha gets a "preview" of final liberation. At that time, the power of consciousness is established in its own form, just as in kaivalya.
[2] vyutthānacitte tu sati tathāpi bhavantī na tathā. vyutthāna-citte = when the mind is in emergence, in the ordinary outward-turned state (n. loc. sg., locative absolute; vyutthāna = rising up, emergence from samādhi, the ordinary waking state; citta = mind)
tu = but, however (indecl.)
sati = being, existing (m./n. loc. sg., pres. part. √as; completes the locative absolute: "the mind being in vyutthāna")
tathā = in that way, thus (indecl.)
api = even, although (indecl.)
bhavantī = being, existing (f. nom. sg., pres. part. √bhū; agrees with citiśaktiḥ — "she [citiśakti] being thus")
na = not (indecl.)
tathā = in that way (indecl.)		 But when the mind is in the state of emergence, even though [citiśakti] exists in that way, it is not [experienced as] in that way. This is one of the most philosophically consequential sentences in the entire bhāṣya. Vyāsa states that puruṣa's nature never actually changes. Even during ordinary experience (vyutthāna), citiśakti IS established in its own form — it always is. The difference is epistemic, not ontological: in vyutthāna, puruṣa is not experienced as being in its own form, because the mind's modifications create the appearance of identification. Nirodha does not create puruṣa's self-abiding — it reveals what was always already the case by removing the obscuration. This is the metaphysical foundation of the entire Yoga project: liberation is not an achievement but an uncovering. But when the mind is in the state of emergence, even though [citiśakti] exists in that way, it is not [experienced as] in that way.
Sanskrit Text Word-by-Word Analysis Literal Translation Notes Clean Translation
Vyāsa's Introductory Line to YS 1.4
kathaṃ tarhi, darśitaviṣayatvāt —
De-sandhi:
katham tarhi, darśita-viṣayatvāt —		 katham = how? in what way? (indecl., interrogative)
tarhi = then, in that case (indecl.)
darśita-viṣayatvāt = because of having objects shown to it, because of the condition of being one to whom objects are displayed (n. abl. sg.; darśita = shown, displayed, PPP caus. √dṛś; viṣaya = object; -tva = abstract suffix "the state of"; ablative of cause)		 How then? Because [citiśakti] has the nature of having objects shown to it (darśita-viṣayatvāt) — Meaning: Vyāsa answers his own rhetorical question from the bhāṣya on 1.3. Why, during vyutthāna, does puruṣa not experience its own nature? Because objects are constantly being "shown" to it by the mind. The passive construction darśitaviṣayatvāt is important — puruṣa does not actively reach for objects; rather, buddhi displays them before puruṣa. The mechanism of bondage is this display: as long as objects are being paraded before awareness, awareness appears to take the form of those objects. Sūtra 1.4 will articulate this. How then [is it not experienced as such]? Because objects are shown to it —
Sūtra YS 1.4
vṛttisārūpyam itaratra || YS_1.4 ||
De-sandhi:
vṛtti-sārūpyam itaratra ||		 vṛtti = modification, fluctuation (f. stem in cpd.; from √vṛt)
sārūpyam = conformity, having the same form, identification with (n. nom. sg.; sa + rūpya, from sa + rūpa + -ya — "the state of having the same form as")
itaratra = at other times, otherwise, in the other case (indecl.; itara = other; -tra = locative suffix — "in the other [state]," i.e. when nirodha is not present)		 At other times (itaratra), [there is] conformity with the modifications (vṛtti-sārūpyam). Meaning: This is the negative counterpart to 1.3. Sūtra 1.3 described what happens when there is nirodha (the seer abides in its own form); sūtra 1.4 describes what happens when there is not nirodha (the seer appears to conform to the mind's modifications). Together, 1.3 and 1.4 form a complementary pair. Sārūpya (from sa-rūpa, "same-form") is the key concept: it is not that puruṣa actually becomes the vṛttis, but that it appears to have their form. This is avidyā in action — the superimposition of the seen upon the seer. The word itaratra ("at other times") implies that vṛttisārūpya is the default state; svarūpe'vasthānam is the exception that yoga makes possible.

Syntax: A nominal sentence (no verb). Itaratra is the temporal-locative adverb ("in the other case" — contrasting with tadā in 1.3). The implied subject is draṣṭuḥ (the seer) carried forward from 1.3.

Metre: Prose (sūtra form).		 At other times, [the seer appears to take on] the form of the modifications.
Vyāsabhāṣya on YS 1.4
[1] vyutthāne yāś cittavṛttayas tadaviśiṣṭavṛttiḥ puruṣaḥ. vyutthāne = in emergence, in the ordinary outward-turned state (n. loc. sg.; vi + ud + √sthā — "standing up and out," the mind's extroverted condition)
yāḥ = whatever, which (f. nom. pl., relative pronoun)
citta-vṛttayaḥ = the modifications of the mind (f. nom. pl.)
tad-aviśiṣṭa-vṛttiḥ = having a modification indistinguishable from them (m. nom. sg., bahuvrīhi cpd.: tad = them, those; aviśiṣṭa = not-distinguished, indistinguishable, identical; vṛtti = modification)
puruṣaḥ = puruṣa (m. nom. sg.)		 In the state of emergence, whatever the modifications of the mind, puruṣa [appears to have] a modification indistinguishable from them. When the mind thinks "I am angry," puruṣa appears to be angry. When the mind thinks "I see blue," puruṣa appears to see blue. Whatever form the mind's vṛtti takes, puruṣa appears to have an aviśiṣṭa (indistinguishable) modification. The word aviśiṣṭa is carefully chosen — it does not say puruṣa has the same vṛtti, only that it appears indistinguishable from it. The distinction between actual identity and apparent identity is the entire basis of Yoga soteriology. In the state of emergence, whatever the modifications of the mind, puruṣa [appears to have] a modification indistinguishable from them.
[2] tathā ca sūtram ekam eva darśanaṃ khyātir eva darśanam iti. tathā = thus, in this way (indecl.)
ca = and (indecl.)
sūtram = the sūtra [states] (n. nom. sg.; refers to another text or a later passage)
ekam = one, singular (n. nom. sg.)
eva = indeed, alone (indecl., emphatic)
darśanam = seeing, vision (n. nom. sg.; from √dṛś)
khyātiḥ = discernment, knowledge (f. nom. sg.; from √khyā — "to be known, to appear")
eva = indeed, alone (indecl.)
darśanam = seeing (n. nom. sg.)
iti = thus (quotative)		 And thus the sūtra [states]: "Seeing is one indeed; discernment itself is seeing." Vyāsa cites what appears to be a lost sūtra or an alternative textual tradition. The citation contains two propositions: (a) ekam eva darśanam — "seeing is singular" — there is ultimately only one seeing, which is puruṣa; (b) khyātir eva darśanam — "discernment itself is [what constitutes] seeing" — what we call "seeing" is really buddhi's discernment (khyāti) being reflected in puruṣa's awareness. The two together express the Yoga position: puruṣa is the sole seer, but the content of seeing is always provided by buddhi. This anticipates YS 4.22 (citer apratisamkramāyās tadākārāpattau svabuddhisaṃvedanam). And thus the sūtra [states]: "Seeing is one indeed; discernment itself is seeing."
[3] cittam ayaskāntamaṇikalpaṃ saṃnidhimātropakāri dṛśyatvena svaṃ bhavati puruṣasya svāminaḥ. cittam = the mind (n. nom. sg.)
ayaskānta-maṇi-kalpam = like a lodestone, resembling a magnet (n. nom. sg., compound: ayaskānta = magnet, lit. "iron-lover" [ayas = iron; kānta = loved, attracted, from √kam]; maṇi = jewel, gem; kalpa = resembling, like)
saṃnidhi-mātra-upakāri = serving by its mere proximity alone (n. nom. sg., compound: saṃnidhi = proximity, nearness; mātra = mere; upakārin = serving, being useful, from upa + √kṛ)
dṛśyatvena = by virtue of being an object of seeing, by its visibility (n. inst. sg.; dṛśya = to be seen, the seeable; -tva = abstract suffix; instrumental of reason)
svam = [its] own, property (n. nom. sg.; sva = own — here functioning as "belonging, property")
bhavati = becomes (3rd sg. pres. √bhū)
puruṣasya = of puruṣa (m. gen. sg.)
svāminaḥ = the master, the owner (m. gen. sg.; svāmin, from sva + -min)		 The mind, like a lodestone, serving [puruṣa] by its mere proximity, becomes the property of puruṣa, the master, by virtue of being an object of seeing. A magnificent simile. A lodestone (ayaskāntamaṇi) attracts iron without intention, without movement, simply by its proximity. Similarly, the mind serves puruṣa not through any action on puruṣa's part, but simply because puruṣa is near — because citiśakti is there. The mind, being dṛśya (seeable, an object), naturally orients itself towards the seer. The legal metaphor of svāmin (master/owner) and sva (property) frames the puruṣa-prakṛti relationship: the mind belongs to puruṣa as a servant belongs to a master, yet the master does nothing to compel this service. The relationship is ontological, not volitional. The mind, like a lodestone, serving [puruṣa] by its mere proximity, becomes the property of puruṣa, the master, by virtue of being an object of seeing.
[4] tasmāc cittavṛttibodhe puruṣasyānādiḥ saṃbandho hetuḥ. tasmāt = therefore (indecl., ablative of tad, used as a conclusive particle)
citta-vṛtti-bodhe = in the awareness/cognition of the mind's modifications (m. loc. sg., tatpuruṣa cpd.: citta = mind; vṛtti = modification; bodha = awareness, waking knowledge)
puruṣasya = of puruṣa (m. gen. sg.)
anādiḥ = beginningless, without beginning (m. nom. sg.; an + ādi)
saṃbandhaḥ = connection, relation (m. nom. sg.; sam + √bandh — "bound together")
hetuḥ = cause, reason (m. nom. sg.)		 Therefore, the beginningless connection of puruṣa is the cause of the awareness of the mind's modifications. The concluding sentence of the bhāṣya on 1.4 introduces a concept of immense philosophical weight: the connection (saṃbandha) between puruṣa and citta is anādi — beginningless. It has no first moment, no original act of "falling into" matter. This is not a temporal claim about infinite past duration; it is a structural claim: the relation between seer and seen is foundational to the cosmos and cannot be traced to an origin. This is why liberation requires the special effort of yoga — one cannot simply "remember" a prior state of freedom, because there is no such prior state. The anādi saṃbandha also explains why mere intellectual understanding of puruṣa's separateness is insufficient: the bond is deeper than thought, woven into the very fabric of experience. Only the sustained practice of nirodha can untangle it. Therefore, the beginningless connection of puruṣa is the cause of the awareness of the mind's modifications.

Architecture of YS 1.2–1.4

The three sūtras form a tightly interlocking triad:

Sūtra Condition State of the Seer
1.2 yogaś cittavṛttinirodhaḥ Definition: yoga IS the cessation of vṛttis
1.3 tadā — when nirodha obtains The seer abides in its own form (svarūpe 'vasthānam)
1.4 itaratra — at all other times The seer conforms to the modifications (vṛttisārūpyam)

Vyāsa's bhāṣya adds three crucial clarifications:

1. Puruṣa never actually changes. Even during vyutthāna, citiśakti is established in its own form (tathāpi bhavantī). The difference is epistemic, not ontological.

2. The mind is like a lodestone. It serves puruṣa by mere proximity, without puruṣa doing anything. The relationship is one of natural orientation, not volition.

3. The connection is beginningless. The anādi saṃbandha between puruṣa and citta cannot be traced to an origin. This is why liberation requires sustained practice, not mere intellectual insight.

---

---
Sanskrit Text Word-by-Word Analysis Literal Translation Notes Clean Translation
Vyāsa's Introductory Line to YS 1.5
tāḥ punar niroddhavyā bahutve sati cittasya —
De-sandhi:
tāḥ punaḥ niroddhavyāḥ bahutve sati cittasya —		 tāḥ = those [modifications, vṛttis] (f. nom. pl., demonstrative pronoun, referring back to vṛttis from 1.4)
punaḥ = moreover, further, again (indecl.; transitions to the next topic)
niroddhavyāḥ = are to be restrained, must be restrained (f. nom. pl., FPP ni + √rudh — future passive participle expressing obligation; the same root as nirodha in sūtra 1.2)
bahutve = in the manifoldness, when there is multiplicity (n. loc. sg.; bahutva = manifoldness, from bahu = many; locative absolute)
sati = being, existing (n. loc. sg., pres. part. √as; completes the locative absolute: "manifoldness being [the case]")
cittasya = of the mind (n. gen. sg.)		 Those [modifications] (tāḥ), moreover (punaḥ), are to be restrained (niroddhavyāḥ) — the mind (cittasya) being manifold (bahutve sati) — Meaning: Vyāsa signals the transition from the fundamental framework (sūtras 1.2–1.4) to the classification of vṛttis. The word niroddhavyāḥ ("must be restrained") is a gerundive — it expresses obligation. This is no longer a descriptive statement about what yoga is; it is a prescriptive one about what must be done. The word bahutve ("the mind being manifold") explains why a classification is needed: the mind has many forms of activity, and the yogin must know what exactly needs restraining. You cannot restrain what you have not first identified.

Syntax: Locative absolute (bahutve sati cittasya — "the manifoldness of the mind being [the case]"), giving the reason why classification is needed. Tāḥ picks up the vṛttis from the preceding discussion.		 Those modifications, moreover, are to be restrained — the mind being manifold —
Sūtra YS 1.5
vṛttayaḥ pañcatayyaḥ kliṣṭākliṣṭāḥ || YS_1.5 ||
De-sandhi:
vṛttayaḥ pañcatayyaḥ kliṣṭa-akliṣṭāḥ ||		 vṛttayaḥ = the modifications (f. nom. pl.; from √vṛt — "turnings" of the mind)
pañcatayyaḥ = fivefold, forming a group of five (f. nom. pl.; pañcataya, from pañca = five + -taya suffix indicating "forming a group of," cf. dvitaya, tritaya)
kliṣṭa = afflicted, painful, tainted by kleśas (PPP √kliś — "to torment, to afflict")
akliṣṭāḥ = non-afflicted, non-painful (f. nom. pl.; a + kliṣṭa)		 The modifications (vṛttayaḥ) are fivefold (pañcatayyaḥ), afflicted and non-afflicted (kliṣṭa-akliṣṭāḥ). Meaning: This sūtra makes two classifications simultaneously. First, a quantitative one: there are exactly five types of vṛtti (listed in sūtra 1.6). Second, a qualitative one: each of those five can be either kliṣṭa (afflicted — rooted in kleśas, productive of karmic accumulation) or akliṣṭa (non-afflicted — oriented towards discriminative knowledge, opposing the guṇas' dominion). The dvandva compound kliṣṭākliṣṭāḥ indicates that these are two categories applicable to all five types. Every single vṛtti — whether it be valid knowledge, error, imagination, sleep, or memory — can be either afflicted or non-afflicted depending on its relationship to the kleśas.

Syntax: A nominal sentence. Pañcatayyaḥ is a predicate adjective modifying vṛttayaḥ. Kliṣṭākliṣṭāḥ is a dvandva compound functioning as a second predicate: "fivefold [and] afflicted-and-non-afflicted."

Metre: Prose (sūtra form).		 The modifications are fivefold, afflicted and non-afflicted.
Vyāsabhāṣya on YS 1.5
[1] kleśahetukāḥ karmāśayapracaye kṣetrībhūtāḥ kliṣṭāḥ. kleśa-hetukāḥ = having the kleśas as their cause (f. nom. pl., bahuvrīhi cpd.: kleśa = affliction [the five: avidyā, asmitā, rāga, dveṣa, abhiniveśa]; hetuka = having as cause, caused by)
karma-āśaya-pracaye = in the accumulation of the karmic deposit (m. loc. sg., tatpuruṣa cpd.: karma = action; āśaya = deposit, storehouse, residue; pracaya = accumulation, heaping up)
kṣetrī-bhūtāḥ = having become the field, serving as the ground (f. nom. pl., compound: kṣetrin = possessing a field, field-like; bhūta = having become, PPP √bhū)
kliṣṭāḥ = afflicted (f. nom. pl.)		 Those [vṛttis] which have the kleśas as their cause and which serve as the field for the accumulation of the karmic deposit — these are kliṣṭa. Vyāsa defines kliṣṭa vṛttis by two criteria: (a) they are caused by kleśas — they arise from ignorance, egoism, attachment, aversion, and clinging to life; (b) they function as the field (kṣetra) in which karmic deposits accumulate. Kliṣṭa vṛttis are thus both the fruit of past kleśas and the seed of future karma. They are simultaneously effect and cause — a self-perpetuating cycle. Those [vṛttis] which have the kleśas as their cause and which serve as the field for the accumulation of the karmic deposit — these are kliṣṭa.
[2] khyātiviṣayā guṇādhikāravirodhinyo 'kliṣṭāḥ. khyāti-viṣayāḥ = having discernment as their object (f. nom. pl., bahuvrīhi cpd.: khyāti = discernment, discriminative knowledge; viṣaya = object, domain)
guṇa-adhikāra-virodhinayaḥ = opposing the dominion of the guṇas (f. nom. pl.; guṇa = the three guṇas; adhikāra = dominion, jurisdiction, power; virodhinī = opposing, obstructing, f. adj.)
akliṣṭāḥ = non-afflicted (f. nom. pl.)		 Those [vṛttis] whose object is discernment, which oppose the dominion of the guṇas — these are akliṣṭa. Akliṣṭa vṛttis are the mirror image of kliṣṭa ones. Instead of being rooted in kleśas, they are oriented towards khyāti (discernment — ultimately, vivekakhyāti). Instead of accumulating karma, they oppose the guṇas' hold over the mind. These are the vṛttis that the yogin cultivates — right knowledge, meditative insight, the progressive discrimination between puruṣa and prakṛti. The word virodhinī is strong: akliṣṭa vṛttis do not merely ignore the guṇas, they actively oppose their dominion. Those [vṛttis] whose object is discernment, which oppose the dominion of the guṇas — these are akliṣṭa.
[3] kliṣṭapravāhapatitā apy akliṣṭāḥ. kliṣṭa-pravāha-patitāḥ = fallen into the stream of kliṣṭa [vṛttis] (f. nom. pl., compound: kliṣṭa = afflicted; pravāha = stream, current, flow; patita = fallen, PPP √pat)
api = even (indecl.)
akliṣṭāḥ = non-afflicted (f. nom. pl.)		 Even [those vṛttis] fallen into the stream of kliṣṭa [vṛttis] are [still] akliṣṭa. A crucial clarification. An akliṣṭa vṛtti does not lose its nature merely because it occurs amidst a flow of afflicted thoughts. A moment of genuine discernment (khyāti) remains akliṣṭa even when it arises in a mind otherwise dominated by kleśas. The classification is intrinsic to the vṛtti, not determined by its context. This is encouraging for the practitioner: even small moments of clarity within a turbulent mind are genuinely liberating. Even [those vṛttis] fallen into the stream of kliṣṭa [vṛttis] are [still] akliṣṭa.
[4–5] kliṣṭacchidreṣv apy akliṣṭā bhavanti. akliṣṭacchidreṣu kliṣṭā iti. kliṣṭa-chidreṣu = in the gaps of the kliṣṭa [vṛttis] (n. loc. pl.; kliṣṭa = afflicted; chidra = gap, opening, interstice)
api = also, too (indecl.)
akliṣṭāḥ = non-afflicted (f. nom. pl.)
bhavanti = arise, exist (3rd pl. pres. √bhū)
akliṣṭa-chidreṣu = in the gaps of the akliṣṭa [vṛttis] (n. loc. pl.)
kliṣṭāḥ = afflicted (f. nom. pl.)
iti = thus (quotative)		 In the gaps of the kliṣṭa [vṛttis], akliṣṭa [vṛttis] arise. [And] in the gaps of the akliṣṭa [vṛttis], kliṣṭa [vṛttis] [arise]. Vyāsa describes the phenomenology of the mind as an interleaving of kliṣṭa and akliṣṭa vṛttis. The mind is never purely one or the other — afflicted and non-afflicted modifications alternate, each inserting itself into the gaps (chidra) of the other. This is a remarkably realistic picture of mental life: even the most distracted mind has moments of clarity; even the most concentrated mind has moments of disturbance. The yogin's task is to increase the proportion of akliṣṭa vṛttis until they crowd out the kliṣṭa ones entirely. In the gaps of the kliṣṭa [vṛttis], akliṣṭa [vṛttis] arise. [And] in the gaps of the akliṣṭa [vṛttis], kliṣṭa [vṛttis] [arise].
[6–7] tathājātīyakāḥ saṃskārā vṛttibhir eva kriyante. saṃskāraiś ca vṛttaya iti. tathā-jātīyakāḥ = of that same kind, of the same class (m. nom. pl.; tathā = thus, in that way; jātīyaka = belonging to that kind/class, from jāti = class, kind)
saṃskārāḥ = latent impressions, subliminal residues (m. nom. pl.)
vṛttibhiḥ = by the vṛttis (f. inst. pl.)
eva = indeed, themselves, alone (indecl.)
kriyante = are produced, are made (3rd pl. pres. pass. √kṛ)
saṃskāraiḥ = by the saṃskāras (m. inst. pl.)
ca = and (indecl.)
vṛttayaḥ = the vṛttis (f. nom. pl.)
iti = thus (quotative)		 Saṃskāras of that same kind are produced by the vṛttis themselves. And by the saṃskāras, vṛttis [are produced]. Here Vyāsa articulates the engine of saṃsāra: vṛttis produce saṃskāras of the same kind (tathājātīyaka), and those saṃskāras in turn produce new vṛttis. Kliṣṭa vṛttis produce kliṣṭa saṃskāras, which produce more kliṣṭa vṛttis. Akliṣṭa vṛttis likewise produce akliṣṭa saṃskāras, which produce more akliṣṭa vṛttis. Each stream is self-reinforcing. This is the mechanism behind both bondage and liberation: bad habits deepen, but so do good ones. Saṃskāras of that same kind are produced by the vṛttis themselves. And by the saṃskāras, vṛttis [are produced].
[8] evaṃ vṛttisaṃskāracakram aniśam āvartate. evam = thus, in this way (indecl.)
vṛtti-saṃskāra-cakram = the wheel of vṛttis and saṃskāras (n. nom. sg., compound: vṛtti = modification; saṃskāra = latent impression; cakra = wheel)
aniśam = without cease, constantly, unceasingly (indecl.; a + niśa, lit. "without respite/night")
āvartate = revolves, turns around (3rd sg. pres. ātm. ā + √vṛt)		 Thus the wheel of vṛttis and saṃskāras revolves without cease. The image of the cakra (wheel) is powerful. The vṛtti-saṃskāra cycle is circular and self-sustaining — vṛttis lay down saṃskāras, which sprout new vṛttis, which lay down further saṃskāras, endlessly. The word aniśam ("without cease") emphasises the relentlessness of this process. Notably, āvartate uses the same root √vṛt as vṛtti itself — the "turnings" of the mind "turn" the wheel. The only way to break the wheel is nirodha: when vṛttis cease, the saṃskāric fuel is gradually exhausted. Thus the wheel of vṛttis and saṃskāras revolves without cease.
[9] tad evaṃbhūtaṃ cittam avasitādhikāram ātmakalpena vyavatiṣṭhate pralayaṃ vā gacchatīti. tad = that (n. nom. sg.)
evam-bhūtam = being of such a nature, being thus constituted (n. nom. sg.; evam = thus; bhūta = being, having become)
cittam = the mind (n. nom. sg.)
avasita-adhikāram = whose function is concluded (n. nom. sg., bahuvrīhi cpd.: avasita = concluded, finished, PPP ava + √so; adhikāra = function, jurisdiction, role)
ātma-kalpena = as if it were the self, self-like (inst. of manner; ātma = self; kalpena = by resemblance, as if — inst. sg. of kalpa in the sense "in the manner of")
vyavatiṣṭhate = subsists, stands firm, remains established (3rd sg. pres. ātm. vi + ava + √sthā)
pralayam = to dissolution, to cosmic dissolution (m. acc. sg.; pra + √lī — reabsorption into undifferentiated prakṛti)
vā = or (indecl.)
gacchati = goes (3rd sg. pres. √gam)
iti = thus (quotative)		 That mind, being of such a nature, when its function is concluded, either subsists as if it were the self, or goes to dissolution. Vyāsa describes the two possible fates of the mind when its active role ends. Either: (a) it vyavatiṣṭhate ātmakalpena — "subsists as if it were the self" — in kaivalya, where buddhi, having served its purpose of revealing puruṣa's distinctness, stands in puruṣa's likeness (luminous, transparent, no longer an obscurant); or (b) it pralayam gacchati — "goes to dissolution" — is reabsorbed into prakṛti during cosmic dissolution (pralaya), only to re-emerge in the next creation cycle. The first is liberation; the second is merely a cosmic pause. The phrase ātmakalpena ("as if the self") is carefully qualified — the mind resembles the self but never becomes it; the Sāṃkhya-Yoga dualism is preserved. That mind, being of such a nature, when its function is concluded, either subsists as if it were the self, or goes to dissolution.
[10] tāḥ kliṣṭāś cākliṣṭāś ca pañcadhā vṛttayaḥ. tāḥ = those (f. nom. pl.)
kliṣṭāḥ = afflicted (f. nom. pl.)
ca = and (indecl.)
akliṣṭāḥ = non-afflicted (f. nom. pl.)
ca = and (indecl.)
pañcadhā = fivefold, in five ways (indecl.; pañca = five; -dhā = suffix of manner "in X ways")
vṛttayaḥ = the modifications (f. nom. pl.)		 Those vṛttis, both kliṣṭa and akliṣṭa, are fivefold. The concluding sentence recapitulates and bridges to sūtra 1.6, which will enumerate the five types. Ca…ca ("both…and") emphasises that the kliṣṭa/akliṣṭa classification cuts across all five types, rather than identifying some types as inherently afflicted and others as inherently non-afflicted. Those vṛttis, both kliṣṭa and akliṣṭa, are fivefold.
Sanskrit Text Word-by-Word Analysis Literal Translation Notes Clean Translation
Sūtra YS 1.6
pramāṇaviparyayavikalpanidrāsmṛtayaḥ || YS_1.6 ||
De-sandhi:
pramāṇa-viparyaya-vikalpa-nidrā-smṛtayaḥ ||		 pramāṇa = valid means of knowledge, right cognition (n. stem in cpd.; pra + √mā — "measuring forth," that by which something is correctly ascertained; the three pramāṇas are listed in YS 1.7: pratyakṣa, anumāna, āgama)
viparyaya = error, misperception, wrong knowledge (m. stem in cpd.; vi + pari + √i — "going around wrongly"; defined in YS 1.8 as false knowledge not based on the real form of the object)
vikalpa = conceptual construction, verbal fabrication, imagination (m. stem in cpd.; vi + √kḷp — "fashioning variously"; defined in YS 1.9 as knowledge that follows from words but has no corresponding reality)
nidrā = sleep, deep sleep (f. stem in cpd.; ni + √drā — "sinking down"; defined in YS 1.10 as the vṛtti whose support is the cognition of absence)
smṛtayaḥ = and memory (f. nom. pl.; smṛti from √smṛ — "to remember"; defined in YS 1.11 as the non-slipping-away of experienced objects; the plural ending -ayaḥ on the final member carries the number for the entire dvandva compound)		 [They are:] valid cognition (pramāṇa), error (viparyaya), conceptual construction (vikalpa), sleep (nidrā), and memory (smṛtayaḥ). Meaning: This sūtra simply enumerates the five types of vṛtti that were announced in 1.5. It is a table of contents for sūtras 1.7–1.11, each of which will define one type. The enumeration itself is significant: every possible mental operation — whether correct knowledge, incorrect knowledge, pure conceptual invention, unconsciousness, or recall — falls under one of these five. There is no sixth category. All mental life, without exception, is covered by this fivefold classification. And all five, as 1.5 established, can be either kliṣṭa or akliṣṭa — giving a 5×2 matrix of ten possible vṛtti-states.

Syntax: A dvandva (copulative) compound. All five stems are in co-ordination: "pramāṇa AND viparyaya AND vikalpa AND nidrā AND smṛti." The final member smṛti takes the nominative plural ending (-ayaḥ) on behalf of the whole compound, marking it as a list of five items. There is no verb — this is a nominal sentence that completes the predication begun in 1.5 (vṛttayaḥ pañcatayyaḥ).

Metre: Prose (sūtra form).

No bhāṣya accompanies this sūtra. Vyāsa treats 1.6 as a mere enumeration, reserving his commentary for the definitions that follow (1.7–1.11).

## Архитектура YS 1.5–1.6

### Двойная классификация

Вьяса предлагает систему, в которой каждая вритти классифицируется по двум осям:

| | Kliṣṭa (поражённая) | Akliṣṭa (непоражённая) | |---|---|---| | Pramāṇa (верное познание) | Верное знание, корни которого — в клешах, и которое накапливает карму | Верное знание, ведущее к различению, противостоящее власти гун | | Viparyaya (заблуждение) | Заблуждение, укоренённое в клешах | Заблуждение, возникающее в контексте практики (напр. ошибки на пути, которые быстро исправляются) | | Vikalpa (конструкция) | Фантазии, порождённые привязанностью и отвращением | Концептуальные построения, направленные к различению | | Nidrā (сон) | Тамасический сон, укрепляющий невежество | Сон, не оставляющий клешических следов | | Smṛti (память) | Воспоминания, питающие привязанность и отвращение | Память о практике, о различении, о йогическом опыте |
